<a id="t-376382caa3"></a>
## Thread: The Fate of COBOL....... — 81 this month / 107 total

_Thread started [1999-05](1999-05.md#t-376382caa3)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Packed decimal was supported in the X86 chips to some extent.
There was a packed decimal load and store which helped to convert
between binary and packed (eg for array indexes).

I did some tests a while back and found on MVS that packed was as
fast as binary. Some shops have packed as standard. 

Don't always assume the worst of people. I am writing a COBOL
compiler - early stages (if one of my anlaysts produced a spec as
vague and ambiguous as the cobol standard I would fire him
immediately)- and I was really annoyed at Intel. My original post
was critical of Intel. I did troll a newsgroup once but that was
a long time ago in a newsgroup far away (one of the MIDI
newsgroups) and I learned my lesson.

Tim Josling


Thane Hubbell wrote:
...
> First, we are assuming the Packed Decimal is not in the chipset.
> Second we assume that at one point it WAS.  Troll bait taken - hook,
> line and sinker.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)

- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>`

```
On Wed, 2 Jun 1999 09:40:52, Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:

> Don't always assume the worst of people. I am writing a COBOL
> compiler - early stages (if one of my anlaysts produced a spec as
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Posted and Mailed

The dinosaurs are a bit defensive I guess.  Sorry :(.  We get weekly 
posts announcing the death of COBOL for this reason or that.

Let's talk about something more interesting in your post.  Tell me 
more about your compiler.  Platform, progress, etc....

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3758B576.4B2CDEDA@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-LKdCWrPmm94R@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Well it's early stages and no promises. It is targeted at Linux
at the moment and COBOL 85. There are several other efforts that
got nowhere so I would prefer to hold fire until I have something
to release (ie nucleus level 1). 

COBOL is a huge language and getting huger (in the new standard).

I understand you don't need the BCD instructions to make it work,
but they sure woudl help to make it fast.

Tim Josling

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> Let's talk about something more interesting in your post.  Tell me
> more about your compiler.  Platform, progress, etc....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote in message <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>...
[snip BCD stuff.]

BTW, hardware BCD support is _not_ necessary for COBOL.  Please, take my
word for it -- and I've used those previously available Intel BCD
instructions.  The speed of your indexed access method is a _lot_ more
important!  (Yes, I know there are compute bound exceptions.)

>I am writing a COBOL
>compiler - early stages (if one of my anlaysts produced a spec as
>vague and ambiguous as the cobol standard I would fire him
>immediately) [snip]

First, much good luck.  If you are working by yourself, I hope you are
fairly young.  Or perhaps you are implementing only the compiler...?

Second, you fail to appreciate the explicit difference between a standard,
such as the COBOL standard, and a specification, such as the Liant
specification for its implementation of COBOL.  In my several years of work
on ANSI committees (in areas other than COBOL) this difference was
constantly emphasized to those who felt compelled to specify too much
explicit implementation detail.

Please, rehire your poor maligned analyst!  :)    License the 1985 COBOL
Compiler Validation Suite (CCVS) (if it is still available) and you will
have a 'specification' that the analyst can use, knowing that most of the
other COBOL compiler vendors have validated their implementations with the
CCVS.  Don't know if there are any plans for a validation suite for the
coming standard.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au>`

Tom Morrison wrote:
> 
> Tim Josling wrote in message <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Why do they need to support it as BCD anyway.  The results must be
correct but could be implemented as anything.  The only problem is
file compatability (if you actually want this and it could be a
compiler option) but if you wrote a BCD translator for the file
access but kept the working storage as binary or whatever is most
efficient for the platform.  Cobol is source code compatible not
binary compatible.

If you talk about 9(06) COMP-3 it does mean different things on
different machines (MVS adds a sign and Vax( or wang) does not)

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375951da.9386243@netnews>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net>`

```
'Twas Thu, 03 Jun 1999 00:47:49 -0400, when William Lynch
<WBLynch@att.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Ken Foskey wrote:
> > 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> hardware spec.

More nits:  It's the IBM compiler that adds a sign -- and an extra digit
when you you specify an even number of them.  The compiler does this in
order to make use of the somewhat deficient hardware implementation, but
don't blame the hardware for the software faults.  Packed decimal can be
(and has been) implemented on hardware with no underlying decimal support
at all.

Of course you can look at things the IBM way:  The IBM software guys say
this is what the hardware guys have implemented.  The IBM hardware guys
say this is what the software guys are using.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375995CF.12E964CA@att.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Thu, 03 Jun 1999 00:47:49 -0400, when William Lynch
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> at all.

I think you're looking in the wrong end of the telescope. IMHO IBM's
COBOL compilers do what they do with Packed-Decimal numbers to
accommodate the programmer's spec, e.g., +123 = x'123c', and, more
importantly, the hardware. Are you suggesting that COBOL/MVS would add
sign nibbles if the hardware didn't require it?

Bill Lynch

PS: The following is from IBM Principles of Operations manual:

"8.2.1 Decimal-Arithmetic Instructions

The decimal-arithmetic instructions perform addition, subtraction,
multiplication, division, comparison, and shifting. 

Operands of the decimal-arithmetic instructions are in the packed format
and are treated as signed decimal integers. A decimal integer is
represented in true form as an absolute value with a separate plus or
minus sign. It contains an odd number of decimal digits, from one to 31,
and the sign; this corresponds to an operand length of one to 16 bytes. 

A decimal zero normally has a plus sign, but multiplication, division,
and overflow may produce a zero value with a minus sign. Such a negative
zero is a valid operand and is treated as equal to a positive zero by
COMPARE DECIMAL. "

(Appendix A - please note the first sentence and let us know if you
still maintain that the compilers "add a sign" because IBM is a bad
joke):

A.1.2 Decimal Integers

Decimal integers consist of one or more decimal digits and a sign. Each
digit and the sign are represented by a 4-bit code. The decimal digits
are in binary-coded decimal (BCD) form, with the values 0-9 encoded as
0000-1001. The sign is usually represented as 1100 (C hex) for plus and
1101 (D hex) for minus. These are the preferred sign codes, which are
generated by the machine for the results of decimal-arithmetic
operations. There are also several alternate sign codes (1010, 1110, and
1111 for plus; 1011 for minus). The alternate sign codes are accepted by
the machine as valid in source operands but are not generated for
results. 

Decimal integers may have different lengths, from one to 16 bytes. There
are two decimal formats: packed and zoned. In the packed format, each
byte contains two decimal digits, except for the rightmost byte, which
contains the sign code in the right half. For decimal arithmetic, the
number of decimal digits in the packed format can vary from one to 31.
Because decimal integers must consist of whole bytes and there must be a
sign code on the right, the number of decimal digits is always odd. If
an even number of significant digits is desired, a leading zero must be
inserted on the left. 

In the zoned format, each byte consists of a decimal digit on the right
and the zone code 1111 (F hex) on the left, except for the rightmost
byte where the sign code replaces the zone code. Thus, a decimal integer
in the zoned format can have from one to 16 digits. The zoned format may
be used directly for input and output in the extended
binary-coded-decimal interchange code (EBCDIC), except that the sign
must be separated from the rightmost digit and handled as a separate
character. For positive (unsigned) numbers, however, the sign can simply
be represented by the zone code of the rightmost digit because the zone
code is one of the acceptable alternate codes for plus. 

In either format, negative decimal integers are represented in true
notation with a separate sign. As for binary integers, the radix point
(decimal point) of decimal integers is considered to be fixed at the
right, and any scaling is done by the programmer. 

The following are some examples of decimal integers shown in hexadecimal
notation: 

   Decimal 
   Value        Packed Format     Zoned Format 

   +123         12 3C             F1 F2 C3 

                or                or 

                12 3F             F1 F2 F3"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net>...
>Randall Bart wrote:
>>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[Note: The prior statements, in this thread, concerned unsigned numbers.
The above example is for a signed number.]

I agree with Randall Bart.

Consider that 9(06) PACKED-DECIMAL could be stored in three bytes
on an IBM mainframe. Neither the sign nor the extra, leading zero digit
are required to conform to the standard. However, the compiler adds
these, I assume, because the underlying hardware has no unsigned
packed decimal instructions. It is more efficient to use a representation
that will work with the existing signed instructions than to emulate
unsigned
packed decimal arithmetic, on IBM.

For those who might argue that a sign value of "F" means unsigned; that is
not the meaning of unsigned within the COBOL standard.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net> <7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick,
  It is true that in some COBOL environments
     Pic 9(06) Packed-Decimal
can be stored in 3 bytes.  HOWEVER, the Standard is absolutely clear that
this is either implementor defined or hardware dependent. (I could look up
which - but it doesn't matter for the sake of this discussion.)

What the IBM mainframe does - which is reasonable and actually helpful in
*SOME* cases is to make a distinction between a X"F" sign nibble and a X"C"
sign-nibble.  The former gives the explicit information that the field
contains *no* sign - while the latter contains the information that the
field contains a positive sign.  In most PC and UNIX environments (and some
mainframe ones) there is no difference between the sign-nibble for an
unsigned value and a positive value.

Although it is not particularly useful information in *MOST* cases, this
distinction can be useful in others (and a I have written IBM Mainframe
COBOL applications that use this distinction).

Now, there are *ALSO* environments that allow a positive sign-nibble to be
distinguished from *no* sign nibble at all - but those are by far the
minority.  I can see some use for that - in the days when storage was at a
premium - but personally can see little advantage today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jhb13$jtu$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net> <7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>...
>Rick,
>  It is true that in some COBOL environments
>     Pic 9(06) Packed-Decimal
>can be stored in 3 bytes.  [...]

I think it to be true in most environments; not just some. ;-)

>What the IBM mainframe does - which is reasonable and actually helpful in
>*SOME* cases is to make a distinction between a X"F" sign nibble and a X"C"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>unsigned value and a positive value.

The picture clause already provides explicit information, to the
compiler, that the field contains no sign; therefore, the presence
of a sign indicator for an unsigned data item is both redundant
and incongruous.

In most PC and Unix environments, the use of the IBM hardware
format for packed-decimal is so horribly inefficient that packed-
decimal should not be used. Given that, any discussion of the
format, in those environments, is a "deck chairs" and "Titanic"
kind-of thing. ;-)

[...]
>Now, there are *ALSO* environments that allow a positive sign-nibble to be
>distinguished from *no* sign nibble at all - but those are by far the
>minority.  I can see some use for that - in the days when storage was at a
>premium - but personally can see little advantage today.

Speaking of storage, is there any text in CD 1.5 which prohibits
(or permits) an implementation of packed-decimal where the
decimal digits are allocated as one digit per byte (octet).
Consider that the additional bits are required for holding
intermediate (unnormalized) values during operations on these
data items. This seems the most efficient representation for
processors which have no decimal arithmetic.

I did not find anything to show that such an implementation
would be "non-conforming;" but, I was not looking for "NO"
as an answer.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Speaking of storage, is there any text in CD 1.5 which prohibits
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> processors which have no decimal arithmetic.

Or even extended binary so that up to 15 digits can comfortably
fit into the defined field but it is maximally efficient for the
platform.

The only problem with this would be the expectation on onversion
from other systems. If you are only working on the one compiler
then this is not an issue.  If you specify all interfaces are
clear text (best for ASCII / EBCDIC translation anyway) there is
no problem.

Ken
```

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375ec060.86067@netnews>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net> <7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7jhb13$jtu$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 7 Jun 1999 16:43:40 -0400, when "Rick Smith"
<ricksmith@aiservices.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Speaking of storage, is there any text in CD 1.5 which prohibits
> (or permits) an implementation of packed-decimal where the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> processors which have no decimal arithmetic.

An implementor is free to implement BIANRY to mean packed decimal and
PACKED-DECIMAL as binary, if he so chooses.  I have heard of a compiler
which implemented PACKED-DECIMAL the same as DISPLAY.  Note also that COMP
is the same as BINARY in some compilers and the same as PACKED-DECIMAL in
others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375f0202.16866808@netnews>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net> <7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Sat, 5 Jun 1999 19:59:39 -0700, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@inospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> What the IBM mainframe does - which is reasonable and actually helpful in
> *SOME* cases is to make a distinction between a X"F" sign nibble and a X"C"
> sign-nibble.  The former gives the explicit information that the field
> contains *no* sign - while the latter contains the information that the
> field contains a positive sign.

How is this useful?  By this logic, every binary field should have one or
more bits to indicate that it's binary, every alphanumeric field should
have a byte to indicate it's alphanumeric.  It's the Cobol code which
designates the format.  If the PIC says it's unsigned the compiler should
allocate exactly zero bits to say as much.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jn7mo$t0s@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net> <7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <7jco8q$2pi@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <375f0202.16866808@netnews>`

```
The distinction between unsigned data and positive data is when passing the
data (in a file or CALL statement) to a procedure written in a NON-COBOL
language - where there is no "PICTURE" to tell you how to interpret it.
```

'Twas Wed, 9 Jun 1999 21:25:21 -0500, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> The distinction between unsigned data and positive data is when passing the
> data (in a file or CALL statement) to a procedure written in a NON-COBOL
> language - where there is no "PICTURE" to tell you how to interpret it.

Either the other program "knows" the format or it doesn't.  If it doesn't
then you need some bits to specify that a field is BINARY or DISPLAY, and
how many bytes long, and how many decimal positions, etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Date: Sat, 5 Jun 1999 22:29:59 -0400
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

posted the following useful distinction

<< Consider that 9(06) PACKED-DECIMAL could be stored in three bytes
on an IBM mainframe. Neither the sign nor the extra, leading zero digit
are required to conform to the standard. However, the compiler adds
these, I assume, because the underlying hardware has no unsigned
packed decimal instructions. It is more efficient to use a representation
that will work with the existing signed instructions than to emulate
unsigned
packed decimal arithmetic, on IBM.

For those who might argue that a sign value of "F" means unsigned; that is
not the meaning of unsigned within the COBOL standard.

>>

In some shops any BCD-like field that has been manipulated to strip off a sign
(such as a constant hex-F) is referred to as a "Nosigned" field to make it
clear that we are not merely dealing with "unsigned". (But nomenclature
varies).

It is certainly worth noting that the exact example Rick mentions, 
   PIC 9(06) PACKED-DECIMAL 
is frequently the source of some very expensive retrofitting these days; as a
yymmdd date value can be stored in this manner in three bytes rather than four.
On files with mulitple dates, and millions of records, the difference makes a
difference.

But the IBM mainframe environments do not support nosigned in hardware. All the
code to manipulate the sign out for storage, and back in for calculation or
compares is custom code. 

This is a factor that impacts a very large portions of the keystrokes being
poured into the Y2K solutions. Since we are applying, in many cases, junior
programmer skills to date remediation work, the date fields and related
algorithms are much harder to understand because of the cloud of manipulations
to provide efficient storage.

The lack of hardware support for nosigned BCD in the IBM mainframe environments
also has a manifold impact on the highest level planning.  Whatever motivated
the originators of 3-byte 9(6) fields to conserve space still motivates them in
199x. And now, of course, we do not have time to do extra stuff like convert
files and expanded record sizes or impact down stream systems unnecessarily. 

The result is megabucks spent to convert 3-byte 9(6) to 4-byte 9(8) and or
3-byte 9(6) to 3-byte nonnumeric that has hex values to reflect a decade beyond
the ninth. The 'A0' solutions will definitely cause enough 0C7's to motivate
management to reverse field early in the next decade and get numerics,
ESPECIALLY DOLLAR FIELDS, back into a format directly supported by hardware or
atleast supported by the comilers.

((Lets count, we paid the original coders to manipulate the sign out add
$coding + $testing.  We pay the Y2K folks a little bit extra just to understand
the tangental code and get down to the date values per se, add $Y2K bonus.  We
will pay the third millenium coders to reanalyze this junk and convert to
hardware supported or compiler supported numerics, add $tommorows bonus.  To
save space we paid three times.  Isn't space hardware? Isn't hardware cheaper
than personnel? Apparently not at the project level. (Translation, most
managers utterly lack systems analysis skills). ))

The presence or absence of hardware support for a particular numeric format
hooks directly into confidence about the reliability of the system.

Speed is really not the issue.

IMHO the compiler writer will have to provide ALL of the support for BCD
formats.  We still need the confidence.

In the BCD subject area we have several varieties of 'PACKED' - signed, dummy
signed, nosigned, and the Intel 80-bit BCD (to name a few).  Someone please
help me here, I think the data coming from the Digital environments is like the
Intel format but can vary in length, is that not true?

In the area of floating point we have atleast two standards, the IEEE single
and double precision, and the IBM mainframe hex floating point. For some time,
compiler writters may find both of these supported in IBM mainframe hardware.

Because we are actually dealling with convergence, COBOL compiler writers
probably need to accept the gruesome fate that you  will need to support
multiple varieties of BCD, atleast semantically if not by syntax extensions in
the future. Think net, think connected, think adapt to all possible formats. If
you can adjust you can survive. If a purpose of the GNU COBOL project is to
make linux even more acceptable, think adapt - don't think reject one format or
the other - expand your horizons: plan to cover them all, even if early
achievements are for a particular platform.

For purposes of optimization, format may be less important than field boundary
alignment of data items. That is somewhat foreign to COBOL, and will
necessitate cultural change. Early compilers may be able to do some things with
Working Storage to enhance efficiency of numeric field references, but will be
greatly constrained on the IO record images (for a long time).

RISC machines like their instructions fixed length and boundary aligned. They
like their data boundary aligned also.  As a consequence it is not a bad first
approach to assume that every numeric field refence has to be (maybe ought to
be) an algorithm of atleast several instructions to get the data into an
aligned location and subsequently back out. 

Eventually the code generator will need numerous adaptors for the variety of
BCD that is out there.  It is useful to remember that the worth of a system is
in the data itself. This is data processing, not program processing. Data and
(even its existing) format are actually sacred. Our sense of reliability is in
large part a belief that the data we view is correct. Tolerating variance
because of format considerations is not the same as having full faith in a
result.

The absence of hardware support for a particular BCD format in the modern chips
teaches us a valuable lesson.  No BCD format is right.  If we are to create
software that works well in a connected diverse context, we will have to deal
with numerous formats.

I believe that it is worth enough millions of dollars, that it approaches
atleast one billion dollars, to get the unsigned / nosigned coding task out of
the hands of application programmers and support it as a syntactic distinction
in the compilers.  It is probably not a bad idea to lay the ground work for
this in the GNU COBOL project.  The savings to business would pay for the
project many times over.

A hidden problem in this situation is that C did not originate as a language
that handles BCD (The IBM mainframe C compiler uses language extensions). So to
a large extent the compiler writer is blind to BCD.  The compiler writer,
coding in C, is certainly blind to _varieties_ of BCD.  The fault here is not
just with COBOL, or one platform or the other. We kind of need to go back to
Kernighan and Ritchie  and ask them, ever so humbly, if they didn't maybe leave
something out.

One further note: it may be useful to use the term fixed-point decimal
arithmetic instead of BCD format or BCD arithmetic:

consider this note extracted verbatim from

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/BOOKS/CBCMGI01/5%2e9?SHELF
=CBCMBS01

"Fixed-point   - 
 C/MVS supports fixed-point decimal (packed) as a   Decimal (Packed)   native
data type for use in business  Data   applications. The packed data type is
similar to the COBOL data type COMP-3 or the PL/I data type FIXED DEC, with up
to 31 digits of precision.    "

The reason I suggest consideration of alternate vocabulary is that we need to
lay down a smooth path for the future programmers who will work in this
context.  So it is most important to distinguish floating point from fixed
point arithmetic in terms of the meaning and reliability of the values
generated.  Then, a further distinction relating (in the future) mostly to
format not hardware, can be recognized between binary and decimal fixed point
arithmetic. (Here it is the difference in calculation rules that counts! Not
the format).

Within the distinction between binary and decimal arithmetic, we see very
important differences in things like rounding rules.

As we step through the doorway into the world of the third millenium
programmers, we might choose to avoid the obfuscation that the term BCD
introduces.  The acronym has the word 'binary' AND the word 'decimal'.  It may
be easier to make things clear for future programmers if we call it by a name
that has only 'decimal' in it: so fixed point decimal, or decimal fixed point.
Regrettably abbreviations FPD and DFP do not look too distinct from
abbreviations for floating point (FP)!

Generally, to avoid a tower of Babel effect, business data processing
professionals will have to have arithmetic functions that display decimal
behavior. So, although it is not readily apparent in a world viewed by compiler
writers using C, the world really does have three numeric formats: floating
point, fixed point binary, and fixed point decimal.  And there is definitely
one realy gigantic amount of value stored in fields that human expect to be
processed according to decimal conventions. And so, although this thread is not
drifting through the C language newsgroups, the real problem here is much
larger than the COBOL language.

We need to have syntax, semantics and teaching tools that easily distinguish
three numeric data types, in all languages. And to deal with hardware
idiosyncracies we probably need COBOL facilities that compensate for a lot of
variations on fixed point decimal storage format.

With respect to the need to deal with three numeric formats, COBOL is
lightyears ahead of other languages. If GNU COBOL and linux are to be near the
center of the mainstream of e-commerce, there will be a need for a long list of
adaptors for the fields once called by the various names BCD. With reference to
any popular BCD format linux and GNU COBOL really can not say 'no'. 
Compatibility means 'yes'. Welcome to the Admiral Dr. Grace Murray Hopper's 
world of COmmon Business Oriented Language.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
9(6) PACKED takes up 4 bytes on IBM (actually 3.5 bytes rounded
up to four).
So I'm at a loss when you talk about "3-byte" 9(6).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37670eab.18610127@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Tue, 15 Jun 1999 18:08:59 -0500, when "Leif Svalgaard"
<lsvalg@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> 9(6) PACKED takes up 4 bytes on IBM (actually 3.5 bytes rounded
> up to four).
> So I'm at a loss when you talk about "3-byte" 9(6).

On non-IBM systems, which don't give you a sign on an unsigned field, PIC
9(6) PACKED-DECIMAL occupies three bytes.  Only IBM and those who imitate
IBM give you a sign when you don't ask for it.

On Burroughs/Unisys computers PIC 9(5) PACKED-DECIMAL occupies two and a
half bytes.  This is less common, there are many implementors who use
three bytes to represent PIC 9(5) PACKED-DECIMAL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00

'Twas Wed, 16 Jun 1999 00:01:58 -0500, when "Leif Svalgaard"
<lsvalg@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Sure, and on GCOS8 it is also 3 bytes, but the original poster had this to
> say:
> > << Consider that 9(06) PACKED-DECIMAL could be stored in three bytes
> > on an IBM mainframe

You're cutting him off to early.  He said 

> << Consider that 9(06) PACKED-DECIMAL could be stored in three bytes
> on an IBM mainframe. Neither the sign nor the extra, leading zero digit
> are required to conform to the standard. However, the compiler adds
> these, I assume, because the underlying hardware has no unsigned
> packed decimal instructions. 

He's saying it _could be_ but it isn't.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k8qka$4os@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net> <37670eab.18610127@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote in message
news:37670eab.18610127@netnews...
> 'Twas Tue, 15 Jun 1999 18:08:59 -0500, when "Leif Svalgaard"
> <lsvalg@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM give you a sign when you don't ask for it.

I would be curious to hear from non-IBM COBOL users.  When I last did
research on this , there was actually only one vendor (UNISYS as I recalled)
that did NOT use 4 bytes to store a Pic 9(06) Usage Packed-Decimal field.
All other vendors (not just IBM) did require the 4 bytes.  I know at least
one vendor (I can't remember which) had a COMP-6 usage (but not Usage
Packed-Decimal) that could store it in 3 bytes.

On the other hand, it may be that most of the vendors that I ever looked at
were "emulating" IBM which was so "popular" in the earlier days of COBOL.

Bottom-line:
  Which vendors do (in their own native mode) support Pic 9(06)
Packed-Decimal in 3 bytes (and which in 4 bytes).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-t5grhRvQMdS7@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net> <37670eab.18610127@netnews> <7k8qka$4os@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
On Wed, 16 Jun 1999 18:32:23, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> Bottom-line:
>   Which vendors do (in their own native mode) support Pic 9(06)
> Packed-Decimal in 3 bytes (and which in 4 bytes).
> 
>

I've seen plenty of IBM code where the date is stored in 3 bytes by 
moving the data  to a comp-3 field, multiplying by 10 and then using  
the redefined first 3 bytes.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376859b9.16268510@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net> <37670eab.18610127@netnews> <7k8qka$4os@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-t5grhRvQMdS7@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
'Twas 16 Jun 1999 19:00:17 GMT, when redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> On Wed, 16 Jun 1999 18:32:23, "William M. Klein" 
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the redefined first 3 bytes.

Multiplying?  Isn't decimal scaling more efficient?

 01  SIGN-STRIP-AREA.
     05  SIX-DIGITS-SIGNED           PIC S9(6)V9  COMP.
 01      REDEFINES SIGN-STRIP-AREA.
     05  SIX-DIGITS-UNSIGNED         PIC XXX.
     05  FILLER                      PIC X.

Move the data into SIX-DIGITS-SIGNED and out of SIX-DIGITS-UNSIGNED, or
vice versa.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-pgCD5vR4Zd3S@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net> <37670eab.18610127@netnews> <7k8qka$4os@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-t5grhRvQMdS7@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <376859b9.16268510@netnews>`

```
On Thu, 17 Jun 1999 02:46:18, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart) 
wrote:


> Multiplying?  Isn't decimal scaling more efficient?
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> vice versa.

Yes of course, but you have to see this code we acquired to believe 
it.  Most of it is Junk and the rest is trash.
-----------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37686262.18482317@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com> <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net> <37670eab.18610127@netnews> <7k8qka$4os@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-t5grhRvQMdS7@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <376859b9.16268510@netnews>`

```
'Twas Thu, 17 Jun 1999 02:46:18 GMT, when Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall
Bart) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

>  01  SIGN-STRIP-AREA.
>      05  SIX-DIGITS-SIGNED           PIC S9(6)V9  COMP.

OOPS!  In Unisys COMP is packed.  Make that:

 01  SIGN-STRIP-AREA.
     05  SIX-DIGITS-SIGNED           PIC S9(6)V9  PACKED-DECIMAL.
 01      REDEFINES SIGN-STRIP-AREA.
     05  SIX-DIGITS-UNSIGNED         PIC XXX.
     05  FILLER                      PIC X.

Move the data into SIX-DIGITS-SIGNED and out of SIX-DIGITS-UNSIGNED, or
vice versa.
```

PIC
> > 9(6) PACKED-DECIMAL occupies three bytes.  Only IBM and those who
imitate

regardless of the underlying hardware requirements is one of the things that
makes COBOL portable.

Rick

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3766dd09@news3.us.ibm.net>...
>9(6) PACKED takes up 4 bytes on IBM (actually 3.5 bytes rounded
>up to four).
>So I'm at a loss when you talk about "3-byte" 9(6).

At that point in the thread, I was discussing the "possibilities"
that would meet the requirements of the COBOL standard
versus the "reality" of IBM mainframe hardware and, more
importantly, the IBM COBOL compiler.

9(6) Packed-Decimal could be stored in 3 bytes; but it is not.

To see that, in context, I would ask that you review my post,
of June 5, on Deja or RemarQ.
------------------
Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Third Millenium Fixed Point Decimal

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fnTa3.8619$A1.810@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<7jcmga$flq$1@server.cntfl.com> <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
First, let me take the wind out of your sails!
DOS which predated MVS JCL was written in the late 50's and early 60's.
Machines and machint TIME was EXPENSIVE compared to programmers.
Disk technology was new and many systems ran TOS! (Tape OS).

The reason Packed Decimal was used is that it is HUMAN-Readable.
Management didn't want to use STRICT BINARY representation because
of the time it took to convert the '1001010110001010100111101'
into 1960, July 1 by hand (they didn't have these smart calculators back
then)

There is really only ONE UNSIGNED Number in the UNIVERSE -- ZERO since it
HAS NO VALUE, It can be NEITHER NEGATIVE OR POSITIVE.

The 'F' means, NO SIGN FOUND,  ASSUMED POSITIVE!  Not UnSigned.
The only way to get an 'F' sign, (in either DISPLAY or P-D format) is to
move a number
from a PIC 9(NN) USAGE DISPLAY  to a PIC [S] 9(NN) COMP-3 or
PIC S9(NN) USAGE DISPLAY.

Since the math routines can't find a sign, it figures that the value is an
'absolute' value which
is always Postive.


All Dates within -2147483648 to 2147483647
that's                    well past 10,000 BC thru 99,999 AD
could be represented within 4 (Four) Bytes in strict BINARY.

RKRayhawk wrote in message <19990615154612.01896.00000156@ng-cd1.aol.com>...
>Date: Sat, 5 Jun 1999 22:29:59 -0400
>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>In some shops any BCD-like field that has been manipulated to strip off a
sign
>(such as a constant hex-F) is referred to as a "Nosigned" field to make it
>clear that we are not merely dealing with "unsigned". (But nomenclature
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>is frequently the source of some very expensive retrofitting these days; as
a
>yymmdd date value can be stored in this manner in three bytes rather than
four.
>On files with mulitple dates, and millions of records, the difference makes
a
>difference.
>
>But the IBM mainframe environments do not support nosigned in hardware. All
the
>code to manipulate the sign out for storage, and back in for calculation or
>compares is custom code.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>algorithms are much harder to understand because of the cloud of
manipulations
>to provide efficient storage.
>
>The lack of hardware support for nosigned BCD in the IBM mainframe
environments
>also has a manifold impact on the highest level planning.  Whatever
motivated
>the originators of 3-byte 9(6) fields to conserve space still motivates
them in
>199x. And now, of course, we do not have time to do extra stuff like
convert
>files and expanded record sizes or impact down stream systems
unnecessarily.
>
>The result is megabucks spent to convert 3-byte 9(6) to 4-byte 9(8) and or
>3-byte 9(6) to 3-byte nonnumeric that has hex values to reflect a decade
beyond
>the ninth. The 'A0' solutions will definitely cause enough 0C7's to
motivate
>management to reverse field early in the next decade and get numerics,
>ESPECIALLY DOLLAR FIELDS, back into a format directly supported by hardware
or
>atleast supported by the comilers.
>
>((Lets count, we paid the original coders to manipulate the sign out add
>$coding + $testing.  We pay the Y2K folks a little bit extra just to
understand
>the tangental code and get down to the date values per se, add $Y2K bonus.
We
>will pay the third millenium coders to reanalyze this junk and convert to
>hardware supported or compiler supported numerics, add $tommorows bonus.
To
>save space we paid three times.  Isn't space hardware? Isn't hardware
cheaper
>than personnel? Apparently not at the project level. (Translation, most
>managers utterly lack systems analysis skills). ))
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>In the BCD subject area we have several varieties of 'PACKED' - signed,
dummy
>signed, nosigned, and the Intel 80-bit BCD (to name a few).  Someone please
>help me here, I think the data coming from the Digital environments is like
the
>Intel format but can vary in length, is that not true?
>
>In the area of floating point we have atleast two standards, the IEEE
single
>and double precision, and the IBM mainframe hex floating point. For some
time,
>compiler writters may find both of these supported in IBM mainframe
hardware.
>
>Because we are actually dealling with convergence, COBOL compiler writers
>probably need to accept the gruesome fate that you  will need to support
>multiple varieties of BCD, atleast semantically if not by syntax extensions
in
>the future. Think net, think connected, think adapt to all possible
formats. If
>you can adjust you can survive. If a purpose of the GNU COBOL project is to
>make linux even more acceptable, think adapt - don't think reject one
format or
>the other - expand your horizons: plan to cover them all, even if early
>achievements are for a particular platform.
>
>For purposes of optimization, format may be less important than field
boundary
>alignment of data items. That is somewhat foreign to COBOL, and will
>necessitate cultural change. Early compilers may be able to do some things
with
>Working Storage to enhance efficiency of numeric field references, but will
be
>greatly constrained on the IO record images (for a long time).
>
>RISC machines like their instructions fixed length and boundary aligned.
They
>like their data boundary aligned also.  As a consequence it is not a bad
first
>approach to assume that every numeric field refence has to be (maybe ought
to
>be) an algorithm of atleast several instructions to get the data into an
>aligned location and subsequently back out.
>
>Eventually the code generator will need numerous adaptors for the variety
of
>BCD that is out there.  It is useful to remember that the worth of a system
is
>in the data itself. This is data processing, not program processing. Data
and
>(even its existing) format are actually sacred. Our sense of reliability is
in
>large part a belief that the data we view is correct. Tolerating variance
>because of format considerations is not the same as having full faith in a
>result.
>
>The absence of hardware support for a particular BCD format in the modern
chips
>teaches us a valuable lesson.  No BCD format is right.  If we are to create
>software that works well in a connected diverse context, we will have to
deal
>with numerous formats.
>
>I believe that it is worth enough millions of dollars, that it approaches
>atleast one billion dollars, to get the unsigned / nosigned coding task out
of
>the hands of application programmers and support it as a syntactic
distinction
>in the compilers.  It is probably not a bad idea to lay the ground work for
>this in the GNU COBOL project.  The savings to business would pay for the
>project many times over.
>
>A hidden problem in this situation is that C did not originate as a
language
>that handles BCD (The IBM mainframe C compiler uses language extensions).
So to
>a large extent the compiler writer is blind to BCD.  The compiler writer,
>coding in C, is certainly blind to _varieties_ of BCD.  The fault here is
not
>just with COBOL, or one platform or the other. We kind of need to go back
to
>Kernighan and Ritchie  and ask them, ever so humbly, if they didn't maybe
leave
>something out.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> C/MVS supports fixed-point decimal (packed) as a   Decimal (Packed)
native
>data type for use in business  Data   applications. The packed data type is
>similar to the COBOL data type COMP-3 or the PL/I data type FIXED DEC, with
up
>to 31 digits of precision.    "
>
>The reason I suggest consideration of alternate vocabulary is that we need
to
>lay down a smooth path for the future programmers who will work in this
>context.  So it is most important to distinguish floating point from fixed
>point arithmetic in terms of the meaning and reliability of the values
>generated.  Then, a further distinction relating (in the future) mostly to
>format not hardware, can be recognized between binary and decimal fixed
point
>arithmetic. (Here it is the difference in calculation rules that counts!
Not
>the format).
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>introduces.  The acronym has the word 'binary' AND the word 'decimal'.  It
may
>be easier to make things clear for future programmers if we call it by a
name
>that has only 'decimal' in it: so fixed point decimal, or decimal fixed
point.
>Regrettably abbreviations FPD and DFP do not look too distinct from
>abbreviations for floating point (FP)!
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>behavior. So, although it is not readily apparent in a world viewed by
compiler
>writers using C, the world really does have three numeric formats: floating
>point, fixed point binary, and fixed point decimal.  And there is
definitely
>one realy gigantic amount of value stored in fields that human expect to be
>processed according to decimal conventions. And so, although this thread is
not
>drifting through the C language newsgroups, the real problem here is much
>larger than the COBOL language.
>
>We need to have syntax, semantics and teaching tools that easily
distinguish
>three numeric data types, in all languages. And to deal with hardware
>idiosyncracies we probably need COBOL facilities that compensate for a lot
of
>variations on fixed point decimal storage format.
>
>With respect to the need to deal with three numeric formats, COBOL is
>lightyears ahead of other languages. If GNU COBOL and linux are to be near
the
>center of the mainstream of e-commerce, there will be a need for a long
list of
>adaptors for the fields once called by the various names BCD. With
reference to
>any popular BCD format linux and GNU COBOL really can not say 'no'.
>Compatibility means 'yes'. Welcome to the Admiral Dr. Grace Murray Hopper's
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375efb54.15159409@netnews>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <375608F5.DE131B34@att.net> <375951da.9386243@netnews> <375995CF.12E964CA@att.net>`

```
'Twas Sat, 05 Jun 1999 17:25:35 -0400, when William Lynch
<WBLynch@att.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Randall Bart wrote:

> > More nits:  It's the IBM compiler that adds a sign -- and an extra digit
> > when you you specify an even number of them.  The compiler does this in
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> sign nibbles if the hardware didn't require it?

As I said "The compiler does this in order to make use of the somewhat
deficient hardware implementation."  The hardware does not "require" it.
The compiler could generate code to handle all the decimal math using only
the binary math in hardware.  That would be very inefficient, but it could
be done.

> PS: The following is from IBM Principles of Operations manual:

I've read POO cover to cover.  In fact two different editions: S/360 and
S/370.  I imagine it's much larger now, but unless I am very much mistaken
the decimal implementation hasn't changed since it was first added to the
S/360 around 1966.

> "8.2.1 Decimal-Arithmetic Instructions
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and are treated as signed decimal integers.

This is the implementation of signed packed decimal.  Where is the
implementation of unsigned decimal?  There isn't any!  Why?  Since these
signed decimal instructions were added over 30 years ago, IBM has added
over 100 instructions to the operator set, yet they haven't seen fit to
add unsigned decimal.  Every Cobol standard since then has included
unsigned decimal fields; why hasn't IBM added instruction to support
Cobol?  Do they still believe Cobol is about to go away?  Cobol is only
used by bankers and accountants; surely banking and accounting aren't
going to last much longer.

But still, the true blame lands on the compiler implementors.  The
compiler could implement unsigned decimal by moving each packed operand to
a work field followed by a byte containing X"0F", then using the signed
decimal operators.  This would be somewhat inefficient, but what does IBM
do now?  AIUI, the current IBM compilers give you a compile time option to
direct how you want your unsigned decimal fields handled mathemematically:
Either generate code on every store to OR the sign to F (which is
inefficient), or let unsigned fields go negative (which is incorrect).
What a choice!  Furthermore, IBM allocates space for the sign on unsigned
fields, and there's no compile time option to fix this.

> (Appendix A - please note the first sentence and let us know if you
> still maintain that the compilers "add a sign" because IBM is a bad
> joke):

IBM Cobol is a joke because the compiler implementors never had the
chutzpah to correctly implement unsigned decimal, so the hardware
designers never had a reason to add unsigned decimal instructions to the
operator set.

> In either format, negative decimal integers are represented in true
> notation with a separate sign. As for binary integers, the radix point
> (decimal point) of decimal integers is considered to be fixed at the
> right, and any scaling is done by the programmer. 

I'm glad the compiler implementors missed that part, because scaling is
the compiler's job, not the programmers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Warning - IBM & Packed Decimal (was: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <375efb54.15159409@netnews>...
>'Twas Sat, 05 Jun 1999 17:25:35 -0400, when William Lynch
><WBLynch@att.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the compiler's job, not the programmers.

Since the reference to scaling was within the context of the
hardware, it is reasonable to state that "any scaling is done
by the programmer." In that case, the programmer would be
an assembly language programmer and would include those
programmers who write compilers for the high level languages
that provide for automatic scaling.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

the comp-3 format to be the same as IBM mainframe and I would
like to offer that as an option. Doing the rounding and
remainders right without holding the values as BCD is also a bit
tricky,

Tim Josling

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au>...
>True, although the X/Open COBOL standard does actually specify
>the comp-3 format to be the same as IBM mainframe and I would
>like to offer that as an option. Doing the rounding and
>remainders right without holding the values as BCD is also a bit
>tricky,


No, not really.  The BCD opcodes, in effect, do the same thing as if you
convert to binary, do the math, then convert back. When dealling with
display data, (whether Ascii or EPCDIC), you have to either do the same
thing anyway, or do the math decimal style, byte by byte.

No matter how you cut it, those binary/binary emulation routines have to be
there.  You could convert to BCD and do the math native mode on a CPU that
had the op-codes, but I cannot see that it would be a lot faster than going
to binary(the conversion that is).

The only real benefit is when the Cobol is run on a specific platform, and
the Cobol code has been tuned for that specific platform. With standard byte
oriented Cobol data, the routines should always be common.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Perhaps I should explain the point about remainder etc a little
more. COBOL requires rounding to the nearest *decimal* digit, and
similar rules for truncation. That is not the same as round to
the nearest binary digit and convert to decimal. It is quite
painful. Have a look at the instructions the IBM mainframe
compiler generates for binary divides. Neither the x86 nor the
Merced have any hardware to help with this problem.

This is one of the problems we have had with various free cobols,
people assume that binary arithmetic semantics will be OK. There
aren't.

On the mainframe, the compiler handles display data in this way.

1. Pack 
2. Decimal Arithmetic
3. Unpack

Steps 1 & 3 have one instruction per operan and step 2 is one
instruction (unless you have really long operands or specificy
non--standard signing).

In this case the decimal arithmetic is in hardware. There is no
byte by byte processing in software. The tests I did showed to my
surprise that packed was as fast as binary.

My plan was always to convert to binary, do the math and convert
back, except for small operands. The x86 had instructions to help
with this but the merced (IA64 instructions) do not.

Tim Josling

donald tees wrote:
> 
... The BCD opcodes, in effect, do the same thing as if you
> convert to binary, do the math, then convert back. When dealling with
> display data, (whether Ascii or EPCDIC), you have to either do the same
> thing anyway, or do the math decimal style, byte by byte.
...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jdrku$r14$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>`

```
I realize the problem with decimal, however it only becomes a problem if you
try to use floating point.  If all calculations are done with integers, then
the problem goes away.  Rounding should be handled during the conversion
back to decimal, not during the math.

Cobol is primarily an integer language.  All those "V"'s are implied, not
actual ... they show the alignment of the decimal digits after math is done.
If it is treated that way, then all truncation will be done correctly, and
rounding is trivial.  The only real problem is how many bits of integer are
required.

The BCD opcodes work well because they are integer oriented, not because
they are decimal oriented.

A small point, though, and perhaps a big philisophical difference. It is not
*Cobol* that requires the handling of decimal math.  It is the world we live
in that requires it.  Cobol is simply a means to impliment it.

The fact that a lot of computer languages force you to go through hoops to
take one third of a dollar, and give you the "correct" answer, is precisely
why Cobol is designed the way it is.  It is designed to reflect the real
world, not a specific hardware architecture.

I have been writing software for a long time, and the one thing that i have
learnt is that attempting to change the world to fit the software rather
than changing the software to fit the world usually fails. <G> In
fact, the real world has a habit of coming back and pounding you into the
ground, as anybody that ever tried to write a financials in a Basic that
insists on floating point has probably found out.

One of the main reasons for having a compiler is to place the various levels
of the software problems into their respective places.  The person writing
the application should not have to worry about the fact that the hardware
has no decimal operands; the person writing the compiler should not have to
worry about a ledger balancing.

Perhaps that is the real reason that your open remarks way back at the
beginning of the thread were jumped on so hard ... Cobol was not designed to
make efficient use of a specific set of opcodes. It was designed to take
care of a specific set of real world problems. How long the language lasts
should have nothing to do with how long a specific set of opcodes lasts.  It
is related, instead, to how long the problems last, and how good Cobol is at
solving them.

Tim Josling wrote in message <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>...
>Perhaps I should explain the point about remainder etc a little
>more. COBOL requires rounding to the nearest *decimal* digit, and
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>...
```

       03  A     PIC   99.
       03  B     PIC   99.
       03  C     PIC   99.9999.

           DIVIDE A INTO B GIVING C ROUNDED.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928742930.15384.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jdrku$r14$1@news.igs.net> <g3x63.16969$eU6.2044@news2.mia>`

```
Don't you just add 5 zeros to the end of B giving B2, divide  B2 by A giving
B3, add 5 to B3 to round, drop off the last digit and edit the rest into C?

Eg.  A = 03, B=10.
        B2 = 1000000
        B3 =  B2/A = 0333333
        B3 rounded = 0333338.
        Move into C ignoring the last digit.
       C = 03.3333

No decimals there.

Rick

To 'add 4 zeros' to B, a binary integer, you would have to multiply
B by the binary equivalent of decimal 10,000.  My point is that,
simply because you are using 'integers' the decimal/binary problem
doesn't 'just go away'. :-)
```

- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jdrku$r14$1@news.igs.net> <g3x63.16969$eU6.2044@news2.mia> <928742930.15384.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <6mV63.569$0a.11@news2.mia>`

```
I thought PIC 99 was character.  I assumed stringing  of B and the correct
number of zeros before converting either to binary or floating point for the
arithmetic.  I also assumed that at compile time the compiler would
calculate from C what it would have to do to B to remove the need for real
decimal positions in the calculation.

I.e. if the compiler writer always thought of implied decimal places and
adjusted the operands accordingly then there is no need for worrying about
actual decimal precision.  Any decimal part of a calculation can just be
ignored.

Rick

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pB773.4448$By5.679@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jdrku$r14$1@news.igs.net> <g3x63.16969$eU6.2044@news2.mia> <928742930.15384.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <6mV63.569$0a.11@news2.mia> <928828107.20658.0.nnrp-09.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
The discussion was about handling decimal rounding (as COBOL standards
dictate) with fractional binary numbers.  Display numeric variables are
always decimal, by definition. ;-)  Donald was saying that using binary
integers instead of binary floating point would 'make the problem go
away', and I was pointing out that was not quite true. :-)
```

normalization problems, but they exist regardless. The biggest problem with
floating point is the fact that repeating decimals are different.

<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> To 'add 4 zeros' to B, a binary integer, you would have to multiply

> doesn't 'just go away'. :-)

handles fixed point decimal scaling correctly, the Cobol programmer
doesn't have to worry about it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Take a look at the C program below.

Please explain the following (not to be taken seriously):

1. Why is it "painful" to provide correct decimal rounding when
    using binary arithmetic?
2. If the rounded results are identical, then how are decimal
    and binary rounding "really" different?
3. What additional instructions would be required in Merced
    to run the program?
4. Can you think of an example where, in your opinion, binary
    arithmetic would not provide correct decimal results?

In fact, IMO, decimal arithmetic is not, and never has been,
necessary for running COBOL programs.

Were it not for engineers designing decimal arithmetic into
early computers, we would not be having this discussion.

Your idea of converting decimal to binary is sound; but I think
I would do it even for small operands.

[Note to donald tees: Notice how the rounding is done in the
calculation. There is no reason to defer the rounding.]

Tim Josling wrote in message <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>...
>Perhaps I should explain the point about remainder etc a little
>more. COBOL requires rounding to the nearest *decimal* digit, and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>aren't.
[...]
>My plan was always to convert to binary, do the math and convert
>back, except for small operands. The x86 had instructions to help
>with this but the merced (IA64 instructions) do not.


----------
/* translation of a COBOL program */

/*-- relevant COBOL statements

 01 A PIC 99 VALUE 6.
 01 B PIC 99 VALUE 10.
 01 C PIC 99.9999.

 DIVIDE A INTO B GIVING C ROUNDED
 DISPLAY A
 DISPLAY B
 DISPLAY C
*/

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

static long CharsToLong ( const char * s, const size_t n );
static void DisplayChars ( const char * s, const size_t n );

static char A[2] = { '0', '6' };
static char B[2] = { '1', '0' };
static char C[7];

int main ( void )
{
    long a, b, c;
    char cf[8];

    a = CharsToLong ( A, 2 );
    b = CharsToLong ( B, 2 );
    c = ((b * 10000) + (a / 2)) / a;
    sprintf ( cf, "%02d.%04d", (c / 10000), (c % 10000));
    strncpy ( C, cf, 7 );
    DisplayChars ( A, 2 );
    DisplayChars ( B, 2 );
    DisplayChars ( C, 7 );
    return 0;
}

static long CharsToLong ( const char * s, const size_t n )
{
    long r = 0;
    size_t x;

    for ( x = 0; x < n; x++ )
    {
        r = (r * 10) + (s[x] - '0');
    }
    return r;
}

static void DisplayChars ( const char * s, const size_t n )
{
     size_t x;

    for ( x = 0; x < n; x++ )
    {
        printf ( "%c", s[x] );
    }
    printf ( "\n" );
}
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jogi7$91j$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
I think perhaps what I stated was missunderstood, or that I did not say it
well.

There is a definite problem with floating point, in that truncation in
decimal and binary are different. The main reason is that different
fractions are repeating.  The classic, of course, is 1/5 .. a repeating
decimal in binary.

Using integer math eliminates all of those problems. When binary integer
math is done, then both binary and decimal results will always be the same.
Truncation and decimal alignment can then be done after converting back to
decimal.  Rounding will be done in binary, by adding the appropriate
5*10**X.  That will always give the correct answer according to the Cobol
spec, and according to the normal conventions of decimal math.

The problems of how many digits, and where the decimal point is still exist,
but then they exist regardless of how you do the math.  Decimal hardware
does not eliminate the problem of aligning your answer correctly in working
storage.  The lack of decimal hardware merely makes one extra subroutine
required.

Rick Smith wrote in message <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>Take a look at the C program below.
>
…[108 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jr1pc$ivd$2@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com> <7jogi7$91j$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In your response to Tim Josling, June 6th, you said, "Rounding
should be handled during the conversion back to decimal, not
during the math."

The program I posted accomplished rounding during the math;
not during conversion back to decimal. Hence, my statement,
"There is no reason to defer the rounding."

Incidently, the rounding method was courtesy of Lawrence Kirby
in his news post "Re: Rounding error & precision loss" in
< news:comp.lang.c > on May 30, 1998.
------------------
Rick Smith

For those interested in rounding versus truncation in COBOL, you might be
interested in the discussion on this and how it is handled with the new
"ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD" facility of the draft Standard.  J4 spent a
reasonable amount of time considering some of the more obscure cases and (I
THINK) came up with some better definitions that previous Standards.
