###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376201A8.1C94C824@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick,

We obviously have a different idea of what is painful. On Intel
the main calculation line generates about 30 instructions. On
IBM, it takes one or two decimal instructions (in both cases
excluding the conversion from display - normally people store in
packed decimal format rather than display when there will be
extensive calculations). If you are doing floating point it is
also one instruction, plus loads and a store if needed.

Your decimal emulation code runs about 11 times as slow as native
floating point on my system. Based on earlier benchamarks I did
on IBM mainframes, the native decimal instructions would have
been similarly fast as floating point hardware.

So - to do decimal without hardware assistance requires more
instructions and runs slower. Do you disagree with any of that?
Particularly the conversion to and from binary is slower and this
is what Intel has taken away.

There is nothing "really" different about decimal than binary,
they are just two different bases - I don't recall using the term
'really different'. But to simulate one using the other's native
hardware is indeed painful - though not impossible. 

The COBOL standard specifically talks about rounding to the
nearest *decimal* digit, so as I read it, you do need to do
decimal correct rounding.

And the standard also states that for PACKED-DECIMAL 'a radix of
10 is used to represent a numeric item in the storage of the
computer. Furthermore ... each digit position occupies the
minimum possible configuration...'. There is some implementor
latitude but I interpret it as meaning you have to store decimal
digits, two per byte.

Mainly it is a problem for the compiler writer but the users will
suffer the performance impact.

I agree with those who suggested that this is not a COBOL
'problem' but is rather a real world requirement. Look at all the
'BCD' classes in the OO languages for example.

Tim Josling

Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Take a look at the C program below.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     using binary arithmetic?
See above
> 2. If the rounded results are identical, then how are decimal
>     and binary rounding "really" different?
Rounding to the nearest binary digit is different from rounding
to the nearest decimal digit. If that is not self-evident I can't
explain it to you.
> 3. What additional instructions would be required in Merced
>     to run the program?
Not extra instructions are needed. They would be useful though.
At a minimum, decimal to and from binary conversion. Optimally,
native decimal instructions as exist on the mainframes.
> 4. Can you think of an example where, in your opinion, binary
>     arithmetic would not provide correct decimal results?
It depends what you mean. If it means that you cannot simulate
decimal arithmetic using binary instructions, of course you can -
although it is harder than your example suggests (what if the
operants don't fit in an int?). 
> In fact, IMO, decimal arithmetic is not, and never has been,
> necessary for running COBOL programs.
True. The early COBOL implementation on mainframes did without
them. However they all then went out and implemented extra
instructions to make it run at an accaptable speed (eg
Homneywells Extended Instruction Set).
> 
> Were it not for engineers designing decimal arithmetic into
> early computers, we would not be having this discussion.
> 
They did it because of the need.
> Your idea of converting decimal to binary is sound; but I think
> I would do it even for small operands.
> 
Possibly, but if you wanted to add 1 to a counter for example, it
would be quicker to do in situ than convertingf to and from
binary.
> [Note to donald tees: Notice how the rounding is done in the
> calculation. There is no reason to defer the rounding.]
> 
> Tim Josling wrote in message <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au>...
... COBOL requires rounding to the nearest *decimal* digit, and
> >similar rules for truncation. ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ju571$ahl$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com> <376201A8.1C94C824@melbpc.org.au>`

```

Tim Josling wrote in message <376201A8.1C94C824@melbpc.org.au>...
>
>We obviously have a different idea of what is painful. On Intel
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>also one instruction, plus loads and a store if needed.

I understood you to say that "decimal" rounding using binary
was "painful." The example, I gave, which added half the
divisior before dividing in binary is, IMO, not painful at all
and gives the correct decimal results.

I am showing 10 instructions for the main calculation.
"c=((b*10000)+(a/2))/a;"

This person does not have an IBM mainframe. I normally store
using COMP or COMP-5. I avoid PACKED-DECIMAL like
the plague. I found it to be about 30 times slower than the
binary forms.

>Your decimal emulation code runs about 11 times as slow as native
>floating point on my system. Based on earlier benchamarks I did
>on IBM mainframes, the native decimal instructions would have
>been similarly fast as floating point hardware.

The program, that I posted, was intended to demonstrate how
processing in binary can, and does, give the same rounding result
as processing in decimal. It was not designed to be fast.
Furthermore, I don't recall any mention of floating-point earlier,
but I could have missed it.

>So - to do decimal without hardware assistance requires more
>instructions and runs slower. Do you disagree with any of that?

No!

>Particularly the conversion to and from binary is slower and this
>is what Intel has taken away.

But this conversion is "generally" a small part of any program
or application and any performance loss is "generally" negligible.

>There is nothing "really" different about decimal than binary,
>they are just two different bases - I don't recall using the term
>'really different'. But to simulate one using the other's native
>hardware is indeed painful - though not impossible.

A favorite "conspiracy theory" of mine is that IBM chose the
Intel 8088 because it was the worst possible processor for
running business applications. This meant that their installed
base of business computers would not be threatened.

At about the same time IBM released the PC, NCR began
offering computers which used the Motorola 68000 and
COBOL. These computers were popular for small
businesses. In fact, NCR was, as I recall from a news item,
the only manufacturer of Unix-based systems that was
profitable, at that time. They changed to the Intel 386, were
purchased by ATT, and promptly started losing money.
One could speculate that the loss was caused by the switch
to Intel. (I could easily accept that!)

The 680x0 computers had a better instruction set for
decimal arithmetic. It even put binary in the "correct" order.

The subject of this thread is "Intel ditches cobol in Merced
Chip." To accept that Intel "ditched COBOL," one must
first believe that Intel, at one time, embraced COBOL.
In fact, they never have (on their microprocessors). The
decimal instructions that were included in the 8088 were
never adequate for the type or volume of decimal
processing that is needed by the typical COBOL program.

The best native mode for arithmetic on Intel is
"ass-backward binary," not decimal. So even if simulating
decimal in binary is painful (and I don't agree that it is)
that is, nonetheless, the best thing to do.

>The COBOL standard specifically talks about rounding to the
>nearest *decimal* digit, so as I read it, you do need to do
>decimal correct rounding.

As I have pointed out, correct decimal rounding can be
accomplished in binary.

>And the standard also states that for PACKED-DECIMAL 'a radix of
>10 is used to represent a numeric item in the storage of the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>digits, two per byte.

I interpret it as, "due to lack of processor support for decimal
arithmentic," one digit per byte.

>Mainly it is a problem for the compiler writer but the users will
>suffer the performance impact.

Users will suffer no performance loss if they understand that
decimal data types are inefficient on Intel and avoid using
decimal.

[...]
>> 2. If the rounded results are identical, then how are decimal
>>     and binary rounding "really" different?
>Rounding to the nearest binary digit is different from rounding
>to the nearest decimal digit. If that is not self-evident I can't
>explain it to you.

It is only different when maintaining the data as decimal fractions,
such as, floating-point. For data maintained as integers there
is no difference. Data with assumed decimal-points are still
integers whether decimal or binary.

[...]
>> 4. Can you think of an example where, in your opinion, binary
>>     arithmetic would not provide correct decimal results?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>operants don't fit in an int?).

For very large operands, multiple-precision math can be emulated.
The proposed C type "long long int" will be adequate for all
cases where the intermediate result will not exceed 64 bits (18
digits). The current C type "long int" is satisfactory for 32 bits (9
digits). In any case, the new ARITHMETIC IS STANDARD will
require at least 128 bits, making multiple precision math
necessary.

[...]
>> Your idea of converting decimal to binary is sound; but I think
>> I would do it even for small operands.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>binary.

The COBOL standard does not require an implementation to
be efficient, only that the implementation provide certain results
consistent with the standard. If a programmer defines a counter
as packed-decimal, they will have a slower program; but the
program will, otherwise, give the expected results.

Programmers have a way of finding that which gives good
performance on their platform. Those who learn that binary
gives better performance will change. Those who don't will
have slow programs.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762E46E.D164CF55@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au> <Pjb53.77$b_6.35102@client.news.psi.net> <37558F86.4289F857@zip.com.au> <3758B60E.675F2A69@melbpc.org.au> <7jbhn2$hps$1@news.igs.net> <3759BAEE.D57C31F3@melbpc.org.au> <7jnc5b$gvp$1@server.cntfl.com> <376201A8.1C94C824@melbpc.org.au> <7ju571$ahl$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

Lots of stuff. Most of which I agree with.

One of the main reasons for using a COBOL compiler is to reuse
programs and skills from mainframes, which has fast packed
decimal, and where the programmers are used to the decimal
semantics. COBOL does have a domain where it is the best language
for new development but it is a niche in my view - mostly in
relation to exact decimal arithmetic for financials actually!
Possibly others may disagree.

This exposes a number of issues with the Merced:

* No packed decimal support.

* No support for decimal to binary conversions.

In the Pentium, there was some - admittedly limited - support

This means that many mainframe programs will require changes to
run well. And mainframe programmers will have to change their
coding practices. (That's even harder than changing code - by the
way the tern 'software' must be the most misleading term I have
ever encountered in the way it suggests that it is easy to
change).

If the programmer specifies USAGE BINARY, there is no need to
convert to binary every time you do a calculation. This is good
but you still have these problems:

* Need to emulate multi-precision arithmetic where the span of
the computation exceeds 9 digits. Divides in particular are slow
and complex.

* Need to add the 1/2 to get the rounding right, and make sure
the half is scaled right. You are quite right that it is not
particularly unique to decimal - the same problem arises with
binary fixed point. But I have never seen a language that
requires binary fixed point semantics similar to the COBOL rules
for decimal - yes I am sure there is one but it is not among the
20 or so languages I have used.

I am still not entirely sure this approach will always work and I
need to spend some time going through all the scenarios. The case
of negative numbers in particular may require an extra if test or
three. I do find it painful mentally but others may find it a
breeze. In contrast as an example Intel floating point gives you
a choice of rounding modes for binary only with no need to add
anything to achieve rounding.

My intention at the moment is to allow a fast but inexact option
and a precise but inevitably slower option.

I found this discussion quite enlightening - thank you,
Tim Josling
```

```
[...]
>This exposes a number of issues with the Merced:
[...]
>* Need to emulate multi-precision arithmetic where the span of
>the computation exceeds 9 digits. Divides in particular are slow
>and complex.

Merced is 64 bits and should have instructions for 64 bit math.
This will allow up to 18 digits without multiple precision arithmetic.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Mayer Goldberg" <gmayer@cs.bgu.ac.il>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FCpuq5.8nq@news2.new-york.net>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net> <37527b71.1073826256@news1.ibm.net> <3754FC24.9E718E92@melbpc.org.au>`

```

On  2-Jun-1999, Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au> wrote:

> Packed decimal was supported in the X86 chips to some extent.
> There was a packed decimal load and store which helped to convert
> between binary and packed (eg for array indexes).

The Merced being an implementation of the EPIC/SIMD architecture, is capable
of performing over 24 byte-size operations in parallel -- probably on the
order of 36, usin instructions that take one cycle to execute (using the 3
MMX units + the I and M/I units).So I don't think it would be a catastrophe
if an elementary packed decimal op takes 2-3 cycles to execute on a 700 MHz
machine. Actually, I think that a good cobol compiler could produce
gloriously successful code for the Merced.

Mayer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpdi.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928230312.25929.0.nnrp-12.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<374EA8EA.1C04E879@netspace.net.au> <7inj17$vke$1@dumber.ktis.net> <374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net>`

```

Robert <rls@nospam.thanks.com> wrote in message
news:7iotm5$lbm$1@dumber.ktis.net...
>
> ----- Original Message -----
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Intel is a "never ran" in the world of COBOL and big business.  MVS COBOL
is
> the tool of choice for the thousands of COBOL shops out there that run it.
> I would never try and run a serious COBOL application on any Intel
platform.
> It just doesn't make good business sense.
>
> IBM is the key player for COBOL.  And I can assure you that they have no
> plans on dropping COBOL support.
<snip>

Strangely enough, I think that back in the early 70s IBM said that they were
going to drop support for COBOL and that the language of the future was
PL/I.   That worked to a certain extent in the U.K. and a lot of sites
started using PL/I.  However I believe that in the states, the big players
held firm and COBOL is still with us.

Also I would be surprised if any RISC chips have packed decimal
instructions.  I thought the idea was to have fewer simpler instructions so
that the majority of computing work happened faster and somethings just took
a bit longer.

With regard to packed decimal instructions at the chip level, I don't think
that's relevant to COBOL.  The important thing in Business applications is
that the total of a column of figures is accurate and that you don't get the
'imbalances' that can be caused by floating point.  As long as the generated
code keeps accuracy to the correct number of decimal places, and is
efficient, it doesn't matter what instructions are used.

This accuracy has been one of the problems with SQL, which is why database
vendors now tend to have a MONEY or CURRENCY type.

Regards

Rick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

- **Message-ID:** `<3753D649.4826AF2E@zip.com.au>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Ken, did you think about changing to COMP-4 (or IBM-style) compilers
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > About 13 years ago I saved 45 minutes from a six hour run time.  I

13 years ago Binary did not exist.  COMP did mind you but is
limited to 9(08) or derivatives.  The Packed Decimal instructions
sit on a separate processor in IBM and therefore perform quite
well.

If you are talking subscripts or references then BINARY is the
only way to go.  See the IBM red books.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j3ioe$md4$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au> <7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net> <37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net>`

```

William Lynch wrote in message <37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net>...
>donald tees wrote:


>"BCD"? That's the internal coding structure for many of IBM's 2nd
>generation machines, e.g., 1400-series stuff; and has nothing to do with
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes Bill, but we all know that when IBM says "X" they are refering to
something that everybody in the world already knows as "Y", and when they
refer to "Y" they are refering to something that everybody else in the world
defined as "X" thirty years back ...

;<)

For most platforms,  BCD means Binary Coded Decimal, and are the digits 0000
thru 1010 inclusive expressed in four bit notation.  That is also true for
every chip manufacturer, right down to the makers of LCD displays for
calculators, intel catalogs, etc., etc.,

Whether or not that is packed two nibbles per byte or four nibbles per word
or eight nibbles per 32 bit word or 16 nibbles per 64 bit word is
irellevant, which is why I tend to use the term rather than Comp-3. BCD does
also, technically, not include any method of idicating a sign.
```

```
you would think, as long as you explain it as a secret you can brainwash

binary than in decimal).
```

```
Actually, that is the way an abacus works.  You have five bead for
each order of ones, and three for each order of fives.  When you add, you
end up with more than nine in the various orders. there is then a set
procedure for clearing the "carry" on each column.
```

```
> rather than one based on the obsolete decimal system like metric.  I was
> thinking something like 4 rods to the chain, four chains to the furlong,
> eight furlongs to the mile, etc.  In liquids we could go to something like
2
> pints to the quart, four quarts to the gallon, four gallons to the bushel,
> four bushels to the hogshead ... that type of thing.
> ;<)

Fads (cubits, els, meters, JAVA) come and go, but "A pint's a pound,
the world around."
```

- **Message-ID:** `<375950bd.9101841@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au> <7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net> <37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net>`

```
'Twas Mon, 31 May 1999 23:39:44 -0400, when William Lynch
<WBLynch@att.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> "BCD"? That's the internal coding structure for many of IBM's 2nd
> generation machines, e.g., 1400-series stuff; and has nothing to do with
> packed decimal data (which arrived with S/360 and the 8-bit byte).

The six-bit code is actually named BCDIC.  BCD was used to mean 4-bit
decimal long before the S/360.

> BCD
> is a 6-bit code and maps well, if not perfectly, to punched cards (for
> those who weren't there or who don't remember, any punched card column
> could contain the digits 0-9, plus two zone punches, "11" & "12").

Isn't 0 a zone punch?  Or did BCD(IC) not support the letters S thru Z?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Intel ditches cobol in Merced Chip

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Paul Keirnan" <paul.keirnan@det.nsw.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375db01d@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<3374F8CE1.B6BC56C6@melbpc.org.au> <7ipru6$ll6$2@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCK0wt.MDH@netcom.com> <7irt7b$909$1@bgtnsc03.worldnet.att.net> <7isplv$ffj$1@news.igs.net> <375281C9.3833B049@zip.com.au> <7ivaat$rvi$2@news.igs.net> <37535600.FCDE31C9@att.net> <375950bd.9101841@netnews>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message <375950bd.9101841@netnews>...
>
>> "BCD"? That's the internal coding structure for many of IBM's 2nd
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>decimal long before the S/360.


Around the same time we also had GBCD (from General Electric) and
HBCD (from Honeywell). These were used to designate the entire 6-bit
codes, not just the decimal subset.

>> BCD
>>is a 6-bit code and maps well, if not perfectly, to punched cards (for
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>--


   A thru I     -->  1 thru 9   with a "12" overpunch
   J thru R    -->  1 thru 9  with an "11" overpunch
   S thru Z   -->  2 thru 9  with a "0" overpunch     (Hope I got that
right)

However A thru I could also indicate +1 thru +9
          and J thru R could indicate -1 thru -9.
Likewise a 0 with a "12" overpunch would be +0, etc.

Numbers with no overpunch would be taken as positive.

Therefore a number such as -123 would normally have been punched
in 3 columns as 1, 2, and then 3 with an "11" overpunch. This would
be indistinguishable from "12L" unless you knew what the columns were
being used for.
```

```
My dislike of exit paragraphs is very closely related to my dislike of
sections.  I don't like having the possibility of drop down code.  (a
maintainer puts a paragraph after a section and discovers his paragraph
gets performed every time the section gets performed).

Your statement implies that sections are somehow an alternative to exit
paragraphs, but I am not picturing this.

Paul Knudsen wrote:
```

```
I agree completely.  And when someone says "time is the fourth

turn around, time would be the 2nd dimension!

The other statement people make which seems silly to me is  "if there

realized".  Besides being a sad image, it is easily disproved - since
there are an infinite number of even numbers, one of those even numbers
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Level 66 - was Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-SD3FbPvdnxmE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <eQhRAdnq#GA.401@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <_he43.4267$LA2.68660@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <are43.209$K2.7987@iad-read.news.verio.net> <375275ec.1072413082@news1.ibm.net> <3753E2FD.C2E6F786@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Tue, 1 Jun 1999 13:41:17, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> 
wrote:
> > Using this rather than redefining the entire record (which will be an
> > IMPLICIT redefines as it is part of an FD) really does make sense.
> 
> But it's almost as easy to use a REDEFINES, and since REDEFINES is much
> more common, there will probably be fewer mistakes in maintenance.

Actually not in this case.  It would mean redefining an FD, which you 
can't do EXPLICITLY.  The implicit redefinition will (IMHO) be more 
confusing than the renames, which will only reference the alt-key 
area.  The whole record, key and alt key appear on a single screen. 

Even after all this, I probably won't use it in production.  

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Level 66 - was Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j1j44$f47$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <eQhRAdnq#GA.401@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <_he43.4267$LA2.68660@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <are43.209$K2.7987@iad-read.news.verio.net> <375275ec.1072413082@news1.ibm.net> <3753E2FD.C2E6F786@NOSPAMhome.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-SD3FbPvdnxmE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...

<snip>
>It would mean redefining an FD, which you can't do EXPLICITLY.
<snip>

I don't see why this is the only alternative.  While 01-level records in an
FD *implicitly* redefine each other, and while *explicit* REDEFINES is
indeed prohibited *at the 01 level* in the File Section, I see nothing at
all wrong with using REDEFINES in the File Section, so long as it's not used
at the 01 level.

The original record layout proposed:

01  The-Record.
      03  Prime-Key.
            05  Key-Name Pic X(30).
            05  Key-Number Pic 9(9).
      03  Key-Zip Pic X(10).
      03  Data-Values Pic X(200).

While the following may be voluminous for some tastes, I don't see that it
is invalid according to COBOL standards or, for that matter, impossible to
maintain:

01  The-Record.
        03  Key-Areas.
                05  Key-Name Pic X(30).
                05  Key-Number Pic 9(9).
                05  Key-Zip Pic X(10).
        03  Filler Redefines Key-Areas.
                05 Prime-Key.
*                    Prime-Key contains Key-Name and Key-Number, described
above.
                        07  Filler Pic X(30).
                        07  Filler Pic 9(9).
                05 Filler Pic X(10).
        03  Filler Redefines Key-Areas.
               05  Filler Pic X(30).
                05  Secondary-Key.
*                    Secondary-Key contains Key-Number and Key-Zip,
described above.
                        07  Filler Pic 9(9).
                        07  Filler Pic X(10).
        03  Data-Values Pic X(200).

I would expect the various subordinate FILLERs (explicit or otherwise) could
be eliminated if the author is willing to presume that PIC X(9) occupies the
same space as PIC 9(9) on the host machine (which I would expect to be true
presuming no explicit USAGE clauses and ANSI-conformant compilers), giving
the somewhat shorter equivalent:

01  The-Record.
        03  Key-Areas.
                05  Key-Name Pic X(30).
                05  Key-Number Pic 9(9).
                05  Key-Zip Pic X(10).
        03  Filler Redefines Key-Areas.
                05 Prime-Key Pic X(39).
*                    Prime-Key contains Key-Name and Key-Number, described
above.
                05 Filler Pic X(10).
        03  Filler Redefines Key-Areas.
               05  Filler Pic X(30).
                05  Secondary-Key Pic X(19).
*                    Secondary-Key contains Key-Number and Key-Zip,
described above.
        03  Data-Values Pic X(200).

For pre-ANSI-1985 compilers that don't permit FILLER at the group level, an
appropriate "dummy" data name may of course be substituted.

Am I missing something here?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Level 66 - was Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00

```
Chuck Stevens wrote in message <7j1j44$f47$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
[snip]
>01  The-Record.
>        03  Key-Areas.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Am I missing something here?


As to style, one might consider the elimination of the noise word FILLER as
well, yielding:

01  The-Record.
        03  Key-Areas.
               05  Key-Name Pic X(30).
               05  Key-Number Pic 9(9).
               05  Key-Zip Pic X(10).
        03  Redefines Key-Areas.
               05 Prime-Key Pic X(39).
*                    Prime-Key contains Key-Name and Key-Number, described
above.
               05                     Pic X(10).
        03  Redefines Key-Areas.
               05                     Pic X(30).
               05  Secondary-Key Pic X(19).
*                    Secondary-Key contains Key-Number and Key-Zip,
described above.
        03  Data-Values Pic X(200).
```

```
Thanks for the suggestion, Tom.  I ordinarily work with "legacy" compilers
to begin with, so even the idea of a FILLER redefining something is a
stretch for me, to say nothing of the optionality of the FILLER word itself!
I kinda like the explicitness of it, but as you say, it's a style issue and
I can certainly see the justification for its elision.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375954c1.10128170@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <eQhRAdnq#GA.401@nih2naab.prod2.compuserve.com> <_he43.4267$LA2.68660@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <are43.209$K2.7987@iad-read.news.verio.net> <375275ec.1072413082@news1.ibm.net> <3753E2FD.C2E6F786@NOSPAMhome.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-SD3FbPvdnxmE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7j1j44$f47$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <6zb53.80$b_6.39641@client.news.psi.net> <7j4avk$t7t$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
'Twas Wed, 2 Jun 1999 15:25:06 -0700, when "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Thanks for the suggestion, Tom.  I ordinarily work with "legacy" compilers
> to begin with, so even the idea of a FILLER redefining something is a
> stretch for me, to say nothing of the optionality of the FILLER word itself!

When I read the 1981 draft of what became X3.23-1985 I liked the
optionality of the word FILLER.  I also liked legalization of single digit
level numbers 1 thru 9.  It was many years before I saw that it opens up
the possibility of some subtle bugs:

01  THE-RECORD.
    05  GROUP-A.
        10  FIELD-A1               PIC 9.
            88 ITS-VALID               VALUES 1, 3. 5.
        10  FIELD-A2               PIC XX.

This doesn't get a syntax error, ITS-VALID is false when FIELD-A1 is 5 and
most bizarre GROUP-A is only one byte long.  I admit this bug is rare, but
whoever hits it will be scratching his head for a good long time.  And
when he finally figures it out, he's also likely to submit a UCF (bug
report for you non-Unisys folks) against a correctly functioning compiler.
```

```
Allright, I'll answer it.  That's the data division's file section.

DATA DIVISION.
FILE SECTION.

FD  INPUT-FILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F.
01  INPUT-RECORD                  PIC X(132).


SD  SORTFILE
    RECORDING MODE IS F.
01  SORTREC                       PIC X(132).


BTW, I like the 'percolate' theory.  The best way to solve a problem is to
give it time.

Jeff
```

```
that is very true and is, incidentally, one of the best reasons for
maintaining a strict coding standard at anyone shop. The visual
impact should be the same independently of who coded it.
Also, as a maintenance programmer never insert changes that
conform to your preferences, but keep the original style.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37593385.1631052@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia> <7isboo$738$1@news.igs.net> <7j4ood$248h@enews1.newsguy.com> <375666b3@news3.us.ibm.net> <7j5vi2$593$1@news.igs.net> <3756de47@news3.us.ibm.net> <3756df32@news3.us.ibm.net> <7j8eql$j79$1@news.igs.net> <3757c116@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 4 Jun 1999 07:05:45 -0500, when "Leif Svalgaard"
<lsvalg@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Also, as a maintenance programmer never insert changes that
> conform to your preferences, but keep the original style.

This is a very important point.  I've been at shops where old programs
were written without any coding standards, and now they're trying to
enforce standards.  When you apply the new standards to an old program you
need to be very selective.  A large new routine should follow the new
standards, but what about small chnages?  There's nothing more jarring
than a program with little bits of new style within a paragraph that's
mostly old style.  If you really need to write in the new style, then you
need to rewrite in large chunks.

At my current assignment there are no standards to speak of, so I follow
my own.  Yet I've written more GO TOs in the last two months than I had in
the preceding 10 years.  It's Cobol-74, and the lack of END-IF (and other
Cobol-85 features) justifies some GO TOs, but I haven't seen code this GO
TO heavy since the 1970s.  

How's this for perverse code:

IF  ACTION-CODE = "ABC"
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
    GO TO 123-AFTER-ABC.
IF  UPDATE-FLAG NOT = "Y"
    GO TO 123-AFTER-ABC.

I can understand this code occurring in one place due to a maintenance
change, but these six lines (with differing literals and paragraph labels)
occur dozens of times in one program.
```

```
.. snipping talking about maintaining very old code...

There are some good references on Micheal Kasten's page on
renovating old code.  He used to be a regular here but I haven't
seen his name recently.  There is a good start to a style guide as
well.

http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Do you program visually or non visually?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37595472.10048913@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk> <Gka43.5053$NJ.762@news4.mia>`

```
'Twas Sun, 30 May 1999 07:34:32 -0500, when "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> I'm very curious about this, and would like to take an informal survey.
> How many of you program by creating a mental 'image' of your program
> and the processes, and how many do something else?  And would you
> describe as best you can how you do it?

Can I say "yes" to "image" and "no" to "visual"?

I've been pondering this one all week.  I don't think of it as visual
exactly, but I suppose that's as close as the English language can come to
describing it.

Visual analogies are common in data processing.  I recall a customer
support rep was mystified when I said our Saab terminals were TC
look-alike.  She had seen both of them, and they didn't look at all
similar.  Also on Burroughs Medium Systems, moving head disk drives with
100 byte sectors were described as head-per-track look-alike.  In both
cases, "look-alike" means the data format is the same.  Some IBM virtual
storage systems use a tranlation look aside buffer, pipelining involves
instruction look ahead, some compilers use peep hole optimization, and in
Cobol we scan with INSPECT and SEARCH to lookup data.  If we say that the
computer sees data and code (as opposed to hearing, feeling, tasting, or
smelling), then I see it in that sense.

To me the real question is whether you see it statically or in motion.
When I worked for Burroughs/Unisys I was surprised at how many otherwise
good programmers didn't have "the multi-threaded mind set".  The KEYEDIOII
library suffered for several months being maintained by a programmer
without the multi-threaded mind set.  

For the most part you didn't need to think multi-threaded to work on the
compilers (a compiler is batch application) but there was an exception.
On pre-beta (pre-ASD) A Series systems, we had this unholy process called
stack searching.  When an area of memory was paged out to disk, all stacks
with visability to that area had to be searched for copy data descriptors,
so they could be marked absent.  Stack searching also occured when an area
was resized; all the unindexed copy descriptors needed to be found to have
their size updated.

Descriptors have four frame sizes:  4-bit, 8-bit, 48-bit, and 96-bit.
Actually we were still supporting the B6900 which had 3-bit and 6-bit as
well.  The hardware had no atomic instruction to change the frame size in
a descriptor; the compilers generated multiple instructions to extract the
bits, modify them, and then put them back.  If a resize occured in
between, the copy descriptor now had the wrong size.  If this was too
short, your program might abort accessing data that was really there, but
worse, if you size was now too long you could clobber data which belonged
to another program.  The Algol compiler rigorously did its descriptor
updates in an order which would never result in too long a length, so I
conclude it was written by someone who understood this, but the other
compilers were sloppy until I rediscovered this bit of wisdom 15 years
later.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: tables arrays subscripting

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753EDAF.53D8AAF0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7i8pok$sr4$1@scotty.tinet.ie> <lDS13.6567$lI5.1706537@news1.mia> <7ijv17$ckf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <rn0rqFAgaGU3Iw6Z@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
Nigel Eaton wrote:
> 
> In article <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-62ZojcC3uYdE@Dwight_Miller.iix.c
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> why?

I guess I'm not visual in the same way.  I don't try to "see" the tables
I am working with.  (you must be an IBMer, you use single quotes around
'see').  I do remember having a problem which wanted a 5 dimensional
table when my compiler only supported 3.  I don't remember the business
problem though.

Since I don't visualize the table, adding on a fourth level is just the
same as adding on the third leve.
```

## Thread: Cobol Search statement optimization? — 14 this month / 36 total

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search question

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753D15F.97DB3A5A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <374AFE53.83E44563@NOSPAMhome.com> <374B08BC.6E3D753A@cat.com> <SsE23.237$D%2.138@news4.mia> <374BECEA.1B20B2F2@zip.com.au> <awS23.1764$v%.180@news3.mia> <374C044D.226E313C@cat.com> <374C0DF8.AF0011E4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ikqil$uue$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <374E8FE9.70BC297@cat.com> <7int8c$4cf$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <7ivhtj$b3@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Bill,

I would have thought that an index used on an inappropriate table
would be handled like:

set subscript to ws-index-of-table-1

Move element-of-table-2 (subscript) to xxxx

In other words it would be grossly inefficient but it would do as
the programmer intended.  (Pure supposition though no testing on
any compilers).

This result is better than indexing directly.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search question

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j1ddg$d6a@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <374AFE53.83E44563@NOSPAMhome.com> <374B08BC.6E3D753A@cat.com> <SsE23.237$D%2.138@news4.mia> <374BECEA.1B20B2F2@zip.com.au> <awS23.1764$v%.180@news3.mia> <374C044D.226E313C@cat.com> <374C0DF8.AF0011E4@NOSPAMhome.com> <7ikqil$uue$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <374E8FE9.70BC297@cat.com> <7int8c$4cf$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <7ivhtj$b3@sjx-ixn1.ix.netcom.com> <3753D15F.97DB3A5A@zip.com.au>`

```
I also haven't checked my manual, but I think you could be a "tiny" more
efficient (in a strictly ANSI conforming compiler) by doing.

Set index-associated-with-table-2 to index-associated-with-table-1

Move Elem-from-Table-2 (Index-associated-with-table-2) to wherever.

In other words, you can set an index (from one table) to the value in
another table (whether they are the same length or not).  The compiler (or
run-time) will then need to convert the offset (if that is how the index is
stored - which it usually is) to a "value" that then gets converted to a new
offset.  Certainly not wildly efficient - but possible.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753DF08.43D89F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <3752F165.5C5AC836@earthlink.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> 
> Mark,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> follows:

The following does not apply directly to tables, but is worth thinking
about:  I worked in an IDMS shop and wanted to walk an area to find all
of the records of a particular type.  I tested a couple of methods - and
easily the fastest method depended upon the key being a number between 1
and 200 (about 90 different numbers).  I just calced into each possible
value.

An other thing which is harder to tell - a real small table can be
searched in memory with a linear search actually being faster than a
binary search.  With virtual memory, sometimes a test would be necessary
even in a larger file to see what happens when memory pages in and
out.   Maybe pre-sorting a file to make the search binary is not an
efficient use of resources after all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j2bql$176$3@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <3752F165.5C5AC836@earthlink.net> <3753DF08.43D89F0@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
With modern machines having almost unlimited memory (note he almost<S>), I
am starting to think more and more in terms of tables instead of Isam files.
Just about anything that has less than a thousand elements will easily fit
into even a PC these days.

A small bussiness GL, for example --- the chart of accounts will seldom go
over several hundred --- in fact, probably seldom exceed one hundred.  It
makes a lot of sense to just make it an array and load it from a flat file.
Even 10,000 transactions will typically fit into a meg, which is not really
significant on a 128 meg machine.  Few companies of under ten million a year
will ever have more than 10,000 GL transactions per year.

Howard Brazee wrote in message <3753DF08.43D89F0@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>>
>> Mark,
>>
>> If you are really concerned about performance, before selecting a lookup
method,
>> look at your data first. Your search keys are integers, in which case
both
>> serial and binary searches are extremely inefficient compared to
key-indexed
>> search and/or hashing, and no compiler optimization would compensate for
a slow
>> search algorithm. If your keys, in addition to being integers, fall into
a
>> narrow range, then use key-indexing; if they can assume any integer
value, use
>> hashing. Your particular situation seems to belong to the first category.
>> Suppose that none of your table entries exceeds 999. Then one can proceed
as
>> follows:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>efficient use of resources after all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3754D72D.BA109CE2@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <3752F165.5C5AC836@earthlink.net> <3753DF08.43D89F0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j2bql$176$3@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees, in part, wrote:
> 
> With modern machines having almost unlimited memory (note he almost<S>), I
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
..........
> >An other thing which is harder to tell - a real small table can be
> >searched in memory with a linear search actually being faster than a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >efficient use of resources after all.

Just because the data distribution happened to work in favor of serial search.
For successful search, best case for both methods is 1 comparison: For serial
lookup - if the matching key is the first, for binary - if it is in the middle,
whilst worst case is N for serial and logN - for binary. For failure, binary
search never makes more than logN + 1 comparisons, and serial always makes N. On
the average, the point where linear and binary searches are equal in performance
is given by logN = N/2 (N < 7) for success and by logN + 1 = N (N < 4) for
failure. Adding some computational and logical overhead associated with binary
search, it will never run slower than serial search for N > 10, on the average. 

Presorting a file with several dozen records is hardly resource intensive. For
really large files it is, but we are talking about sorting a file whose keys and
satellites can fit in a table in memory, and SyncSort swallows much bigger meals
without choking in a matter of a CPU second. On the other hand, for a lookup
table exceeding mere 1000 entries, serial search is out of question, so sorting
is more than justified. In addition, if keys are distributed evenly throughout
the range, interpolation search (which is almost as trivial to code as binary
search) reduces the average number of comparisons for both success and failure
to loglogN. The latter reaches 5 at N = 4.3 billion; practically it will never
exceed 4. 

If sorting, nonetheless, is not desirable, hashing should be used. It does
require some programming and realistically takes about 50% more memory than
binary/linear search, but has the unique property of searching in constant time.
Because of the nature of the algorithm, it never makes more than 1.5
comparisons, on the average, for either success or failure regardless of the
number of lookup keys. That is, a hash table with 1,000,000 keys is searched
exactly as fast as one with 100 keys, provided that both are equally sparse.  

As Donald noted, memory is cheap and abundant nowadays, so algorithms capable to
putting it to a good use may be well worth resurrecting, coding, and tuning.

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@ford.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j33ka$1vr2@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<374AF731.1421AFED@cat.com> <3752F165.5C5AC836@earthlink.net> <3753DF08.43D89F0@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j2bql$176$3@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote in part
>With modern machines having almost unlimited memory (note he almost<S>), I
>am starting to think more and more in terms of tables instead of Isam
files.
>Just about anything that has less than a thousand elements will easily fit
>into even a PC these days.


This may really be the same thing.  An effient I/O system will make use of
the memory to store all of the indexes and much of the data of the indexed
file.  Although the program may use I/O commands, they may well be executed
as memory searches.

In this case, the advanage that you gain from all the programming work may
be minimal.

Ed Milne
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFCttIC.MF9@netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37bryantFCpIBp.CAF@netcom.com> <7j4lo6$bur$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:

>That is interesting, though I am not surprised.  As a matter of curiousity,
>how big a table did you test on?  (Items and item length).

The point of the article was to show how using the features
available in the language could save a lot of runtime.  Far
too many of the programmers I run into have never opened the
manual and do so much by handcoding.

In the example, I used a 5-digit ZIP code and looked up an
18-digit city name.  I think there was a table of 250 which
was populated with the 227 local postal codes but SEARCH ALL 
scales very well and a narrow table of 10,000 would still be 
practical if you needed to table the entire list of ZIPs.

The example cut 98% from the original starting point (direct,
individual disk reads).  In a realworld application it cut a
22 hour posting job down to 5.5 hours with additional room for  
improvement.  When the SEARCH ALL technique was pointed out to
the client's programmers the response was "But then we'd have
to think too much."  (Referring to use of INDEX and SEARCH ALL)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3758C038.772D83B8@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<37bryantFCpIBp.CAF@netcom.com> <7j4lo6$bur$1@news.igs.net> <dbryantFCttIC.MF9@netcom.com>`

```
David,

   But there is a much faster way to do the trick! I wonder what your client's
programmers would say about the following. Given that there can be no more than
100000 zip codes, create an array indexed from 0 to 99999, with items long
enough to hold a city name and initialized to whatever a city cannot be, say,
blanks. If 'Cobol City' corresponds to 12345, move it to the bucket 12345, and
likewise for every city and zip you have. You will end up having 227 populated
buckets; the rest will be empty. 
   Now, to search, for instance, for zipcode 32256, look at the occurrence 32256
of the table. If it is not blank, the search has been successful, and that
bucket's contents is the city name found; if it is blank, the search has failed. 
   No search method gets any faster or simpler. As to performance, first, no
sort of any kind is required, and duplicates, should any occur amongst the
lookup keys, are eliminated automatically. Second, SEARCH ALL is the binary
search, and for 227 zips it requires, on the average, 7 to 8 comparisons
(coupled with internal binary search logic) per search key to either find or
reject it. Key-indexed search described above needs 1 comparison per key,
success or failure, coupled with no logic or coding effort whatsoever.
Furthermore, its performance does not depend on the number of keys comprising
the lookup set. If instead of 227 zips you had 7 or 99997, key-indexed table
would work at precisely same speed, only instead of 8, it would be 3 and 17
times faster than SEARCH ALL, respectively.
   Granted, key-indexed search is good only for integer keys falling into a
limited range, but this is exactly what you have got here, not to mention that a
situation like this arises surprisingly often. Zip codes, digital codes in
healthcare, dates expressed in days relative to a fixed origin... all lend
themselves to this kind of lookup. For keys of this nature, key-indexing is by
far the fastest method of matching files, none of which, by the way, needs to be
sorted. Granted, if a key-indexed table is sparse, most of its slots are wasted.
But the price paid for up to 17-fold increase in search speed is (100000-277)*18
< 2 megabytes is a bargain today. The lowly PC I am using to write this has 196
Mb of RAM, and the Big Iron Brother in the shop I am currently consulting for
never frowns at 600 Mb consumed by a batch job.
   If key-indexing is too difficult a concept for the "client's programmers" to
grasp, they must be a very special breed, indeed... 

Kind regards,

===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
===================
        

"David K. Bryant" wrote:
> 
> "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> writes:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> to think too much."  (Referring to use of INDEX and SEARCH ALL)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dbryantFCw3LE.CvM@netcom.com>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net> writes:

>David,

>   But there is a much faster way to do the trick!

Faster because, in this example, the data itself helps.
It's all numeric and of a finite range.  Wouldn't work
if the key were alpha would it?


The ZIP/city lookup was just what I used to write the
series of articles... the realworld basis was <drum roll>

up the dollar amount.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Search statement optimization?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375A2C69.35E409FB@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<dbryantFCttIC.MF9@netcom.com> <3758C038.772D83B8@earthlink.net> <dbryantFCw3LE.CvM@netcom.com>`

```
"David K. Bryant" wrote:
> 
> "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net> writes:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's all numeric and of a finite range. 

Right, and I have stressed it explicitly.
 
> Wouldn't work if the key were alpha would it?

   Yes it would, although not _exactly_ the same way. The extension of the idea
of key-indexing called hashing will work with keys of any type. (Even though
digital strings - now of any length - are still hashed easier and more
efficiently than a deliberate alpha.) This is precisely how a COBOL compiler
looks up entries in the symbol table: It hashes identifiers you use in your
program to find their associated addresses. As you can tell from compilation
speed, this is a very fast process. Properties of hashing are similar to those
of key-indexing: a) Search time is independent of the number of lookup keys N,
i.e. it occurs in constant, O(1), time (rather than O(logN) for binary or
O(log(logN)) for interpolation search). Instead, it depends on the sparsity of
the hash table (dictated by you and available memory); b) No sort is needed, and
duplicates are weeded out automatically.
   Choosing a good hash function X, which is perfectly doable in COBOL, and a
half-full hash table will guarantee that any search key will be found or
rejected in a matter of less than 1.5 key comparisons (again, regardless of the
number of lookup keys). Working somewhat slower than key-indexing because of
extra logic and data movement, hashing is still light years ahead of binary
search the more, the more keys are to be searched. At only N=1024, binary search
requires 10-11 comparisons, hashing - 1.5, at N=1048576, it is 20 and still 1.5,
and so on. 

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
=================       
   
> 
> The ZIP/city lookup was just what I used to write the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I'm using Fujitsu Cobol 3.0 and am having a problem with the linking.
For example when trying to link an obj file called hello.obj this is
what happens:

NMAKE : fatal error U1077: 'C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\LINK' : return code '0x68'
Stop.

Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

/OUT:C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\SAMPLES\KEVIN\HELLO.EXE

C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\KERNEL32.LIB
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\USER32.LIB

Please email me with the response as well so I am sure not to miss the
posting.
Thank you in advance.
paradise
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** fj.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,umbc.ifsm.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j1sgk$m75@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<375464B7.E7B8029E@computerpro.com>`

```
Use the compiler option that tells Fujitsu that your program is

    MAIN

This is NOT the default.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Cobol 3.0

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@ford.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** fj.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,umbc.ifsm.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j32tl$cae2@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<375464B7.E7B8029E@computerpro.com>`

```
You are missing the module definition (.DEF) file or it is incomplete.

In the Programming Staff Project menu, set the "Create Module Definition
File" option.

If you already have a module definition file, make sure it contains an entry
for the called program.
```

```
Volker:

Yes it can!  I can state with all honesty that I have NEVER used
Babelfish to translate a German.

>A correct translation (including spelling) would have been
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Time flies like an arrow

Like this:

Die Zeit vergeht wie im Flug

...which in turn is translated back to:

The time passes as in the flight

>Fruit flies like a banana?

Fruchtfliegen wie eine Banane?

I know enough German to know that "fruchtfliegen" is "fruit flies"

Translated back to english, it look something like this:

Fruit flies like a banana?

Pretty good!


Bob Wolfe, flexus
```

## Thread: What is TSO/JCL? — 14 this month / 47 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [1999-05](1999-05.md#t-09ce89eadb)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews>`

```
> > I have cut the
> > run times of small MVS jobs down by 90% just by tweaking the JCL.
> 
> Or put the other way around, you can make a small MVS job take ten times
> as long by writing poor JCL.

I thought I knew JCL, but I am not picturing what can be done in JCL to
change performance this way.  Could you provide examples?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>> > I have cut the
>> > run times of small MVS jobs down by 90% just by tweaking the JCL.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>change performance this way.  Could you provide examples?

Certainly, a "classic case" is providing too small a set of sort-work
datasets.  Another is where you use multiple step utilities to "copy" then
"sort" then "gener" then "copy" again the same file.  I think most of these
have to do with "mis-using" the IBM (and 3rd party) utilities and their
datasets, but they are closely tied into "bad" JCL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37547D0C.3A5D@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Another JCL tuning consideration is dataset blocksizes.
   DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=80,BLKSIZE=80)
performs MUCH slower than...
   DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=80,BLKSIZE=26960)

There are also BUFNO= parameters that can improve performance by
allocating more dataset buffers than your system defaults to.

I have gotten in trouble for using BUFNO too much however, because the
batch performance gain I experienced was at the expense of the online
systems.  :-)   I'm not sure that BUFNO makes much difference anymore
with System Managed Storage(SMS) in effect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j283b$mff@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37547D0C.3A5D@saif.com>`

```
Yes, blocksize makes a big difference in performance.  Our shop (tries to)
uses BLKSIZE=0 in the DCB and a space parameter like
SPACE=(0,(2000,200),RLSE) or similar.  I had a job that was allocating
SPACE=(CYL,(200,60),RLSE) and the dataset was going across several volumes
and was very slow.  By changing to block allocation and letting MVS allocate
the blocksize, the job ran in 1/10th the time, no joke.  One line of code.

BTW, we use SyncSort and it grabs as much extended secondary memory that it
needs and automatically extends region up by 4096k so, at least with this
sort package, there is little benefit from coding more SORTWORK and region.

I've also found large datasets to work better from tape than from DASD, at
least sequential datasets.  One reporting job we have has 1.5 million 500
byte records and it was very slow sorting on DASD, but going to UNIT=TAPE,
SPACE =(0,0), and 0 blocksize, the sort times dropped in half.

So I guess it's not so much more efficient coding, it's more just using your
resources better.

Jeff, the Newby

----------
In article <37547D0C.3A5D@saif.com>, Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com> wrote:


> Another JCL tuning consideration is dataset blocksizes.
>    DCB=(RECFM=FB,LRECL=80,BLKSIZE=80)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> with System Managed Storage(SMS) in effect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eE3daXur#GA.348@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37547D0C.3A5D@saif.com> <7j283b$mff@enews2.newsguy.com> <3GEpHCAo$wV3Ewqa@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
    Reguards the sort job that ran faster on tape than disk:

    You did not say WHEN that job ran.  1.5 million 500 byte records take up
750Meg roughly.  Sorting could take twice that much work space.  Could the
system you were running on have been short on disk space?  If it was, than
using tape might easily be faster, assuming that the sort program avoids
using tape for work space.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37595B15.7DB7A479@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com> <37547D0C.3A5D@saif.com> <7j283b$mff@enews2.newsguy.com> <3GEpHCAo$wV3Ewqa@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <eE3daXur#GA.348@nih2naad.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
>     Reguards the sort job that ran faster on tape than disk:
>     You did not say WHEN that job ran.  1.5 million 500 byte records take up
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> using tape for work space.

  Insufficient DASD for SORTWRK would most likely cause sort failure not
degraded performance.  I'm sure the earlier post was referring to just
having the input/output files on tape vs. DASD.  For an unloaded 3490
tape controller, tape is inherently faster than native 3390 on large
sequential unstriped datasets because not only is the tape native data
transfer rate high but there is no delay for seek or rotational latency
(in fact, response is best when you keep the drive busy enough so that
physical tape movement is never interrupted).  I'm not sure about the
physical disk transfer rates in IBM RAMAC or IBM RVA, but at least in
our experience it is difficult to avoid at least some contention which
restricts the effective sustained transfer rate for sequential access,
and even on native 3390 the max sustained physical data transfer is
limited to 1 cylinder of data before one is forced to incur seek and
latency delays.  Note we are talking about large enough files that
physical disk I/O would be required, not just transfers from DASD cache.
  Now the catch is that tape controllers in a busy shop are rarely
"unloaded".  Four concurrent dump processes to 4 drives on a 16-drive,
two-path 3490E string can pretty well tie up both the controller and
channel paths.  The Load on both tape and DASD subsystems can
significantly alter response times of both these subsystems, so for
systems under normal system workload, sequential access may sometimes be
faster from tape and at other times faster from DASD.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is TSO/JCL?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37552F84.F7DDE20E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990511164804.22393.00000709@ng-cc1.aol.com> <7if7gh$j1r$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ikuo5$dh8@enews3.newsguy.com> <374f2f1d.638173@netnews> <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7j1e0t$dhd@dfw-ixnews8.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3753F00C.E519E13E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> datasets, but they are closely tied into "bad" JCL.

I remember having charts assisting me in finding optimal sort work
sizes.  I haven't seen one used in some time - it's easier to allocate
way more space than is needed.  I don't think I've seen the multiple
copy - except when the same parm file is to be read by multiple programs
in a proc.  Then the first step copies the parm file to a temporary data
set which is read by the different programs.  Anyway, these go real fast
compared to the programs themselves.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol mantenance levels

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3753DCA9.CB1F6C4A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
David K. Bryant wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> writes:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> to see what commands are available?

Sometimes people are unfamiliar with the latest COBOL functions.  We're
moving to COBOL for MVS and I showed someone a function (someone on this
NG mentioned to me), which probably saved him that much code.  He didn't
know we had a manual for COBOL for MVS.  I can often code 100 lines of
code more quickly than I could find a current manual (all of my manuals
are older).  And of course, code could have been written before the
function was available.

Still, I wish our shop would have a meeting to review new features with
all COBOL programmers.  Just knowing about them is usually sufficient.
```

## Thread: Reading a non-COBOL file — 6 msgs

### Reading a non-COBOL file

- **From:** theisb@21st.dk
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi there.

A file consists of 10 chars, and I'd like to read them into a variable
like:

01 names
  05 name1 pic X(5)
  05 name2 pic X(5)

BUT: COBOL willl only read from files made by COBOL programs ????

Hope there's some help out there...

Regards Theis (Denmark)


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading a non-COBOL file

- **From:** Moncho Castelo <moncho_castelo@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j02vc$6g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  theisb@21st.dk wrote:
> Hi there.
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>


You can read a text file in COBOL without problem. You must process it
like a line sequential file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading a non-COBOL file

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37567DD3.7AE7656C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
theisb@21st.dk wrote:
> 
> Hi there.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hope there's some help out there...

COBOL will read any files.  As long as you understand the data within
the files, you can have COBOL handle those data.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a non-COBOL file

- **From:** "David Mowat" <david@dandm.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ra063.1354$H21.5733@ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37567DD3.7AE7656C@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Yes, you can read any file as if it was a sequential file with each record
being PIC X, or you can read it as line sequential if each record is
delimited by an end-of-line character, and you can read as if each record
has a structure (if you know the structure or can deduce the structure by
observing it in a text editor)
David

Howard Brazee wrote in message <37567DD3.7AE7656C@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>theisb@21st.dk wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>the files, you can have COBOL handle those data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading a non-COBOL file

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37596CC8.9D1242E@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7ivvol$v87$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37567DD3.7AE7656C@NOSPAMhome.com> <Ra063.1354$H21.5733@ozemail.com.au>`

```
David Mowat wrote:
> 
> Yes, you can read any file as if it was a sequential file with each record
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> observing it in a text editor)

Right. COBOL can be made read any file as a sequence of bytes, yet the entire
purpose of reading is understanding the meaning of data. Sure, I can read a
Chinese text as a sequence of characters but it is futile if I do not know
Chinese. Deducing the structure of such a file by observing it in a text editor
is a formidable task, if possible at all. For example, from the standpoint of
OS, a SAS library is just a flat file with RECFM=FS, but try to make sense out
of its data by browsing it, even though the data are meaningful and valid.
Having engines that recognize a variety of special data structures (e.g. VSAM,
RDBSs), COBOL is unable to read (that is, understand) a lot of other file types.
This is one reason SAS able to transparently read,  understand, and write ANY
existing file format (including all proprietary ones, even SAP) is so
indispensable in clinical trial settings or any shop where data flow in and out
in every imaginable format. SAS is expensive to license, but one looking at a
single program (and I mean a real program, not a cluster of cryptic signs)
reading DB2 under OS/390, Oracle on UNIX, Excel on NT, SAS libraries on VAX,
flat files sitting on AS/400, processing all that, writing data back in the
native formats, and distributing user reports on the web, may think the price is
not too high.

Kind regards,
+++++++++++++++++
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
+++++++++++++++++
```

---

<a id="t-dd595ee917"></a>
## Thread: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work? — 8 this month / 9 total

### DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

- **From:** "Chloe" <greg@primesoft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ivk7f$fpl$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Overview
I am currently looking at converting COBOL code from Microsoft (aka
Microfocus) COBOL to some compiler that will simplify the conversion to
windows. In a fit of extravagance I purchased Fujitsu Cobol for the task
however the conversion utilities are limited and there is alot of work just
to get the code compiling correctly. The program itself is a modular finance
package marketed primarily to the agricultural sector but very flexible in
it's use. It consists of a Cashbook/GL, Debtors, Creditors, Report
Generator, Payroll, Land, Livestock. There would be close to 400,000 lines
of code in approx 200 programs which have already been converted to be Y2K
compliant and are relatively bug free.
A user group has asked what it would take to make the product windows based
with the view of either funding as means to controlling development or as
equity partners. Based on this I have the following questions for this
newsgroup.

1.    Are there better development alternatives to Jujitsu for the Project?
2.    Is there anyone with experience in such projects interested in doing
freelance coding in COBOL?
3.    Is there anyone interested in providing equity in a company setup for
the project either in funds or time?

dev@sis.bytes.gen.nz
```

#### ↳ Re: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-vetXKlqiBlhd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7ivk7f$fpl$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```
On Tue, 1 Jun 1999 02:51:45, "Chloe" <greg@primesoft.co.nz> wrote:
> 1.    Are there better development alternatives to Jujitsu for the Project?

I would still consider using Fujitsu, as it is a fine, fast, supported
compiler.
However, for the GUI part I would suggest looking into COBOL sp2 from 
Flexus, in conjunction with Fujitsu COBOL the two make a remarkably 
reliable and fast combination.  (http://www.flexus.com)

> 2.    Is there anyone with experience in such projects interested in doing
> freelance coding in COBOL?

I'm too booked for a project of this magnitude, but the folks at 
Flexus can put you in touch with just such a group.

> 3.    Is there anyone interested in providing equity in a company setup for
> the project either in funds or time?

Your accent (or mine, it's a matter of perspective) has be confused.  
What does this mean?

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j2cph$1t3$4@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ivk7f$fpl$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-vetXKlqiBlhd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```

>> 3.    Is there anyone interested in providing equity in a company setup
for
>> the project either in funds or time?
>
>Your accent (or mine, it's a matter of perspective) has be confused.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

- **From:** "Chloe" <greg@primesoft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00

```
news:7j2cph$1t3$4@news.igs.net...
>
> Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the action <giggle>.  The above is relatively straight-forward
bussiness
> double-speak ... somewhat Orwellian but standardized English to be sure.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, but it also means if you invest 1000 hours of spare time for say a 50%
shareholding in a company that goes on to make a profit of say $1,000,000
each year for the next 20 years you effectively make $10,000 per hour and
still own your original investment.
Bill took the punt but admittedly it is not everyones game.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DOS -> Windows using Jujitsu COBOL? Anyone looking for work?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j4lo7$bur$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7ivk7f$fpl$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-vetXKlqiBlhd@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7j2cph$1t3$4@news.igs.net> <7j497a$f2h$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```

Chloe wrote in message <7j497a$f2h$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>...

>Yes, but it also means if you invest 1000 hours of spare time for say a 50%
>shareholding in a company that goes on to make a profit of say $1,000,000
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
True enough.  However, odds of that happening are about 10,000 to 1 in my
opinion.  Personally, I have started four companies in the last 25 years,
and I have had no other income except from those software ventures.  For
most of that time, I been approached about once every two weeks with a
similar proposal.  My answer is always the same. I gamble on my own
judgement, not on the judgement of someone that cannot do the job
themselves, and cannot afford to pay to have it done.  I have never heard,
in all that time, of anybody making money as a programmer that way.

In the cases where I have made money, I was approached by someone with a
sound business plan, and enough capital to do the job.

Keep in mind that the marketing will cost at least triple the cost of
writing the software once the software works.  If you cannot afford the
software, then it is a sucker play to write it free, because you will not be
able to afford to sell it eithor.
```

```
Too true, and hence the query for freelance contract programmers was part of

those who are interested take the investigation further.
```

```
Chloe wrote:
```
