technologies.
See http://www.software.ibm.com/ad/cobol/

14.10 The Flexus WWW server
Contains all sorts of information, including a copy of the  COBOL
650 compiler. It's at http://www.flexus.com/

14.11 COBOL User Groups
There are a large number of Micro Focus User Groups. Rather  than
reproduce  the list here and have it constantly out of  date,  it
can be found at
http://pita.microfocus.com/UserGroup_PC/
The  list  and the web pages are maintained by Debbie  Vincent  <
tommygigio@aol.com>.

15. Information required for the FAQ.
Corrections  and  additions  to  existing  material  are   always
welcome. I'd like to add a section of reviews of different  COBOL
books. If I am sent any reviews I will collate them and add these
to the FAQ. More information on mainframe COBOL products would be
useful.
A section covering COBOL programming could be worthwhile.
Note:  I still have a "folder full" of comments sent to the  last
owner of this FAQ that have yet to be applied. It is my intent to
get them verified and applied as soon as possible.

16. Contributors to the FAQ.
Many  people  have  contributed  to  this  FAQ  in  each  of  its
iterations. In the past an ongoing list of these people have been
included  within  the  FAQ.  At this time,  i  am  stopping  this
practice, although I ,in no way, want to deprecate all  the  work
and  input  that these people have provided. I have continued  to
identify people whom I am quoting (directly or indirectly) and do
still  hope  that everyone who has input for this  document  will
continue to provide it.

17. What about the Y2K (Millenium) Issue?
  �    Yes, the year 2000 IS a leap year
  �     Yes, the millenium really starts on January 1, 2001 - but
     the software "bug" refers to January 1, 2000 (and several other
     dates)

17.1 Where can I get information about the Y2K problem?
The  Year 2000 Information Center home page on the World Wide Web
is at
     http://www.year2000.com.
The Information Center has a countdown clock, articles on various
aspects  of the problem, vendor information, this FAQ, and  links
to related information.
There is an Internet mailing list operated by Peter de Jager  for
discussions  of year 2000 computer problems. The  list  has  over
1200  members,  and  gets an average of  about  25  messages  per
business day. This is a moderated mailing list managed by a  paid
administrator and fully supported by its members on a "shareware"
basis.  Each member is asked to pay a subscription fee of US  $50
yearly. (You may contribute more if you wish.)
New subscribers get a 30-day free trial.
To  subscribe, select the "Year 2000 Announcement List" link from
the    Year    2000    Information   Center    home    page    at
http://www.year2000.com. On the subscription form, select the Yes
adio  button  for the Discussion List. You can also subscribe  by
sending e-mail to 2k@tor.hookup.net with
     SUBSCRIBE
in  the  subject line of the message. Subscriptions are processed
manually,  so  please  be  patient. The list  can  optionally  be
received  in digest form instead of individual messages. Invoices
and  receipts are available if needed. Details will be sent  when
you subscribe.
The  Year  2000 Forum on CompuServe has discussions of  all  year
2000 issues, and a collection of files, including this FAQ. There
are over 1000 members. To reach the forum, GO YEAR2000.
The  Usenet  newsgroup comp.software.year-2000  is  dedicated  to
discussions of year 2000 computer issues.
The  current  version  of this Year 2000 FAQ  is  available  from
several web sites, an FTP site, and on CompuServe. It is in ASCII
text form at all these sites.
On the Year 2000 Information Center home page at
http://www.year2000.com,
select  the  "Year 2000 Archive" link. The FAQ is the  last  item
listed on the Archive page

18. What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
Both comp.lang.cobol and alt.cobol are UN-moderated newsgroups.
This means that there is no controlling authority regulating  the
content  of the posts in those groups. Anyone is able  (but  most
assuredly  not welcome) to post anything, relevant  to  COBOL  or
not.   Off-topic  posts  (and  particularly  prolonged  off-topic
threads)are  discouraged, but it is impossible to  prevent  them.
Different  readers of these newsgroups have different  thresholds
for  tolerating off-topic comments. The more that you  post,  the
more  likely  it  is  that  some  readers  will  simply  apply  a
"killfile"  to all of your posts - often after sending  you  (and
possibly the newsgroup) rather pointed comments on your use/abuse
of the newsgroup.
You  should, therefore, apply the following guidelines that  will
help  you  get the most out of both your viewing and postings  at
these sites.
  �     Comp.lang.cobol is the preferred site to use - especially
     for technical issues related to COBOL.
  �    Although threads do stray from the topics, the more targeted
     your inquiry is - and the more closely it relates to COBOL, the
     more likely you are to receive a useful and prompt response. (If
     you  know that you are leaving the current subject, consider
     changing the subject line in your post.)
  �     When asking about a specific structure, error message, or
     situation, it is always best to specify both the compiler that
     you are using and the operating system where you are working.
     (What you may think is a general COBOL question may actually be
     very specific to your current environment.)

Note:  For  those  who  are interested,  the  "charter"  for  the
comp.lang.cobol newsgroup can be found at:
ftp//ftp.uu.net/usenet/news.announce.newgroups/comp/
comp.lang.cobol

18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?
Yes,  you  can get help with your homework via these  newsgroups.
HOWEVER, that does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT
find many participants who will be very happy if you ask them  to
do  your  homework FOR YOU. Some hints that you  should  consider
when drafting your post for assistance with homework include:
  �     Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking  for
     homework help. (Most of the newsgroup participants are very good
     at sniffing out those who try and pose homework questions as
     "business need" questions - and they are not very polite  in
     replying to such questions).
  �     Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating
     system  your  homework is targeted at.  Solutions  may  vary
     significantly based on this. (You might also want to include what
     text book you are using.)
  �     The  more information that you can give that demonstrates
     that you really have tried to solve the problem yourself (using
     your text book, class material/presentations, lab assistance,
     etc), the more likely it is that you will get useful and friendly
     responses. If you let us know what you have found on your own and
     why you are still uncertain or confused, you will usually get
     helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
     without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very
     pointed replies.

NOTE:  if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't  want  to
see  the  "Do  your own homework" notes that occur in  the  COBOL
newsgroups), you might want to try,
     http://www.tutoraid.org/

18.2 Can I post job openings in the newsgroups and if so what
should I include?
These newsgroups are an excellent place for posting job openings.
Some  participants (those with jobs) often wish that less of  the
bandwidth  was  spent on job postings, but,  for  those  who  are
looking for positions/contracts, these postings are quite useful.
There  are, however, some guidelines that you should follow  when
posting  positions  - unless you like getting  abusive  and  non-
responsive replies to your postings.
  �     Always, ALWAYS, include rates, a range of rates, or  your
     best information on what rates are available. Phrases like "based
     on experience" and "competitive" are bound to receive replies
     questioning your motives. The assumption is that ALL such posts
     are "trolling" for resumes or rates and not serious job searches.
  �     Try  to give the best summary of job location and desired
     expertise in the subject line of your posting. This will assist
     you  in  attracting  readers that are  actually  potentially
     interested in your openings.
  �     The  newsgroups  are international  in  nature,  but  are
     dominated by Americans. If you are posting a position outside the
     US,  please make it clear whether foreigners are or are  not
     welcome to apply - and if so what visas and other paperwork is
     required If you are posting for a job with specific citizenship
     requirements, please make that clear from the start.
  �     In general, the more information that you provide in your
     postings, the better the match will be from those who reply. If,
     however, you are "attaching" a job description, please  make
     certain that you make it clear what type of document-reader is
     required to process it AND make certain that you have  virus
     scanned the document before you post it.

NOTE:  For  those looking for jobs, you might want to  check  out
Dice at
     http://www.dice.com

Appendix A. Changes in recent revisions
This  appendix  documents the major changes  that  were  made  to
create the various (recent) versions of this document.
Appendix A.1 - Changes to create Version 2.0
Huge  numbers of changes! Version numbering jumped from 1.xxx  to
2.0 to reflect this.
FAQ availability details.
Added section on Commercial COBOL Products.
Removed 'what happened to Realia?' section.
Removed references to Wang, as I can find no information  on  any
Wang COBOL Products.
Checked all URLs, and updated/added as appropriate.
Removed reference to IBM 'COBOL Products WWW site'.
Added  entries  in  'GUI Tools' section for  Micro  Focus  Dialog
System and Net Express, Flexus COBOL spII, and CA-Visual Realia.
Removed  ADS/Online from 'Code Generators' section as  it's  not,
apparently.  Also noted that Cullinet have been acquired  by  CA,
who now sell the Telon product.

Appendix A.2 - Changes to create Version 2.05
  �    Significant reformatting
  �    Updated OO COBOL Section
  �    Add URL for IBM COBOL Web site
  �    Added information on "COBOL for Dummies"
  �    Added initial Y2K section
  �    Added information on using the newsgroups
  �    Added information on PCYACC

Appendix A.3 - Changes to create Version 2.08
  �    Started adding Logos
  �    Removed the detailed list of FAQ contributors
  �    Fixed erroneous Standards information
  �    Corrected information about Liant (who acquired RM)
  �    Added information on Amiga freeware compiler
  �    Added information on Siber tools
  �    Corrected CA company and product information
  �     Added information on where to turn for information not in
     the FAQ
  �    Rephrased the information on what can/should be put in the
     newsgroups.
  �    Revised the information on the CobCy project/product
  �    Updated Micro Focus information
  �    Added URL for "Dice" for those looking for COBOL jobs.
  �    Added information on getting a "COBOL Tutor"

Appendix A.4 - Changes to create Version 2.0x

  �    Re-instated the information on obtaining a copy of the draft
     COBOL Standard.

<a id="t-996c850e2a"></a>
## Thread: A new source/jcl library maintenance utility — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### A new source/jcl library maintenance utility

- **From:** wb4huc@texas.net
- **Date:** 1999-06-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37556bf5.1617227@news.texas.net>`

```
While not strictly a COBOL posting, I believe this is
of interest to COBOL programmers, especially those
involved in maintaining production applications.

Thank you for your time. Here goes:


The PDS-to-PDS Comparison/Matching Utility Version 3
is now available.

This utility was formerly called PDSMATCH, but has been
completely rewritten and renamed.

PDS2PDS is an ISPF/REXX utility that will display
the directories of two partitioned datasets side-
by-side on the screen.

You can copy or move members from one pds to the 
other, you can edit or browse members of either
pds, you can invoke SuperCe to compare datasets
or members, and much more.

PDS2PDS offers these advantages over PDSMATCH:

o  You can now specify separate member name patterns
   and wildcard searches for each pds.
o  Error detection and reporting is improved.
o  The Help screens are now a set of ISPF Tutorial panels
o  Each Global and Line command is now a separate external
   subroutine which makes for easier modification and 
   maintenance.
o  You can easily add your own global or line commands
   to the utility.
o  The command confirmation panels are now more "ISPF"-like,
   such as the ones used in ISPF options 3.1, 3.4, etc.
o  PDS2PDS is much easier to install than PDSMATCH. One
   example of this is that the included COBOL program is now
   supplied as an executable load module. You no longer
   have to compile it yourself.

If you need to keep track of matching pds members, such
as test/production jcl, source, data, and other libraries,
then perhaps PDS2PDS would be of use. Best of all, it's free
for the taking.

You can get more details at http://wb4huc.home.texas.net/pds2pds


Thank you,

Mike Newell
Austin, TX
wb4huc@texas.net
```

- **From:** trout59727@aol.com (Trout59727)

- **Message-ID:** `<19990602143655.26195.00000126@ng-fv1.aol.com>`

I'm working with os/vs cobol. I have a variable-content record coming in to my
program that is 34 bytes long. The first 20 bytes are always the same, the last
14 bytes can contain different data (amount of withdrawal, deposit amount,
etc). I described the last 14 bytes as 

05 Variable-content.
    10 fld-a       pic 9(05).
    10              pic x(09).
05 code1 redefines variable-content.
    10 fld c       pic 9(04)v99.
    10 fld d       pic xx.
    10 fld e       pic 9(04)v99.
05 code2 redefines variable-content.
    10 yada
    10 yada
    10 yada

Is this the proper way of describing a variable content record in the
working-storage section? 

TIA,
RICH
```

<a id="t-69fd6633a3"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Power Form — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Power Form

- **From:** "Jack van der Hout" <woodcomp@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j5jbs$hgk$1@reader.wxs.nl>`

```
Is it possible to change the paper-tray (hopper) so that I can switch
between the 2 hoppers. I want a copy of  a  form to be printed on a blank
paper in hopper 2 and the original form on pre-printed paper in hopper 1.

Tx.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Power Form

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bjv53.1256$LI5.124329@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7j5jbs$hgk$1@reader.wxs.nl>`

```
Jack van der Hout wrote in message <7j5jbs$hgk$1@reader.wxs.nl>...
>Is it possible to change the paper-tray (hopper) so that I can switch
>between the 2 hoppers. I want a copy of  a  form to be printed on a blank
>paper in hopper 2 and the original form on pre-printed paper in hopper 1.


Define two printer files and a ws switch to indicate whether you are on an
odd or even page.  Your header routine would toggle the switch and your
print routine would check it to know which printer to use.  If the output of
your even printer comes out in reverse order with your hardware setup, your
"even" print routine might have to release your print lines to a sort file
to correct the problem.

HTH

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

## Thread: Reading from a .txt file — 4 this month / 5 total

_Thread started [1999-05](1999-05.md#t-d041a1892d)._

#### ↳ Re: Reading from a .txt file

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3756BB27.3E06@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
grob99@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi there.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
Status 35 means FILE NOT FOUND, 39 (i think) means wrong file attribute
Try with variable record length

SELECT MyFile ASSIGN TO "whatever.dat" FILE STATUS IS MyFile-Stat.

FD MyFile RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM 10 TO 20 CHARACTERS 
	  DEPENDING ON MyFile-Len.
01 w-Names.
   05 w-Name1 pic x(5).
   05 w-Name2 pic x(5).
   05 w-reserve pic x(10).
...
...

OPEN INPUT MyFile 
IF <StatusOk>
   READ MyFile NEXT
   IF MyFile-Len = 10
      <It is my record - hope so>
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading from a .txt file

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Oz53.1268$LI5.125238@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

grob99@my-deja.com wrote in message <7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I'm trying to read from a textfile made in Visual Basic. But COBOL
>returns with an error code 35 or 39. The file consists of 10 chars, and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   05 w-name2   pic X(5)


One small point (no pun intended), there should be a period at the end of
the above three lines.  Assuming your input file is a text file, i.e., a CR
(x'0d') and/or LF (x'0a') separates the lines, specifying 'ORGANIZATION LINE
SEQUENTIAL' in your SELECT statement should solve your problem.  HTH

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading from a .txt file

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j6v8u$mav$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
A 39 error is file not found, so that is probably a bug in your VB.  In the
VB file, write it out as a text file, and test that the data is ok with a
standard text editor.  You can also create a test file with a text editor,
and read that into your Cobol.  That way you are testing one program at a
time.

In Cobol, a line sequential file is a standard file just like source code,
each line terminated by a CR,LF.  Make sure your record description is large
enough to hold the longest line in the file.

grob99@my-deja.com wrote in message <7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi there.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading from a .txt file

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@inospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j7hpe$o6a@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7iu6ge$ftm$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7j6v8u$mav$2@news.igs.net>`

```
If you are getting an "ANSI" file status of 39 - it is NOT a "not found"
condition but a "fixed file attribute conflict".
```

## Thread: 3GL vs 3 layers — 14 msgs

### 3GL vs 3 layers

- **From:** "David Mowat" <david@dandm.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bj%53.1333$H21.5259@ozemail.com.au>`

```
Hi people,
Another request for information and experience.
On the one hand there is an application that has COBOL code with embedded
SQL accessing a proprietry database. On the other hand there is an
application written in COBOL using indexed files (described in the FDs) to
hold the data. Both are networked in the range of up to 200 terminals on the
microsoft NT platform. What are the pros and cons of the two methods.
Thankyou
Daivd
```

#### ↳ Re: 3GL vs 3 layers

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#MMny8wr#GA.309@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<Bj%53.1333$H21.5259@ozemail.com.au>`

```
re SQL vs native indexed, using NT server and NT workstations.

    You did not give details about the setup, ie does "NT PLATFORM" refer to
server only, workstations only, or both.  If we assume both, then we can
make some assumtions.

1.  The native indexed file method is easiest to program, but can easily
overload a network or server if pushed too hard.  Note that with current
software (Novell 4.x and NT 4.0), you will need roughly double the hardware
when using an NT server.

    An "independant" report is on Novell's web site with all of the
information.  Do you suppose it is unbiased?  However, experience has taught
me that this is true.  We are assured that NT 5.0 (Win2000) will solve these
problems.   We have also been told that win 2000 is the largest project yet.
I am not willing to trust my professional reputation on something that large
and overdue.  Win98 was not as relaible as win95.  Win2000 will probably not
be any better.  My opinion.

    A native indexed file will go corrupt easily.  Naive users turn
workstations off, reboot, who knows what.  Even Novells vaunted
Transactional system that worked so well with dos workstations does not
really help (win98 and client 3.10).

    I just looked at your message again.  There is no way in hell that a
native indexed file system will support 200 users on any pc network, let
alone NT.  If you can get 30 active users up on an NT 4 based network I
 would be pleasantly surprised.  The thin client concept holds some promise,
 but scaling up to 200 users would be a problem.  Once you exceed the
capacity of a single
Terminal server (memory, CPU, etc) you would be back to a network.  A small,
busy network, but a network.


2.  I have no direct experience with SQL, but it HAS to be more reliable
than native files!   The SQL server would handle a lot of the IO internally,
would handle transactions, etc.

PS: If SQL works for 200 workstations, tell us about it.  We would like to
hear about something other than problems some of the time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 3GL vs 3 layers

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375aa7e7.1609576885@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<Bj%53.1333$H21.5259@ozemail.com.au> <#MMny8wr#GA.309@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com> <5O263.1419$H21.6253@ozemail.com.au>`

```
Snipped it all...


I've performed all 4 types of development.

1.  Unix style (VMS/CICS) where the processing and files reside on the
same box, and the interface is "thin client" in it's most basic sense.

2.  Thick client - where files reside on the file server, but all
processing is on the local PC - Indexed based client.

3.  Client/Server - where the client does processing, but access the
data that is presented to the client from a process running on a
server.  (This is what Oracle and SQL server do).

4.  Client/Server thin client.  The database runs on the server, as
does the process accessing the data.

By far the first is the fastest - obviously fewer layers of
processing.

2 can be faster than 3 depending on the load on the database and the
speed of the process running at the workstation.  Even for large
numbers of users.  However, option 2 has the disadvantage of the fact
that a workstation crash will likely corrupt data.  Speed vs 3 depends
on how loaded the database is.  I have had systems that were much
faster as type 2 because of the load on the database serve and/or
organization of the database tables.

Type 4 has the potential to be the fastest and most stable.  However,
you have now put all your eggs in one basket.

Another thing to consider is expense, both is hardware and software.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 3GL vs 3 layers

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B_v63.16847$eU6.2045@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<Bj%53.1333$H21.5259@ozemail.com.au> <#MMny8wr#GA.309@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
In general, I agree with what Russell says below.  However, there are
some exceptions.  For example, some PC based COBOL compilers permit
you to use Btrieve as your indexed file handler.  Btrieve is a very
powerful transactional based file system.  I wrote a non-trivial (100k+
LOC) multi-user inventory system in COBOL that uses Btrieve.  It has
been online for about 12 years, and my client has never had to reload
the database because of a corrupted file.  Assuming the same file I/O
engine, compared to record level I/O requests, SQL is very inefficient.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 3GL vs 3 layers

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#4g5Y$Ps#GA.56@nih2naac.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<Bj%53.1333$H21.5259@ozemail.com.au> <#MMny8wr#GA.309@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
    There have not been any flames (yet - Monday morning), but I feel that I
should qualify my previous message by noteing that I was referring to a pure
native indexed file, that is nothing runs on server but file and print
services, and no 3rd party database services, such as Btrieve, Fileshare
(Microfocus), Oracle, etc.

    I also assumed that each terminal is modestly busy, and that a fair
degree of contention exists for record locking.

    The runtime version also matters.  I have been using MF v3.1 for dos (16
bit).  V2.5 to 3.1 are known to be unnaturally slow when accessing shared
data files (even if open input).  Version 3.2 is faster, but we have had
stability problems with 3.2 (dos 16 bit).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: help, please!!!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pb773.4308$By5.23@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<375978AA.BA25FD8@istar.ca> <ROw63.16943$eU6.2068@news2.mia> <375C2D40.33DBBE50@istar.ca>`

```
"COBOL Unleashed" is an intermediate to advanced book on programming
in COBOL.  Going much farther than simply covering COBOL, 'Unleashed'
is about 'how' to write good COBOL.  The author is Jon Wessler, but
much of the book was written by other experienced COBOL programmers,
several of whom frequent this newsgroup.  I wrote chapter 25, and the
sample program for that chapter covers most things you might want to
do with screens.  Chapter 26 covers GUI interfaces.  Unleashed comes
with a CD that contains all the source code in the book, plus a number
of third party tools.  You can get "COBOL Unleashed" from most good
book stores.  You can get it at www.bookpool.com for about $33 U.S.,
or www.amazon.com for about $40 U.S.

"COBOL Unleashed" is not a beginner's book, but for people who know
how to program in COBOL already, but want to know more.  If you want
a beginner's tutorial, try Thane Hubbell's excellent "Teach Yourself
COBOL in 24 Hours", also from Sams.  'TYC 24 Hours' covers screen
handling, but not in as much detail as 'Unleashed'.  'TYC 24 Hours'
is about $17 U.S. from www.bookpool.com.

Both books include the Fujitsu COBOL 3 compiler, and all the sample
code in 'TYC 24 Hours' is written using that compiler.
```

#### ↳ Re: What is the error RTS 148?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-05T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<375929aa@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7jav1u$hb2$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
148 is "wrong access mode for write".
E.g. you opened the file for input and now you are trying to write to it.

<a id="t-6230375fdb"></a>
## Thread: will cobol stick around? — 29 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### will cobol stick around?

- **From:** "Misha" <samvel65@altavista.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
 I was wondering if Cobol is going to be around in 5-10 years.  Because
 people say that it's not worth learning it, C++, or VB is better.  Are they
 right, and should I go with C++ instead of COBOL?  My brother is a COBOL
 programmer with the Ameritech, and he says COBOL is boring as a language.
 Any suggestions on what language is gonna dominate in the future?
```

#### ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375ad745@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```

Misha <samvel65@altavista.net> wrote in message
> I was wondering if Cobol is going to be around in 5-10 years.  Because
>  people say that it's not worth learning it, C++, or VB is better.  Are
they
>  right, and should I go with C++ instead of COBOL

COBOL will be around 20 years from now, because it is good at solving
Business Problems. C++ or VB very likely will have been supplanted by
something else, e.g. Java or *its* successor(s).

But, you should learn *both* COBOL and C++ (and a smattering of VB)
because these skills are useful *right now*.
```

#### ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jgmnm$b3n$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Misha wrote in message <7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>...
> I was wondering if Cobol is going to be around in 5-10 years.  Because
> people say that it's not worth learning it, C++, or VB is better.  Are
they
> right, and should I go with C++ instead of COBOL?  My brother is a COBOL
> programmer with the Ameritech, and he says COBOL is boring as a language.
> Any suggestions on what language is gonna dominate in the future?

People keep asking for this. First of all I would like to say that it isn't
Cobol that is boring, but your brothers lack of skills. An experienced
programmer will never say a programming language is boring.
This is just like the numerous religious "wars" of what is a "real"
programming language. If all the arguments being used in these discussions
had been true, we would all do machine level byte code.

I for one would say it like this: If you can do a program with it, it is a
programming language. Considering whether Cobol is boring or not, what is
its purpose? to entertain the programmer? Your brother have missed a very
important fact, Cobol, as any programming languages is a tool. Nothing more,
nothing less. I could say that a saw is a boring tool. However, I guess many
would find it to be an exciting tool if I were to cut a 10 Megawatt power
cable ungrounded...

A tool can't be boring, but the one who performs with the tool.

Cobol and the future to come. "Wise" men has predicted the end of Cobol for
years, if not before, at least at the y2k. Time has proven them to be wrong.
My 2 cents is that Cobol will increase even more in the future. Why? At the
current time, computing has never been more open, and it will be even more
open in the time to come. There are things happening behind the scenes,
which I can't tell you about, but you will understand when you see, read and
at not at least experience. So far has the big issue been the fact that once
inside a programming environment you're locked. This is not quite true, to
some extent there has been options of workarounds (linked in binaries, file
communication, CORBA, COM, DLL) These options will increase in numbers,
flexibility and how easy they are to use. In parallel, the strength of each
individual language in targeting their particular core task will be even
more appreciated. The need for replacing what some would consider obsolete
languages will fade away.

Another important issue to consider, is that there are an infinite number of
lines of Cobol code out there, which cannot be replaced, both because it
would take too long and because it would be too expensive. It is my believe
that the demand for Cobol developers will increase as downsizing from
Mainframes to cheap unix clusters (may be wolfpack too) will increase.

On the other side, it is jerks like your brother who make sure I have a good
pay today, please tell him to continue to spread his nonsense and I will
have a comfortable pay well beyond my retirement.

Yours sincerely,

Cheesle
```

On Mon, 7 Jun 1999 19:18:07, "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net> 
wrote:

> I could say that a saw is a boring tool. 

Actually, a saw is a cutting tool, an auger is a boring tool!

COBOL, on the other hand, is one of the great computer languages and 
doesn't make my eyes hurt to read as C does.
If I were really good in C and were writing code that only I would 
ever maintain and were a real fanatic about in-line commenting, then I
 might not write a lot of COBOL, but since others have to maintain my 
work and since I sometimes have to go back to code I wrote decades 
ago, COBOL still does the trick for me!

=Dwight=
{who is also quite competent in rexx, fortran, basic, pl1, sas, jcl, 
codap, irl, forth, beef, cogo, and at least a dozen assemblers)
X1=L, X2=L & the domain is phonetic
```

- **Message-ID:** `<375ebc4b.11564340@news.enter.net>`

Cheesle:

Isn't English wonderful?  Some words are spelled identically, but mean
different things and others sound identical, are spelled differently
and mean different things.

Ate dear eight for pounds of there corn every too ours.

Eight deer ate four pounds of their corn every two hours.

A spell checker would have never caught a problem with the first
sentence.  I'll stop...this is getting "boring."

good reason...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00

> would be very expensive. No one will want to spend that money without a
very

I don't think COBOL will be around for a long time because it is expensive
to rewrite all those Business applications, but because COBOL is the
best there is for these applications. It simply does the job best.
That is why COBOL is still around and why it will be around for a long
time to come.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<3764c415@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7jgmnm$b3n$1@news.cerf.net> <7jgvub$c74@netnews1.apci.com> <929240553.895898@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <37631624@news3.us.ibm.net> <376492A5.3CF578E7@earthlink.net>`

```
PL/I was quite Cobol-like (e.g. its data structures) but was proprietary and
was much more complex than Cobol (granted more powerful).
A problem with PL/I was the complicated rules for default behavior. Almost
anything was allowed, the language taking an appropriate (!) decision as
to what a construct meant, except few (any?) programmers could always
say what that decision was.

- **From:** nisus@mindspring.com (Frank Clarke)
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00

```
On Mon, 14 Jun 1999 03:57:59 -0500, "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:

>PL/I was quite Cobol-like (e.g. its data structures) but was proprietary and
>was much more complex than Cobol (granted more powerful).
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>news:376492A5.3CF578E7@earthlink.net...

>> And why exactly is it the best? Compared to what? Say, what makes PL/I so
>> inferior to COBOL in business applications?

That is: the programmer actually had to know what s/he was doing.  Yes, I can
see where that would be a critical problem.  It probably also explains why
poorly-written PL/1 will run rings around all but the very best COBOL.

It's really too bad that IBM's greed and pride got in the way of PL/1's wide
acceptance.  But for that, this would be a PL/1 world and COBOL would be in as
wide-use as APL... and for the same reason.


Frank Clarke
Tampa Area REXX Programmers' Alliance (TARPA)
Member of the REXX Language Assn
  Join us at http://www.rexxla.org
(remove currency symbol when replying)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<37650709.5A5@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7jgmnm$b3n$1@news.cerf.net> <7jgvub$c74@netnews1.apci.com> <929240553.895898@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <37631624@news3.us.ibm.net> <376492A5.3CF578E7@earthlink.net> <3764c415@news3.us.ibm.net> <3764f97a.18758377@NEWS.mindspring.com>`

```
Frank Clarke wrote:
> It probably also explains why
> poorly-written PL/1 will run rings around all but the very best COBOL.

I'm not sure this is a true statement.  Because of PL/I's plethora of
features the compiler can't always generate the best code, even for the
simple cases.

> 
> It's really too bad that IBM's greed and pride got in the way of PL/1's wide
> acceptance.  But for that, this would be a PL/1 world and COBOL would be in as
> wide-use as APL... and for the same reason.

One of PL/I's problems was that it was ahead of its time.  I learned
PL/I back in '67 on what was probably version 1 of the compiler.  At the
time it would only run on the biggest 360's under OS/360 - probably
model 50 and above.  At the time this was a *big* machine and most 360
users were running mod 25's and 30's under DOS.  Heck, there were still
a lot of 14xx machines around.  COBOL would run on all of 'em, although
the 14xx people tended to prefer Autocoder for performance.

When PL/I was introduced for DOS a couple of years later it was a subset
only and the feature set was comparable to COBOL, but with more
overhead, and by this time everyone had converted those Autocoder
programs to COBOL anyway.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Mudak Pidaras <mpidaras@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37682A0D.84401DED@hotmail.com>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7jgmnm$b3n$1@news.cerf.net> <7jgvub$c74@netnews1.apci.com> <929240553.895898@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <37631624@news3.us.ibm.net> <376492A5.3CF578E7@earthlink.net> <3764c415@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Peter Norton provided an interesting insight into PL/I vs. COBOL back in 1984:

**********
PL/I was a great idea, in many ways far ahead of its time. It wasn't until some
years after PL/I's creation that the structured programming concept was
developed, yet PL/I was a "structured" language from its very beginning.
Unfortunately, PL/I didn't catch on too well in the mainframe world, partly
because lots of deadheads really liked everything that was wrong with COBOL and
FORTRAN, partly because PL/I was too complex for the average programmer to
master, and partly because IBM's efforts to tell people how to program raised
anti-IBM hackles. (Haven't you noticed that the success of the PC has brought
lots of antiIBMers out of
the closet again?) 
**********

Mudak


Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> PL/I was quite Cobol-like (e.g. its data structures) but was proprietary and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

> > > I don't think COBOL will be around for a long time because it is
> expensive
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<7kje6u$8i9@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7jek7r$2sm$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <7jgmnm$b3n$1@news.cerf.net> <7jgvub$c74@netnews1.apci.com> <929240553.895898@gorgoroth.esoterica.pt> <37631624@news3.us.ibm.net> <376492A5.3CF578E7@earthlink.net> <7k66kr$rim$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jack Lam <jack_lam_98@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:7k66kr$rim$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> It appears that many shops are converting Pl1 to COBOL or C.
> Can anybody explain this phenomenon?
>

Does the phrase "buzz-word du jour" mean anything to you?

I don't have the history in data processing that some readers/contributers
to this NG have, but I haven't seen a year go by when SOME
product/language/methodology wasn't viewed as the "salvation and future" of
the world.  Now, I do believe that C is a perfectly valid programming
language (as is C++, PL/I, Java, etc) - but I rarely see ANY advantage in
"converting" an existing FUNCTIONING application from one language to
another (even if the "coverted to" language is COBOL).  HOWEVER, it is true
that computer resources are (relatively) cheap and programmers are
(relatively) expensive today (unlike in the past).  Therefore, I *do* fully
understand shops trying to do developement (and even in SOME cases
maintenance) in the "language of the day" where they can get programming
resources most easily and at the cheapest price available.

As long as universities, computer training schools, etc are turning out C
programmers, then I can see shops looking at this as a "good" language for
their sites.  On the other hand, if a manager (usually a mid-level manager
who can barely spell "C" much less "COBOL") thinks that "COBOL or PL/I or
even Assembler is DEAD", then I will make it pretty clear to that manager
that they are wrong - as history has proven.
```

<a id="t-95e1004eb2"></a>
## Thread: listing combinations from DB2 table — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### listing combinations from DB2 table

- **From:** Jacob Elgart <j_elgart@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375A19D9.7336BBF2@hotmail.com>`

```
Folks:

In a DB2 table ZZG.TZGAZZ01 with about 3 million rows I have, among others, 4
columns A, B, C, and D. They can assume about 500 to 1000 distinct values each.
I need to create a simple report listing all unique combinations of A, B, C, D
in the table. My first impulse was to have a cursor with a query like, say,
select distinct A||B||C||D ordered by this entire concatenation, count
occurences for every combo in a control break, and unstring the cluster before
printing. Any thoughts about doing it more efficiently? In COBOL, that is.
(Someone in the meeting mentioned like 'you can do it in 80 or so lines of SAS'.
As usually with SAS, this was probably quite a bit exaggerated, plus we do have
SAS here but in our group nobody programs it.) 

Any advice?

Thanx
Jacob Elgart
```

#### ↳ Re: listing combinations from DB2 table

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375A382E.6584F74D@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<375A19D9.7336BBF2@hotmail.com>`

```
Jacob,

Your COBOL plan looks fine to me, even though COBOL gurus may have sharper eyes.

As far as the SAS brevity goes, your esteemed colleague did exaggerate. Here's a
_complete_ SAS program accomplishing this task, together with JCL (less the job
card):

// EXEC SAS OPTIONS='MEMSIZE=100M ERRORABEND'
//SYSIN DD *,DLM='$$'  
PROC SQL;
   CONNECT TO DB2 (SSID=DB2P);
   SELECT * FROM CONNECTION TO DB2 
   (CREATE VIEW ABCD AS SELECT A,B,C,D FROM ZZG.TZGAZZ01);      
quit;

PROC FREQ data=abcd;
   TABLES A*B*C*D / LIST;
run;
$$
 
Replace DB2P with whatever your subsystem ID is. Statements in lowercase are
optional. In addition to the frequencies, cumulative frequencies, percents and
cumulative percents will be printed also. If you do not want them, add the words
NOPERCENT NOCUM after the slash. If you have nulls on the DB2 table and want to
list them as valid values, add MISSING after the slash. Default output DD is
SASLIST; the report goes there. If you do not want the output to go to SASLIST
but rather to an output file whose DD is OUT, add it to the JCL and then before
the PROC FREQ, add the line

PROC PRINTTO PRINT=OUT;

Watch the semicolons!!! Your colleague must have meant '8 or so lines of SAS'.
Either way, one who is equally good at COBOL and SAS, is guaranteed to have the
SAS report on his boss's desk long before he would start debugging his first
compile should he decide to code it in COBOL (or anything else, for that
matter).   

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL      
===================
 
     
Jacob Elgart wrote:
> 
> Folks:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jacob Elgart
```

## Thread: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43 — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43

- **From:** Carlos Featherston <featherston@intranet.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375AB8B4.A3C67AC5@intranet.ca>`

```
Hi all,

Actually I am not even a newbie, I am a student just starting to learn
COBOL. Problem I have is interpreting properly the description for an
error 43 and make the necessary changes to the code. My text describes
it as:

"An attempt was made to rewrite a record that wasn't first read from the
file."

This error code pops up in my assignment for a non-sequential update
program. What can anyone else add to the above description that might
help me understand the problem I am encountering. I obviously believe I
have coded my program properly, however, 43 proves me wrong. What part
of the code should I be re-examining in order to correct this problem?

FYI: The portion of code dealing with the REWRITE in question has a part
that tests for presence on all fields of the transaction file (not the
master file), using REDEFINES, in order to update only those fields on
the master file that have any data to update.

I hope I have explained myself well enough to make sense to the reader.
Thanking all in advance...Carlos
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7Sz63.1543$U51.78701@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<375AB8B4.A3C67AC5@intranet.ca>`

```
The error message makes sense: you can never REWRITE a record unless it was
first READ and that READ was the most recent I-O operation against that
file; but it sounds like you are doing that.

So... I don't think you can REWRITE a file which is SELECTed with ACCESS IS
SEQUENTIAL. (The ORGANIZATION can be sequential). Without looking at the
SELECT or the code, I can't tell if this may be causing a problem.

So check your SELECT; and if you have to change that, you've got some other
work to do as well.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375b48fc.1650819665@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<375AB8B4.A3C67AC5@intranet.ca> <7Sz63.1543$U51.78701@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
On Sun, 06 Jun 1999 19:20:35 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>The error message makes sense: you can never REWRITE a record unless it was
>first READ and that READ was the most recent I-O operation against that
>file; but it sounds like you are doing that.

This isn't "Exactly" accurate.  One can rewrite and indexed file
record, using Random or Dynamic access mode, even when one has not
first read the record.   

That said, the OTHER conditions could cause a 48.  (Access sequential
without first reading, etc).
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie Question: Indexed I-O File Status Error 43

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eSt8X$Ps#GA.56@nih2naac.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<375AB8B4.A3C67AC5@intranet.ca>`

```
Did you read the record in question WITH lock before starting to update it.
Note: do not do any screen input after locking record, as a general rule of
politeness.
```

```
I would appreciate comments (good or bad) from anyone who has deployed
programs using flexus sp2, FormPrint and/or their Thin Client products.

I am most interested in their performance and stability in a production
end-user environment using Windows 9x/NT and NetExpress 3.0 COBOL. We
have downloaded their demo versions and our initial evaluations look
promising (although we have been waiting for a response to our e-mail
request for pricing and support information for about 4 days now).

#### ↳ Re: Experiences with flexus products

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-1dCK0HDEy5Xc@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au>`

```
On Mon, 7 Jun 1999 13:40:40, Robert Kovacic <rjk@onaustralia.com.au> 
wrote:

> I would appreciate comments (good or bad) from anyone who has deployed
> programs using flexus sp2, FormPrint and/or their Thin Client products.
> 

This is important.  I have worked on several projects - some 
MicroFocus Dialog system and other using SP2.  The Sp2 applications 
perform in the field exactly as designed, and performance is 
consistant.  The Dialog applications have a habit of "looking" 
different in the field, and not performing the same and they are 
unstable.  These problems are correctable, but with a lot of work on 
machine configuration.  The Sp2 products I have deployed always work.

I'll get you the address of a regular here who uses Sp2 with 
Netexpress 3.0 for a POS application (Bob (not Wolfe) are you there?).

FormPrint will knock your socks of.  It's great.



-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Flexus and Fujitsu

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jlovv$jr9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au> <375bfd85.1114128@news.enter.net>`

```
This is really to Bob, but I am posting it as there have been several
questions regarding Barcodes in the last few weeks.  Perhaps others are
having the same problem.

The problem is with spaces in barcodes.  It seems the FormPrint uses space
as alignment only, and does not send them to the printer. This is not a
problem (as far as I know) with any font but barcodes. However, in the
barcode font, space is a "printable" character, and the barcode omits them.

Am I making an assumption here Bob?  Or is there a way of actually
"printing" a space?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flexus and Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375FB47C.24C30AF3@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au> <375bfd85.1114128@news.enter.net> <7jlovv$jr9$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-g072tbHWhrLe@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7jncc8$iio$2@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> It could very well be the Font.  Bob has asked for the font so that he 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Barcodes generally do not fall into standard formatting.  If you
use barcode 128 then 20 hex stands for 20 numeric.  If spaces are
absorbed on the way out then the result will be a real space in
the barcode not the digits 20.

Most formatting tools have special code to send in digits and
output the mapping font directly to the printer.  Maybe this could
be a future extension for Bob.

If you have any real problems take a look at the font itself.  You
could remap the hex 20 value to something else and then use that
hex value instead.  I had exactly the same problem with a Xerox
DJDE conversion to IBM AFP (hex 40 for IBM though :->).

Happy hunting
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flexus and Fujitsu

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "David W. Furin" <dfurin@larich.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8D65E331AB83E49A.73538C006A1C862F.45C7411BFA668CBD@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au> <375bfd85.1114128@news.enter.net> <7jlovv$jr9$1@news.igs.net> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-g072tbHWhrLe@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <7jncc8$iio$2@news.igs.net> <375FB47C.24C30AF3@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Barcodes generally do not fall into standard formatting.  If you
> use barcode 128 then 20 hex stands for 20 numeric.  
> 
(snip)

I believe this is true only in subset "C" of code 128.  In subsets "A"
or "B" hex 20 would be the number 4.  Things can get really confusing
because any of the three sets can be invoked within the same label by
printing the appropriate start character.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Barcodes: Re: Flexus and Fujitsu

- **From:** Charles Hall <charles.hall@platinum.brooks.af.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375EC261.7249@platinum.brooks.af.mil>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au> <375bfd85.1114128@news.enter.net> <7jlovv$jr9$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Hi! I'm not currently using COBOL but barcodes are a constant problems
in medical labs.  There are several different barcodes in use, some
which include spaces in the character set while others don't.  However,
we've run across cases in which instrument manufactures advertise one of
the space supporting barcodes, but then find out the instrument doesn't
know how to read a space character.  Our software will print the space
character fine but it does us no good if the instrument can't read a
space in a barcode label.

A good introduction to several barcodes is at
http://www.idbearing.com/symbol.html.  It lists the most popular bar
codes in use, their technical specs and character sets, and show
examples of these symbologies.

Charles Hall
Brooks AFB TX

donald tees wrote:
>

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "printing" a space?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flexus and Fujitsu

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375eb8e6.10695340@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au> <375bfd85.1114128@news.enter.net> <7jlovv$jr9$1@news.igs.net>`

```
"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote:

>This is really to Bob, but I am posting it as there have been several
>questions regarding Barcodes in the last few weeks.  Perhaps others are
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>"printing" a space?


Hi Donald!

I created a field in FormPrint with a PIC X(34) and initialized it
with the following value:

0123456789  0123456789  0123456789

(3 sets of zero through nine characters with 2 blank spaces between
each set)

The font was established as a times new roman 10 point normal weight.
I then duplicated the field and changed only the font to the
ZipCodeBarCode font and printed the Form.  It printed blank spaces
between the sets of 0 through 9 bar codes.

I then created exactly the same text strings in Word for Windows on
the same machine and printed them with the same printer and it printed
the bar code and the blank spaces in the same manner as FormPrint
printed them.

Perhaps it's the bar code font you are using.  Is it a True Type Font
or some other type of font?  What was the field format used in your
FormPrint bar code field?

Can you send the font to us for testing purposes or will we need to
license it?


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00

```

Bob Wolfe wrote in message <375eb8e6.10695340@news.enter.net>...


<Barcodes revisited and snipped>

Bob, I have done the following.  In MSword, I formated five spaces followed
by a 1 with the barcode font.  I then formated the single digit 1 the same
way, on the following line.  The difference is obvious both on the screen,
and to the eye.

Then, using formprint, I defined a field as X(6), and moved a 1 to it.  That
comes out on paper the same as the single digit 1.

I have forwarded both the MSword document and the font to you by mail.  If
anyone else, BTW, wants the barcode font, I can send it. It is from a
"freeware" CD, and though copyrighted, says in the docs that it can be given
but not sold.
```

On Mon, 07 Jun 1999 23:40:40 +1000, Robert Kovacic
<rjk@onaustralia.com.au> wrote:

>I would appreciate comments (good or bad) from anyone who has deployed
>programs using flexus sp2, FormPrint and/or their Thin Client products.

>request for pricing and support information for about 4 days now).

We have been using SP2 for over two years now and have just
implemented a Tuxedo, Fujitsu Cobol, SP2, FormPrint, and SP2 thin
client combination on Windows NT connected to a Unisys Clearpath
(Unisys 2200) mainframe.  So far we have not encountered any problems
at all.

In regards to your waiting for your response, this is news to me.
They always response on time within few hours.

Tony M. Mina
```

#### ↳ Re: Experiences with flexus products

- **From:** John <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jj5d6$kj7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au>`

```
In article <375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au>,
  Robert Kovacic <rjk@onaustralia.com.au> wrote:
> I would appreciate comments (good or bad) from anyone who has deployed
> programs using flexus sp2, FormPrint and/or their Thin Client
products.
>
> I am most interested in their performance and stability in a
production
> end-user environment using Windows 9x/NT and NetExpress 3.0 COBOL. We
> have downloaded their demo versions and our initial evaluations look
> promising (although we have been waiting for a response to our e-mail
> request for pricing and support information for about 4 days now).
>

Our experience with Flexus products and people have been great. I
highly recommend them. They seem to understand the way COBOL works and
have designed their products to fit. The learning curve is extremely
short. Support is excellent. Flexus is always improving and growing
their products. Deployment is a breeze. we have not experienced a
single problem.

Just so you'll know I'm not a shill, I started programming COBOL in the
mid-sixties and have used an inordinate number of development tools
since then. I have used at least 5 other languages and their
development tools as well. I have developed systems using ISAM, VSAM,
Oracle, DB2, and many other data bases. I have developed and deployed
systems on more OSes than I care to remember. I have been developing
client server products since 1986. My language preference is COBOL. My
compiler preference is Realia. My GUI development tool preference is
Flexus SP2. My report development tool preference is Crystal Reports.
My Internet/Intranet deployment tool preference is Flexus Thin Client.
My client server database preference is Pervasive.SQL 7.0. All of these
were chosen for their reliability, applicability, and track record.

Hope this helps.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Experiences with flexus products

- **From:** mojo@chesapeake.net (Mojo)
- **Date:** 1999-06-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928868620.164.7@news.remarQ.com>`
- **References:** `<375BCBD8.8BDB8AB4@onaustralia.com.au>`

```
Robert Kovacic <rjk@onaustralia.com.au> wrotf:

>I would appreciate comments (good or bad) from anyone who has deployed
>programs using flexus sp2, FormPrint and/or their Thin Client products.

>I am most interested in their performance and stability in a production
>end-user environment using Windows 9x/NT and NetExpress 3.0 COBOL. We
>have downloaded their demo versions and our initial evaluations look
>promising (although we have been waiting for a response to our e-mail
>request for pricing and support information for about 4 days now).

****  I'll add my two cents better late than never.

       My project has 4 software modules comprising a time and
inventory tracking system.  All are character based and we've
finished converting the first to GUI using SP2.  They were
originally developed using MF COBOL and we're still using
Workbench 4.0.32 as the COBOL 'engine'.

       I agree totally with the other comments regarding SP2 and
Flexus.  The product, for us, was the right fit to go Gui.  Support
has been excellent.  And I can't agree more with Thane Hubbell's
comments regarding MF Dialog.  We evaluated an earlier version,
2.0, and were totally turned off by the frequent aborts/abends during
application development.  Now mind you, all the problems we
experienced might be fixed in the latest release incorporated into
NetExpress.  But, the bad taste still lingers.

Mike Madara
```

#### ↳ Re: I need source code or the syntax to add days to a date

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)

In article <7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net>, "Luke Medland"
<luke.medland@virgin.net> writes:

>
>Add 3 Days to 28/07/1999 for instance.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Have you tried something like 

 compute new-date  = function date-of-integer(
               function integer-of-date(19990728) + 3 )

 compute daysdiff = function integer-of-date(19990728)
                           -  function integer-of-date(19990601)
```

#### ↳ Re: I need source code or the syntax to add days to a date

- **From:** "Louleon" <trinhhuynh@sprint.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OLG73.47333$r_1.25135022@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`
- **References:** `<7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
Hi Luke,
have no code for you but it might be a help if you want to have some logic
idea to start with.
If not , please disregard my Post.
    1. Create 2 tables which contain the number of each day of the regular
year and of the leap year ( which divide by 4 return reminder =0)
                ex:  Jan.01 = number 1 of year
                       Oct.10 = number283 of year
        In leap year, Feb.29 becomes day 60 and the numbers in the table
increase by 1 for all following days.
        So, the number between dates is  ...........283 - 1 = 282 days.
        28/07 = 209    + 3 => 212  => 31/07.    OR
        30/08 = 242     +3 => 245  =>02/09    and so on.....
    2. You will probably use condition level (88).

Good Luck in coding,
Please post your code when you finish
Have fun,
Lou
Luke Medland <luke.medland@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net...
> Add 3 Days to 28/07/1999 for instance.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need source code or the syntax to add days to a date

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7joi58$aq9$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net> <OLG73.47333$r_1.25135022@newscontent-02.sprint.ca>`

```
If I might make a suggestion to the following method, set up your tables as
if March 1 was the first day of the year.  Before using them, check the
month for jan. or feb., and if found, decrement the year by one. Convert the
month so that march is month 1, december month 10, jan 11, and feb is 12
(ETC.).  Leap years day is then always the last day of the year, and the
number of leap year days since your base date is the revised year divided by
4. The day integer is (until 2400) (CURRENT-YEAR - BASE YEAR)/4
+(CURRENT-YEAR - BASE-YEAR)*365 + MONTH-TABLE(MONTH) + DAY.

Louleon wrote in message ...
>Hi Luke,
>have no code for you but it might be a help if you want to have some logic
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: I need source code or the syntax to add days to a date

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<07P73.1629$LI5.160076@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
We don't have the integer-of-date function in our shop, but have copybooks
to do the following:

We assume Jan 1, 1901 is day zero and do offsets from that point.  First
subtract 1901 from the year.  The "days" part is equal to the (julian
days -1) plus years * 365.  Add (year - 1901) / 4 to get the count of prior
leap year days.  I'll leave the computation of julian days (day within a
year) and the checking for a Feb 29 before the date you are working on as an
exercise.

To find the number of days between dates, run each date through the above
logic and subtract.

To convert days back into a date,  first divide by 1461 (number of days in 4
years) and be sure to save the remainder.  After checking for specific
values which represent Feb 29 and Dec 31 of a leap year, divide by 365 and
you will be able to determine the year while converting the (julian days -
1) will give the month and day.

This example will work through 2099.  Correcting this logic to handle
century years not divisible by 400 would add a few more levels of complexity
to the process, starting by dividing by (365 * 400 - 3).  A new pivot date
(1/1/1701) would be needed for both parts.

Luke Medland wrote in message
<7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net>...
>Add 3 Days to 28/07/1999 for instance.
>
>And also the same to find the number of days between the dates.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I need source code or the syntax to add days to a date

- **From:** "Simon Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Se83.3862$EU4.2364@stones>`
- **References:** `<7jhedj$m37$1@nclient5-gui.server.virgin.net> <hiU73.3317$hh1.844@news1.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:hiU73.3317$hh1.844@news1.mia...
> Luke Medland wrote:
> >Add 3 Days to 28/07/1999 for instance.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Thanks for your help

I had a simular problem to yours only I needed to test if a given date was >
than 15 days if so then I would return a flag, I will post my code you may
be able to use it in some way.

The input is a date from a log file, and this is compared to current-date.
Note* i have'nt tested this yet, new code.

       method-id. "TestIfOld".

       local-storage section.
       01  Day-Month-Type        pic xx.
           88 30-Day-Month values are '4' '6' '9' '11'.
           88 31-Day-Month values are '1' '3' '5' '7' '8' '10' '12'.


       linkage section.
       01  lnk-status            pic x.
       01  lnk-dateText.
           05 lnkDayText         pic 99.
           05 filler             pic x.
           05 lnkMonthText       pic 99.
           05 filler             pic x.
           05 lnkYrText          pic 9(04).



       procedure division using lnk-dateText, returning lnk-status.

           subtract log-month from current-month giving
           ws-left

           if ws-left > 1 *>file is old, so flag up delete key.
              move 'YES' to lnk-status
           else
              if ws-left = 1
                 evaluate true
                   when 31-Day-Month
                      move 31 to Current-Month
                      perform Test-1-Month-Less
                   when 30-Day-Month
                      move 30 to Current-Month
                      perform Test-1-Month-Less
                   when log-month = 2
                   *>test if leap yr.
                   divide Current-yr by 4 giving Result
                   move Result to Manipulate-Result
                   if M-Result3 = 0 and M-Result4 = 0
                      move 29 to Current-Month
                      perform Test-1-Month-Less
                   else
                      move 28 to Current-Month
                      perform Test-1-Month-Less
                   end-if
                 end-evaluate
           else
             if ws-left = 0 *>then same month.
                Subtract Log-day from current-day giving ws-sum
                *>Test if the file is > 15.
                if ws-sum > 15
                   move 'YES' to lnk-status
                else
                   move 'NO ' to lnk-status
                end-if
             end-if
           end-if

       exit method.

       Test-1-Month-Less.
           Subtract log-day from Current-Month giving ws-sum
           compute ws-Total = ( ws-sum + current-day )
           *>test if the file is > 15 days old.
           if ws-Total > 15
              move 'YES' to lnk-status
           else
              move 'NO ' to lnk-status
           end-if
           .

       end method "TestIfOld".

Simon Hart.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL slow down RUMOR is not true. Read about it... — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL slow down RUMOR is not true. Read about it...

- **From:** "Gary Roush" <gkr@adtools.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ucW63.16$H73.702@typ42b.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
We were recently informed about a rumor suggesting that "Fujitsu is slowing
down its support for its COBOL product". As there is absolutely no truth to
this rumor, we thought we should send you some facts to reassure you that
Fujitsu is very strongly committed to its COBOL product - so you won't have
to waste time thinking about such rumors in the future.

1. We believe that Fujitsu has one of the largest, if not the largest,
COBOL compiler and tools development teams in the world.

2. Fujitsu started working with COBOL in 1965 and now delivers COBOL on
platforms from PCs to mainframes in U.S. and international markets.
Fujitsu's commitment to COBOL is long term and strategic.

3. Version 4.2 of Fujitsu COBOL was released in March 1999 and we are
developing Version 5 for release later this year. It will contain
enhancements such as support for the Windows 2000 Application Specification
and further tuning of our already superior run-time performance.

4. Fujitsu is the only COBOL vendor to have announced support for Intel's
IA-64 hardware. By building what is likely to be the only COBOL-specific
code generator for IA-64, we are continuing our tradition of being the
highest performing COBOL on all platforms that we support.

5. The Fujitsu COBOL group is actively working on the next COBOL standard
and has the closest OO COBOL implementation of any COBOL vendor.

6. Fujitsu has built the COBOL Cartridge for Oracle's Web Application
Server that allows COBOL programs to exploit all Oracle web server
technology

7. Fujitsu Software Corporation has put substantial effort into delivering
high quality code converters for such dialects as IBM COBOL, Micro Focus
COBOL, Microsoft COBOL and ICOBOL. This indicates our increasing support
for those moving to Fujitsu COBOL.

From these facts, we hope that you will agree with us that the message
should be: "No one is as committed to COBOL as is Fujitsu!"

If you hear someone passing on rumors that down play Fujitsu's commitment
to COBOL, please suggest to them that they contact us - as some of you
have - so that they can get the facts first-hand. (Email:
cobol@adtools.com; Phone: 408-428-0500)

With our best regards and thanks to all of you who wait to check out the
facts before acting on rumors!

The COBOL Team at Fujitsu Software Corporation.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL slow down RUMOR is not true. Read about it...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jn6c9$s5s@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<ucW63.16$H73.702@typ42b.nn.bcandid.com>`

```

## Thread: Microfocus XM over Windows NT server — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus XM over Windows NT server

- **From:** "Julia Sabater" <juli@infotelecom.es>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01beb0df$20fe87e0$2443e0c2@tiago>`

```
Hi, group:

I have random problems when I execute the MicroFocus eXtended Memory
manager (XM) over a Windows NT server (in server computer). The most
frequent problem is if the COBOL application launched with XM calls the DOS
command (either command.com or cmd.com), the DOS session (or the complete
system) hangs. 

After trying many configuration combinations, it seems to me the cause is
the XM version, a bit old-fashioned.

My currently XM is:

XM V1.4.6 - The Micro Focus DOS Extender Copyright (c) 1987-1994 Micro
Focus Ltd
URN AXCPA/000074570    [Protocol: DPMI]                                 
Ref 009

NT version is 4.0, with Service Pack 4.

Anybody knows there is a newer version or workaround to fix that problem?

(Yes, I know, I shouldn't use the system console to run user applications,
but...)

Thanks a lot!

Julia Sabater.
juli@infotelecom.es
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus XM over Windows NT server

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#rBDwk6s#GA.323@nih2naae.prod2.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<01beb0df$20fe87e0$2443e0c2@tiago>`

```
Have you tried my method of using the X"91" function call?
See "Re" Call wordpad from mf cobol v3.2"
The exact wording can make a BIG difference.  It works on my NT just fine,
although I have not installed SP4 yet.

Note that this method does not mention command or cmd at all (unless you
want
a blank command prompt).

    Here is a sample program.  Note that when calling a dos program,
it pauses and does not continue until the external program has exited.
When I tried it with WORDPAD however, the calling program continued running.
I suppose that the easy solution would be an accept after the external call.
This would
also allow the user to see that an error other than program not found
existed.

    If you want a blank command prompt, you must specify "COMMAND".


000100 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000200
000300 01  FUNCTION-X          PIC X VALUE X"91".
000400 01  RESULT              PIC 99 COMP         VALUE ZERO.
000500 01  CALL-PGM            PIC 99 COMP         VALUE 35.
000600 01  SET-CMD-LINE        PIC 99 COMP         VALUE 2.
000700 01  PARAMETER.
000800     05  PARM-SIZE       PIC 99 COMP.
000900     05  PARM            PIC X.
001000
001100 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
001200 0100-MENU.
001300     DISPLAY SPACES AT 0101.
001400     DISPLAY "CALL WORDPAD" AT 0101.
001500     DISPLAY "WORDPAD"   UPON COMMAND-LINE.
001600
001700     MOVE ZERO       TO PARM-SIZE.
001800     MOVE SPACES     TO PARM.
001900     CALL FUNCTION-X USING RESULT CALL-PGM PARAMETER.
002000     IF RESULT NOT = ZERO
002100       DISPLAY "ERROR CALLING EXTERNAL PGM" AT 1001.
002200     DISPLAY "ALL DONE"   AT 1501.
002300 THE-END.
002400     STOP RUN.





Thane Hubbell wrote in message ...
>On Tue, 8 Jun 1999 05:28:04, "Massimo Morgia"
><areasoftware@tiscalinet.it> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>






Julia Sabater wrote in message <01beb0df$20fe87e0$2443e0c2@tiago>...
>Hi, group:
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

3.0
voici les messages d'erreur:

Microsoft (R) 32-Bit Incremental Linker Version 3.00.5270
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1992-1995. All rights reserved.

C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\samples\SAMPLE4\SAMPLE4.OBJ
C:\FSC\PCOBOL32\PROJECT.RES

Est ce que vous possï¿½dez l'adresse du serveur ou l'e-mail de fujitsu ?

<a id="t-f39fbdbfca"></a>
## Thread: file size — 3 msgs

### file size

- **From:** theisb@21st.dk
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jgcvk$m2k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi There.

I've got a variable with a size of 2000 bytes I'd like to write to a
file, but when I write the file it's 2002 bytes long???

Where does the 2 bytes come from, and how do I get rid of them?

Regards Theis (Denmark)


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: file size

- **From:** Ed Stevens <Ed.Stevens@nmm.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jgfaf$mqc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7jgcvk$m2k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7jgcvk$m2k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  theisb@21st.dk wrote:
> Hi There.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What COBOL are you using?  If Micro Focus and your file is ORGANIZATION
LINE SEQUENTIAL, the extra two bytes are the CR-LF pair.  If it is
RECORD SEQUENTIAL, with a variable record length, it is the record
length descriptor.
```

#### ↳ Re: file size

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oj_63.1615$1Z6.240577@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7jgcvk$m2k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<theisb@21st.dk> wrote in message news:7jgcvk$m2k$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi There.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And if it's the FJ compiler, the compiler adds two bytes to the FRONT
of the record. The two bytes contain the record size.
```

<a id="t-31d8932b26"></a>
## Thread: Help on an API structure. — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help on an API structure.

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z5V63.20$sw6.477925@news1.i1.net>`

```
I'm trying to write a cobol program that uses tcp/ip.  I've finished the
server part of the program but am stuck on the client part.
can anyone explain to me how to set up this particular structure?

struct hostent FAR * gethostbyname

    const char FAR * name

The hostent structure is setup like:

struct hostent

    char FAR *       h_name;
    char FAR * FAR * h_aliases;
    short            h_addrtype;
    short            h_length;
    char FAR * FAR * h_addr_list;
};
I've converted that hostent structure like so:
01  hostent           is typedef.
 02 h-name            usage data-pointer.
 02 h-aliases         usage data-pointer.
 02 h-addrtype        usage short.
 02 h-length          usage short.
 02 h-addr-list       usage data-pointer.
but I don't know how to setup the api call.....


call winapi "gethostbyname" using
    by reference "server_name"
    returning hostent <----------------------------------------------I get
an error here.


I'm writing this in mfcobol workbench 4.0.32 on a winnt workstation.

Thanks for any help
Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-07T00:00:00

- **References:** `<Z5V63.20$sw6.477925@news1.i1.net>`

```
Tom,

Head on over to my webpage:

http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/download.htm

and scroll down to SOCKETS.ZIP and get that.  Inside is an example of 
client and server with a demonstration of how to get the address you 
need from this call.  It's not really straight forward.


On Mon, 7 Jun 1999 19:29:09, "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net> 
wrote:

> I'm trying to write a cobol program that uses tcp/ip.  I've finished the
> server part of the program but am stuck on the client part.
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

-------------------------

this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.
