Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> production unchecked on at least two occassions.

So, it goes into production.  When things are working correctly, they
never display.  When things are working incorrectly, then you get warned
quickly.

> If you want to put in these sort of messages make the message
> terminal. This applies even if you put off the inevitable for the
> end of program execution to maximise the warnings reported.
> 
> Ken

Why?  I want them to exist when someone does poor maintenance (even for
an emergency fix) somewhere down the line.   If the code is working
correctly, the displays don't happen.

Of course, I don't write THRU statements, and the only time I write
sections is where I have an IDMS COPY containing a SECTION, so this only
would occur in maintenance.

Hmmm.  In regular copies can we do a COPY REPLACING to eliminate SECTION
?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Style Recommendations

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770CEFA.1EBFCEF7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<375EA3B2.A714744@saif.com> <375ec149.26054954@usenet.acw.vcu.edu> <375EFAD9.745C@saif.com> <#uFRtzpu#GA.308@cpmsnbbsa03> <376E3BBB.A79BBCDE@NOSPAMhome.com> <376F7408.2889EE5F@zip.com.au> <376F8CB3.DCDF2824@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I will simplify. In MVS (where I am) displays do not appear in
your face, you must go looking for them in a job log.  This is
probably not the case in most other systems (syserr in unix and
the console (sorry window) on the PC).  The only thing that
mainframe programmers notice is a condition code (move X to
return-code) or an abend.

This may be a comment specific to MVS, however the C assert macro
does not display and quietly go on, it kills the program.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Style Recommendations

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jmsj1$8v8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<375EA3B2.A714744@saif.com>`

```
I am of the opinion that you should use neither GO TO's or PERFORM THRU's,
though I will grant that having a single exit and allowing goto's to that
exit is probably the most controversial issue in Cobol.

I tend to use pre-reads and more performs to get rid of the nested IF's.
However, with the new features, even those can be avoided.  The new scope
delimiters and the NOT AT END between them eliminate just about every reason
for the GO TO EXIT-PARAGRAPH conundrum.

Certainly, a GOTO a termination should NOT be allowed, even if you decide to
use the "every paragraph has an exit" method.  The reason is simple.  A
termination procedure is always error handling, and can be performed.  As
the languages and platforms evolve, error *correcting* procedures can be
developed.  For example, most of my code performs a print message and abort
procedure for Isam error status codes.  However, Fujitsu allows a dynamic
rebuild for scrambled Isam files.  Since the procedure is always performed
(but never returns because it has a GOBACK), it is going to be trivial to
impliment that new feature. If there were GO TO's, it could turn into a
nightmare.

Tina Hayworth wrote in message <375EA3B2.A714744@saif.com>...
>There has been much debate in our shop lately about the use of the GOTO
>and PERFORM THRU verbs.  At issues is weather they should be adopted as
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Style Recommendations

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375FBB3E.BF5E9B50@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<375EA3B2.A714744@saif.com> <7jmsj1$8v8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Certainly, a GOTO a termination should NOT be allowed, even if you decide to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> nightmare.

I agree with your other statements.  I prefer to design my code so that
there's no need for a GO TO EXIT.  But it seems a bit dishonest to
PERFORM ABORT-ROUTINE  or PERFORM GOBACK-ROUTINE.  While this is pretty
obvious, in general, dishonest code, or code which implies that it will
do one thing but which does another thing, should be avoided.

At any rate, I am very happy to approve any standard which proscribes GO
TO's, despite my misgiving here.  Then my goal is to teach people to
avoid replacing GO TO's with switches, but to structure their code
around the spirit of the standard.
```

---

<a id="t-c2948f1f8a"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com>`

```
!For COBOL FOR MVS/VM ONLY!

When I exit a paragraph pre-maturely, I usually do it like this:
     PROCESS1-PARAGRAPH.
         ...
         IF <condition1>
             SET ERROR-FLAG TO TRUE
             GO TO PROCESS1-EXIT
         END-IF
         ...         
     PROCESS1-EXIT.
         EXIT.

Does IBM COBOL FOR VM/MVS have something like this?
     PROCESS1-PARAGRAPH.
         ...
         IF <condition1>
             SET ERROR-FLAG TO TRUE
             **<exit this paragraph>**
         END-IF
         ...         

If such an instruction exists, it would eliminate the need for a "GO TO"
and would eliminate the need to code an EXIT paragraph.  Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jn3p1$l2p@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com>`

```
No, the current IBM mainframe COBOL products do NOT include support for what
you want.  This feature is defined in the draft of the next Standard (see
the "EXIT PARAGRAPH or EXIT SECTION feature).

There used to be a GUIDE requirement for IBM to add support for this
feature.  Since GUIDE as "basically died," I do not know how to find IBM's
current position on this feature (i.e. is there any chance of their
implementing it before the next Standard becomes official).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N8G73.13147$6y2.159747@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com>`

```
>Does IBM COBOL FOR VM/MVS have something like this?
>     PROCESS1-PARAGRAPH.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>         ...


What you are looking to do is use a GO TO statement without actually using
the command. The best way to do this is re-write your code to make proper
use of structured code to have it fall through your if statement to the end
of the paragraph. In some very, very, very rare occasions this cannot be
done. It takes a little time to code and you may have to break the code up
into multiple paragraphs.

Sorry, no mention of sections. Don't use them unless a gun is put to my
head.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com>`

```
The CONTINUE statement is close, it means CONTINUE EXECUTION at the
NEXT LOGICAL COBOL SOURCE STATEMENT.  (Not verb, but complete delineated
syntactically complete STATEMEMENT.)  i.e., it goes to the next outer level
of
a nested 'IF', perform, etc.

We'll always need the occaissional 'GO TO' statement for severe errors, and
the -EXIT
statement really should be used as a range checker.

Pete Wirfs wrote in message <375EDB36.63C@saif.com>...
>!For COBOL FOR MVS/VM ONLY!
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>and would eliminate the need to code an EXIT paragraph.  Thanks!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** John Piggott <John_Piggott@compuserve.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia>`

```
James King wrote:
 
> The CONTINUE statement is close, it means CONTINUE EXECUTION at the
> NEXT LOGICAL COBOL SOURCE STATEMENT.  (Not verb, but complete
> delineated syntactically complete STATEMEMENT.)  i.e., it goes to the
> next outer level of a nested 'IF', perform, etc. 
Oh no it doesn't!  CONTINUE is a no op.  It doesn't affect execution and 
certainly doesn't cause a change of level.

> We'll always need the occasional 'GO TO' statement for severe errors,
> and the EXIT statement really should be used as a range checker.

Only from the point of view of the person checking or reading the 
program.  EXIT is also a no op!

I wonder why Pete and so many others talk about "eliminating a GO TO". 
After all, the keyword is nothing; the structure is everything. If we 
have a new verb to exit the paragraph or section, what is it but a GO TO 
under another name?  

> 
> Pete Wirfs wrote in message <375EDB36.63C@saif.com>...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> >and would eliminate the need to code an EXIT paragraph.  Thanks!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c_u83.334$S2.23279@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com>`

```
In article <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com>,
John Piggott  <John_Piggott@compuserve.com> wrote:

[snippolinio]

>I wonder why Pete and so many others talk about "eliminating a GO TO". 
>After all, the keyword is nothing; the structure is everything. If we 
>have a new verb to exit the paragraph or section, what is it but a GO TO 
>under another name?  

Hee hee hee... that starts to get a bit deep and arcane; the closer one
gets to the 'bare metal' the more difficult it becomes to retain any sort
of 'purity' beyond:

IF PROGRAM-RUNS
    PERFORM NEXT-ASSIGNMENT
ELSE
    PERFORM CODE-LIKE-HELL UNTIL DAMNED-THING-WORKS.

After all, consider the 'structured' statement of:

IF SINGLE-CHAR-88-IS-SET
    PERFORM A-PARAGRAPH-OUTSIDE-OF-THIS-ONE

... versus ...

IF SINGLE-CHAR-88-IS-SET
    GO TO A-PARAGRAPH-OUTSIDE-OF-THIS-ONE.

On the Big Iron with which I am familiar the governing conditional in both
cases generates two Assembley statements:

CLI  (address),X'value'
BC   (address)

... and as anyone with even a lowly 'yellow card' can tell you a BC is a
Branch Condition... a conditionally predicated GO TO.  How dare anyone
write code that makes GO TOs?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com> <c_u83.334$S2.23279@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>
(snip)
> 
> Hee hee hee... that starts to get a bit deep and arcane; the closer one
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> write code that makes GO TOs?

This response to structured programming comes up from time to time, yet
it's completely specious. Structured programming relates to how one
codes the source for the program and has nothing to do with the binary
file developed from that source. I see no problem or conflict with
coding structured programs and have been since I started coding COBOL
for production programs in 1984. I code simple PERFORMs and no GOTOs. I
sometimes think of myself as the "Uncola" of COBOL coders, never had
GOTOs, never will:-)

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1TA83.388$S2.26256@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com> <c_u83.334$S2.23279@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net>`

```
In article <3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net>,
William Lynch  <WBLynch@att.spam.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>file developed from that source.

Quite rightly... but let's move along here.

>I see no problem or conflict with
>coding structured programs and have been since I started coding COBOL
>for production programs in 1984. I code simple PERFORMs and no GOTOs. I
>sometimes think of myself as the "Uncola" of COBOL coders, never had
>GOTOs, never will:-)

In terms of structure, then, consider the following:

    PERFORM 5000-MAIN-LINE THRU 5000-ML-EX
     UNTIL NO-MORE-INPUT.
    PERFORM 9000-EOJ THRU 9000-EOJ-EX.
...
5000-MAIN-LINE.
    READ INFILE
        AT END MOVE 'N' TO WS-MORE-INFILE-FL
               GO TO 5000-ML-EX.

    MOVE INFILE-REC-TYPE TO WS-REC-TYPE.
    IF NOT THIS-KIND-OF-REC-PROCESSED
        GO TO 5000-ML-EX.

... and ask yourself 'what is the structural purpose of the two GO TOs
shown?  The second one, it seems, is save CPU cycles and perform another
iteration of the loop while the first one is to transfer execution to the
statement following the invoking statement.

Now, replace both GO TOs with EXIT PARAGRAPHs... how is there a change in
structure?  I can see none, obviously, but would be interested in learning
how it might be seen as such.

Of course, there's no need to respond to this at all... *everyone* knows
that since I am a 'complete wanker' that I never post anything worthy of
consideration or reply...

... just as *everyone* knows that I am the King of England, I guess.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37633DA2.C69C495F@att.net>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com> <c_u83.334$S2.23279@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net> <1TA83.388$S2.26256@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> In terms of structure, then, consider the following:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>         GO TO 5000-ML-EX.

How about:

5000-MAIN-LINE.
	READ INFILE
		AT END MOVE 'N' TO WS-MORE-INFILE-FL
		NOT AT END
			deal with the record we just read
	END-READ

If you want the record in WS-REC-TYPE, why not just read into it?

> Now, replace both GO TOs with EXIT PARAGRAPHs... how is there a change in
> structure?  I can see none, obviously, but would be interested in learning
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> consideration or reply...

Never bothered me before.

> ... just as *everyone* knows that I am the King of England, I guess.

I thought it was the Queen, but I stand corrected:-)

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2gJ83.445$S2.30465@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net> <1TA83.388$S2.26256@iad-read.news.verio.net> <37633DA2.C69C495F@att.net>`

```
In article <37633DA2.C69C495F@att.net>,
William Lynch  <WBLynch@att.spam.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
>> 
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>	END-READ

How about it, indeed?  I fail to see how this code provides any insight
into the structural differences between GO TO and EXIT PARAGRAPH... am I
missing something?  It strikes me as:

'Well, you say that a yellow rose is impermissible... why, then, are you
suggesting the use of a red one?'

'How about a tulip?'

>
>If you want the record in WS-REC-TYPE, why not just read into it?

Now *that's* easy... because site standards dictate that all READ INTOs
must have as their targets copybooks which are standard and brought into
WORKING STORAGE with COPY REPLACING while a field used for range-checking
is left to the programmer's discretion.

>
>> Now, replace both GO TOs with EXIT PARAGRAPHs... how is there a change in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Never bothered me before.

Just as long as I keep my hands to myself?

>
>> ... just as *everyone* knows that I am the King of England, I guess.
>
>I thought it was the Queen, but I stand corrected:-)

What one 'thinks' and what one 'knows' might well be two different things,
also.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k0vi9$54j@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <3762C9EC.B153269D@att.net> <1TA83.388$S2.26256@iad-read.news.verio.net> <37633E20.2776D958@att.net> <GhJ83.446$S2.30319@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

<docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:GhJ83.446$S2.30319@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <37633E20.2776D958@att.net>,
> William Lynch  <WBLynch@att.spam.net> wrote:
> >docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> >>
> >(snip)
    <more snip>
>
> Just this past Friday, for debugging purposes, I coded an EXHIBIT NAMED...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For the "amusement" of those who are unaware of this fact, Micro Focus COBOL
has (and still does - and probably always will) support the intermixing of
"old" and "new" language elements in the same program.  So you can use an
EXHIBIT NAMED in a nested program or (God forbid) a TRANSFORM in the middle
of an OO program.  All you need to do is to set the directives "correctly".

Personally, I *hated* this feature when I worked at MF, but I have seen a
number of production PC, UNIX, and client/server applications that take
advantage of this "feature".

P.S.  I think that some of the other non-mainframe compilers may work this
way too - but I know it is true for MF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ju8hu$jq7@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com>`

```
The difference between EXIT PARAGRAPH/SECTION and GO TO paragraph-name-exit
is one of avoiding "accidental" unstructured errors.   If a NEW
paragraph-name is added between the verb and the original
exit-paragraph-name, then the two will perform very differently.  Now, there
may be some question about WHICH of these is what programmers might want -
on the other hand, what the EXIT enhancements mean is that you can now
TOTALLY get rid of the PERFORM THRU structure and still get the "get out of
this paragraph/section" semantics that are often desired.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL FOR MVS: "EXIT" Question

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762d75f.12699758@netnews>`
- **References:** `<375EDB36.63C@saif.com> <Haj83.7770$Ej6.1587@news3.mia> <3762E352.5B07@compuserve.com>`

```
'Twas Sat, 12 Jun 1999 15:46:42 -0700, when John Piggott
<John_Piggott@compuserve.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> I wonder why Pete and so many others talk about "eliminating a GO TO". 
> After all, the keyword is nothing; the structure is everything. If we 
> have a new verb to exit the paragraph or section, what is it but a GO TO 
> under another name?  

It's a more structured GO TO, somewhat less likely to point to the wrong
place or to be broken by shoddy maintenance.
```

I was the guy asking if cobol is gonna stick around.  Got another question
for you.  What if I did decide to learn cobol, but had no programming skills
whatsoever, in any language, where would be the good place to start.  I
tried to search the web for some free on-line tutorials on cobol, but came
up with almost nothing (for a newbie that is).  Is there something like
"Cobol for dummies" out there on the web?  I wouldn't want to spend money
before I actually find out if this is what I want to do.  If I sound silly,
I appologize. Misha
```

#### ↳ Re: Attention Cheesle

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jr8ma$e85$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7jmu9j$r77$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Misha wrote in message <7jmu9j$r77$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>...
>I was the guy asking if cobol is gonna stick around.  Got another question
>for you.  What if I did decide to learn cobol, but had no programming
skills
>whatsoever, in any language, where would be the good place to start.  I
>tried to search the web for some free on-line tutorials on cobol, but came
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I appologize. Misha

Sorry for being so late with my response, been busy these days. However, to
start with your ending, please do not be afraid of sounding silly. An honest
question never sounds silly. We all have to start one place, we all have
been at the same level once.

So, Interesting question. What should you do... There is a couple of things
that are important to remember in such a consideration. At least of my
humble opinion;

1. There is nothing that is free. By saying so, I mean you will not
necessarily have to spend a lot of money, actually, most could be free,
except for the computer. Well, you even get a computer for free these days.
What you will have to pay, is your attention,  dedication and frustrations
for a large number of hours ahead. When I sit here now, it feels like I have
been doing software programming for all of my life. But, you and I, we know
that this isn't true. I, as for others out there, has spent thousands of
hours behind a keyboard, to get where we are today. I am not saying that it
is demanded several years of studying and practise to become a software
developer, but you will no doubt have to spend a lot of time with it, and,
unfortunately, there will be times and tasks that are tedious and dull once
in a while. These moments are your crossroads, if you turn there you're
gone. If you keep moving, another barrier is broken. This of course applies
to any occupation one chooses.

2. The study, this may be the point where you would appreciate input from as
many as possible. I have one idea, my personal idea about this. Others
believe they have a better idea and that I am wrong, I leave to you to
choose. I started my career as a software developer learning myself basic on
a Dragon32, many, many years ago. There was PC's those days, but they were
insane expensive. Back then I was not really sure what I wanted to do. Later
on, I had some more basic in school, still without knowing that this was the
real thing. Finally, when I entered university I realized that software
development was my choice of occupation. The study I took was covering a lot
of topics, and a lot of languages. In the sense of being any expert back
then, I would say pascal was it. I had hardly seen a Cobol compiler then.
However, my first job after school was developing in Cobol, and I had no big
thoughts about that. Because, it is just another language. In my opinion,
learning a new language for software development is just as a new language
to speak. You learn the words, the grammar rules and there you go.

In my first email to you I said that a good programmer wouldn't care whether
he/she was using Ada, Cobol, C, Pascal, Basic, Fortran what ever. From my
point of view, the essential is to know the methods, methods, which should
be language insensitive.

I use to say that there are two kinds of programmers, those who just write;
They are rarely innovative, they do what the book says, if the book doesn't
cover the problem they have two options, either get another book or they
don't do it. I use to compare these kinds with myself needing a table. If I
need a table I would go to a warehouse selling such construction kits with
precut and all that stuff. If I cannot find such a shop, I would improvise,
but I would never start over like a carpenter and create a table from
scratch. The carpenter here would be the variant 2 of the developers; These
guys uses books as well, but for them methodology are superiour to the
language. They know what to do to calculate interests of a loan, they know
how to reuse code, they know how to improve performance and reliability.
They know to pay attention to the goal of their task in a wide perspective,
not isolated in the single problem ahead of them.

So, you have already had many suggestions about books that teach you Cobol
in 21 days. First, remember that the book is not going to teach you
anything, it is you that has to use dedication, attention and frustration
*with* the book. Second, you do not learn Cobol or any other language in 21
days, that's a commercial, it sounds better than "Teach yourself Cobol in
365 days". What you would do in 21 days is to get a more or less
understanding of the basics. From which you may extend if you like. I
haven't seen these books, but I believe in those people that has recommended
them for you.

It seems to me, that you are not 100% sure of what you want to do. I don't
know how old you are, if you are at school, working or what ever. As a
starter I think the approach of a book would be good. If you are a family
man, going back to school could be hard, but I think it would be a good
choice. It is hard to capture new stuff without a stimulating crowd of
people to do it together with. You do need to learn methods and techniques,
and have them explained.

If you go to school. Wants to become a developer. Choose generic courses.
Learn how computers are built. Study operating systems basics, learn your
language insensitive methods and you are ready for any language, be it Cobol
or assembly.

I am sort of afraid though, that you may believe that you could do a course
of 6 months or so and get a job with a salary of $50' - $100', I hate to
disappoint you, but those guys are very, very experienced.

Well, this turned out as something else than I thought of when I started the
writing. I hope you find anything useful in my answer.

Cheesle
```

> a Dragon32, many, many years ago. There was PC's those days, but they

Ken

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Attention Cheesle

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

<a id="t-a0c50fb28f"></a>
## Thread: Variable length records in Cobol MVS — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Variable length records in Cobol MVS

- **From:** "gee" <grant_englebrecht@nospam.compuware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375db00a@199.186.16.51>`

```
I have a variable length dataset that I am trying to read from and output
some records from.
If I don't have the right copybook how can I determine the length to output
I know I can define it as a 1 to n table of 1 byte fields and the set the
length as the index of this table but this seems a lengthy process.
the FD says
record contains 1 to n chars
block contains 0 records.

when run it takes the maximum lenght of the record layout as the record
length to write out
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records in Cobol MVS

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ggj73.28$_F2.3493@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<375db00a@199.186.16.51>`

```
If you don't know the length of the records, you could try something like:

FD THE-MYSTERY-FILE
     RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO 1000 CHARACTERS
     DEPENDING ON MYSTERY-FILE-REC-LENGTH.
01  MR-RECORD   PIC X(1000)


WORKING STORAGE...
77  MYSTERY-FILE-REC-LENGTH   PICTURE S9(04) USAGE BINARY.


.. and something similar on the "write" side.

If 1000 characaters isn't enough, make it bigger and try again.
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records in Cobol MVS

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990609094945.07916.00002814@ng-fs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<375db00a@199.186.16.51>`

```
"gee" <grant_englebrecht> writes ...

 >I have a variable length dataset that I am trying to read from and output
>some records from.
>If I don't have the right copybook how can I determine the length to output

What are you saying here? Why do you need a copybook? You need the record
layout? Is it not documented anywhere? Is there no copybook? How can you do any
work with no record layout? At the least, use ISPF 3.4 or 3.2 and find out the
record length and blocksize from the label.


>I know I can define it as a 1 to n table of 1 byte fields and the set the
>length as the index of this table but this seems a lengthy process.

Actually, I don't see how you can "set the length as the index of this table";
where would you get this index value?

>the FD says
>record contains 1 to n chars
>block contains 0 records.
>
So you have a program that tells you the [maximum] record size? Or is this what
you made up in your program? What's "n"?


>when run it takes the maximum lenght of the record layout as the record
>length to write out

Well, you need an FD for the output [hmm, was your earlier FD for the input or
the output?] and the record description has to give COBOL some clue how to
determine what record length to use. So, the 01 after your FD needs to include
an "occurs depending on" clause. Usually, but not always, the object of the
depending on clause is a field in the record. So, something like:

FD  outfile
       block contains 0 records.
01   out-rec.
      02   fixed-part     pic x(200).
      02   no-var-parts  pic s9(4) binary.
      02  var-part occurs 1 to 200 times
                     depending on no-var-parts.
             03  field-a   pic...
             03   field-b  pic....
             .
             .
             .

Then, you're responsible for building the output record, including putting the
right value in no-var-parts.

There is no magic in this business. You need information. If it is not given to
you, it is your responsibility to get it.

Regards,

       

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Variable length records in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jokop$kck$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<375db00a@199.186.16.51>`

```
In article <375db00a@199.186.16.51>,
  "gee" <grant_englebrecht@nospam.compuware.com> wrote:
> I have a variable length dataset that I am trying to read from and
>output some records from.

<snip>

> when run it takes the maximum lenght of the record layout as the
> record length to write out
>

You simply have to set the record-length of your output record equal to
the length of the input record:

 FD  FI-INFILE
     RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO nnn CHARACTERS
     DEPENDING ON LENGTH-INFILE
     RECORDING MODE IS V.
 01 .......
 FD  FI-OUTFILE
     RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO nnn CHARACTERS
     DEPENDING ON LENGTH-OUTFILE
     RECORDING MODE IS V.
 01 .......

 01 LENGTH-INFILE  PIC 9(4) COMP.
 01 LENGTH-OUTFILE PIC 9(4) COMP.

      READ FI-INFILE
*------ that's the point!
      MOVE LENGTH-INFILE TO LENGTH-OUTFILE
      WRITE ...... FROM ....
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Variable length records in Cobol MVS

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761B10B.763D9908@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<375db00a@199.186.16.51> <7jokop$kck$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Daniel Jacot wrote:
>  FD  FI-INFILE
>      RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO nnn CHARACTERS
       DEPENDING ON WS-REC-LENGTH    ***
>      RECORDING MODE IS V.

>  FD  FI-OUTFILE
>      RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO nnn CHARACTERS
       DEPENDING ON WS-REC-LENGTH    ****
>      RECORDING MODE IS V.

   01 WS-REC-LENGTH  PIC 9(4) COMP.


>  ***     MOVE LENGTH-INFILE TO LENGTH-OUTFILE

Move no longer required, very small optimisation.
Ken
```

## Thread: Mantis COBOL + SUPRA — 4 msgs

### Mantis COBOL + SUPRA

- **From:** "Larkin Cunningham" <larkin@indigo.ie>
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7RB73.5853$yr2.2501@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Can anybody tell me where to find information on Mantis COBOL and/or SUPRA
database by CINCOM.

I may very well be working on a project using these products in a couple of
weeks time, but my experience is limited to MVS COBOL, ADABAS, DB2, CICS,
etc.  Is there much of a learning curve involved?  I know that Mantis is a
4GL, so that should make it easy to pick-up.  Am I right?  And Supra is
supposedly a relational database which accesses VSAM files using ODBC (and
therefore SQL).  Is this near the mark?

Any references to web-sites (my searches on the web have been unsuccessful
apart from the official Cincom site), books or other material would be very
welcome.

Larkin.
```

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-vbRHhLoHVfNB@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7RB73.5853$yr2.2501@news.indigo.ie>`

```
On Wed, 9 Jun 1999 22:30:18, "Larkin Cunningham" <larkin@indigo.ie> 
wrote:
> 
> Any references to web-sites (my searches on the web have been unsuccessful
> apart from the official Cincom site), books or other material would be very
> welcome.

I went to http://www.google.com and typed in Mantis COBOL and found

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Mantis COBOL + SUPRA

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375FBD1A.13425BA9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7RB73.5853$yr2.2501@news.indigo.ie>`

```
Larkin Cunningham wrote:
> 
> Can anybody tell me where to find information on Mantis COBOL and/or
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Larkin.

I use Mantis on MVS about 13 years ago so my knowledge is more
than a little dated.

Supra is not relational it is a network database, for example:

customer has transaction list:
customer contains pointer to first transaction
each transaction contains pointer to next transaction.

In relational the transactions would contain a customer number.

The access is very fast but if it is corrupted a restore is the
only choice. (It only happened once in five years though).

When I left they were just starting to support SQL which makes the
database pretend to be relational.  If you are using this it
really is no great worry so long as you know SQL.

MANTIS:  This is a pascal like language that works very easily on
sets of data (for example 20 rows of transactions to present on
the screen). It really requires you to use a lot of subroutines
(not paragraphs more of a call with parameters).  If you have had
some non cobol experience then it should be a breeze.

ODBC was not invented when I was playing with Mantis but it did
have DB2 interfaces which (assuming it worked) would be really
easy to work with using Mantis.

Good luck
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Mantis COBOL + SUPRA

- **From:** rdong@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jsl3c$1a1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7RB73.5853$yr2.2501@news.indigo.ie>`

```
In article <7RB73.5853$yr2.2501@news.indigo.ie>,
  "Larkin Cunningham" <larkin@indigo.ie> wrote:
> Can anybody tell me where to find information on Mantis COBOL and/or
SUPRA
> database by CINCOM.
>

I last used Supra and Mantis about 4 years ago.  There are 2 versions
of Supra.  The 1.x version is a network database with a relational
layer bolted on top but the database itself does not use SQL.  The 2.x
version is a completely different engine that is relational and
supports SQL.  Both databases are somewhat dated and lack all of the
features and functionality one would find in a RDBMS like Oracle.

MANTIS is a character based (non GUI unless you buy some add on
products) 4GL.  I enjoyed using it especially against DB2 on MVS; it
allowed the use of embedded SQL from which you could generate DB2 plans.

I doubt whether if you will find many online references to either
product due to their waning popularity.
```

files - preferably available freeware/shareware/demo (Windows platform).

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Datafile Editor?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

Paul Shanley wrote in message <7jmm4v$a73$1@lure.pipex.net>...
>Hi all
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>workbench. Any ideas?

I have been using the Semware editor for years, it is independent of
language but has a wide configurability and an extensive macro language. You
may for instance make it syntax sensitive if you like. A couple of
predefined attributes are delivered with it, like for example C. It is
incredibly fast and handles as large files as you may have on your host.
Available for dos, os/2, win32 and supports the clipboard in windows.

It is cheap and delivered with a very good printed manual and a library of
macros to use.

I encourage you to give it a trym take a look at: www.semware.com

Cheesle
```

## Thread: Need help with GetOpenFileName API Call — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Need help with GetOpenFileName API Call

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com (Doug Blaze)
- **Date:** 1999-06-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375e8003.4056112@news.icix.net>`

```
Hello all,

I'm at it again - trying to use the API!  You think I'd learn by now!

However, I need to allow the user to select a program (*.exe) to use
as a text viewer (ie: notepad or wordpad).  I have a button on my
screen called 'BROWSE'.  When they click it, I invoke the
GetOpenFileName API call to allow them to select a program.  I can get
the actual file name they selected in OFN-Title (ie:  notepad.exe) but
I need the complete path including drive letter.  This is supposed to
be returned in a variable pointed to by 'lpstrFile'.  However, this
field is blank after the call.  What's interesting is 'hFileOffset'
contains a value that looks correct (this represents the number of
characters from the beginning of the path to the start of the filename
pointed to by lpstrFile).  Below is the WORKING-STORAGE define of the
structure and the actual call.  I have to think I'm missing something
very obvious but after two days of trying, I can't figure it out.

I'm running NT4.0 with MF 4.0.32.  The dialog is presented fine and
the call returns TRUE.  In the displays after the call, the following
values are returned if I select the following file:  

d:\winnt\notepad.exe

          Display "Call GetOpenFileNameA Successful"
          Display "File Title Selected = " OFN-Title    (NOTEPAD.EXE)
          display "complete file name  = " OFN-FileName (spaces)
          DISPLAY "nMaxfile            = " nMaxFile     (+000000000)
          display "nFileOffset         = " nFileOffset  (+00009)
          DISPLAY "lpstrFile           = " lpstrfile    (0005275936)


Any help would be appreciated!

Doug

 01  OFN-Struct.
     05  lStructSize                 pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  hwndOwner                   pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  hInstance                   pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lpstrFilter                 POINTER.
     05  lpstrCustomFilter           pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  nMaxCustFilter              pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  nFilterIndex                pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 1.
     05  lpstrFile                   POINTER.
     05  nMaxFile                    pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lpstrFileTitle              POINTER.
     05  nMaxFileTitle               pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 260.
     05  lpstrInitialDir             POINTER.
     05  lpstrTitle                  pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  OFNFlags                    pic s9(9)  comp-5 value
                                     h"00081800".
     05  nFileOffset                 pic s9(4)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  nFileExtension              pic s9(4)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lpstrDefExt                 pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lCustData                   pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lpfnHook                    pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.
     05  lpTemplateName              pic s9(9)  comp-5 value 0.

 01  OFN-Filters.
     05  Filler                      pic x(8)   value "Programs".
     05  Filler                      pic x      value x"00".
     05  Filler                      pic x(5)   value "*.exe".
     05  Filler                      pic x      value x"00".
 01  OFN-Title                       pic x(260) value SPACES.
 01  OFN-Init-Dir                    pic x(9)  value spaces.
*                                     "D:\WINNT".
 01  OFN-FileName                    pic x(260) value SPACES.

 Browse-For-File.
      move length of OFN-Struct    to lStructSize
      SET lpstrFilter              to address of OFN-Filters
      SET lpstrFileTitle           to address of OFN-Title
      SET lpstrInitialDir          to address of OFN-Init-Dir
      move low-values              to OFN-FileName.
      move length of OFN-Struct    to lStructSize
      CALL WinApi32 "GetOpenFileNameA" using
            BY REFERENCE OFN-Struct
            RETURNING    WReturnCode
      end-call
      SET lpstrFile to address of OFN-FileName.
      If WReturnCode not = 1
          Perform CommDlgExtendedError-Dialog
      else
          Display "Call GetOpenFileNameA Successful"
          Display "File Title Selected = " OFN-Title
          display "complete file name  = " OFN-FileName
          DISPLAY "nMaxfile            = " nMaxFile
          display "nFileOffset         = " nFileOffset
          DISPLAY "lpstrFile           = " lpstrfile
       end-if.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with GetOpenFileName API Call

- **From:** Steve Biggs <steve_biggs@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jo7cd$fd5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<375e8003.4056112@news.icix.net>`

```
dblaze@merchants-fla.com wrote:
>  Browse-For-File.
>       move length of OFN-Struct    to lStructSize
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>       SET lpstrFile to address of OFN-FileName.

Doug,
I think the last line should come before the API call (ie set up
lpstrFile before making the call). Also, you should set nMaxFile to the
size of the buffer (ie 260).

Steve.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with GetOpenFileName API Call

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com (Doug Blaze)
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375fbae8.84669368@news.icix.net>`
- **References:** `<375e8003.4056112@news.icix.net> <7jo7cd$fd5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

Hi Steve,

You are the man!  Upon reading the nMaxFile verbage again, you are
absolutely right - and it works too!  I found that if I did the 'SET
lpstrFile to address of OFN-FileName' BEFORE the call I got a
CDERR_INITIALIZATION error.  If I set it AFTER the call, I got the
dialog box but not the correct results.  nMaxFile does say it returns
FALSE if the size of OFN-Filename is too small to contain the name -
it would be since it's size was zero!  The error indicated not enough
memory but it didn't make much sense since I have 64K!  Now I know
it's referring to the size of the receiving item for the name of the
item!  Duh!

Thanks again.
Doug





>Doug,
>I think the last line should come before the API call (ie set up
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

<a id="t-0215a4fb5b"></a>
## Thread: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard) — 33 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
This is another topic where I keep "hitting my head against a wall" when I
try to get J4 (or WG4) to fix a problem in the draft Standard.  (In fact,
this may even be a problem in the '85 Standard.)  J4 (fairly) consistently
tells me there is no problem, but I just can't find what they do in the
actual words in the Standard.  If you are interested in this topic and want
to express an opinion that there *IS* a problem, please send your comments
to the J4 chair at:
    doncobol <at> mediaone.net

If you read the draft Standard and agree with J4 that I am "crazy," I don't
think you need to do anything. (You may or may want to send me public or
private mail telling me how stupid I am and why.)

The bottom-line problem (IMHO) is that the draft Standard does not allow
there to be any "data" (explicitly called "characters" in the draft
Standard) between the R-margin (after column 72 in IBM mainframe compilers -
and many other compilers) and the physical end-of-line (however, the
specific implementation defines this) - when you use "fixed format reference
format" (which is what we are now calling the "old-style" source lines).

The following text (from CD 1.5) is what makes me think this:

"Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character position of
a line. The rightmost character position of a line is a fixed number defined
by the implementor."

The following is the latest reply that was drafted by a J4 member to my
concerns. (This has *not* yet been processed by all of J4 - but I do think
reflects a common if not universal opinion at J4.)

"3. Item 14 � what to do to allow (and then logically convert) characters
between the R-margin and the end of the physical line in fixed form
reference format.

Analysis:   No action is needed.  The standard does not define whether there
are or are not characters between the R-margin and the end of the line in
fixed-form.  J4 has rejected the notion of adding anything about this.
Logical conversion looks only at indicators and the program-text-area of
fixed-form.  It does not look at sequence numbers and it does not look
beyond margin R."

***

FYI - if you want to see either of the FULL documents that I am quoting
from, the draft Standard can be viewed at:
  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0273.doc

The draft reply to my concern can be viewed at:
  http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/99-0273.doc

***

P.S.  I don't know how common it is in IBM mainframes to STILL include data
(often program-name or revision information) in columns 73-80 - but I know
that it sure used to be.  I also don't know if this is allowed with other
compilers, so this may or may not be an issue for other programmers too.
```

#### ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yD_73.13646$6y2.166308@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
The idea that useful information is contained in either the left or right
margins is pointless. No one has or will ever care what information is
there. It is forced upon programmers to slow down the development process.
Pencil pushers never even look at it. It has been used to track changes in
programs from one generation to the next. That information then promptly
falls off into the bit bucket never to be seen again. Arguing about it is as
pointless as asking Clinton to be a patriot.
```

```

I find this feature extremely useful.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

```
I have now read lots of "we do NOT use 73-80" and "we do use 73-80"
comments.

Have those who *do* use columns 73-80 read my original post?  If so, do you
agree with Rick that the current words (about implementors defining "lines"
and "characters") make you feel safe about the next Standard?  If not,
please, PLEASE, send a comment to J4 (via doncobol <at> mediaone.net)
stating that you are concerned that the rules about the R-margin being to
the right of the *LAST CHARACTER POSITION" in a fixed-format source line.

Unless, J4 hears from some people who are concerned about this, then I think
that Rick's view that this is a non-issue will prevail - and I am really
concerned about what may happen with future implementations.

Obviously, for those who do NOT use columns 73-80, this is a non-issue and I
can't imagine that anything that J4 does or does not do will impact them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ujd83.1719$LI5.166364@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <yD_73.13646$6y2.166308@storm.twcol.com> <376077ab@news3.us.ibm.net> <929086527.6439.0.nnrp-08.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk> <7jrl5e$da1@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7jrl5e$da1@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>...
>I have now read lots of "we do NOT use 73-80" and "we do use 73-80"
>comments.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>the right of the *LAST CHARACTER POSITION" in a fixed-format source line.


In using AccuCobol (a RM variety), we can specify that a line be Included or
eXcluded depending on command line arguments when the compiler is invoked.
If the condition is met, commented code can become live.  This done to keep
a special "flavor" of a program for a specific client or other command line
options a certain client may want.
For example (deleting a number of chars to prevent wraparound):
    DISPLAY MY-FIELD LINE 5 POSITION 25 ERASE EOL
!FLAG
*  DISPLAY MY-FIELD LINE 5 POSITION 25 ERASE TO END OF LINE   FLAG

When we specify (-Si FLAG -Sx !FLAG) on the command line only the 2nd line
is seen by the compiler.  The absence of those arguments or a different
value for them would cause the first line to be read as expected.  Not
exactly my favorite feature, but this company was using it before I got
here.

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
Rainy days OR Mondays always get me down.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01beb470$eec9ec20$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <yD_73.13646$6y2.166308@storm.twcol.com>`

```
>The idea that useful information is contained in either the left or right
>margins is pointless. No one has or will ever care what information is
>there.
Obviously you've NEVER dropped a deck of punched cards on the floor,
on you'd D*** well know who cares if (for example) sequence numbers
are put in cols 73-80.

Cards (or unit records, if you'd rather) may be long gone, but don't
try to tell me that "no one has or will ever care" 'cause I know from
personal experience that that is just plain untrue.  We used to care a lot.
Not from dictates on high, but from the difference in dropping a realigned 
deck in a sorter and getting back a useful deck or spending hours to days 
reordering it by hand.

Side note, it is a little arrogant (quite common and human, but still
arrogant)
to act as if history began shortly after one's birth.  You just made that
mistake,
in case you hadn't noticed.  It is quite clear you've some axe to grind,
probably
because some pointy-haired-boss clone is imposing make-work on you, but
your irritation at it should not spill over into Usenet postings as
flamebait.

Once upon a time there was a real use for info in cols. 73-80.  Whether 
there is now is another matter.  I expect anyone who is maintaining old
code
would appreciate some authorship indication in cols73-80: who mucked with
this line or when it was last mucked with.  But if all you do is write
throw-away 
code, then filling in those cols. with anything is a waste of time.  Except
that 
throw-away code rarely is thrown away . . .
```

#### ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Maybe I'm missing something (I doubt it), but if what you want to do is:
put some kind of comment/note in the 73-80 column to track Revision numbers,
Change Requests, etc, then all you need to do is tell the Compiler that
there
are NO Sequence Numbers in the Source.  It will then ignore (as comments)
cols 73-80, and print them on each line.  IBM,Honeywell, WANG all support
this.

The COBOL SEQUENCE NUMBER option tells the compiler to look in 73-80 for
COBOL sequence numbers, otherwise it looks in 1-6 for relative sequence
numbers.

No change to the standards is needed.


William M. Klein wrote in message <7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
>This is another topic where I keep "hitting my head against a wall" when I
>try to get J4 (or WG4) to fix a problem in the draft Standard.  (In fact,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Standard) between the R-margin (after column 72 in IBM mainframe
compilers -
>and many other compilers) and the physical end-of-line (however, the
>specific implementation defines this) - when you use "fixed format
reference
>format" (which is what we are now calling the "old-style" source lines).
>
>The following text (from CD 1.5) is what makes me think this:
>
>"Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character position
of
>a line. The rightmost character position of a line is a fixed number
defined
>by the implementor."
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Analysis:   No action is needed.  The standard does not define whether
there
>are or are not characters between the R-margin and the end of the line in
>fixed-form.  J4 has rejected the notion of adding anything about this.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

See Randall's reply to another part of this thread about what concerns me.
If the Standard *did* allow there to be "stuff" after the R-margin which the
vendor could do with however they wanted to, then I would have no problem
with this.  My problem is that the current draft says that the R-margin is
to the right of the LAST CHARACTER POSITION in the source line.  (not to the
right of the last "meaningful" character)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3763ea65.13887676@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <6151E67657E66667.7E636F2CFE7EAEE9.8909DE2DB8A8615A@lp.airnews.net>`

```
'Twas Sun, 13 Jun 1999 16:31:01 GMT, when swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> Would this imply that you could write Cobol statements that, e.g.,
> ended in column 75 and they would be compiled and assembled correctly?

Cobol-74 said that the program text was in columns 8 thru 72.  Positions
73 thru whatever were comments.

Cobol-85 replaced column 72 with margin R.  The implementor was free to
set margin R between 72 and 73 and have it function just as in Cobol-74,
but the implementor could set margin R elsewhere.  This made any program
non portable if it contained continued literals, or if it made use of
positions which were within margin R on one compiler, but past it on
another compiler.  There were objections for exactly that reason, but the
committee let it through.  I thoguht there was a rule stating that margin
R couldn't be set to the left of column 72, but there's no such rule in
the draft.

As I understand the intent of the current draft is to handle fixed format
source exactly like Cobol-85.  This is the wording at p 36, 6.1.1 that has
Bill worried:

"Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character position
of a line. The rightmost character position of
a line is a fixed number defined by the implementor."

The second sentence makes it clear that margin R is fixed by the
implementor.  Bill is concerned that the first sentence prohibits comments
in character positions past margin R.  After due consideration, I'm not
worried.  I don't think any implementor who allowed commnets past margin R
will now ban them.  This is a general rule, and according to the decoder
ring an implementor may relax a general rule without flagging as an error.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <karl.kiesel@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37663961.C7F7412B@mch6.siemens.de>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <6151E67657E66667.7E636F2CFE7EAEE9.8909DE2DB8A8615A@lp.airnews.net> <3763ea65.13887676@netnews> <7k1249$6vo@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein schrieb:

> Is there anyone (reading this NG) who has a real copy of the '74 Standard
> that can confirm this?  It wasn't my memory of the '74 Standard - but I
> don't have an official copy, so I could be wrong.

Looking up our copies of varoius Standards, i found the following:
COBOL 1961 
  'L an R are the left and right-hand margins, respectively of the
  physical input-output medium (page III-2) 
COBOL 1968
  'Margin R designates the right-most character position of a line'
  (8.1 page 1-94) 
COBOL 1974
  'Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character 
  position of a line' (5.8.1, page I-105; column 72 is not mentioned!)
COBOL 1985
  'Margin R ias immediately to the right of the rightmost character 
  posiotion of a line' (7.2 page IV-41)
CD1.5 (1999)
  'Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character
position of a line. The rightmost character poistion of a line is a
fixed numer defined by the implementor' (6.1 page 36)

the correspondig figure is almost the same for 1968 - 1999, except that
1968 the margins were not marked between the columns, but above the
columns, ie margin A marked col 8.

hope that helps to clarify things
K. Kiesel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766c9b4.982773@netnews>`

```
'Twas Tue, 15 Jun 1999 13:30:41 +0200, when Karl Kiesel
<karl.kiesel@mch6.siemens.de> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> William M. Klein schrieb:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>   physical input-output medium (page III-2) 

Margin R dates back to 1961?  I was far more wrong on this than I would
have thought possible.  Your saying that column 72 is not mentioned,
right?

And it specifically refers to the physical medium.  So if you used 80
column cards (and lots of people did) COBOL-61 required that margin R be
column 80.  Did anyone ever implement this the way the CODASYL standard
said?

I got an email which says COBOL-74 says margin R terminates area B.  This
is quite different from right most character position on the line.  There
could be comments to the right of area B but not to the right of the
rightmost character.

Historically it has been accepted as legal to have comment characters to
the right of the rightmost, so it's not as though the current standard is
breaking anything in this case.

My previous comments were based on having read the 1981 draft durring the
public review period.  It came with a few hundred pages of comments which
X3J4 members had submitted with the vote to send the draft out for public
review.  Some of them were griping about margin R.  What _could_ they have
been griping about?

The more I learn about the standard, the less sense it makes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k1214$6tn@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <6151E67657E66667.7E636F2CFE7EAEE9.8909DE2DB8A8615A@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com> wrote in message
news:6151E67657E66667.7E636F2CFE7EAEE9.8909DE2DB8A8615A@lp.airnews.net...
> On Sat, 12 Jun 1999 00:36:23 -0500, "William M. Klein"
> <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:
>
   <snip>
>
> Bill,
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Steve

With IBM mainframe compilers (and I believe some others), you can put
"useful" information columns 73-80.  This information is *NOT* compiled nor
does it impact the compilation process.  (Usually, this includes such things
as versioning, update, or even program name information. I have never seen
it include sequence numbers, but I suppose it could.)

It is my contention that the current draft (and probably the '85 Standard as
well) prohibits a conforming implementation (at least when running in
"Standard-mode") from allowing such "character positions" after the
R-margin.  As I indicated, I think this may have been a problem with the '85
Standard as well as the draft - and I believe that J4 (under its previous
name of X3J4) *DID* receive comments about this "problem" in the '85
Standard.  What I would like the draft Standard to do is to FIX this problem
(or ambiguity) so that an implementation explicitly *MAY* allow such data
(as "commentary" only) between the R-margin and the physical end of the
source line (however, the implementor or file system indicates that).

Does that make both my position and the problem any clearer?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>...
>[...]  My problem is that the current draft says that the R-margin is
>to the right of the LAST CHARACTER POSITION in the source line.  (not to
the
>right of the last "meaningful" character)

Except that, in the draft, the word "source" is not used to
describe this "line."

The first paragraph of 6.1, page 35, uses the term "fixed-form
reference format line." Later in 6.1, on page 36, is the sentence,
"Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character
position of a line."

This implies that Margin R is immediately to the right of the
rightmost character position of the fixed-form reference format
line.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k3qj6$kqs@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote in message
news:7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com...
>
> William M. Klein wrote in message <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And it is true that the vendor can define what "a line" is - but I don't see
how (according to the draft) they can define a "source line" to be anything
that is different from any other type of line.  Therefore, I still don't

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
>William M. Klein wrote in message <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>...
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
[Due to problems with the news server, I have pasted Bill's response
as retreived from Deja.]

And it is true that the vendor can define what "a line" is - but I don't see
how (according to the draft) they can define a "source line" to be anything
that is different from any other type of line.  Therefore, I still don't
know why Rick thinks that the draft allows there to be "anything" after the
R-margin that is still "in the line" or before the next line.

[End]

Both "source line" and "reference format line" are called "lines"
because they have a "linear" relationship.

A source line is an undifferentiated arrangement of characters such
that we may describe the character at any character position within
that source line. (As viewed in a COBOL text editor, the source line
may be differentiated; but, as input to a COBOL compiler, it is not.)

A reference format line is an arrangement of fields identified by
their relative character positions such that we may attribute meaning
to values that are contained within those fields.

By mapping the reference format line to the source line we may
then provide meaning to the characters that exist at each position
in the source line.

So, a source line is similar to a reference format line because of
the linear relationship found in each; but they are different because
one has values and the other has meaning. They are related because
the mapping of one to the other describes, in this case, a COBOL
program in fixed-form.

When an implementor establishes a value for Margin R, that
implementor is describing the minimum length of a source line
that may be mapped to the reference format line. Therefore,
the description of Margin R does not impose any upper limit
on the length of the source line. What it does provide is that,
should the source line be longer than the line described by the
reference format, the additional characters shall have no meaning
to the COBOL compiler.

What should an implementor do with any additional characters?
Since the standard doesn't address that issue, it is up to the
implementor.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Should the draft be ammended to clarify Reference Format? (was: Re: Information in columns 73 to 80)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kb7bf$sg1$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com> <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com>...

[snipped everthing - added to the thread for relevance]

To explain the relationship between source line and reference
format line, I held in my mind, two visual images.

The first was a blank 80 column punch card with COBOL source
text printed in the top line. The second was a card record
layout form with lines drawn at the appropriate margins.

It was only after I was able to visualize this antique technology,
that the relationship between source line and reference format
line became clear.

My point is that, if one must resort to punch cards to understand
the meaning of "fixed-form format reference line" and its
relationship to the source line, then the draft needs to be
amended to clarify both the meaning and the relationship!

Having taken a couple days to think about it, I have decided
that adding the following text at the top of page 36 would do that.

"The fixed-form format reference line describes that portion of
the source line that is relevant to the COBOL compiler."

Comments?
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Should the draft be ammended to clarify Reference Format? (was: Re: Information in columns 73 to 80)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kbf9m$h99@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com> <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com> <7kb7bf$sg1$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
Rick,
  Am I correct that you now agree that the draft (as written) doesn't say
what you would "like" it to say?  If so, I agree with you.

I wouldn't agree with your current proposal, because the sequence area
(columns 1-6) are very DEFINITELY a part of "fixed-format" source lines -
but it does NOT "relevant to the compiler".  Having said, that I would love
to see someone communicating to J4 what the problem is that they see - and
any possible solutions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Should the draft be ammended to clarify Reference Format? (was: Re: Information in columns 73 to 80)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kdskc$lis$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com> <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com> <7kb7bf$sg1$1@server.cntfl.com> <7kbf9m$h99@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>`

```
First, I will reword the suggested text to eliminate the word "relevant"
and to correct a transposition error.

"The fixed-form reference format line describes that part of
the source line that shall be accepted by the COBOL compiler."

This sentence needs to be followed by another to make it clear that
any additional content is not, otherwise, addressed by the (draft)
standard. Chuck Stevens has posted some comments addressing
this point. I want to read his post before phrasing the sentence.

The current draft, as I understand it, neither acknowledges nor denies
the existence of source line content outside the reference format. Yet,
it is a common existing practice for implementations to permit such
content. In my opinion, the draft should acknowledge the practice.

Also, as previously stated, I found the relationship between source
line and fixed-form reference format line to be unclear.

So, yes, the draft does not say what I would like it to say!

I am working on an e-mail that I will send to the J4 chair.
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <7kbf9m$h99@dfw-ixnews12.ix.netcom.com>...
>Rick,
>  Am I correct that you now agree that the draft (as written) doesn't say
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>any possible solutions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Should the draft be ammended to clarify Reference Format? (was: Re: Information in columns 73 to 80)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kbgoi$og6$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <r5j83.7762$Ej6.1659@news3.mia> <7jsrl0$bbp@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <7k3bqs$3cn$1@server.cntfl.com> <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com> <7kb7bf$sg1$1@server.cntfl.com>`

```
I like the basic approach, but I'm not sure I'd be comfortable with the
standard defining whether areas outside the reference format are "relevant
to the compiler" or not.  Thus, rather than

<<The fixed-form format reference line describes that portion of the source
line that is relevant to the COBOL compiler.>>

I'd rather see something like

<<The fixed-form format reference line describes the portion of the source
line that contains information relevant to the language as defined by the
standard.  The format, meaning or use of any information within the source
line but outside the fixed-form format reference line is implementor
defined.>>

or even

<<The relationship between a "source line" and a "fixed-form format
reference line", as well as any differences between the implementations for
these two terms, are defined by the implementor.>>

    -Chuck Stevens

Rick Smith wrote in message <7kb7bf$sg1$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote in message <7k5v3v$2d3$1@server.cntfl.com>...
>
>[snipped everthing - added to the thread for relevance]

<more snippage>

>"The fixed-form format reference line describes that portion of
>the source line that is relevant to the COBOL compiler."
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jq2dc$683$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>...
[...]
>The bottom-line problem (IMHO) is that the draft Standard does not allow
>there to be any "data" (explicitly called "characters" in the draft
>Standard) between the R-margin (after column 72 in IBM mainframe
compilers -
>and many other compilers) and the physical end-of-line (however, the
>specific implementation defines this) - when you use "fixed format
reference
>format" (which is what we are now calling the "old-style" source lines).
>
>The following text (from CD 1.5) is what makes me think this:
>
>"Margin R is immediately to the right of the rightmost character position
of
>a line. The rightmost character position of a line is a fixed number
defined
>by the implementor."
[...]
What about 6.1.c, page 35?

c) The implementor shall specify the meaning of lines and character
    positions.

It seems to me that an implementor could define Margin R at 72 and
define a "source input line" as 80 characters.

The reference to line in the text you quoted appears to refer to
"fixed-form reference format lines" in 6.2.a, page 35.

The standard specifies the meaning of the line through Margin R
and the implementor defines the rest! I don't see any problem with
the wording of the draft standard.

(The use of the word "line" is somewhat ambiguous but I have
come to accept that a language standard can have "some"
ambiguity.)
------------------
Rick Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>

```

[...]

Should be "6 sub-paragraph 1), sub-paragraph c), page 35"
[...]

Rick Smith

#### ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>

>try to get J4 (or WG4) to fix a problem in the draft Standard.  (In fact,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Standard) between the R-margin (after column 72 in IBM mainframe
compilers -
>and many other compilers) and the physical end-of-line (however, the
>specific implementation defines this) - when you use "fixed format
reference
>format" (which is what we are now calling the "old-style" source lines).

For all those years I have been into Cobol, I have never understood why a
Cobol compiler should ever pay attention to what there is right of column
72. I have to admitt that I many times have whished the 72 column limit in
particular and the formatting issues in general to a special hot place :-)
However, may you tell me why the draft has to have anything about this area
at all? Why can't it just say that: "A Cobol compiler ignores whatever there
is in column 73 and to the end of the line."

You know, this is why the committe spends so much time, they are discussing
items that does not really count in the real world. If I am wrong, and there
really is a reasonable reason the discuss column 73+ please enlighten me.

Cheesle
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761b919.16053698@netnews>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com> <7jr9c9$ed9$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Jun 1999 08:19:10 -0700, when "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> You know, this is why the committe spends so much time, they are discussing
> items that does not really count in the real world. If I am wrong, and there
> really is a reasonable reason the discuss column 73+ please enlighten me.

First, COBOL-85 got rid of column 72 in the standard and replaced it with
margin R, a limit specified by the implementor.  I was under the
impression that X3.23-1985 specified that margin R had to be after column
72 or later, but there's no such requirement in the current draft (p
35-36, 6.1).

The problem Bill is refering to is the wording at p 34, 6.1 which says
"Margin R is immediately to the right of of the rightmost character
position of the line."  This seems to preclude the presence of character
positions after margin R, which historically has been used for historical
information.

Bill's right, for compatibility with COBOL-85 it should say that the
implementor specifies margin R and characters past margin R are ignored.
What did it say in COBOL-85?  I really need a copy of COBOL-85.
```
