#### ↳ Re: Information in columns 73 to 80 (Of particular interest to IBM mainframe programmers interested in the next COBOL Standard)

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uFuFm5pu#GA.296@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<7jpo4u$r7n@dfw-ixnews5.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Columns 73-80 are used by the Number Standard option in your edit profile,
which greatly helps track changes to the program if used correctly and
supported by other software and practices. Proper usage of this feature
keeps programmers from having to manually flag every line with some change
number in these columns or in 1-6. The new standard should NOT do anything
to sabotage this useful feature.

Personally, I prefer Num Std Cob so I get numbers in both, and use the Num
compiler option, so any messages include the line number so I can go
directly to the source line in the editor instead of having to find it in
the listing first.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA

```

```
older ISPF/PDF) environment.  If these are "meaningful" to you, then just
ignore it.
```

<a id="t-4153544d93"></a>
## Thread: length of an e-mail address??? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TXV73.13456$6y2.163461@storm.twcol.com>`

```
I am working on a project written mostly in COBOL. We need to define a field
to store an e-mail address. The designers decided to make the field only 30
bytes in length. I thought this was too small and did some research on the
net. To my dismay I was not able to find where the absolute length of an
e-mail is defined.

I tend to believe that there isn't one. Does anyone know for sure if there
is one? If so, where can I find the documentation on said length?

Is there an accepted standard length in industry?
```

#### ↳ Re: length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37609366.4377993B@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<TXV73.13456$6y2.163461@storm.twcol.com>`

```
I don't have the complete answer, but 30 characters is definitely way
too few.

An email address consists of a username and domain name
(username@domainname).
A domain name can consist of multiple subdomains (e.g.
service.dingbats.algamatedelectric.com). Generally, subdomains can be up
to 63 characters long. The total domain name can be up to 255 characters
long (See RFC 1035).

Most email addresses use a simple, two-level domain name (e.g. ibm.net).
According to Network Solutions (the current domain name registrar),
second level domain names can be up to a total of 26 characters long
(including the .com).

I can't find a definitive limit to user name, but would wager it would
be close to the 63 character limit for subdomains.

--John


Jeff wrote:
> 
> I am working on a project written mostly in COBOL. We need to define a field
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there an accepted standard length in industry?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** mixxerdw@eye_eye_echs.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1tGZ16mMk7i4-pn2-NmJdpRE4Jlwj@dsm4merlin.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<TXV73.13456$6y2.163461@storm.twcol.com> <37609366.4377993B@ibm.net>`

```
On Fri, 11 Jun 1999 04:41:10, John Hicks <johnhicks@ibm.net> wrote:

> 
> An email address consists of a username and domain name
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Plus, there is often a "beautified" name before the actual email name 
in the header, e.g.:

"Josephina L. Morgenthaler" 
<jomorthlr234@c801811-a.slnt1.respq.net.au>

Although we don't encourage it (and until recently we were a major 
email provider, so we saw a lot of variants),
we routinely handle 90 character addresses and find 110 to be 
marginal.  I'd allow at least 100 unless you are in a position to 
dictate.

=Dwight=
X1=L, X2=L & the domain is phonetic
```

#### ↳ Re: length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** Ekky.Panneck@hanse.antar.com (Ekky Panneck)
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<699_9906110753@antares.antar.com>`
- **References:** `<TXV73.13456$6y2.163461@storm.twcol.com>`

```
 "> From: "Jeff" <a@a.com>

 "> I am working on a project written mostly in COBOL. We need to define
 "> a field to store an e-mail address. The designers decided to make
 "> the field only 30 bytes in length. I thought this was too small and
 "> did some research on the net. To my dismay I was not able to find
 "> where the absolute length of an e-mail is defined.

I too wrote an E-Mail part in Cobol for my BBS: as there seems no fix
length to exist - but I found some up to about 50 Bytes, I decided
to make the length = one line. I.e., 72 bytes minus the preceding
four bytes (To: ) brings us to a max of 68 bytes.

 "> I tend to believe that there isn't one. Does anyone know for sure if
 "> there is one? If so, where can I find the documentation on said
 "> length? 

 "> Is there an accepted standard length in industry?

Ekky
```

#### ↳ solution#1: Re: length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** "Jeff" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Xe83.13795$6y2.170248@storm.twcol.com>`
- **References:** `<TXV73.13456$6y2.163461@storm.twcol.com>`

```
I found the answer at http://www.wu-wien.ac.at:8082/rfc/rfc821.hyx/$$root
after reading the following response:


Hi Jeff,

take a look at RFC 821 'The Simple Mail Transfer Protocol' (SMTP).

On page 42 you'll find what you need:
*** cut
      4.5.3.  SIZES

         There are several objects that have required minimum maximum
         sizes.  That is, every implementation must be able to receive
         objects of at least these sizes, but must not send objects
         larger than these sizes.


          ****************************************************
          *                                                  *
          *  TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT POSSIBLE, IMPLEMENTATION  *
          *  TECHNIQUES WHICH IMPOSE NO LIMITS ON THE LENGTH *
          *  OF THESE OBJECTS SHOULD BE USED.                *
          *                                                  *
          ****************************************************

            user

               The maximum total length of a user name is 64 characters.

            domain

               The maximum total length of a domain name or number is 64
               characters.

            path

               The maximum total length of a reverse-path or
               forward-path is 256 characters (including the punctuation
               and element separators).

            command line

               The maximum total length of a command line including the
               command word and the <CRLF> is 512 characters.

            reply line

               The maximum total length of a reply line including the
               reply code and the <CRLF> is 512 characters.

            text line

               The maximum total length of a text line including the
               <CRLF> is 1000 characters (but not counting the leading
               dot duplicated for transparency).

            recipients buffer

               The maximum total number of recipients that must be
               buffered is 100 recipients.


          ****************************************************
          *                                                  *
          *  TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT POSSIBLE, IMPLEMENTATION  *
          *  TECHNIQUES WHICH IMPOSE NO LIMITS ON THE LENGTH *
          *  OF THESE OBJECTS SHOULD BE USED.                *
          *                                                  *
          ****************************************************

*** cut


So I think 256 Bytes should be enough.


HTH - Stefan Meyer, Triumph International
```

#### ↳ Re: length of an e-mail address???

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eSd7h#pu#GA.310@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
and there are probably other possible variations too.
```

```
You could also call routines from a traditional subroutine library.
This is indeed a powerful way of working and makes everything
so much easier, but is still not OO, if this is the only thing you use
it for. Do you use sub-classing, inheritance, or any other "real"
OO stuff?

news:fQr83.2990$Hy4.2833@wards...

> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Simon Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Vuv83.3073$Hy4.2881@wards>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <nO083.871$NY6.774@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <K5e83.3848$EU4.2238@stones> <3761BB54.285E120F@zip.com.au> <fQr83.2990$Hy4.2833@wards> <37625f04@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:37625f04@news3.us.ibm.net...
> You could also call routines from a traditional subroutine library.
> This is indeed a powerful way of working and makes everything
> so much easier, but is still not OO, if this is the only thing you use
> it for. Do you use sub-classing, inheritance, or any other "real"
> OO stuff?
Lib routines easier than OO yes I also use subroutine libarys,
OO is not hard, in fact I find it easier, to solve problems. I also use
C++ .dlls and API calls, to add extra power where OOCobol lacks.

Inheritance is the meaning of using class libarys, so you can create
instances of
the class libarys.
I find it ironic that Fujitsu should go and develop a programming compiler
that hides OO technology from the programmer.
MF does not do this, it is like we are trying to shake of OO among Cobol
developers.

The project im doing now you could'nt do, or at least it would be very time
consuming
with traditional technology.  If OO Cobol was not avaliable I would use
VC++, namely for
GUI programming.
```

- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<u2O83.4314$EU4.2920@stones>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <nO083.871$NY6.774@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <K5e83.3848$EU4.2238@stones> <3761BB54.285E120F@zip.com.au> <fQr83.2990$Hy4.2833@wards> <37625f04@news3.us.ibm.net> <Vuv83.3073$Hy4.2881@wards> <37634099.5394C721@zip.com.au>`

```
> Ok you give us some hints but I do not understand some of your
> comments. What is it about the Fuji OO stuff that is not done very
> well?  (I'll admit I have scanned the doco not actually read it
> nor done it).

Dont get me wrong Fujitsu create good application software,
only they have ommited the OO technology from their
compiler this is why I would not use their product.

>
> How real is your system.  How many lines of code?  How many copies
> running (or users running it)?

Backround on my system:
It is a Engineering system that creates scripts by use of G code, that
manufacture's Electronic trunking channels and covers, these then surround
high current "Busbars" for example: In any shop you need to transfer
current from one end to another, these G coded scripts create the channels
and covers
from a large Engineering machine to act as a "cover".

When the system compiles a users request for a cover or channel depending on
where this trunking
will fit, my program also generates a chart showing the user the ratio of
the G-coded program,
with this information the user can improve the performance of the G-coded
program.

It runs accross a LAN, which is fully configarable. It nowm, so far stands
at about 75000 lines of code
with 38 classes, fully designed and developed by myself.
It is not running atm, because I am still developing it, but it is almost
complete.


> Finally what are you doing that really needs the inheritance
> stuff.  As Lief pointed out using a very good modular system will
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken

I briefly described the system above.
I dont understand what hassles you are talking about, with inheritance it
makes
the job that much easier, because you are simply invoking code that is
already their
for the most common processes, this is the whole point of OO, reuseable
code.


Simon Hart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k0u6s$mm2$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <nO083.871$NY6.774@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <K5e83.3848$EU4.2238@stones> <3761BB54.285E120F@zip.com.au> <fQr83.2990$Hy4.2833@wards> <37625f04@news3.us.ibm.net> <Vuv83.3073$Hy4.2881@wards> <37634099.5394C721@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message

>Ok you give us some hints but I do not understand some of your
>comments. What is it about the Fuji OO stuff that is not done very
>well?  (I'll admit I have scanned the doco not actually read it
>nor done it).


The biggest problem is that it uses the C convention of null termination for
dynamically passing arguements.  God help you if you put binary data in a
record. To compound the problem, they explicitly initialize everything to
low values, so every byte that is not explicitly changed/initialized to
something else acts as a delimiter and truncates the rest of the data for
all moves into and out of objects.

I could not get anything solid enough to use, and dropped it after six
months of trials.  I am using the Fujitsu Compiler, and am reasonably happy
with it, but I am scrapping all my PowerCobol, and rewriting it with regular
Cobol and sp2.  Even that is a chore, as all the source is kept in a
proprietary format, and cannot be salvaged.

IMHO, the PowerCobol package is *very* badly designed, at quite a
fundamental level.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

- **From:** someone <none@osu.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:

> The Fujitsu compiler is a very GOOD compiler.  What was your problem
> with it?  It is certainly not "Crap".  I've sold a ton of books (Sams
> Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours) with this compiler included!
>

Well, i took me a while to figure how to get it to work (i had to use the
MAIN option while compiling).  But the thing that bugs me is that the
executable created for Windows does not perform the same way that a OBJ
file does on the the Unix  HP-ACUCOBOL.

for example, I run an interactive program which ACCEPTS info from a user,
such as a last name.  I create a variable in W/S with a PIC X(25) clause.
With ACUCOBOL, when i run the program the input does not have to contain
25 characters, but i with the Windows EXE it does?

so, for something like       ENTER LAST NAME:   SMITH  <ENTER>   ...works
ok in ACUCOBOL
but with the FUJITSU       ENTER LAST NAME:   SMITH  <ENTER>  ...nothing
happens until you enter enough spaces to cover the PIC clause?

Am I missing something with the environment variables or what?

thanks,

arav
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net...
>
> someone wrote in message <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu>...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> debugging tools. If you are going to use them for any kind of real
program,
> then you would use them as pipes for a gui, or something of that nature.
>
>

It is also worth noting that the existing (not just draft) Standard is
really quite "wishy-washy" about what happens when you use ACCEPT with
either input that is smaller or larger than the "receiving" field.  I
haven't looked it up (today) but as I recall, it is pretty much "implementor
defined" what you do - which is why AcuCOBOL may be different from Fujitsu
which may be different from Micro Focus which may be different from IBM.

P.S. I haven't tried this with Fujitsu, but doesn't it "solve" the original
problem (because you are using "enhanced" Accept/Display) by just adding an
"at 0101" to the ACCEPT statement?  I know that adding that changes the way
that Micro Focus works.
```

```
Bill, there are just too many things in the new standard that are
"inmplementor
defined". These things nullify the value of a 'standard'. A 'standard' where
many
of the important and often used things are 'implementor defined' is no
standard at
all. Any realistic program would become non-standard in the sense that it
would
execute differently  (if at all) on different platforms. I hope the next
'standard' will
be delayed until past 2012 (when I plan to retire).

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
As the major proponent of "killing off" (or more accurately radically
modifying) the draft's "processor dependent" definition, I certainly agree
with your concern.  Unfortunately, J4 (and WG4) just are NOT hearing from
many "users" who are concerned about this.

It seems to me (always has - probably always will) that COBOL's biggest
"selling point" was/is its portability.  The current draft doesn't destroy
this - but it does "wound" it quite a bit.

I should (just so you understand why I SOMETIMES disagree with what I am
saying here), say that I think that the next Standard *must* interact as
well as possible with C, C++, and all those operating system APIs that have
become so popular.  To do this, I think that COBOL *must* add some
non-portable stuff - just to interact successfully with other languages and
functions that aren't portable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
No it *must* not. You can still accomplish interoperability by
"encapsulating"
the non-portable stuff behind a portable interface.
why is this so hard to get across?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
I find the introduction of such things as USAGE POINTER and USAGE
BINARY-CHAR (a binary item that works like most current compilers
non-conforming NOTRUNC option) to be extremely USEFUL extensions to COBOL -
but they are "by definition" non-portable.

Similarly, STOP with STATUS will be useful - but its implication are
definitely implementation/hardware/operating-system specific.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Why are they non-portable?
What is non-portable about:
01  mydata.
      02  mypointer  usage pointer.
      02  mybinary   pic s9(3) usage binary-char.

if all compilers support them and their effect and maximum sizes
are defined in the standard instead of being left as 'implementor defined'.

CALL "STOP" USING STATUS could be a portable way
of encapsulating the STOP STATUS verb as long as it is
defined in the standard what the minimum effect is.

Many other languages (e.g. C and Java) deals with OS-specific
things through standard library calls.

I do understand that my quest is a hopeless one given the current
climate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ju8us$kc9@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
USAGE POINTER and USAGE BINARY-CHAR are not portable because the "size"
(actual storage) required for such fields is operating system specific.  For
example, (IBM platforms only) a POINTER on OS/390 is 4 bytes long while it
is 8 bytes long on OS/400.  Therefore,

01 Group1.
      05  Group2.
           10 Othr1 Pic X(6)
      05  Group 3 redefines Group2.
            10  aPtr  Pointer
           10  aChr  Binary-Char
           10  Othr2   Pic X.

is legal under OS/390 but has a larger redefining a smaller on OS/390.  (The
fact that the size of a "char" item in C is NOT portable causes significant
problems - or so I hear - for those doing C development across platforms.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7juhhv$h7v$1@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net> <7ju8us$kc9@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
REDEFINES always has potential problems.

01 Group1.
    05 Group2.
        10 Othr1 Pic X(6).
    05 Group3 redefines Group2.
        10 aComp Comp Pic S9(5) occurs 2.

One computer defaults Comp to Packed-Decimal giving Group3
a length of 6. Another defaults Comp to word Binary giving Group3
a length of 8.

I believe OS/400 to OS/390 will cause this problem.

As will byte mode Comp to word mode Comp on the same platform
(Micro Focus).
------------------
Rick Smith
```

```
Further down in my posting I advocated defining a maximum size for
these constructs. A pointer on OS/400 is 16 bytes BTW. Define a pointer
to be 16 bytes. Disallow redefinitions if group contains pointers or
binary-char.
Define the portable size of all "non-portable" constructs. It CAN be done.
The problem of backwards compatibility can be solved via a compiler switch.
But, as I said, it takes guts to do this and I know a lost cause when I see
one, just can't give it up without *some* kickin' and screamin'.
The solution to the C and VB problem is easily done using my interface
idea: you always go through a portable interface to go to any of these
languages (or old Cobol programs). The interface program resolves
differences in size, character sets (e.g. Unicode), endian-ness, etc.
(sigh)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jufva$g90$2@server.cntfl.com>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```

Leif Svalgaard wrote in message <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net>...
>Why are they non-portable?
>What is non-portable about:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Binary-Char cannot have a picture clause.

The Binary-xxx usages are portable but the maximum and
minimum values are not limited. The draft standard defines
them so as to be able to hold a value of at least X.

The draft standard says that Binary-Char must be able to
hold values in the range -127 through +127. An implementation
with 9-bit characters could allow a range of -255 through +255.
If a program depended upon the larger range of the 9-bit
implementation, the program could not be ported to an
implementation with 8-bit characters, without modification.
If the programmer did not exceed the ranges given in the
draft standard, the program would be portable.

In any case, the data may not be portable due to endian-ness.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376344CC.30493D83@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> Why are they non-portable?
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The main thing about pointers is that they will be internal to the
application.  There should not be any rules about sizes IMHO and
there is not any in C about pointer sizes (16 on 8 bit, 32 on 16
bit, etc).  The important point is to ensure that the same source
can be compiled on multiple platforms in a reliable fashion.  the
only discourse I have seen on portability of C code is the issue
of changing sizes of integer.

The problem occurs when a redefinition of a pointer variable
occurs.  This is a matter of style not of the compiler.  If you
redefine a pointer variable it had better be another pointer
variable!  To redefine pointers to different types is for memory
saving issues and as turned over a number of times just is not a
real issue anymore (although we should not squander memory
artificial conservation is a waste of time). This is where a
concept of Union comes in rather than redefines, it takes on the
size of the largest element within it.  Perhaps this would be a
useful solution.

Since pointers will be effectively invalid (or machine specific)
if written to a file this should never happen so data portability
is not an issue.

When we look at COMP-3 the code is slightly different between
compilers but this only comes to a head when it is sent between
compilers or platforms a non issue for pointers.  If you are doing
this sort of stuff for client server then you would encapsulate
inside corba or whatever and isolate your interfaces.

2 cents spent, is it wisely?
Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3764706d@news3.us.ibm.net>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ On the evils of "should" was Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01beb86b$2d3349e0$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net> <7jrlsp$do3@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761a5d5@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jselg$1a9@dfw-ixnews7.ix.netcom.com> <3761c451@news3.us.ibm.net> <7jsran$b4r@sjx-ixn5.ix.netcom.com> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net> <376344CC.30493D83@zip.com.au>`

```
>Since pointers will be effectively invalid (or machine specific)
>if written to a file this should never happen so data portability
>is not an issue.           ^^^^^

Let me take issue with the "should" in the above.  As any working
engineer or programmer who spends even a nanosecond of thought
on the matter will confirm: "should" should be spelled "SHUD" 'cause
it IS CLEARLY a FOUR letter word!  

Any dependence upon "should" is asking for trouble; if not immediately
then eventually.

Yes, pointers SHOULD never be written to files.  Guess what?  
Someone will do it, and expect it to work.  Then, if we're lucky,
he's a low level twerp who can be told off in suitably sulfurous
terms.  Then again he may be the boss's nephew . . .

Do NOT depend upon "should"!
```

```
In article <01beb86b$2d3349e0$LocalHost@stupidwin95>,
Kevin G. Rhoads <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM> wrote:

[snippolinio]

>Kevin G. Rhoads, Ph.D. (Linearity is a convenient fiction.)
                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

... are you being straight with us about this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On the evils of "should" was Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01beb930$ed369a60$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net> <376344CC.30493D83@zip.com.au> <01beb86b$2d3349e0$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <os8a3.1321$S2.77565@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
>... are you being straight with us about this?
Which?   The PhD?  or  Linearity?  or should?

re: PhD
Self taught programmer during High School in the mid/late '60s (Fortran,
COBOL, PL/I & 360 BAL mostly)
DPMA's certification as Registered Business Programmer '72
S.B - Theoretical Math '74 MIT
S.M - Electrical Eng. '83  MIT
PhD - EE '89 MIT

Re: Linearity
One of the things I do is analog circuitry for measurement and
instrumentation -- usually weirdo, high precision stuff for lab.
experiments -- Linearity (in analog circuits, at least) is a fiction,
an often tolerable approximation, which is quite convenient when
it is applicable, but a fiction nontheless.

Re: should
And "should" should be "shud", that's an assertion I'll stand 
squarely behind  :-)  It is CLEARLY a four letter word!
Anybody who depends on "should" when they can avoid it
is asking for trouble.

For those well out of academia, a translation chart:
S.B. == go into lab, fail to kill self
S.M. == go into lab, fail to kill self, get meaningful data
Ph.D. == go into lab, fail to kill self, get meaningful data, explain data
(I got that from Prof. Tom Eager at MIT, I don't know if it originated
with him.)

These days I teach Calc I and Differential Equations to Freshman,
I do electrochemistry research, work with an instrument company
maintaining and extending their proprietary magnetic diffusion code
written in Fortran77 and consult on various things.  And help grad.
students to make their experiments work and their interpretations of
their data work.  Between them all I keep fairly busy.  Unfortunately,
nothing I've been doing the last 25 years has given me a reasonable
excuse to keep up my COBOL skills, what little I remember is -68,
so I spend some time (mostly) lurking here.  And I've got MS COBOL
4.5 and 5.0 and Fujistu 3.0 and 4.0 on my system (as well as too 
many Fortran and C compilers to mention and some assemblers
and a few oddball development tools).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: On the evils of "should" was Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376A4476.EAEA4F12@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <3762286a@news3.us.ibm.net> <376344CC.30493D83@zip.com.au> <01beb86b$2d3349e0$LocalHost@stupidwin95> <os8a3.1321$S2.77565@iad-read.news.verio.net> <01beb930$ed369a60$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`

```
Kevin G. Rhoads wrote:
> 
> Re: should
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Ahhh...  accademics gotta love 'em.  :->

The difference between experience and inexperience.   When you CAN
break the shoulds and make it work.  This is a paraphrase of
something else I read.

Basically set simple black and white rules but do listen to and
accept reasonable arguments to break them.  Inexperienced
programmers learn the rules and abide, good programmers are taught
better ways to do things if they try to break a rule and the
experienced programmer knows when to break the rules but avoid it
at all costs.

Should be right now....
Ken
```

```
>> Anybody who depends on "should" when they can avoid it
>> is asking for trouble.
>The difference between experience and inexperience.   When you CAN
>break the shoulds and make it work.  

Ayup!  That's why I said "when they can avoid it".

In college we teach 'em to avoid "shoulds".  Real-world experience then
SHOULD add when to bend the rule, or ignore it.  

However, I expect anyone with code maintainence experience (does that
describe anybody here ?  :-)  will likely agree that "shoulds" get depended
upon more often than they SHOULD.  At least that has been my experience
when maintaining code . . .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: i need a decent cobol compiler!!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376199f9.8103799@netnews>`
- **References:** `<37608385.AB13E655@osu.edu> <37608a35.7928203@news1.ibm.net> <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu> <7jrknj$jco$1@news.igs.net>`

```
'Twas Fri, 11 Jun 1999 14:26:43 -0400, when "donald tees"
<donald@willmack.com> illuminated alt.cobol thusly:

> someone wrote in message <37608ECA.FF8C517E@osu.edu>...

> >so, for something like       ENTER LAST NAME:   SMITH  <ENTER>   ...works
> >ok in ACUCOBOL
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> then you would use them as pipes for a gui, or something of that nature.

I've never used a Cobol compiler where ENTER didn't terminate the ACCEPT.
I would consider that a bug.

What you actually get in the input field varies from one platform to the
next.  Some would give "SMITH" followed by spaces, some would put CR or LF
or ETX in the sixth byte and leave the rest unmodified.

I guess you could write your program to loop ACCEPTing one byte at a time,
and exit when you find the ENTER.
```

## Thread: MF FileShare on Sinix/Unix — 3 msgs

### MF FileShare on Sinix/Unix

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<375F7BEE.68F7D0BF@Triumph-International.de>`

```
Hi,

we need more informations using MF FileShare running on Sinix/Unix
machines than described within the original MF documentation....i.e.
speed is up to 4 times slower using FileShare instead of 'callable file
handler'. On the Unix side we use already named pipes (ccinampu) as
transport protocol 'cause the fileshare server and the fileshare client
are running on the same machine.

How can we improve speed?

Any help would be gratefully appreciated.

TIA- Stefan Meyer, Triumph International
```

- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00

- **References:** `<375F7BEE.68F7D0BF@Triumph-International.de>`

```
We're planning to use FileShare on HP unix and AIX.   As a matter of
interest how many users do you have?  Also do you get a steady degradation
as more users log on?

TIA
Rick

Stefan Meyer <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de> wrote in message
news:375F7BEE.68F7D0BF@Triumph-International.de...
> Hi,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF FileShare on Sinix/Unix

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761108C.9740E438@Triumph-International.de>`
- **References:** `<375F7BEE.68F7D0BF@Triumph-International.de> <929086772.6548.0.nnrp-08.9e98b131@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Hi Rick,

depends on the country: approx 20 (don't laugh).

At the moment FS is not running, because of the speed. But we have to use FS
'cause we need access to the COBOL files from NT-Workstations (including
updates with ODBC-Driver from Parkway).

Interesting is that when accessing the cobol files from NT-Workstations using
ODBC and FS (with ccitcp) the speed is up to 8 times faster than using ODBC
without FS.

Next week I have the chance to test within a country. I'll check if the speed
depends on how many users are logged on (Both NT and Unix side). Will be back
on Friday....

cu - Stefan Meyer

Rick Price wrote:

> We're planning to use FileShare on HP unix and AIX.   As a matter of
> interest how many users do you have?  Also do you get a steady degradation
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-6a25e8e68f"></a>
## Thread: oracle & cobol & char — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### oracle & cobol & char

- **From:** kkitowski@issre.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jp1fh$psv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Greetings,

I am having a problem SELECTing with a CHAR column that has trailing
spaces in an Oracle database.  I'm using MicroFocus Workbench 4.0.32 and
Pro*COBOL 7.3.
Example:
column:         ClaimId CHAR(10)
column value:   'ABCD123   '
host variable:  S-CLAIMID PIC X(10).
variable value: 'ABCD123   '
Embedded SQL:   Select ClaimUID from Claim where ClaimId = :S-CLAIMID

This SELECT produces no match on the database, however

Select ClaimUID from Claim where ClaimId = 'ABCD123'
and
Select ClaimUID from Claim where Rtrim(ClaimId) = :S-CLAIMID
both produce a match on the database.

Of course we don't want to use Rtrim because it is not very efficient,
also we would like to be able to use the same code for Sybase and SQL
Server.

If anyone has the answer to this or knows who has the answer, pls email
me at kkitowski@issre.com.

Thanks in advance for your help.

Regards, Kevin


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: oracle & cobol & char

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dx9UgnVbJddB@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`
- **References:** `<7jp1fh$psv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We realized this bug working with Oracle and DEC/OpenVMS with the 
Pro*COBOL pre-compiler.  The bottom line is that the variables on the 
right side of the condition are ALL rtrimed automagically.  So to get 
a match the field has to be full or you have to rtrim it.  We Rtrim 
both sides.  JUST IN CASE down the road Oracle fixes the bug.

On Thu, 10 Jun 1999 18:51:33, kkitowski@issre.com wrote:

> Greetings,
> 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

<a id="t-3243e91bec"></a>
## Thread: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming — 12 msgs

### Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

- **From:** "Yazmin Wickham" <ywickham@mp-cpa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com>`

```
Just curious as to what experienced programmers believe the definition is of
these two styles of programming. We believe them to be basically the same
thing, but there is some difference, from what we are told:

bullet-proof programming - a program that has been tested using real or live
input for test data by someone other than the programmer to diminish bias

defensive programming - a program that has been thoroughly tested and has
been written with error paragraphs and/or error control procedures

Any corrections or insights are very much welcomed!
Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com>`

```
you are too simple-minded here. We had a discussion about this
a while ago. In my not so humble opinion (IMNSHO), as program should be
written in so a way that it maximizes the chance of crashing if something
is wrong. I called such programs 'brittle'. The crash will ensure that the
bug gets
fixed ASAP.

Yazmin Wickham <ywickham@mp-cpa.com> wrote in message
news:3761457d.0@news.kivex.com...
> Just curious as to what experienced programmers believe the definition is
of
> these two styles of programming. We believe them to be basically the same
> thing, but there is some difference, from what we are told:
>
> bullet-proof programming - a program that has been tested using real or
live
> input for test data by someone other than the programmer to diminish bias
>
```

```
> > is wrong. I called such programs 'brittle'. The crash will ensure that
the
> > bug gets
> > fixed ASAP.

An application is never just ONE program. The application as a whole must be
robust and have a graceful way of dealing with failing programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3762da64.13471015@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com> <3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net> <3761fbc8.33097420@netnews> <376224e8@news3.us.ibm.net>`

```
'Twas Sat, 12 Jun 1999 04:14:31 -0500, when "Leif Svalgaard"
<lsvalg@ibm.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> 
> Randall Bart <Barticus@att.spam.net> wrote
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> robust and have a graceful way of dealing with failing programs.

First, it is quite possible for an application (in the sense you're using
the term "application") to be one program, but I don't see how that's
germane.

Second, that's not what I meant by "application".  By "application" I
meant the type of job being done.  If you have a computer which
coordinates a steel mill, and it receives an unexpected message from some
senser, you want that program to keep chugging if at all possible.  Even
if this situation indicates that the software in the senser needs fixing,
even if it means the routine in this program needs fixing, you do not want
the steel mill to shut down.  You want the computer to keep running as
much of the steel mill as possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376306ae@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com> <3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net> <3761fbc8.33097420@netnews> <376224e8@news3.us.ibm.net> <3762da64.13471015@netnews>`

```
Of course we want the steel mill (the application in my view) to keep on
running, but we do not want the mill to react to the failing sensor program
and to something anyway. The application is robust and has default
safety bounds on the result of any program, which in turn should be allowed
to fail ASAP.
I'm a practical man and am not accustomed to split hairs on the
difference between programs and applications.

that

must be
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k0sp1$mh5$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com> <3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net> <3761fbc8.33097420@netnews> <376224e8@news3.us.ibm.net> <3762da64.13471015@netnews>`

```
If you are programming real time, you have a different program for each
sensor.  Each is standalone.  Hell, nowadays, we just devote a computer to
each one.  The fibre link is the "system".

With most of the mills that I control, you could just start an emulator for
any given sensor, and the programs would not even know that it was not
working, someone was typing in data instead.  Thats how we test ... start
all the sensor emulators, turn off all the equipment power, and watch the
"pseudo-mill" run on the screen.

If I ever get back into doing some post grad psychology work, I plan to use
that as a model for human sleeping/dreaming. IE-Sleeping/dreaming is the
biological method of testing software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3764FF09.A49F99F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com> <3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net> <3761fbc8.33097420@netnews> <376224e8@news3.us.ibm.net> <3762da64.13471015@netnews>`

```
It depends.  You should examine each business need to decide what you
want to do in the case of failure.  If that sensor fails, you may wish
to avoid moving molten steel around the factory blindly.

You can't have a single rule on what to do when the unexpected occurs -
you must anticipate types of errors analyze what should happen when
those errors occur and work accordingly.

> > > > you are too simple-minded here. We had a discussion about this
> > > > a while ago. In my not so humble opinion (IMNSHO), as program should be
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> l    |/ Has anyone in Washington DC read the US Constitution?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37659f9e.6972069@netnews>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com> <3761a6cb@news3.us.ibm.net> <3761fbc8.33097420@netnews> <376224e8@news3.us.ibm.net> <3762da64.13471015@netnews> <3764FF09.A49F99F8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 14 Jun 1999 07:09:29 -0600, when Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> You can't have a single rule on what to do when the unexpected occurs -
> you must anticipate types of errors analyze what should happen when
> those errors occur and work accordingly.

Drifting somewhat off topic, but this still has to do with code that was
_not_ bullet proof.

In the early 1980s, a fire broke out on a BART (no relation) train under
the San Francisco Bay.  The computer responded to the fire alarm by
stopping the train.  Everyone died.  The official post-mortem on the
incident concluded that the train should have continued on to the next
station so the the people could get off.  Whoever designed the program
hadn't thought it through.

Ten years later, a fire broke out in a freight car on a Chunnel train
under the English Channel.  As soon as the second fire senser was tripped,
the computer stopped the train!  This time, no one died, because the fire
was small and all the way at the back of the train far from the
passengers, but if the fire had been bigger and/or closer to the
passengers people would have been killed.  This time it wasn't just a
matter of not thinking it through, they hadn't even studied the history of
tunnel disasters.  There wasn't even a way to restart the train from the
control station until the fire was put out.  They had to send little
shuttles in from the other end to retrieve the passengers.

There arre applications where you just can't write brittle code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990611160555.24712.00000501@ng-fs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com>`

```
Yazmin Wickham writes ...

>Just curious as to what experienced programmers believe the definition is of
>these two styles of programming. We believe them to be basically the same
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks.

Well, there's English grammar. "bullet-proof programming" cannot be a program;
nor can "defensive programming". These terms relate to a style of programming.

Alternatively, you can say "a bullet-proof program is a program that ..." and
so on.

In any event, in my mind, a program is bullet-proof if it cannot fail no matter
what data the user throws at it. This means that it checks data on entry an
ensures that it is correct format before using it; it also means that all kinds
of errors (e.g., hardware failure on a tape or disk drive) are trapped and
dealt with.

Very few programs are written with this kind of rigor (and that's appropriate:
it takes a lot longer to write and test such as program; the reality is that
for most programs there is not enough payback to write to this level; if you
have a problem, abend with a message, fix the problem, and rerun the step /
job).

The term "defensive programming" has about the same connotation. Perhaps the
distinction would be that program written in a defensive style will prevent bad
data but not trap hardware errors. I don't know that there is any definitive
take on this (at least I don't recall seeing any).

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761D05F.D4ABACAF@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3761457d.0@news.kivex.com>`

```
Yazmin Wickham wrote:
> 
> bullet-proof programming - a program that has been tested using real
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Unfortunately the definitions and testing are unrelated.  You can
program defensively and test attrociously :->.  For a definition
of defensive programming see Writing solid code by Steve Macguire
page 33. He describes what I will call bullet proofing.  You may
want to look up Xtreme programming (great name) which is about a
concept close to what you discuss.

Coding defensively is a matter of ensuring that you code does not
get stuck on unexpected results.  For example

perform varying i from 1 by 1 until i > 20

This code does not need the '>' EQUALS will do fine.  Code changes
later may affect the content of I and memory problems may mean
that 'i' could be corrupted sending the program into an infinite
loop.  Incorrect results are better (in some instances) than no
results at all.

Bullet proofing the same code would be

perform varying i from 1 by 1 until i > 20
D  if I > 20
D	   something has gone seriously wrong ring bells!!!
D  END-IF

Again this code may NEVER be tested!  Because the i varaiable
should never ever be greater than 20, the code under the if will
be redundant and in most cases removed by the compiler optimiser. 
It is surprisingly rare for a programmer to force the error to
test the error routines and the tester cant without instrumenting
the code which is not something they should do.

There is a great cost (about 3 times according to Steve McConnell)
to bullet proofing systems.  A program for inhouse must have a
single path that works sucessfully, if it does not work then train
the user to make it work.  A system that is bullet proof must have
no path that does not work, if it does have a broken path then fix
the program.  It is a big difference in coding style.

The main problem is that a particular program may be bullet proof
but the design is shot.  Quality is about the whole system. 
Analyse well, code well, test well and all will be well, stuff
anything up and it wont!.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bullet-Proof Programming and Defensive Programming

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990618202706.10714.00000556@ng-fy1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3761D05F.D4ABACAF@zip.com.au>`

```

 bullet-proof programming - a method whereby professionals create items which
can not be executed at all, thus having no actual errors, and same items have
no name so that you can not even say how it could be otherwise; method also
requires disarmament of test team members, a vexacious thing to administer.

 defensive programming - a programming style where all assignments are given to
the other programmer.
```

---

<a id="t-ef51a57440"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS) — 8 msgs

### Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "Paul Shanley" <paul.shanley@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
To anyone familiar with the RWCS:

I have recently written a number of Cobol report programs (using Microfocus
Cobol under Unix) using the facilities of the report writer control system
(RWCS) and have a question: Is it possible to cause the report output to use
form feed characters (as if a "write rec after advancing page" type
statement were used) rather than printing blank lines to fill the "page"
definition in the RD.

Such a capability would make reports more portable between printers with
slightly different page lengths without having to alter the definitions.

Any experience/advice gratefully received.

Regards,

Paul Shanley
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "Jay Moseley" <jayNO_SPAMmoseley@ibm-dot-net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wnlzbfryrlvozarg.fd7uli0.pminews@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
On Fri, 11 Jun 1999 23:53:27 +0100, Paul Shanley wrote:

>To anyone familiar with the RWCS:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Paul Shanley

Define a detail report item with the clause 'NEXT GROUP NEXT PAGE' and either
explicitly GENERATE the item or use control fields to automatically generate
the group. That will force the next detail group to be produced on the next
physical page.

Jay Moseley, CCP
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ju9b9$kvg@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
If your only concern is whether you fill out a page (from the last
"information" on one page to the top of the next page) with blank lines or
whether you do a "page feed" control character, there is a MF
compiler-option that controls this.  I don't have my system reference with
me, but I think the name has "form feed" in it.

If your concern is doing blank lines versus control characters for skipping
1 or 2 lines (like IBM mainframe DCB=FBM or FBA) then I don't think that MF
(or the PC world in general, i.e. in a printer independent way) provides you
a way to do that.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3763462E.DBAC8E62@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net> <7ju9b9$kvg@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> If your concern is doing blank lines versus control characters for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Control-L is the printer independant way of doing this. 
Everything except page definition printers (postscript for
example) which the text has to be 'converted' in order to print
anyway.  There is even an EBCDIC equivalent but no-one uses it to
my knowledge.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "Jay Siegel" <ka2csh@nospam.nowhere>
- **Date:** 1999-06-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A4.74.L22v04c9otq47S6w4.NR@nospam.nowhere>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
"PS" == "Paul Shanley" writes:

PS> To anyone familiar with the RWCS:
Is it possible to cause the report output to use
PS> form feed characters (as if a "write rec after advancing page"
PS> type statement were used) rather than printing blank lines to
PS> fill the "page" definition in the RD.

You might try creating a PF (Page Footing that can appear anywhere
that your DEtail lines do  + 1 more line with the Form Feed character
described as its hex value.  That's the best that I can think of.
Let me know it works.

Jay
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "Paul Shanley" <paul.shanley@dial.pipex.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k3o7g$hnf$1@lure.pipex.net>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
Thanks for the responses.

William Klein's suggestion of a compiler directive sounds like the best
option, however I have scoured the MF cobol documentation without success -
anyone know better?

I had a go at Jay Siegel's idea (reproduced below), defining the report one
line shorter that the shortest page printer it will be output to and put a
"type PF" report group with a x'0C' (formfeed) character. This now produces
the desired results - cheers Jay (though I can't help feeling its a bit of a
hack). Still, problem solved.

>Jay Siegel wrote:
>You might try creating a PF (Page Footing that can appear anywhere
>that your DEtail lines do  + 1 more line with the Form Feed character
>described as its hex value.  That's the best that I can think of.
>Let me know it works.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** John Piggott <Piggott@spc-systems.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376802C9.436C@spc-systems.com>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net> <7k3o7g$hnf$1@lure.pipex.net>`

```
Paul Shanley wrote:
> 
> Thanks for the responses.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Let me know it works.

Paul: This is just a matter of a doubtful implementation by this 
particular vendor.  In all other compilers I've come across a form feed 
is generated.  I think you should try to make a really good case, such as 
a *serious* loss of efficiency or some error situation, and then ask MF 
to fix the problem in a new release.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Report Writer (RWCS)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k8q7t$4eg@dfw-ixnews6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7js410$8n3$1@lure.pipex.net> <7k3o7g$hnf$1@lure.pipex.net> <376802C9.436C@spc-systems.com>`

```
The Micro Focus directive that you want to look at is:

  RWHARDPAGE

It is true that the default is NORWHARDPAGE - but RWHARDPAGE is available
with all current Micro Focus compilers - and will issue a "control
character" to send out the desired control character.
```

```
Hello Erie

I changed from RMCobol 5.32 for Dos to RmCobol 6.51 for Windows.
Our programs are running under Win98.
We use since the beginning the Function Key from F1 to F30. Our NCR
Keyboards have these Function Keys. You can generate them also with
combined keys like CNTRL and F2 = F22
We could use all the Functon Keys with Version 6.51 except F20 and F30.
These keys seem not to be available under 6.51.
With NT we have no experience.

Have a nice weekend

john@casagrande.ch
```

#### ↳ Re: PROG. FUNCT. KEYS IN RMCOBOL FOR NT

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ju6mq$pmj$1@news.imp.ch>`
- **References:** `<emdd0SCt#GA.349@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com>`

```
Hy Erie,

for a little help please read the following sample:

      **********************************
      *  TESTPROGRAM FOR FUNCTION-KEYS  *  RM
      **********************************
      ***
      ***    FOR DOCUMENTATION I MENTIONED SOME VALUES OF OFTEN
      ***    USED KEYS .....
      ***
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      ***
       01  W-WORK.
           05  W-ESC-KEY                 PIC 9(2)  VALUE 0.
               88 ESC-HELP                 VALUE 1.
               88 ESC-CONFIRM         VALUE 7.
               88 ESC-STOP                 VALUE 9.
               88 ESC-FINISHED         VALUE 9 27.
               88 ESC-BACK               VALUE 10 52.
               88 ESC-ENTER              VALUE 13.
               88 ESC-FIELD-NEXT     VALUE 0 13 53 56.
               88 ESC-FINISHED         VALUE 27.
               88 ESC-ARROW-BACK       VALUE 52.
               88 ESC-ARROW-FORWARD    VALUE 53.
               88 ESC-LINE-NEXT        VALUE 53.
               88 ESC-HOME             VALUE 54.
               88 ESC-NEXT             VALUE 52 53 67 68.
               88 ESC-FORWARD          VALUE 13 53 68.
               88 ESC-BACK             VALUE 52 67.
               88 ESC-PGUP             VALUE 67.
               88 ESC-PGDN             VALUE 68.
               88 ESC-CTL-HOME         VALUE 81.
               88 ESC-END              VALUE 82.
               88 ESC-CTL-END          VALUE 83.
               88 ESC-CON-ERROR        VALUE 98.
               88 ESC-TIME-OVER        VALUE 99.
           05  FIELD                   PIC X(5).
*****************************************************
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       TEST-1.
           DISPLAY "ENTER A WORD,  THE VALUE OF THE FUNCTION KEY"
                                       LINE  1 POSITION  2.
           DISPLAY "OR THE KEY WILL BE STORED IN FIELD W-ESC"
                                       LINE  2 POSITION  2.

       TEST-2.
           ACCEPT FIELD                LINE  5 POSITION 29
                                       TIME 2500
                                       NO BEEP ECHO
                                       UPDATE TAB PROMPT "."
                                    ON EXCEPTION W-ESC-KEY
                                       CONTINUE.
           IF   ESC-STOP
                GO TO TEST-90.
           IF   ESC-BACK
                GO TO TEST-1.
           IF   ESC-FINISHED
                GO TO TEST-EXIT.
           IF   ESC-TIME-OVER
                DISPLAY "THIS HAPPENS IF YOU DID NOT PRESSE A KEY"
                                        LINE 15 POSITION  1
                DISPLAY "WITHIN ABOUT 20 SEC. (TIME 2500)"
                                        LINE 16 POSITION  1.
           GO TO TEST-2.
       TEST-90.
           DISPLAY "I LIKE GO TO'S !!!" LINE 20 POSITION  1.
           STOP RUN.
       TEST-EXIT.
           EXIT.

hope it helps.

greetings john@casagrande.ch
Erie <73042.311@CompuServe.COM> schrieb in im Newsbeitrag:
emdd0SCt#GA.349@nih2naaa.prod2.compuserve.com...
> I was just wondering if there is a way that anyone knows of to
> program function keys when running RMCOBOL for Windows NT vs. 6.5.
> We are currently moving from an older system where our users had
> this ability.  So, if anyone has any idea of how to do this please
> help.  And, thanks in advance for all you help.
```

<a id="t-fc796fc1b5"></a>
## Thread: Euro Phase Two. — 3 msgs

### Euro Phase Two.

- **From:** "Charles Handy" <charles_handy@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ODiDcLEt#GA.283@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
We have just finished the high level analysis stage of Euro phase two which
involves converting financial systems to dual currency mode i.e holding and
manipulating data in both NCD and Euro currencies.  We estimate the effort
to be at least twice that required for Y2K with the major impact being on
numeric field sizes in our mainly COBOL programs and performance issues
related to back office processing and data storage. For example there is a
requirement to hold numeric values to six decimal points and possibly a
requirement to process credit card transactions in both the NCD and EURO,
and print combined NCD and Euro statements.  I am interested in talking to
anyone on this newsgroup who has carried out similar investigations with a
view to sharing ideas and strategies.
```

#### ↳ Re: Euro Phase Two.

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376173BA.AEE49FE9@semdesigns.com>`
- **References:** `<ODiDcLEt#GA.283@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Such a huge effort almost demands automation to carry it out.
Check out www.semdesigns.com for the DMS Reengineering Toolkit,
which could be used to define and carry out the transformations
required to update the data schemas and the code modifications.

Charles Handy wrote:

> We have just finished the high level analysis stage of Euro phase two which
> involves converting financial systems to dual currency mode i.e holding and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> view to sharing ideas and strategies.
```

#### ↳ Re: Euro Phase Two.

- **From:** "Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-06-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k2ghn$9u4$1@duke.telepac.pt>`
- **References:** `<ODiDcLEt#GA.283@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
We've been doing that for the past 18 months now. The system is stable and
running, but the solution was to recode most of the pakage. Of course the
currency conversion is held in a single module, but we had to include
information in almost all files. The selection parameters for reporting was
also an issue since know, the user as the choice of the NCD (or Euro) in
wich the report is to be printed.
For historical data, every program performs a conversion based on dates, if
applicable, in order to make comparisons possible.

----------------------------------------------
Paulo Vieira
- pvieira@emporsoft.pt,http://www.emporsoft.pt
- Sailor on Weekends :)
- Programmer on business days

>involves converting financial systems to dual currency mode i.e holding and
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
program with a parameter?
What i need to do is load my application with the file that the usr may have
double clicked.
I know this can be done very easily with VC++

any help, thanks
Simon Hart
```

#### ↳ Re: Register program

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761BDB7.62F5BF54@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<Mng83.2882$Hy4.2590@wards>`

```
Simon Hart wrote:
> 
> Does any one know how to execute a MF visual Object Cobol for windows
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


I assume file manager...

View / options / filetypes

New type

Fill in the blanks and click New

Type in a descriptive name under action and the put in you command
name under application.  For example to edit a file my command is:

"C:\Program Files\vim-5.3\gvim.exe" "%1"

If you want options then put them in.  If you want multiple
selections then keep adding new actions and define one as the
default.

HTH Ken
```

<a id="t-3fbf853328"></a>
## Thread: Bits & Bytes in Packed & Unpacked Decimal — 2 msgs

### Bits & Bytes in Packed & Unpacked Decimal

- **From:** "John H. Lindsay" <eil@aracnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37611FE7.A5269056@aracnet.net>`

```
This one is for the people who love COBOL data representation
details on any machine type to the assembly language level.

I'm writing a set of routines to handle some COBOL packed and
unpacked decimal data (typically pictured (NOT edited) numeric
(only picture characters  S, 9 and V) and some forms of
computational data) for a SNOBOL 4 system (the application
involves some intense character pattern matching and hence the
SNOBOL 4).  The details of the internal machine data
representation vary greatly from one machine to another, and from
one compiler (even on the same machine) to another, and I can
account for the representation in the various IBM mainframe
COBOLs.  But while I'm at it, I'd like to do a thorough job of it.
Any details that anyone can supply about the representation of
packed and or unpacked decimal data on various other machines
and COBOLs to the bit, hex. digit and byte level would be
appreciated.

In particular, does anyone know the details for Fujitsu Cobol and
IBM Visual Age for Cobol, both on x86 type PCs (Windows xx,
OS/2, .... ) ?  Details about the subject for other recent or current
machines and OS's would be appreciated too.
```

#### ↳ Re: Bits & Bytes in Packed & Unpacked Decimal

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7_h83.696$vK.32571@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37611FE7.A5269056@aracnet.net>`

```
Download my free tutorial on COBOL datatypes. It's COBDATA.ZIP at
http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html
```

- **From:** "Software Pool s.a.s." <softwarepool@galactica.it>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wwJi2Q$s#GA.77@news.galactica.it>`

```
How can I call a C routine from a cobol program.
I need simple examples!

Mirko.
```

#### ↳ Re: I need help: cobol and c-routines

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aZ583.107$303.218@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<wwJi2Q$s#GA.77@news.galactica.it>`

```
In COBOL:
CALL "cfuncname" USING WS-VAR1 WS-VAR2 WS-VAR3 ...

In C:

void cfuncname (type1 * var1, type2 * var2, type3 * var3);

- Darius Cooper
```

<a id="t-1733de67f4"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Inspection (Walk-Through) Checklists — 3 msgs

### COBOL Inspection (Walk-Through) Checklists

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3761EC43.35682892@techie.com>`

```
My group is getting serious about code inspections.  Can any of
you share a check list of things you look for when reviewing code?

If you send the to me jared@techie.com I will combine and post
what I get.  Maybe even get them on a web page.  Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Inspection (Walk-Through) Checklists

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uMuqvbpu#GA.150@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<3761EC43.35682892@techie.com>`

```
Sure. If it's totally new development and the programmer doesn't have to
cope with existing garbage, then scan the Proc. Div. for any Alter, Go To,
Perform Thru, Exit, Section, or Next Sentence, or periods on any executable
statements except e.g. the final one and only GoBack permitted in the
entire program, at the end of the main paragraph and not within any
structure but at the top level only (periods should be by themselves in
column 12 as only the last "statement" in each paragraph); if any of these
are found, hand it back to the author to be coded right. If it passes this
step, then on to the rest of the inspection process to actually look at the
code. This initial step should eliminate a large percentage of likely bugs
before they ever get into production.


Robert M. Pritchett, President - RMP Consulting Partners LLC
http://rmpcp.com - rmp@rmpcp.com - Dallas, TX - Member ICCA
"Quality means doing it right the first time!"
See http://www.headhunter.net/jobstv/0j/j04651mjxt8trch80j.htm?ShowJob
Contractors: tired of hearing "W-2 only"? Join us and let us help you get
that same contract on a 1099 as a self-employed independent contractor!


Jared Thomas wrote in message <3761EC43.35682892@techie.com>...
>My group is getting serious about code inspections.  Can any of
>you share a check list of things you look for when reviewing code?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.radiks.net/~jmthomas
```

- **References:** `<3761EC43.35682892@techie.com> <uMuqvbpu#GA.150@cpmsnbbsa03>`
