<a id="t-11d8a22ef5"></a>
## Thread: Help! Question on 'Overpunching' — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** info@techsol.com.au (Myles Wakeham)
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net>`

```
We are a group of PC developers in Australia and have been handed an
ASCII data file from a Cobol application running on a mainframe.  It
contains 'overpunched data'.  I haven't come across this before
(forgive my ignorance), but what exactly is overpunched data?  And how
can I convert this data to 32-bit decimal values?  Are there any
algorithms around that I can use?

info@techsol.com.au

Thanks
Myles

=====================================================
Myles Wakeham
Tech Solutions (Aust) Pty Ltd
Adelaide, Australia
www.techsol.com.au
E-Mail:  info@techsol.com.au
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A5A93.1949$LI5.191597@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net>`

```

Myles Wakeham wrote in message ...
>We have been handed an
>ASCII data file from a Cobol application running on a mainframe.  It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>algorithms around that I can use?

That term comes from (where else?) the punched card days.  A digit is
represented by one hold in a given column in a card, but a field may be
negated by punching another hold in the 11 zone of the low end of a numeric
field.  If a positive value is needed, another punch in the 12 zone (very
top of the card) would be added.  The two punches in one column usually
translate (in punched card format) to alphabetic characters.

A + zero = {     positive 1-9 is A-I respectively    [uppercase "i" not a
digit]
neg zero = }    negative 1-9 is J-R respectively

If a field in cols 1-4 represents an unsigned value of 1234, that is exactly
what you would find in the first 4 columns.  A positive 1234 would be 123D
while -1234 would be
123M

This scheme makes much more sense in EBCDIC than in ASCII when you look at
the hex code for a digit (signed or not)--it is continued in ASCII for
compatibility with older machines, programs, etc.

HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766D360.A3CC5036@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net>`

```
The IBM punch card can contain all 256 possable byte values.  If
you still had a keypunch and card, the punch combinations for most
of these values would be entered by holding down the "overpunch"
key and then pressing several key to punch the individual rows
needed to make up the desired code.

Ascii uses byte values 0-127.  In EBCDIC, a zero is 250 or x'F0'. 
In ASCII, a zero is x'30'.  If you had a keypunch and needed to
punch an ASCII zero into a card, you would have to look up the
special code for x'30' and use the overpuch key to enter it.  This
is because there is no EBCDIC character assigned to x'30' which
appears on the keypunch keyboard.  (Or, you could find an ASCII
keypunch!).

If the data on the card is binary decimal data, then no conversion
is necessary.  x'30' is 48 decimal in both EBCDIC and ASCII.

If the data is character data (or decimal data) then you read the
data business as usual.  BUT as soon as you read it, you need to
translate the character field from ASCII code to EBCDIC.  INSPECT
REPLACING is the verb.

For MVS, if a record is all ASCII Characters, code OPTCD=Q on the
DD statement to have the system translate from ASCII to EBCDIC
during the read operation.

If you are sorting or comparing character fields that contain
ASCII data (i.e. have not been translated) the you need to adjust
the character set or COLLATING SEQUENCE in your COBOL program.

Myles Wakeham wrote:
> 
> We are a group of PC developers in Australia and have been handed an
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> E-Mail:  info@techsol.com.au
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766dc37@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net> <3766D360.A3CC5036@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com> wrote in message
news:3766D360.A3CC5036@techie.com...
>In EBCDIC, a zero is 250 or x'F0'.

240 = 15(F)*16 + 0
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766ed7e.10131245@netnews>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net>`

```
'Twas Tue, 15 Jun 1999 21:12:55 GMT, when info@techsol.com.au (Myles
Wakeham) illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> We are a group of PC developers in Australia and have been handed an
> ASCII data file from a Cobol application running on a mainframe.  It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> algorithms around that I can use?

Overpunched Data:

In the olden days of punched cards, there were 12 holes in each column.
Ten of them were labeled 0 thru 9, the other two had various names: 12 and
11, 11 and 10, X and Y, or + and -.  Various combinations were used to
indicate alphabetic characters and special characters. 

The letters A thru I were the 12 (+) punch plus a digit 1 thru 9.  The
letters J thru R were the 11 (-) plus a digit 1 thru 9.  The letters S
thru Z were the 0 plus a digit 2 thru 9.

Digits were indicated by just punching the apropriate digit (0 thru 9).
Signed fields were indicated by overpunching the sign over the first or
last digit position.  On IBM 360 and its successors it's almost always the
last digit position.

So if the value +123 was put in a three byte field you end up with "12C".
The last position is a combination of a 3 and a +.  Here's a conversion
table:

Unsigned  Overpunched   Overpunched
 Digit       Plus          Minus

   0           {             }
   1           A             J
   2           B             K
   3           C             L
   4           D             M
   5           E             N
   6           F             O
   7           G             P
   8           H             Q
   9           I             R

These symbols would be generated in EBCDIC, but they survive translation
to ASCII, because these are all valid ASCII characters.

All the forgoing assumes that you are actually talking about overpunched
data.  If you are actually getting packed data, then you are screwed,
because it does _not_ survive EBCDIC to ASCII translation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3766fa7f.294729622@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net> <3766ed7e.10131245@netnews>`

```
On Wed, 16 Jun 1999 01:01:43 GMT, Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
wrote:

>All the forgoing assumes that you are actually talking about overpunched
>data.  If you are actually getting packed data, then you are screwed,
>because it does _not_ survive EBCDIC to ASCII translation.

Having performed this task quite a few times, I can tell you that I'd
rather have the packed fields than the overpunched.  I can do a BINARY
transfer then convert the alphanumerics to ASCII and leave the binary
alone.  The usage display signed numbers on the mainframe do not
correlate to the same values on the PC - whether converted EBCDIC to
ASCII or not.

PS - nice to see you here again Randall!
```

#### ↳ Re: Help! Question on 'Overpunching'

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1nTa3.8617$A1.810@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<A24234092E6B97F0.6836F0C7ED395A33.0209CBE91DEA2A60@lp.airnews.net>`

```
The values are 'A' thru 'I'
Here's a Table
                                -Number Value
+Number    OverPunched Value
    0                0                        0
    1                A                        J
    2                B                        K
    3                C                        L
    4                D                        M
    5                E                        N
    6                F                        O
    7                G                        P
    8                H                        Q
    9                I                          R
Myles Wakeham wrote in message ...
>We are a group of PC developers in Australia and have been handed an
>ASCII data file from a Cobol application running on a mainframe.  It
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-346fb083fd"></a>
## Thread: Carriage returns at end of records? — 5 msgs

### Carriage returns at end of records?

- **From:** quimby72@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k5o0t$ngh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all,
I have a question I was hoping someone could help me with.
The company I work for has a tape we create and send out over ftp to a
client. The file contains fixed block records created by a VS COBOL II
program on an MVS mainframe.   The records are delineated by a line feed
at the end of each record.  However, our customer wants the records to
be delineated by both a carriage return AND a line feed character, in
that order.

I've been looking at how to do this, but have become stuck.  I feel the
answer is either in a JCL statement (maybe a DSORG or something like
that) or some kind of COBOL statement. If anyone has any suggestions,
they'd be greatly appreciated!
TIA,
Rob



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Carriage returns at end of records?

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37688632.2962998@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<7k5o0t$ngh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
FTP has this built in if you use the ASCII mode.  It detects that you
are transmitting a file between systems that only use a line feed
(Unix) and systems that use a CR and LF (DOS, windows, NT).

FTP will do the necessary translation, stripping the CR when xfering
from DOS to unix, and adding it when transferring from unix to DOS.

I am assuming that your mainframe uses only the LF, and your clients
are on dos.

Hope this helps,

Paul


On Tue, 15 Jun 1999 14:30:03 GMT, quimby72@my-deja.com wrote:

>Hi all,
>I have a question I was hoping someone could help me with.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Carriage returns at end of records?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k71jl$5m2@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7k5o0t$ngh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37688632.2962998@news.enteract.com>`

```
Actually, the whole thing (original description) is "odd" because MVS very
definitely does NOT use LF, CR, or any similar facility for record
delimiters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Carriage returns at end of records?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3768E4BD.50E27A53@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7k5o0t$ngh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <37688632.2962998@news.enteract.com> <7k71jl$5m2@sjx-ixn6.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Actually, the whole thing (original description) is "odd" because MVS very
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Klein

Bill,

FTP puts in a line feed for you when you transmit (the client
FTP).  The FTP server on the other end takes linefeed and
translates it into the local format (Mac uses CR only, Unix leaves
it alone, and DOS puts in the additional CR).

Rob,

The answer is that it is the recipients job.
-  If they are on Unix then tell them to jump.  They may need to
map on the way out of the Unix server to a PC box (which is not
your problem).
-  If they are on DOS tell them to get a better FTP server.

As pointed out the ASCII option might help (it does send commands
down the line to kick start the other side).

I have FTP'ed both ways several hundred times to DOS, UNIX/LINUX,
and Mac successfully without changing our JCL.

Good luck
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Carriage returns at end of records?

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hnTa3.8620$A1.810@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<7k5o0t$ngh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
First, check your FTP settings, the COBOL program doesn't put a CR/LF at the
end
of each record unless someone specifically coded that to be MOVEd there.
The FTP
may be putting that there esp. if it's going to an NT/Win machine, as 'Text'
files usually
are LF/CR-LF delimited.

The Codes are HEX 'OA' (Dec 10) and HEX '0D' (Dec 13)
Define Special names :  C-R is  X'0A', L-F is X'0D' and move them
to a two byte struct.
05  WS-CR-LF.
      10  WS-CR        PIC X.
      10  WS-LF         PIC X.

…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

I assume you tried:
OPEN I-O MYFILE
IF MYFILE-FS = '35'
    OPEN OUTPUT MYFILE
    CLOSE MYFILE
    OPEN I-O MYFILE.

#### ↳ Re: Opening and Writing to Empty VSAM File

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3767d3c8$3$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<7k6rad$oe3$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In <7k6rad$oe3$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>, on 16 Jun 1999 at 00:32,
X@yz.com (X) said:

>Anybody know how to open and write to empty VSAM file from a batch COBOL 
>program and avoid a FILE STATUS of 35 without having to resort to loading
>the file with a dummy record?

Specify the word OPTIONAL in the file's SELECT statement. When a VSAM file
in CREATE mode (i.e. just DEFINE'd) and specified as OPTIONAL is OPEN'd
for I-O, OPEN automatically does the dummy record trick and return status
05 instead of 35.

As an alternative, you can use the INITKSDS utility found in file 183 of
the CBT tape (http://cbttape.org) which performs the dummy record trick in
a separate job step.

Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
```

Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US> writes: 
> Frank Clarke wrote:
> > It probably also explains why
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> model 50 and above.

I think the F compiler ran under model 40, in 1966.
It certainly ran with 128K main memory, and I think
even smaller memories.

>  At the time this was a *big* machine and most 360
> users were running mod 25's and 30's under DOS.

The DOS version ran on model 30, and there was even a
version that ran on the 1100 (?)

>  Heck, there were still
> a lot of 14xx machines around.  COBOL would run on all of 'em, although
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs to COBOL anyway.
```

#### ↳ Re: will cobol stick around?

- **From:** Peter Flass <Flass@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>
- **Date:** 1999-06-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.pl1
- **Message-ID:** `<376656D7.6151@Leginfo.LBDC.State.NY.US>`
- **References:** `<aDj93.10460$PN5.27309@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Ah, another old-timer.

robin wrote:
> 
> I think the F compiler ran under model 40, in 1966.
> It certainly ran with 128K main memory, and I think
> even smaller memories.

Sure, but a "typical" system around this time was around 32K.  64K would
have been a big machine. (I worked for several consultants, and got to
see a lot of installations.)
> 
> The DOS version ran on model 30, and there was even a
> version that ran on the 1100 (?)

Sure the DOS version ran, but very slooooowly.  People were complaining
about COBOL overhead.  The bigger PL/I compiler had to be broken down
into so many "phases" (overlays) that it was probably 50% or more slower
than COBOL.  

The other machine was an 1130 - a small FORTRAN-oriented machine.  They
typically came with 8K 16-bit words and a small removable disk.  
"SL/I" was an interpreter.  It parsed fast, but ran slowly.  I played
with it at one time, but it was basically a toy developed as EWS
(employee-written software).
```

=====================================
               COBOL DEVELOPER NEWS  - Volume 3,
1999

=====================================

               A newsletter for the COBOL
development community from Fujitsu Software
               Corporation.

               In this issue:

in Japan
                    - Next PowerCOBOL Boot Camp -
July

Processors

                    - COBOL + Web Support =
Opportunity for Orbis Systems

                    - Printing with Printer Escape
Sequences

               1.  NEWS
               ========

               Fujitsu COBOL V5 Announced in Japan

--------------------------------------------------
---

               Fujitsu Japan recently announced
the availability of Fujitsu COBOL V5 for the
               Japanese market,
                and we can assure you that we are
working hard to bring this release to the
               U.S. market as quickly
               as possible. Key features announced
include:

               - Multithreading support
               - Multi-instance support for
PowerCOBOL OCXs (the same PowerCOBOL OCX can be
               used more
                 than once on a form at run-time)
               - Trouble shooting information
provided on abends with COBOL and PowerCOBOL. The
               "Dr.
                 Watson"-like information includes
the program name and the location of the
               error in the program.
                 When the program has been
compiled for debug, line number information is
               provided for the location.
               - ISAPI added to the collection of
protocols supported by our Web application
               interface (whether you
                 are developing for CGI, ISAPI or
Oracle Application Server your code is the
               same)
               - Distributed object environment
(CORBA/DCOM) support
               - Object performance improvement
for Pentium machines
               - Printing performance improvement
by downloading FORM overlay patterns to a
               printer.

               You might also be interested to
know that Fujitsu Japan also announced that the
               PowerCOBOL97 series
               now has the largest share of the
COBOL market in Japan.

               Next PowerCOBOL Boot Camp - July

--------------------------------------------------

               The PowerCOBOL Boot Camp* continues
to garner rave reviews. Bill Leroy, Project
               Leader at Investors
               Heritage Life Insurance Company,
had this to say:

               "The biggest advantage at the
PowerCOBOL Boot Camp was meeting other users and

               other users. I certainly appreciate
knowing that when I have a problem I can

help I've received from the Fujitsu team

               There are still seats available in
the July Boot Camp (July 12-15, 1999). If you

               early-bird course fee is only
$1,500. If you enroll by July 1, 1999, the course

$2,000.

               *PowerCOBOL Boot Camp is a 4-day
course designed to help you get the most out of
               Fujitsu PowerCOBOL
               and Fujitsu COBOL Version 4. This
hands-on class will focus on GUI programming
               methods, creating ActiveX
               controls, accessing ODBC databases
as well as a general introduction to Fujitsu
               COBOL.

               Visit our website at
http://www.adtools.com/bootcamp/index.htm to
enroll or call
               (800) 545-6774.

               Beta Program for Dialect Processors

-------------------------------------------------

               We are enhancing our line of COBOL
Dialect Converters with a series of matching
               Dialect Processors. The Dialect
               Converters do a one-time conversion
of your COBOL code from another dialect to
               Fujitsu COBOL. The Dialect
               Processors integrate with the
Fujitsu COBOL compiler so that you can maintain
               your sources in their current
(non-Fujitsu)
               dialect yet compile them using
Fujitsu COBOL. This can be useful if you are
               converting large suites of programs
to Fujitsu
               COBOL but you know that you must
maintain and execute the system in the current
               dialect for at least the duration
of
               the conversion project.

               Before going on full release, the
Dialect Processors will be available through a
               beta program. If you are interested
in
               testing a dialect processor for:

               - Micro Focus COBOL
               - Microsoft COBOL
               - IBM COBOL dialects

               Please contact us at
cobol@adtools.com.

               Note: Only customers with annual
maintenance agreements are eligible to
               participate in our beta programs.


               2.  USER STORIES
               ===============

               COBOL + Web Support = Opportunity
for Orbis Systems

--------------------------------------------------
--------------------------

               Over the last 16 years, Orbis
Systems, a consulting company based in the Chicago
               area, has been developing
               applications, mostly in COBOL, for
the medical industry. For many companies this
               might have been how the story
               would have continued, but Randy
Kriz, the company's entrepreneurial president,
               saw a big opportunity when he
               found out about Fujitsu COBOL's Web
support. Kriz said, "We used to focus only
               on the medical industry, but
               now we are offering all kinds of
Internet applications."

               Kriz enthusiastically describes one
of the applications that has been
               implemented using Fujitsu COBOL
products.

               Dental Benefits System Saves Time

               "One of our clients, Affiliated
Network Services, is having a lot of success
               with the ANS-LINK system we helped
               them develop. You have probably
observed the challenges dental offices go
               through in verifying benefits for
different
               patients. The office often has to
make several telephone calls to get through to
               the insurer and exchange faxes
before
               being able to determine the
appropriate benefits. For participating insurance
               companies, ANS-LINK provides
offices with
               the ability to check this
information over the Web, 24 hours a day, 7 days a
               week. This offers enormous
timesavings for
               both offices and providers.

               "A couple of years ago I didn't
think we would be able to provide this sort of
               application. However, we got there
very
               quickly by using Fujitsu COBOL and
the COBOL CGI support. Also the prototype
               HTML feature allowed us to break up
               pages into customer-specific and
generic pieces - this makes it easy for us to
               tailor the application for our
different customers.

               "The ANS-LINK system runs on a
dedicated server installed at a local ISP. It's
               running IIS 4.0 on Windows NT. ANS
               has another NT machine in their
office that they are able to use for testing. We
               have provided them with maintenance
               utilities written in PowerCOBOL to
run on this machine."

               The Fujitsu COBOL Experience

               Kriz concludes, "We assessed a
couple of COBOL vendors when we realized that
               there were COBOL options for Web
               applications. Fujitsu won
hands-down when it came to the costs of
distributing
               the applications. On top of this,
we've
               found that the Fujitsu products are
well designed and reliable, with just the
               add-on features we need. The
Debugger
               has been excellent for working with
Internet applications - I haven't had a
               problem that I couldn't debug.

               "As you can see the Web support in
Fujitsu COBOL has opened doors for us and is
               helping us position our business
for the
               21st Century. Feel free to talk to
us about getting Fujitsu COBOL applications
               up on Web servers!"

               Thank you, Randy Kriz, for sharing
your story with COBOL Developer News.

               The full text of the story,
including descriptions of another application and
               other tools used, can be found at

http://www.adtools.com/customerstories/index.htm.

               3. HINT AND TIPS
               ==============

               Printing with Printer Escape
Sequences

--------------------------------------------------
---

               Some customers, particularly those
coming from other COBOL vendors, have
               developed applications that achieve
particular
               printing effects by writing printer
escape sequences to their files. Fujitsu
               COBOL's print files are directed
through the Windows
               printer drivers, so escape
sequences do not work.

               In the long term we would recommend
getting away from using printer escape
               sequences, as they are difficult to
code and
               tie applications to particular
printers. PowerFORM is probably a much better
               solution for the long term.

               However, if you are currently using
printer escape sequences, we may have a
               solution for you. We have created a
program
               that can take text files with
embedded escape sequences and use Windows system
               calls to pass these files directly
to the
               printer. If you can create the text
files, we can help you print them!

               If you are interested in seeing if
this program can work for you contact us at
               support@adtools.com.


--------------------------------------------------
----------------------------------------

because you're a user of one of

another expressed an interest in

support announcements. You

http://www.adtools.com/news/unsubscribe.htm

## Thread: eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error" — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error"

- **From:** bfetml@aol.com (BFETML)
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
hi,
I am trying to do some file processing using ezt+ on mvs and have a few
questions.

1) How can I create an abend with ezt+.

2) I am getting a "file not referenced" error.   I am using 3 files and 1
output file, and disabling auto input.  I get the error at "GET".

FILE PARMIN
CYCLE     1  2  A

WS-CYCLE   S  2  A

FILEIN1  
RECIN1    1  34  A

FILEIN2
RECIN2     1 34  A

JOB INPUT NULL
  GET PARMIN
  WS-CYCLE = CYCLE

  ETC...............


thanks in advance
eric
```

#### ↳ Re: eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error"

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kaf02$ef5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com>,
  bfetml@aol.com (BFETML) wrote:
> hi,
> I am trying to do some file processing using ezt+ on mvs and have a
few
> questions.
>
> 1) How can I create an abend with ezt+.
>
> 2) I am getting a "file not referenced" error.   I am using 3 files
and 1
> output file, and disabling auto input.  I get the error at "GET".
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Eric,

I'm missing your problem here. What was the exact error-message? I
cutted and pasted your piece of code into my host window and tried it.
First, a syntax error: how about the keyword FILE in front of your
FILEIN1 and FILEIN2 ;-)?
The DISPLAY of WS-CYCLE was OK, so I assume you either got your error
for another file which was not read at this moment, or you forgot to
code DD PARMIN in your JCL.

To your first question: AFAIK, there is no other 'builtin' abnormal end
in E+ than STOP EXECUTE which stops all activities of the program. But
you get nor an ABEND nor a CONDITION CODE. So, if you really want an
ABEND, you can:

- force a system abend like 0CA (zerodivide)
- CALL your own abend routine written in ASSEMBLER or COBOL
- CALL the COBOL abend routine ILBOABN0 using a WS-CODE W 2 B,
  indicating the user-abend-code

In either case, you should DISPLAY some lines of dignostic messages and
a blank line before you abend since the last line of your DISPLAY may be
lost in case of an abend.

If you CALL ILBOABN0 directly, you have to

- code PARM ENVIRONMENT(COBOL) in your program
- and include COBOL or LE runtime in your STEPLIB or LINKLIST
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error"

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3768F52F.66702272@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com> <7kaf02$ef5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> > 1) How can I create an abend with ezt+.

> To your first question: AFAIK, there is no other 'builtin' abnormal end
> in E+ than STOP EXECUTE which stops all activities of the program. But
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   indicating the user-abend-code

We have a COBOL program which does a divide by zero.  We use JCL to run
or bypass this abort step depending on condition codes from the
EasyTrieve step(s).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error"

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kb3bs$lie$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com> <7kaf02$ef5$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3768F52F.66702272@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <3768F52F.66702272@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
> > > 1) How can I create an abend with ezt+.
>
> > To your first question: AFAIK, there is no other 'builtin' abnormal
end
> > in E+ than STOP EXECUTE which stops all activities of the program.
But
> > you get nor an ABEND nor a CONDITION CODE. So, if you really want an
> > ABEND, you can:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> We have a COBOL program which does a divide by zero.  We use JCL to
run
> or bypass this abort step depending on condition codes from the
> EasyTrieve step(s).
>

Oh yes, I see:

RETURN-CODE = 16
STOP EXECUTE

And then, in the JCL:
// IF RC = THEN
//S2 EXEC ABENDPGM
//  ENDIF

That works for shure, but no abend-handling in the EASY-step can be
done: What about the files (DISP=(NEW,CATLG,DELETE)) and the databases?
Every database must be roll-backed (IMS ROLB call, DB2 EXEC SQL
ROLLBACK, IDMS whatever). In these cases, an abend directly from the
EASY-program via a CALL could be a better (foolproofer) solution ;-)
```

#### ↳ Re: eztrieve 2Questions;create an abend and "file not referenced error"

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376AE39C.6715@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<19990616002218.28988.00000047@ng-cj1.aol.com>`

```
BFETML wrote:
> 
> hi,
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> eric

We abend our eztrieve programs by calling an assembler abend module from
within the eztrieve.  But as someone else mentioned, this also results
in loosing the last DISPLAY line, so we issue a "DISPLAY ' '" to force
the significant DISPLAY to not get lost.

The cause of your invalid file reference depends upon seeing the rest of
your procedure division.  It could be caused by reading one of your
input datasets after EOF has already been reached.  It can also be
caused by referencing data inside of a input record area that has not
yet had a record read into it.

Pete
```

## Thread: Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value? — 5 msgs

### Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value?

- **From:** Calvin <calvin_ryo@hongkong.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37679281.7F19@hongkong.com>`

```
Hello,

As title, are there any cobol function allow me to change a hexadecimal 
value to a ASCII character?

Calvin.
```

#### ↳ Re: Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3768E7F3.FB98D02F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<37679281.7F19@hongkong.com>`

```
Calvin wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calvin.


Look at the there is FUNCTION CHAR intrinsic function.  Convert
the string hex digits to a number is a minor problem.  Hint search
might be good here!

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value?

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37693917.4E1B@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<37679281.7F19@hongkong.com>`

```
Calvin wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calvin.
If you need a constant value, you can do with X"<hex-number>"
Example:
01 w-Char PIC X.
   88 w-Enter VALUE X"0D".
```

#### ↳ Re: Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kc26u$8vj@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37679281.7F19@hongkong.com>`

```
(Sorry for the delay in replying - I was hoping someone else was going to
give you the easy way.)

*ASSUMING* you are using a "current" compiler (with Intrinsic Functions) and
are running in an ASCII environment (so characters are stored in ASCII),
then you can use:

  * convert hexadecimal to decimal - which is "simple arithmetic" and then

 Move Function Char (decimal-ordinal-position-in-collating-seq) to
Character-field

(Remember, that the CHAR function starts at "1" while ascii starts at "0" -
which is why you need to add 1 in figuring out the place in the collating
sequence.)

(FYI, the opposite of the CHAR function is the ORD function - which can be
used to convert a character to an ASCII value.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Can i generate a ASCII character from a hexadecimal value?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uY93.2513$Tx1.38116@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<37679281.7F19@hongkong.com>`

```
Um, it's either a character or a value, so one does not convert to the
other.

Or, do you mean, "find the numeric value of  the two-character string '1F'
if the radix (base) is 16?"

If so, you just divide the digits by 16^n (n= digit position from the right,
0-based), acummulate and subtract the remainders, etc just as in any other
base conversion.
```

- **From:** astraltrawlers@ns.sympatico.ca (Peter Gerard)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3769290a.79298949@news1.ns.sympatico.ca>`

```
Is there any sites out there that would have this routine coded and
available for download ?  I am not just looking for the $100.00 dollar
example, but the code to turn any numeric value into a written out
dollar value.....for printing on cheques.

Any help would be greatly appreciated!!!
```

#### ↳ Re: code to turn $100.00 into "one hundred dollars"

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ouba3.2082$LI5.201261@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<3769290a.79298949@news1.ns.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Gerard wrote in message <3769290a.79298949@news1.ns.sympatico.ca>...
>Is there any sites out there that would have this routine coded and
>available for download ?  I am not just looking for the $100.00 dollar
>example, but the code to turn any numeric value into a written out
>dollar value.....for printing on cheques.

I did a little work on something to demo that a few years ago--this might be
enough to get you started.  You might need to change a thing or two in case
your compiler or run unit doesn't like zero subscripts.
       77  PNTR                   PIC S9(4) COMP-1.
       01  ACC-NUM                PIC 9(6)V99.
       01  ACC-AREA REDEFINES ACC-NUM.
           05  AA-THOU            PIC XXX.
           05  AA-HUNS            PIC XXX.
           05  AA-CENTS           PIC XX.
       01  SET-OF-THREE.
         05  HUNS                 PIC 9.
         05  TENS                 PIC 9.
         05  UNITS                PIC 9.
       01  TARGET-AREA            PIC X(80).
       01  FILLER REDEFINES TARGET-AREA.
         02  TA-COL OCCURS 80     PIC X.
       01  TABLES.
         05  UNIT-TAB.
           10  FILLER             PIC X(6) VALUE "ONE~".
<snip for brevity>
           10  FILLER             PIC X(6) VALUE "NINE~".
         05  FILLER REDEFINES UNIT-TAB.
           10  DIGIT OCCURS 9     PIC X(6).
         05  TENS-TAB.
           10  FILLER             PIC X(8) VALUE "UNREF".
           10  FILLER             PIC X(8) VALUE "TWENTY~".
<ibid>
           10  FILLER             PIC X(8) VALUE "NINETY~".
         05  FILLER REDEFINES TENS-TAB.
           10  A-TEN OCCURS 9     PIC X(8).
         05  FILLER               PIC X(10) VALUE "TEN~".
         05  TEEN-TAB.
           10  FILLER             PIC X(10) VALUE "ELEVEN~".
<ibid>
           10  FILLER             PIC X(10) VALUE "NINETEEN~".
         05  FILLER REDEFINES TEEN-TAB.
           10  A-TEEN OCCURS 9    PIC X(10).
-----------------------
           MOVE SPACE TO ACC-AREA.
           ACCEPT ACC-NUM LINE 3 POSITION 1 SIZE 8 CONVERT.
           IF ACC-NUM NOT NUMERIC
             GO TO EXIT-IT.
           IF ACC-NUM = 0
             GO TO EXIT-IT.
           MOVE 6 TO PNTR.
           MOVE "******" TO TARGET-AREA.
           IF ZERO = AA-THOU AND AA-HUNS
             MOVE "*****ZERO" TO TARGET-AREA
             MOVE 11 TO PNTR
             GO TO DISP-CENTS.
           IF AA-THOU > ZERO
             MOVE AA-THOU TO SET-OF-THREE
             PERFORM SPELL THRU SPELL-EXIT
             STRING " THOUSAND" DELIMITED BY SIZE
                   INTO TARGET-AREA POINTER PNTR
             IF AA-HUNS > ZERO
               MOVE "," TO TA-COL (PNTR)
               ADD 2 TO PNTR
             ELSE
               ADD 1 TO PNTR.
           IF AA-HUNS > ZERO
             MOVE AA-HUNS TO SET-OF-THREE
             PERFORM SPELL THRU SPELL-EXIT
             ADD 1 TO PNTR.
         DISP-CENTS.
           IF AA-CENTS = "00"
             MOVE "NO" TO AA-CENTS.
           STRING "& " AA-CENTS "/100*********" DELIMITED BY SIZE
                 INTO TARGET-AREA POINTER PNTR.
           DISPLAY TARGET-AREA LINE 5 POSITION 1 SIZE 79.
----------
         SPELL.
           IF HUNS > ZERO
             STRING DIGIT (HUNS) " HUNDRED " DELIMITED BY "~"
                   INTO TARGET-AREA POINTER PNTR.
           IF TENS = 1
             STRING A-TEEN (UNITS) DELIMITED BY "~"
                   INTO TARGET-AREA POINTER PNTR
             GO TO SPELL-EXIT.
           IF TENS > 1
             STRING A-TEN (TENS) DELIMITED BY "~"
                   INTO TARGET-AREA POINTER PNTR
             IF UNITS > ZERO
               MOVE "-" TO TA-COL (PNTR)
               ADD 1 TO PNTR.
           IF UNITS > ZERO
             STRING DIGIT (UNITS) DELIMITED BY "~" INTO TARGET-AREA
                   POINTER PNTR.
         SPELL-EXIT.
           EXIT.
```

#### ↳ Re: code to turn $100.00 into "one hundred dollars"

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t5rb3.459$112.60@news1.mco>`
- **References:** `<3769290a.79298949@news1.ns.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Gerard wrote:
>Is there any sites out there that would have this routine coded and
>available for download ?  I am not just looking for the $100.00 dollar
>example, but the code to turn any numeric value into a written out
>dollar value.....for printing on cheques.

Download MONEY.ZIP from my web page below.  It would convert, for example,
123.45 into:

       **ONE HUNDRED TWENTY THREE DOLLARS AND FORTY FIVE CENTS**

or 123,456,789,012,345.67 into:

 **ONE HUNDRED TWENTY THREE TRILLION FOUR HUNDRED FIFTY SIX BILLION**
  **SEVEN HUNDRED EIGHTY NINE MILLION TWELVE THOUSAND THREE HUNDRED**
             **FORTY FIVE DOLLARS AND SIXTY SEVEN CENTS**

The routine places the text lines centered into a multi-line buffer.
you can easily change the number and size of the lines to fit your
checks.  The asterisks are for check protection, of course.  Changing
the names of three digit groups (e.g. trillion, billion) for U.K.
requirements is trivial.
```

## Thread: Cobol on Linux? — 4 msgs

### Cobol on Linux?

- **From:** Maktos <maktos@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kbfnu$r4c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Has anyone had any success using a cobol compiler
with Linux?

I'm trying to recompile a program (that was written
for Sco 5 Unix) on Red Hat Linux (6.0 release).

We are currently using Micro Focus Cobol v4.1
Micro Focus doesn't have a Linux version, so we're
unable to recompile our legacy code for Linux.

I've heard about several other cobol compilers -

Realia
Accura
Rand-McFarland (RM Cobol)

Will one of these work on Linux?


If anyone has any ideas where I might look, or has
any information on Cobol under Linux, please let me
know!

Thank you for your time,


Matt McDevitt
maktos@maktos.com



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on Linux?

- **From:** areymond@csi.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3769f351.2050647@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<7kbfnu$r4c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>Has anyone had any success using a cobol compiler
>with Linux?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have been told by some MF internals that MF is working on a Linux
version of its MF suite for Unix. No date of issue has been planed to
the moment.

You can run the binary of your code under Linux with the IBCS
compatibility option. I think you have to relink two SCO libraries
(tcp/ip? I can't remember) under Linux in order to make it run
properly.

Anyway, I didn't try the procedure myself, but I have been confirmed
that it works. Of course, without support from MF...

Faithfully

Alain Reymond
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol on Linux?

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770DD70.753EE5CA@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7kbfnu$r4c$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dear Matt McDevitt,
Synkronix PERCobol is an excellent COBOL compiler for the Linux.
PERCobol also is available for SCO Unix Ware 7 and most other platforms.
Synkronix does not charge a runtime license.

PERCobol enables users  to create traditional COBOL applications, and
also enables users to create server side (servlets), client side
(applets) and also to encapsulate COBOL business logic as COBOL Beans.
PERCobol  supports CICS Clients, IMS Clients, MF COBOL syntax (syntax
subset), SSL and extended graphical syntax.  Refer to
http://www.synkronix.com/sync_perc1.html

Synkronix also has a graphical application painter called BlueJ(tm) that
will create GUIs in both COBOL and Java. BlueJ contains a interaction
wizard that will connect objects and actions.  You can deploy the result
as an application or applet.   Refer to
http://www.synkronix.com/bluej.html

Charles Townsend
Synkronix, Inc.
925 467-1598
ctowns@synkronix.com
http://www.synkronix.com

Maktos wrote:

> Has anyone had any success using a cobol compiler
> with Linux?
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Compiler Source Available

- **From:** Rildo Pragana <rpragana@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kj5sn$khn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.LNX.3.96.990617150317.13835A-100000@andy.ibi.co.za> <376AB891.54B5FB6D@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Hi fellows,

I'm looking for a Cobol expert to help us port and improve our Cobol to
Linux. It was written for MsDos, but we (myself and Alan Cox) have
already changed the compiler for Linux, but we still have many problems
to overcome. We need people for testing, documenting, even coding ;)

For now it's outputting x86 assembly language, but we plan to make it
generate plain C. We need also to implement the runtime library (all C,
partially available) and would like to get ideas about the formats for
data files the community is interested.
We look also for web and mailing list managers/implementors.

You may get the (updated to Linux) pre-alpha compiler at my homepage:

http://members.xoom.com/rpragana/

Please answer to my e-mail about what you can do to help us.

regards,
Rildo


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

## Thread: Cobol - A/P's and Developers — 2 msgs

### Cobol - A/P's and Developers

- **From:** Element Limited <resume-cv@element-limited.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** euro.jobs,uk.jobs,uk.jobs.offered,comp.lang.cobol,comp.databases.ibm-db2
- **Message-ID:** `<e2BgImJu#GA.201@ntdwwaaw.compuserve.com>`

Cobol - A/P's and Developers.

<a id="t-1560610497"></a>
## Thread: Problem with EXEC CICS LINK — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Problem with EXEC CICS LINK

- **From:** Alex Weirig <aweirig@vo.lu>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3768A37B.BB35821D@vo.lu>`

```
Hi,

I have a problem LINKING together 2 COBOL/CICS programs. My first
program has to pass A LOT of parameters to some other programs and I
would like to do this using the COMMAREA and passing the parameters by
pointer not by value.

So this is what I have:

01 PARAM1.  about 22k
  05 ...
  05 ...
etc

02 PARAM2. about 15k
  05 ...
  05 ...
etc

01 DFHCOMMAREA.
  05 PTR1 USAGE IS POINTER.
  05 PTR2 USAGE IS POINTER.

My question is: How do I initialize PTR1 and PTR2 to point to PARAM1 and
PARAM2 respectively? And need PARAM1 and PARAM2 to be defined in the
LINKAGE SECTION or in the WORKING STORAGE SECTION?

I'm not a specialist in COBOL/CICS (rather in C or C++) therefore I
chose this kind of solution. Other people might have chosen CICS QUEUEs
but I am afraid it might take me several months to get that working ...

If anybody could help me I would be REALLY REALLY pleased. Thanks a lot,

Alex
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with EXEC CICS LINK

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3768B1DA.2BE608B4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3768A37B.BB35821D@vo.lu>`

```
Alex Weirig wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> LINKAGE SECTION or in the WORKING STORAGE SECTION?

Assuming IBM COBOL II or higher (ANSI85):

SET PTR1 TO ADDRESS OF PARAM1
SET PTR2 TO ADDRESS OF PARAM2
EXEC CICS LINK   
    PROGRAM('PROGRAM2')
    COMMAREA(COMMAREA-FOR-PROGRAM2)
    RESP(MY-EIB-RESPONSE)
END-EXEC

It's probably easier for PARAM1 and PARAM2 to reside in the calling
program's working-storage, but you should be okay with PARAM1 and PARAM2
in the calling program's linkage section, provided you've already
established addressability to them.

In the called (or linked-to) program PARAM1 and PARAM2 must be defined
in your linkage section.  They can have different names from the calling
program, of course.  You would then establish addressability to them as
follows:

SET ADDRESS OF PARAM1 TO PTR1
SET ADDRESS OF PARAM2 TO PTR2

If you don't have COBOL II or higher, then you have to do service
reloads, and I'm forgetting how to do that.  If you need that, then you
must be very careful about the order of level 01 items in your linkage
section, your BLL cell pointers must reside in linkage, and you will
need multiple pointers for any 01 area that exceeds 4K bytes (assuming
you're talking about CICS in a system 370 environment - I wouldn't know
about other platforms).  For example, with OS/VS COBOL you would need
SIX BLL cell pointers for PARAM1, since it's 22k bytes long.  Since your
brief example includes USAGE POINTER I'm assuming COBOL II or higher. 
It's much easier.

> 
> I'm not a specialist in COBOL/CICS (rather in C or C++) therefore I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alex

Passing around large COMMAREA's can create performance problems, but
perhaps that was more of a problem in older releases of CICS.  It =is=
more efficient to pass addresses.
Lots of ways to do that.  You can stuff them into your TWA and do EXEC
CICS ADDRESS TWA(MY-TWA-POINTER) END-EXEC.  Then establish
addressability to your TWA and pull out all the pointers you need.  You
could certainly pass addresses through temp storage queues, but then all
programs would have to use the same hard-coded queue name as a key.

I've done a lot of CICS programming with conversational tasks and no BMS
maps.  We generally pass dozens of pointers through the TWA.

I hope that helps.

Regards,
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with EXEC CICS LINK

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kah15$f28$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3768A37B.BB35821D@vo.lu>`

```
In article <3768A37B.BB35821D@vo.lu>,
  Alex Weirig <aweirig@vo.lu> wrote:
> Hi,
>
> I have a problem LINKING together 2 COBOL/CICS programs. My first

Don't LINK together the two programs if you 'EXEC CICS LINK' the
submodule! You just link the two programs separately to executing
programs.
You could CALL the subprogram as well, passing as many parameters as you
like (but the first two must be DFEIBLK and DFHCOMMAREA if the called
subprogram contains any 'EXEC CICS' command. In this case, the called
subprogram may not end with 'EXEC CICS RETURN' but rather with a
'GOBACK' COBOL verb.

> program has to pass A LOT of parameters to some other programs and I
> would like to do this using the COMMAREA and passing the parameters by
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> My question is: How do I initialize PTR1 and PTR2 to point to PARAM1
and
> PARAM2 respectively? And need PARAM1 and PARAM2 to be defined in the
> LINKAGE SECTION or in the WORKING STORAGE SECTION?
>
> I'm not a specialist in COBOL/CICS (rather in C or C++) therefore I
> chose this kind of solution. Other people might have chosen CICS
QUEUEs
> but I am afraid it might take me several months to get that working
...
>
> If anybody could help me I would be REALLY REALLY pleased. Thanks a
lot,
>
> Alex
>
>

OK, now to the POINTER usage in IBM COBOL: In the calling main program,
you have to define the parameters in the WORKING-STORAGE (comparable to
static or automatic in C language) since LINKAGE definitions don't
define storage (like typedef strukt in C).
Now, the problem is, that you cannot SET a pointer to the ADDRESS OF a
WORKIN-STORAGE field, only to a LINKAGE field. So, you need another
submodule which gets the WS field (in its LINKAGE SECTION) and can SET a
pointer to the ADDRESS OF that field. This little submodule can be a
nested program in the main-module.
Let's look at a sample:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  main-prog.
...
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 WS-PARAM-1 PIC X(22000).
01 PTR1 USAGE IS POINTER.
...
    CALL 'ADDR' USING WS-PARAM-1, PTR1
...
IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  ADDR.
DATA DIVISON.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 PARAM-1 PIC X.
01 PARAM-2 USAGE IS POINTER.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARAM-1, PARAM-2.
    SET PARAM-2 TO ADDRESS OF PARAM-1
GOBACK.
END PROGRAM ADDR.
END PROGRAM main-prog.

Now, you can MOVE your pointer to whatever area you want and CALL or
EXEC CICS LINK your module with that area.

And now to the sub-module. This one has to declare both the pointer it
gets from the main program and the real data in its LINKAGE SECTION. It
gets the pointer via the USING statement of its PROCEDURE DIVISION and
the has to SET ADDRESS OF the real data to the pointer:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.  sub-module.
...
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 DFHCOMMAREA.
   05 PTR1 USAGE IS POINTER.
   ....
01 PARAM-1 PIC X(22000).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING DFHEIBLK, DFHCOMMAREA.
    SET ADDRESS OF PARAM-1 TO PTR1 OF DFHCOMMAREA.
....

Cheers
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with EXEC CICS LINK

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-06-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2DD2BD7E91DA3E83.3C4406FE24649A29.666274881CC955EC@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<3768A37B.BB35821D@vo.lu>`

```
On Thu, 17 Jun 1999 09:27:55 +0200, Alex Weirig <aweirig@vo.lu>
enlightened us:

>Hi,
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>Alex

The two posts following mine (or maybe before depending on how your
newsreader sorts messges) give you good explanations for doing what
you want.  What I am wondering is why you want to pass fields that are
pointers to your parameters rather then the parameters themselves?
The thing to remember is the actual data is not passed to the called
or linked to program.  Only the address of the data is passed.  The
actual storage area is shared between the two (or more) programs.  No
extra storage is allocated or used.  

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

         A Sarcastic remark to get you through the day:
            
         If I throw a stick, will you leave?


 Steve
```

<a id="t-927a8f8b1c"></a>
## Thread: File renaming from VA COBOL on Win NT 4 — 3 msgs

### File renaming from VA COBOL on Win NT 4

- **From:** "Gregory Bruno" <gbruno@csc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kdr1h$g0v@topgun.es.dupont.com>`

```
I'm new to PC-based COBOL and I have a need to rename a file while my
program is running.  It will be something like changing LIST.DAT to
LIST.OLD.

What would be the best way of doing that in VisualAge COBOL on an Intel box
running Windows NT 4?  Is there a way to shell out to NT and execute
commands?

Greg
```

#### ↳ Re: File renaming from VA COBOL on Win NT 4

- **From:** Gary Mazo <mazo@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376FFBCE.C5ABC80C@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7kdr1h$g0v@topgun.es.dupont.com>`

```


Gregory Bruno wrote:

> I'm new to PC-based COBOL and I have a need to rename a file while my
> program is running.  It will be something like changing LIST.DAT to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Greg

Greg,

For this particular purpose a much faster way would be to call a Win32 API,
MoveFile.  Here is a sample (VisualAge COBOL for Windows):

CBL pgmname(lm)
       Identification Division.
       Program-id "fubar".
       Data Division.
       Local-storage Section.
       01 fu  pic x(12) value z"c:\list.dat".
       01 bar pic x(12) value z"c:\list.old".
       01 rc  pic 9(9)  comp-5.

       Procedure Division.
           Call "MoveFileA" Using fu bar Returning rc.
           If rc Is Not Zero Then
               Display "success"
           Else
               Display "failed"
           End-If.
       End Program "fubar".

Hope this helps.

Gary Mazo
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File renaming from VA COBOL on Win NT 4

- **From:** "Gregory Bruno" <gbruno@csc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7krh0g$b6i@topgun.es.dupont.com>`
- **References:** `<7kdr1h$g0v@topgun.es.dupont.com> <376FFBCE.C5ABC80C@ibm.net>`

```
Gary Mazo wrote in message <376FFBCE.C5ABC80C@ibm.net>...
>Greg,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Gary Mazo


Thanks Gary.  It absolutely did help.

Greg
```

## Thread: MicroFocus PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT Runtime Abort — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MicroFocus PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT Runtime Abort

- **From:** dblaze@merchants-fla.com (Doug Blaze)
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376a4fcf.4256059@news.icix.net>`

```
Hello all,

I'm running MF 4.0.32 and using the PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT to set the
default printer.  I have the name of the printer in an INI file.  I'm
checking the return code on the call to detect if there is a problem.
However, if the user changes printers and the name in the INI file no
longer exists, the program aborts with: 

error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
114 Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)

In other words, I don't a chance to detect the problem before the
program aborts.  Is there a compiler option or something else I need
to specify in order to trap runtime errors so I can deal with them
programmatically?

Thanks,
Doug Blaze
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT Runtime Abort

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kdt39$9f1$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<376a4fcf.4256059@news.icix.net>`

```
Hi Doug,

There is a Win32 api call "EnumPrinters" that will return details on the
printers defined to the system. You check the INI file definitions against
the details from the API call and deal with it according.

You could also look at CBL_ERROR_PROC which allows you to trap the RTS
Errors.

Hope this helps.
David.


Doug Blaze wrote in message <376a4fcf.4256059@news.icix.net>...
>Hello all,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>Doug Blaze
```

#### ↳ Re: MicroFocus PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT Runtime Abort

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<376a4fcf.4256059@news.icix.net>`

```
Doug,

You are missing the entry point in working storage that is necessary 
along with the SET to set the addres of "mfwinp32" (that might be 
wrong, but it's cose) to that entry point.  Get those in there and 
it'll work.  Read PRINT.DOC in the docs directory and it'll explain 
it.

Hello All,

Sorry to be so late posting the code for my problem, but fires will
burn!

Here's the code:

    move spaces to prt-printer-name.
    String IF-PrinterNameValue delimited by "  ", x"00"
        delimited by size into prt-printer-name.
    Call "PC_WIN_SET_PDEFAULT" using
        by reference prt-printer-name
        returning return-code
    End-Call.
    If return-code not = 0
        Perform Printer-Error
        go to print-reports-exit
    End-If.

I'm getting the 114 RTS abort and never getting a chance to check the
return-code value.  If you want to try to replicate the situation,
provide a name in 'prt-printer-name' that doesn't exist on your system
(ie:  'My Printer').  Then try the same call with a valid printer name
(ie: the EXACT name that appears when the printer dialog is invoked in
Windows).  It will work. I don't want the program to abort with the
RTS but allow me to handle my error routine.  I have to assume I don't
have a compiler option/directive set that would allow me to do this.

Thanks,
Doug Blaze

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java for Cobol Programmers

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770EE4F.500D3328@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<376AA0C1.3DFF83DC@newagenetworkny.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dhLHcd2zOqNU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <HYJa3.2834$w61.694@stones>`

```
PERCobol is an excellent COBOL compiler(might be some prejudice).  PERCobol is
a standard compliant COBOL compiler and also supports many of the popular COBOL
dialect extensions from IBM, HP, Wang, MF and others. Using PERCobol you can
create CICS clients, IMS Clients, use MQSeries, access SQL (DB2, Oracle and
others),    See: http://www.synkronix.com/sync_prod.html

PERCobol enables users to create applications, applets, servlets and Java
Beans. You can also imbed Java Beans within your COBOL application as well.
PERCobol is a multi-threaded COBOL and with it you can create powerful server
side applications. PERCobol GUI extensions support building platform
independent GUIs.

Synkronix also has created BlueJ(tm) a graphical application painter for COBOL
and Java.  BlueJ was demonstrated last week at JavaOne in San Francisco where
we were connecting active Java elements to create a COBOL program.

Charles Townsend
Synkronix, Inc.
ctowns@synkronix.com
http://www.synkroinix.com

> Thane Hubbell <redsky@ibm.net> wrote in message
> news:Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dhLHcd2zOqNU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Simon Hart
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java for Cobol Programmers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "M.Kobobel" <classicmek@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktitk$mgj$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<376AA0C1.3DFF83DC@newagenetworkny.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dhLHcd2zOqNU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com> <HYJa3.2834$w61.694@stones> <3770EE4F.500D3328@ix.netcom.com>`

```
>>PERCobol is an excellent COBOL compiler(might be some
prejudice).  PERCobol is
a standard compliant COBOL compiler and also supports many
of the popular COBOL
dialect extensions <<

Charles is the president of the company that makes it.  I'm
not affiliated in any way.  PERCobol IS an excellent
product, and combined with the new BlueJ it is even better.
I'm using it with SQL, applets, servlets, and applications,
but none of the vendor specific extensions.  The compiler is
great.

There is one caveat though.  JAVA is not quite mature.  If
you're doing it on your own, you're going to learn a lot
about JAVA if you want to take advantage of all the
features.  Setting up the environment for JAVA development
and run-time and keeping it stable takes some finagling if
you want to use all of the cool features.  JAVA as delivered
is not a complete product no matter who you get it from - at
least not yet.  The best thing about it is that it is
run-time compatible (no recompiling) across most
environments - it uses JAVA routines instead of OS calls. If
you don't use vendor specific extensions, you can distribute
your executables anywhere there is a JAVA virtual machine.
Because the JAVA virtual machines are not yet fully
developed for most environments, it is slower than
traditional compiled programs at run time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java for Cobol Programmers

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770E9CC.B2FF3781@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<376AA0C1.3DFF83DC@newagenetworkny.com> <Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dhLHcd2zOqNU@Dwight_Miller.iix.com>`

```
PERCobol allows the user to continue to write in COBOL and deploy in the Java
environment.  Using PERCobol you can create a single COBOL application and
have it run anywhere that Java exists. PERCobol can create powerful server
side applications or Servlets, and you can build client side applications or
applets.   In addition you can imbed Java beans within a COBOL application or
you can create a COBOL Bean (Java Bean) which can be imbedded in a Java
application.   See http://www.synkronix.com/sync_prod.html

What better way for COBOL programmers to build Java applications....

PERCobol is currently being used in government agencies and businesses around
the world.  Versions of PERCobol exist on most platforms including the
mainframe (OS/390).

Charles Townsend
Synkronix, Inc.
http://www.synkronix.com

old Dialog which MF used to market and then withdrew a few years ago.

1) I am wondering if anyone out there is using the new Dialog.

2) What are some of the pros and cons using MF GUI/OO modules (awfully
cumbersome) vs. using Dialog.

3) We are wondering if we should jump ahead and use NetExpress web 100% for
GUI/OO  - pros and cons?

TIA

screen section (allowing users to take their green screen anywhere) or build a
platform independent GUI.  PERCobol supports building IMS Clients, CICS Clients,
using MQSeries, WebNFS (remote data access), servlets, applets, COBOL Beans (Java
Beans out of COBOL).

or Java.

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress Dialog vs. OO GUI

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376AC775.FD16D566@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7kdotv$k6p$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Young Kim wrote:
> 
> We are evaluating NetExpress and have noticed that Dialog is basically the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA

Having gone straight from DOS to Visoc(which didn't have Dialog System) 
I committed to OO/GUI. Now that I have NetExpress V 3 - it does have
Dialog System.

Purely a guesstimate - but probably 90% of N/E developers are using D/S.
Those I've spoken to swear by the new D/S. If you are already familiar
with D/S - then that would be a sensible route to pursue. Be aware
though, it does have some limitations, tables with multi-column entry
fields, and should you wish to do something like a Progress Bar - you'll
have to do it in OO/GUI. Look at the D/S demos to see if it gives an 80%
"fit" to what you are trying to achieve with GUIs.

Having committed to OO/GUI I've stuck with it; a L-A-R-G-E learning
curve to understand Windows inter-action, (if you haven't been the
Windows route before), but the actual Object Orientation is a snip. Your
comment about OO/GUI being "awfully cumbersome" is a legitimate
reaction. I was horrified when I first looked at the Phone and Bank
demos - so much coding - but yuou have to look at it in the context of
separating the business application from the windowing techniques! 

For OO - a MicroFocus version of Dialog Editor is provided, includes
four more symbols than Microsoft - and you can generate your menus,
dialogs, retaining them in your myapp.rc. My own application is largely
table-driven and I intermix windows drawn with Dialog Editor, (giving a
frame, text labels etc), and add subpane tables of dynamically created
right-justified entry-fields.

Strictly speaking, there is nothing to stop you mixing OO/GUI with
Dialog System windows in an application - you can get the best of both
worlds.

As you know, you can describe to a layman, what COBOL is about in a
couple of sentences - not so with OO COBOL. If you want to get a handle
on OO before making a decision, check out following, which go beyond
theoretical, and use the MicroFocus compiler with coded examples :-

ISBN 0-13-261140-6 - Object-Oriented COBOL - Edmund C. Arranga and
Frank 	P Coyle ( I believe Edward Arranga has produced a later book)

	This one takes you through the theory and finishes up with a 
	complete application for a corporate in-house library system.
	(They do "cheat" however; the data-files are tables in Working
	Storage - but nevertheless does illustrate OO well).

ISBN 0-9655945-0-5 - Elements of Object-Oriented Cobol - Wilson Price
	(http://www.objectz.com) 

	Tutorial-based, aimed at students progressing from 		"conventional" 
into OO. The neat thing is Will Price shows 		REUSABILITY - I've adapted
his concepts for file handling. 		Briefly,  :-

        Main Menu       DBIMethods(Super)       FileMethods(Super)
           .                    .               .     .
           .                    .               .     .
        ClientsEdit     ClientsDBI              .   ClientsFile
                                                .
                                                FileErrors

For file handling ClientsEdit invokes ClientsDBI(*** - see below) which
automatically passes on requests to DBI Methods (a generic class -
containng methods for all COBOL file VERBS), accessing ClientsFile which
does following :-

"good-data" - record is returned from ClientsFile to DBIMethods which 
can either return complete record or CliensEdit can make additional
invokes for specific data in the record. (I could be accessing 30
different files and DBIMethods "knows" which file I'm calling because
for each there is an object reference handle).

"file-errors" - ClientsFile sends a message to FileMethods for file
status errors, which in turn invokes FileErros. 

*** ClientsDBI - I use this basically for creating dictionaries and
collections, (to become listboxes or droplists) and it invokes super
(DBIMethods) to access records.

*> ---------

Chances are you will go the Dialog System route - but come the Year 2005
we should all be using OO COBOL or the other OO languages, C's, Java etc
will take over.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Dialog vs. OO GUI

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376C6F0B.B78958D0@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7kdotv$k6p$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net> <376AC775.FD16D566@home.com> <WWJa3.2833$w61.725@stones>`

```
Simon Hart wrote:
> 
> Have you ever wrote your own class to interact with a GUI object, im
> thinking about taking the GUI script from MSVC++ to handle IP numbers, > as their is no object in the MF dialog editor, nor  is there any 
>class's that handle this, then writting a class in the GUI 
> libary to handle this new object.

The quick answer to your question is No. Thank goodness I got Microsoft
Press Computer Dictionary on Friday before you wrote. I'm assuming 
MSVC++ must be M/S Visual C++ and IP(internet Protcol) - got me floored!

But being a rare breed, analyst turned programmer, rather than the other
way around - let's take it from an analytical perspective. Using the
Base class as your gateway to the platform (Windows98/NT), in theory at
least, there would be nothing to stop you inventing all your own classes
to replace MicroFocus classes; in practice of course, you would be nuts
if you did so! But there is nothing to prevent you sub-classing.

The class hierarchy structure isn't clear from the on-line help, because
for convenience, classes are shown as two groups Base and GUIs, with
subordinates listed alphabetically. But the name of the game is that
they are all subordinate to Base, including GUI itself.

The on-line help for class methods is sometimes terse, (taken from
comment notes written by the class developer) and in certain instances
method-names can be ambiguous. Consider the method "growto" for
collections - implies it can 'grow' to size n - but in fact the same
method will 'decrease' your collection to size n. (I only mention this
to illustrate that you could be led on a red herring trail).

I assume you've already done some browsing on the class source code -
not being clear what you are after I guess it could be a combination of
intrinsics and GUI. Before you re-invent the wheel, check-out with
Microfocus on your specific query; one of the OO gurus may confirm that
you could do it "this way" or "if you take a bit of this class and a bit
of that class" etc.....

As a last measure, you could write a completely new class as long as it
is subordinate to the relevant Super and obeys the rules about calls to
WinAPI.

Isn't this what C is all about, developers writing libraries of
routines(sub-classes). Instead of dynamically creating sub-pane tables
of entry-fields I would have preferred an elegant grid, (as in
spread-sheets) - but just don't have the time to research it. Such a
class, with parameters specifying say, subpane size (x y w h), number of
lines, number of columns, column widths etc. would be subordinate to
subpane(?) or paint(?)

Curious - let me know how you make out.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376A15C6.760CDD18@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<376A0A6D.6CA894AF@sib.fr>`

```
Hi,

You have not linked in the runtime support, the best way of linking
COBOL and C together is to use 'cob' for creating the exe.

eg: cob -x appel.c testcob.cob

A quick example would be:

--START: bld --
cob -vx appel.c TESTCOB.cob
--END: bld --

--START: appel.c --
#include <stdio.h>

extern int TESTCOB();

int main(int argc, char **argv)
{
#ifdef _AIX
	cobinit();
#endif

	TESTCOB();

	cobexit(0);
}
--END: appel.c --

--START: TESTCOB.cob --
	display "Hello world".
--END: TESTCOB.cob --


You might also want to take a look at a demo called 'cmain.c' 
in $COBDIR/demo.

Yours
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing CGI in Cobol Ans85

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PjFa3.11163$NY6.4115@news.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7kdp2d$kce$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
here's a simple example i have for an older MERANT compiler (Visual Object
COBOL). the example should work just fine with the current product (Net
Express).

a nice/slick alternative to using external-forms to output a page is to
simply embed the HTML into your program. in this case the 01 output-page
item is not needed and instead of the 'display output-page', you have:

exec html
    copy "output.htm"
end exec

well, something like that anyway. it's been a while. you can of course just
enter HTML directly; you don't have to use copybooks. anyway, pretty simple,
COBOL/SQL-like implementation.

==>start of cobolcgi source
identification division.

working-storage section.
01 input-page is external-form.
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

01 output-page is external-form identified by "output".
   03  name-field pic x(30) identified by "Name".
   03  phone-no   pic x(30) identified by "Phone".
   03  email      pic x(30) identified by "Email".

procedure division.

    accept input-page
    move corresponding input-page to output-page
    display output-page
    .
==>end of cobolcgi source

==>start of input.htm
<HTML>
<HEAD>
<TITLE>Sample Form for VISOC</TITLE>
</HEAD>
<BODY>

<P>Visual Object COBOL Input Form

<FORM METHOD="POST" ACTION="/cgi-bin/cobolcgi.exe"></P>
<PRE>
Name     <INPUT TYPE="TEXT" NAME="Name" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Email id <INPUT TYPE="TEXT "NAME="Email" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="30" >
Phone    <INPUT NAME="Phone" VALUE="" MAXLENGTH="12">
<INPUT TYPE="SUBMIT" VALUE="Send Form"><INPUT TYPE="RESET">
</PRE>
</FORM>

</BODY>
</HTML>
==>end of input.htm

==>start of output.htm
<HTML>

<P>*** SAMPLE OUTPUT FORM ***</P>
<P>Dear :name-field</P>
<P>blah blah blah</P>

</HTML>
==>end of output.htm
```

<a id="t-9cba54ed3d"></a>
## Thread: QUESTION USING SCROLLABLE CURSORS IN EMBEDED SQL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### QUESTION USING SCROLLABLE CURSORS IN EMBEDED SQL

- **From:** "Juan Rodriguez" <juanr@wilwel.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YBya3.1042$RL.27806@news.ntr.net>`

```

We are running into some obstacles when Using Cursors with Embeded SQL and
we are wondering if maybe you have some suggestions that may help us.
We are currently using Microfocus Netexpress 3.0 and Flexus SP2 for the GUI
front-end.
We are also accessing data from an MS SQL Database using Embeded SQL.

We have a situation where we have a Cursor tha retrieves 20,000 rows from a
database Table.  We have this SP2 GUI Window that displays 10 of those rows
at a time and allows the User to scroll forward(FETCH NEXT), scroll
back(FETCH PRIOR), reposition at the end(FETCH LAST), and reposition at the
beginning(FETCH FIRST).
 Because the number of rows is very large, we need to also allow the User to
randomly reposition within the Cursor.  At this time the User inputs an item
number and presses a fuction key that causes the program to performs
consecutive Fetches until it finds the item requested.   Then it displays 10
items from that point forward.

Is there a way to reposition within the Result set from a Cursor without
having to FETCH NEXT , FETCH NEXT, ...FETCH NEXT,
or FECTH PRIOR, FETCH PRIOR,....FETCH PRIOR  to get to the item desired.?

There exists a FETCH RELATIVE and a FETCH OBSOLUTE, but they are not
supported.

Any help from you is greatly appreciated.
Best Regards,
JCR
```

#### ↳ Re: QUESTION USING SCROLLABLE CURSORS IN EMBEDED SQL

- **From:** Jeen van der Meer <JMEER@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376BB884.FF6C4831@t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<YBya3.1042$RL.27806@news.ntr.net>`

```
Hi Juan,

If could show us the declaration of the cursor I or someone else, might
be able to show you how to solve your problem.
Without having seen the code, I could suggest an additional SQL
statement returning a single row. So you would have to have data from a
unique defined field in your table.
I hope this makes some sense to you.
If not you can always e-mail me.

Jeen

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
