```
(say less than a hundred) in which case you just read them all and sort them
internally in any order you want.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed sequential file read question

- **From:** Bob Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377184C6.26CB@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<slrn7mq7cs.tq.jonathan.vaughan@turnip.clara.co.uk> <J7fb3.3110$T25.83260@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> You can't read this file in this program, using this SELECT and FD, in any
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

With end-date in format ddmmyy, you will never be able to sort by date.
May 30,1999 (300599) would be greater than june 1, 1999 (010699).  Try
format yymmdd...works much better.  I know it is awkward for British
people, but what isn't ?

Bob
```

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>

```
The keys must be in that order, or you must sort them into the order you
want
and write out the key fields, then read that (or return) and randomly read
the ISAM file.
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed sequential file read question

- **From:** Alex Romaniuk <ales.romaniuk@zag.si>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376DD238.720A@zag.si>`
- **References:** `<slrn7mq7cs.tq.jonathan.vaughan@turnip.clara.co.uk>`

```
Jonathan Vaughan wrote:
> 
> I have an indexed sequential file in the format
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please remove nocack. to reply
You can solve this using two methods:
1. Sorting before reading

SELECT SORTFILE ASSIGN TO "SORTFILE.SOR".
...
...
SD SORTFILE.
01 SORT-Rec.
   03 SORT-Pr-Ref 	PIC 9(8).
   03 SORT-End-Date 	PIC 9(6).

SORT SORTSD ON DESCENDING KEY SORT-Rec
               ----------



2. Using secondary reverse-key

01 rec-key
   03 pr-ref     pic 9(8).
   03 end-date   pic 9(6).
01 end-date-reverse PIC 9(6).


then
   move this-date to end-date
   compute end-date-reverse = 999999 - this-date


then
   START MYFILE KEY NOT < END-DATE-REVERSE
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed sequential file read question

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376fc0d4.835257@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<slrn7mq7cs.tq.jonathan.vaughan@turnip.clara.co.uk>`

```
Assuming you have access to all programs that use this file, you could
change the way the date is stored so that it will be in the order you
want.  IOW, use an algorithm to change a high date into a low date,
and vice versa when writing records to the file.  Then just convert
the date before you print/use it after reading.

For example, change the date field to be pic 9(8).  Then when writing
records to the file, subtract your date from 99999999 and store the
result in the date field.  Then after you read the records, add
99999999 to the date field.  

Kinda wacky but it would work if the main use of this file is
accessing it in Descending date order. 

Paul

On Sun, 20 Jun 1999 17:00:11 GMT, jonathan.vaughan@nocack.bigfoot.com
(Jonathan Vaughan) wrote:

>I have an indexed sequential file in the format
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jon

--------------------------------------

words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

```
PAL3000 wrote:
> 
> Assuming you have access to all programs that use this file, you could
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> words REMOVETHIS from my email address

No, your suggestion isn't the least bit wacky - I do exactly that.
Producing calculations and writing them to an indexed file with
alternate keys, I then have to produce a standard summary report by
various criteria :-

	a) Life expectancy (ascending) - the date this item is likely
	   to expire - so you get those that are 'forecast' to expire
	   in the current year, first
	b) Losses greater than     40 mils per year
	   Losses	..	   25 mils per year
	   Total Loss greater than 25 mils etc......

For Group (b) I create a "negative key descending" - losses being shown
in descending sequence for each sub-title - it works fine.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Indexed sequential file read question

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FaQb3.2490$LI5.228335@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<slrn7mq7cs.tq.jonathan.vaughan@turnip.clara.co.uk> <376fc0d4.835257@news.enteract.com>`

```
PAL3000 wrote in message <376fc0d4.835257@news.enteract.com>...
>Assuming you have access to all programs that use this file, you could
>change the way the date is stored so that it will be in the order you
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>99999999 to the date field.


Close, but no cigar.  Subtract the result from 99999999 again to undo a
nine's complement date.

Our shop has a file of rate records in which the last field in the key is a
nine's complement date representing when the rate went/goes into effect.
Before issuing a START statement we create a nine's complement of the date a
customer used our client's service.  The READ NEXT will pick up the rate
record with the most recent change but not a rate change going into effect
in the future.

Warren Porter -- Remove numbers to reply.
Chessplayers make better mates.
```

```
On Tue, 22 Jun 1999 17:01:51, pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
wrote:

> For example, change the date field to be pic 9(8).  Then when writing
> records to the file, subtract your date from 99999999 and store the
> result in the date field.  Then after you read the records, add
> 99999999 to the date field.  
>

I know what you mean, but you didn't say it! <G>

Read that again slowly.  

take 19990201 for example.  

99999999 - 19990201 = 80009798
80009898 + 99999999 = 180009797

What I think you mean was subtract the "date" read from the file from 
99999999

99999999 - 80009798 = 19990201 

No addition required <G>. 

-------------------------
Trust the computer industry to shorten "Year 2000" to Y2K.  It was 
this
kind of thinking that caused the problem in the first place.

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Indexed sequential file read question

- **From:** scottp4-remove-this-to-reply@mindspring.com (Scott Peterson)
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37735a1d.27043867@news.mindspring.com>`
- **References:** `<slrn7mq7cs.tq.jonathan.vaughan@turnip.clara.co.uk>`

```
jonathan.vaughan@nocack.bigfoot.com (Jonathan Vaughan) wrote:

>I have an indexed sequential file in the format
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jon,

Even if you could make this work this would be pretty ugly. You'd be
bouncing all over the file for each sequential read on pr-ref. to find
the correct sequence on the date.

I've had to handle this in two different ways.  The better way was
sort the file and the process the whole thing sequentially.  

In another situation where that was not possible, I've read the file
sequentially and for each pr-ref, loaded all the records into a
two-dimensional table and then processed the records out of the table
in the correct sequence. 

Then go back and read the next pr-ref series. This actually proved to
be quite quick where the number of records for each ref was not too
large. 


                         
                         		Scott Peterson


Follow your dream! Unless it's the one where you're 
at work in your underwear during a fire drill, or the 
one where you're naked and in the supermarket.
```

## Thread: Converting C to COBOL — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting C to COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376C87F4.E38E914C@home.com>`

```
I'm not into C. (Had I been interested in hieroglyphics - I would have
taken up Egyptology - then I could have spent my autumn years studying
and deciphering the Rosetta Stone in the British Museum).

But it would be nice to translate C into something meaningful. We doff
our caps to the 'cryptic' languages making calls from COBOL. There are
sites containing useful C routines, or the M/S SDK. Anybody know of a
routine which would let you paste a C routine into an empty text file
and then run a COBOL program to convert it to intelligible COBOL code ?

Just hoping ! Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-77a3ed2caf"></a>
## Thread: Value of learning Pc Cobol — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Value of learning Pc Cobol

- **From:** goble@kin.on.net (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376c8d31@duster.adelaide.on.net>`

```
Hi All;

I have done to courses in cobol, one in 1989 we learned cobol75 and a
bit of cobol85 and I have just finished the exam for cobol as part of
my BcompSc degree.

My question is this, in both these courses, we learned cobol on Pcs,
what value is this in the real world?

The main reason for this question is that most of the jobs I see are
for mainframe cobol programmers.

--Regards :                David. E. Goble
                           goble@kin.on.net
           http://www-mugc.cc.monash.edu.au/~degob1/goble
      Po Box 648 Kingscote Kangaroo Island South Australia 5223
```

#### ↳ Re: Value of learning Pc Cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9_8b3.2549$T25.65265@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<376c8d31@duster.adelaide.on.net>`

```
It is very valuable. Once you get pass the SELECTs and sometimes the FILE
SECTION, mainframe COBOL is PC COBOL is Unix COBOL.  You still have tables,
MOVEs, PERFORMs and all the other goodies.

That is, a programming language may have different dialects, but it's still
the same language.
```

#### ↳ Re: Value of learning Pc Cobol

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376dbff5.14244941@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<376c8d31@duster.adelaide.on.net>`

```

>
>The main reason for this question is that most of the jobs I see are
>for mainframe cobol programmers.

I keep hearing about the "50,000 Mainframes" in the world.  I have to
believe there are more... but in any event, there are MANY platforms
where COBOL exists:

Mainframe (of all types)
Midrange - AS/400, RS/6000
Unix
System 36/38 (Don't hear about them much anymore)
PC's.

There have to be a LOT of PC jobs.  There's more PC based COBOL out
there than you might know about.  It seems when people "admit" there
applications are written in COBOL the customer frowns, or expresses
suprise!  I've had it happen more than once.

"How did you get your Visual basic ot be so FAST?" 

"Uh.. it's COBOL not VB."

"COBOL?  You have got to be kidding, this isn't a mainframe!  Won't
this have a Y2K problem?"

Many people just won't admit the product they are selling is from
COBOL source!
```

#### ↳ Re: Value of learning Pc Cobol

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kljch$khu$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
David. E. Goble wrote in message <376c8d31@duster.adelaide.on.net>...
>Hi All;
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>what value is this in the real world?

The nice thing about Cobol, it is portable, file handling is basically what
differs from platform to platform, and that is something you will have to
deal with no matter language / dialect.

>The main reason for this question is that most of the jobs I see are
>for mainframe cobol programmers.

Shouldn't be an issue, really.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-f075ca5113"></a>
## Thread: Copybook syntax questions — 15 msgs

### Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "Regina Morris" <Regina_Morris@bmc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
I am trying to write a Cobol Copybook parser. I have 850 Copybooks and my
parser can read all but 33 of them. These last 33 files have the following
syntax "errors" :

1. I have some copybooks which only contain comments. Is it valid to only
have comments in a copy book?

2. I have some copybooks which begin:

        05 any_name PIC(9).

   Doesn't a copybook have to start with the record name? Like:  "01
any_name."  or "01 any_name, comp." ? Is it valid to start the copybook
without giving it a record name?

3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with numbers?
For example:
        05 10any_name PIC x.

   Where 'any_name' is some alphanummeric expression? Names like "name10"
work will, but my parser is expecting
  names to begin with a letter, not a number. It would be confusing to
parse"10A" because you won't know if it is a hex
  number or a name.

 4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in the
7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?


I'd appreciate any help you can give me.

Thanks in advance.

Regina Morris
regina_morris@bmc.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7klusm$iih@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```

Regina Morris wrote in message <929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>...
>I am trying to write a Cobol Copybook parser. I have 850 Copybooks and my
>parser can read all but 33 of them. These last 33 files have the following
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>have comments in a copy book?

Copybooks can contain any legal COBOL code.  Since comments are valid, a
copybook containing only comments is certainly valid.
>
>2. I have some copybooks which begin:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>without giving it a record name?

Yes, this is valid as well.  You can have entire records, just sequences of
fields, paragraphs, just a series of procedure division sentences. etc.
>
>3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with
numbers?
>For example:
>        05 10any_name PIC x.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
These are valid COBOL datanames.  As long as there is one letter it should
scan properly.  Letters, digits, and dashes are OK.

> 4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
>program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
>continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in
the
>7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?
>
This depends on whether you are using a compiler which defines fixed
starting positions or not.  For most mainframe COBOL compilers, your
statement is correct.  However, many other compilers do not have the first 6
columns reserved, therefore comments are those lines which have an asterisk
in column 1, etc.
>
>I'd appreciate any help you can give me.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376E829A.65B5996B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
> 2. I have some copybooks which begin:
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>   number or a name.

I suspect those may be designed for COPY REPLACING.  This makes it
obvious which strings should be replaced.
 
>  4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
> program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
> continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in the
> 7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?

Some compilers (VAX) have options to bump code over to column 1.  Maybe
these copymembers were designed for such an environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F5A16ACE3E1C9FB3.72E7525723E15C12.D5507754A517EB01@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
The answers given below apply to the mainframe world.  You did not
state your platform, but most of the answers are valid for both the PC
and mainframe world.  


On Mon, 21 Jun 1999 12:12:32 -0500, "Regina Morris"
<Regina_Morris@bmc.com> enlightened us:

>I am trying to write a Cobol Copybook parser. I have 850 Copybooks and my
>parser can read all but 33 of them. These last 33 files have the following
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>have comments in a copy book?

Yes.  A copybook can contain any valid Cobol statement or can be just
be comments so long as there is an * in column 7.
>
>2. I have some copybooks which begin:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No.  Some shops write copybooks that are copied after an 01 line in
either their Working-Storage Section or in their FD section.

>3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with numbers?
>For example:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The rules for the assignment of data names in Cobol are as follows:

1.  Names may range from 1 to 31 characters in length;
2.  No spaces (blanks) may appear within a name;
3.  Names may be formed from the alphabet, numerals, and the hyphen.
No special characters may appear in a name except the hyphen.  (I
believe some manufactureres of Cobol for the PC do allow underscore);
4.  Although the hyphen may appear in a name, no name may begin or end
with a hyphen;
5.  The procedure name may consist entirely of numerals, but all other
names must have at least one alphabetic character;

> 4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
>program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
>continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in the
>7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?
>

I would think that the copybooks that have the "01" line starting in
the 7th column and the ones that start in the 1st or 2nd column are
either in error or not for Cobol.  Your impressions are correct.

>
>I'd appreciate any help you can give me.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Your welcome.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

         My Law:  You can't fall off the floor


 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mAwb3.427$Xr2.20576@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
I know you want to do this for the experience, but if not, you can always
download my free COBOL source and copylib parser / record analyzer COBFD.ZIP
at http://www.flexus.com/softwaredownload.html.

As far as your specific questions...

>1. I have some copybooks which only contain comments. Is it valid to only
>have comments in a copy book?

I don't see why not. Most compilers just treat copylibs as though the data
were typed in, except when using one of the REPLACING ... options in the
COPY statement.

>2. I have some copybooks which begin:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>without giving it a record name?

Perfectly OK. See above. (My parser/record analyzer adds '01
SYSTEM-GENERATED-01' at the front of any record which does not begin with
'01'). This is quite common in OS/VS COBOL copylibs. You'd see in the source
code something like:

01  THE-RECORD.
      COPY 'FDLRESTOFRECORD'.

>3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with
numbers?
>For example:
>        05 10any_name PIC x.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's legal. It can only be a "hex number" if part of VALUE clause, and the
parser should already know that.

> 4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
>program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
>continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in
the
>7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?
>

Some compilers offer a "free format" source option which ignores the
columns. I don't know if that's ANSI standard, though. Someone else will
know, I'm sure.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
Most of the other comments in this thread have answered your question.
HOWEVER, from the fact that your data name ("any_name") has an underscore in
it (rather than a hyphen), I would suspect that this is one of the PC
compilers - probably one with LOTS of extensions.

If it is one that I am most familiar with, it DOES allow your source line to
start in column 1 (not just 7 or 8) - even when you are running with "fixed
format" source code.  You may ALSO run into code written in free-format
source code - in which a WHOLE different set of rules apply. (also look out
for lines beginning "$SET")

FYI, COBOL has ALWAYS allowed copybooks to start with any level number (i.e.
01 *or* 05 or 16, etc).  If, however, you are dealing with a compiler that
handles the (very) OLD '68 Standard COBOL COPY statement, there are very
special rules for how copybooks starting with 01-levels are processed.
(Look at a Micro Focus documentation under "OLDCOPY" or IBM's OS/VS COBOL
under LANGLVL(1) for documentation on the impact of this.)

NOTE WELL:
  Not all copybooks have valid source code UNTIL after they are "copied in"
to a program.  The classic example (VERY valid in '85 Standard COBOL and
allowed sort-of as an extension in some earlier ones) is:

Copybook1  has

 01  :tag:rec.
      05  :tag:field-1  Pic X.
      05  (tag2)field-2  Pic 9.

and a COPY statement of

   COPY Copybook1
      Replacing ==:tag:== by ==pref1-==
                        ==(tag2== by ==pref2-==
      .

In this case, the actual copybook has INVALID syntax, but once the
COPY/Replacing is applied, the syntax is valid.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376F97FA.35402859@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Most of the other comments in this thread have answered your question.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> for lines beginning "$SET")

If I remember correctly, VAX Cobol allows both underscores and column 1
source code.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "Paul Keirnan" <paul.keirnan@det.nsw.edu.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3771e3fe@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com>...
>Copybook1  has
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>


It has always amazed me that the replacement which I most normally
want to do, is so hard to achieve. Why has the replacement facility
never allowed the above to be written simply as:

01   ppp_rec.
       05  ppp_field_1     pic x.
       05  ppp_field_2     pic 9.

Copy Copybook_1 replacing ==ppp== by ==prefix1==.

P.S. You will note my use of underscores -- I have for years worked
only on COMPAQ (previously DEC) using the OpenVMS operating
system. This also means that the norm is for variable length lines,
no line numbers, and no concept of columns 73-80. Line numbers
are not required because the editor works on the entire source file
in a scrollable window, and using 73-80 for version information is
much better handled in the source code management tool.  The
code looks a lot better when I don't have to wrap statements onto
two lines, and have the ability to view them in a window which may
be 24x132 (normal character cell maximum) or possibly 48x150
which I quite often use on the workstation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktdse$1ik@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <3771e3fe@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au>`

```
The draft of the next Standard *does* introduce "leading" and "trailing"
options to both COPY/REPLACING and REPLACE.  This should (finally) make
prefix changing more "intuitive".

P.S.  There are some long complicated reasons why COBOL (historically) has
required changes only to FULL "text words" (which were not the same as
"COBOL words" which added to the confusion).  I won't go into them, but the
restrictions do seem (finally) to be going away.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3772cd33.7129704@netnews>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <7kmort$a28@dfw-ixnews17.ix.netcom.com> <3771e3fe@htdev1.isd.tafensw.edu.au> <7ktdse$1ik@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Thu, 24 Jun 1999 09:03:13 -0500, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> The draft of the next Standard *does* introduce "leading" and "trailing"
> options to both COPY/REPLACING and REPLACE.  This should (finally) make
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> restrictions do seem (finally) to be going away.

The truly odd thing is that multiple text words (durring source text
manipulation) can turn into a single COBOL word.  I don't think any other
language has this "feature".  An interesting one is this

  COPY TEXT-NAME REPLACING THIS-THING BY ==X(3)==.

In this case X(3) is four text words, and will be four COBOL words if it
lands in the procedure division, but if it lands after PIC or PICTURE it's
only one COBOL word.  Conventional compiler tools which aren't written for
COBOL just can't deal with this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>

```
In order:
1.  Yes
2. No, you can COPY XX REPLACING == 05 == BY == 01 ==.
3. No, must start with ALPHABETIC character not space, not number not
under_score, not dash
4. Yes, No.
Regina Morris wrote in message <929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>...
>I am trying to write a Cobol Copybook parser. I have 850 Copybooks and my
>parser can read all but 33 of them. These last 33 files have the following
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with
numbers?
>For example:
>        05 10any_name PIC x.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in
the
>7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kne0g$aip@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <5WBb3.2208$6Q3.857@news3.mco>`

```

James King <mangogwr@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:5WBb3.2208$6Q3.857@news3.mco...
   <snip>
> 3. No, must start with ALPHABETIC character not space, not number not
> under_score, not dash
> >        05 any_name PIC(9).
> >
   <more snip>
> >
> >3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

As pointed out correctly (in other notes), there is no restriction saying
that a variable name or paragraph-name cannot start with a number.

P.S.  Lots of your original questions seem to "imply" (or I infer) that you
think that copybooks are limited to data division structures.  It is
perfectly acceptable (and medium common) to have procedure division - or
even multi-division copybooks.  In fact, it is getting to be more and more
common to have Select/Assign statements in copybooks - to ease in "porting"
from one environment to another.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37718ffd.9883808@netnews>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <5WBb3.2208$6Q3.857@news3.mco> <7kne0g$aip@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```
'Twas Tue, 22 Jun 1999 02:27:50 -0500, when "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> P.S.  Lots of your original questions seem to "imply" (or I infer) that you
> think that copybooks are limited to data division structures.  It is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from one environment to another.

Actually I inferred that he was familiar with the COPY rules from
COBOL-68, which did allow COPY in the procedure division, but only
following a paragraph name.

At my last shop we had a whole family of programs which were one line
long:

COPY DBDELETE REPLACING SETNAME BY INVOICES.

We had one such program for every data set in the test database.
```

#### ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** Barticus@att.spam.net (Randall Bart)
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376ef727.14725557@netnews>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com>`

```
'Twas Mon, 21 Jun 1999 12:12:32 -0500, when "Regina Morris"
<Regina_Morris@bmc.com> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:

> 1. I have some copybooks which only contain comments. Is it valid to only
> have comments in a copy book?

This may have been invalid in COBOL-68, but it is certainly legal in
COBOL-74 and beyond.

> 2. I have some copybooks which begin:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> without giving it a record name?

COBOL-68 had some bizarre rules about copybooks, but these rules all went
away in COBOL-74.

> 3. Is it valid to have a record/segment/variable name to begin with numbers?
> For example:
>         05 10any_name PIC x.

Perfectly legal in COBOL-74 and beyond.  It was also allowed by most of
the COBOL-68 compilers I used, but the standard may have said otherwise.
I know there were COBOL-68 compilers that didn't allow it.

>  It would be confusing to
> parse"10A" because you won't know if it is a hex
>   number or a name.

That form isn't a hex number in COBOL.  It may have been a hex number in
some weird compiler, but I've never seen it.  All the compilers I have
ever used which had hex literals required the literal to be enclosed in
delimiters of some sort.  The current technique is X"10A", but in
Burroughs (Unisys) it was @10A@ and I think I saw something else.

>  4. I'm under the impression that the first 6 columns are reserved for
> program numbers and that the 7th column is reserved for a comment or
> continuation. I have some copybooks that begin the "01" line starting in the
> 7th column, and some which start it in the 1st or 2nd column. Is this okay?

This is non-standard, but you'll run into it.  Some compilers accept
anything other than "-", "*", "/", or "D" in column 7 as part of the
program text.  The curent draft has a ">>SOURCE FORMAT IS FREE" directive
which eliminates the sequence field and starts the program text in column
1.  Some compilers have implemented this, or a different free form option.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Copybook syntax questions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376F9899.4CC88F1C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<929985114.255.88@news.remarQ.com> <376ef727.14725557@netnews>`

```
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> 'Twas Mon, 21 Jun 1999 12:12:32 -0500, when "Regina Morris"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL-74 and beyond.

We have such a copy in most of our code indicating that a program has
been checked out for Y2K.  This was done right before conversion to
COBOL for MVS, and our Cobol consisted of a few Cobol II programs, and
lots of ANSI-68 & ANSI-74.
```

## Thread: Please help - Screens question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Please help - Screens question

- **From:** Jonathan Maton <jmaton@unf.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9906211822050.2453-100000@osprey.unf.edu>`

```
I'm using Microfocus v2.0.02, writing in COBOL-85.

My problem is with using Screens (for interactive processing.)
A user selects *Change Record* from the menu, and enters the item-no-code.
I retrieve the record (item-no-code is the record key) and want to display
this record to the user so that (s)he can tab to the field(s) (s)he wants
to change and leave the rest.  Don't I use the USING clause?  E.g.:

LINE 4  COLUMN 4  PIC X(10)  USING  MF-ITEM-NAME.

It doesn't seem to be displaying the initial values of my record.  Any
assistance is greatly appreciated.

Please email me if you can help, my newsreader keeps crashing today.


_____________________________________________________________________
Jonathan Austin Maton     icq:7834706     University of North Florida
   UNF Enrollment Services Web Master and a huge Led Zeppelin fan!
_____________________________________________________________________
        Artificial Intelligence usually beats Real Stupidity
_____________________________________________________________________
```

#### ↳ Re: Please help - Screens question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<x0Nb3.3578$qq.1223@news4.mco>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9906211822050.2453-100000@osprey.unf.edu>`

```
The data values are only placed on the screen when you use the DISPLAY
verb to display the screen.  'FROM' fields are output to screen only,
'USING' fields are both input from and output to the screen, and 'TO'
fields are input from the screen only.  No data fields are displayed
by the ACCEPT verb, even USING or FROM fields or VALUE literals.
```

#### ↳ Re: Please help - Screens question

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kocj4$jv5$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<Pine.OSF.4.10.9906211822050.2453-100000@osprey.unf.edu>`

```

Jonathan Maton wrote in message ...
>I'm using Microfocus v2.0.02, writing in COBOL-85.
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Please email me if you can help, my newsreader keeps crashing today.

I assume you are talking about screen section items? I don't know MF Cobol,
so, what I am suggesting is based on ANSI only.

Do you display your item before accepting? Try this;

DISPLAY SCRN-ITEM.
ACCEPT SCRN-ITEM.

Cheesle
```

<a id="t-70b3519f6c"></a>
## Thread: Internal representation of some Cobol data ? — 1 msgs

### Internal representation of some Cobol data ?

- **From:** "John H. Lindsay" <eil@aracnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376EA228.CA10FBA5@aracnet.net>`

```
I'm looking for detailed information to the byte and bit level
of COBOL pictured numeric data (only S, 9 and V picture
characters) and packed decimal data on various hardware types
and under various compilers, as might be needed by an assembly
language programmer having to handle such data in files.  Where
it does exist, packed decimal data is ordinarily available as one
of the COMPUTATIONAL or COMP-n USAGEs.  The internal
format of edited data or of other forms of COMP- data such as
machine floating point or integer (fixed point) binary is not of
immediate interest to me.  The meaning of the various forms of
COMPUTATIONAL or COMP-n data varies greatly from
machine to machine and from compiler to compiler even on the
same machine.

I'm already familiar with the above for the I.B.M. mainframe
computers and the COBOLs that run on them.

Any information or pointers that anyone can give would be
greatly appreciated.  I'm trying to do a run-time library for a
SNOBOL4 (Spitbol) system to enable it to handle such data; I
can guess at a spectrum of possibilities, but I'd like to do a
thorough job here.  SNOBOL4 is a very powerful string
processor, character pattern matching and data structures
language.
```

## Thread: How do you use Cobol to convert a cobol print job onto Wordpad — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### How do you use Cobol to convert a cobol print job onto Wordpad

- **From:** alisonchan@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kl378$6j0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am a new comer to Cobol and my first assignment is to convert a cobol print
job using cobol on the AS400 system onto Wordpro or any other ASCII file so
that i can use it later and send out by e-mail as an attachment.

Any help will be greatly appreciated.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you use Cobol to convert a cobol print job onto Wordpad

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<376E2F68.DAD01187@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7kl378$6j0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
alisonchan@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I am a new comer to Cobol and my first assignment is to convert a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help will be greatly appreciated.

I will assume you have FTP.

Create a job containing 

1. the cobol program output to a sequential file.
2. an FTP step to the PC (lan server whatever)

Voila....

Make sure your output from cobol is simple text, no format
characters or ANSI control characters (does AS400 have these?).
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie

- **From:** "Kevin G. Rhoads" <T_Rhoads@NoSpam.CLASSIC.MSN.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bebd0f$7d961720$LocalHost@stupidwin95>`
- **References:** `<3770132e.21782866@news.btinternet.com>`

```
Cobol is a great language, it is easier to get something done in than
assembly,
easier to make a program that won't blow up with pointer errors than C,
more portable than just about anything else.

It is not considered "sexy" by most people today, but then a lot of
"sexy" things came in with great foofourah and then quietly faded away,
some decades ago.  Cobol is the "energizer bunny" of programming 
languages.  It keeps going and going and going . . .
```

```
Stu wrote in message <3770132e.21782866@news.btinternet.com>...
>Joining a new (first) job on Monday, I've been told I'm do to COBOL
>for the forseeable furture.
>
>Someone say something nice about it :)

It is reliable, portable and efficient. It challenges you as a programmer if
you have to do things that normally would be accomplished by C or Pascal. It
is powerful because it has the capability to simplify tasks that would be
tedious and complex in other languages.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbie

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l0c2n$i6r$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<3770132e.21782866@news.btinternet.com> <7kqu14$gpi$1@news.cerf.net> <3772CB77.CE0D450B@earthlink.com>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote in message <3772CB77.CE0D450B@earthlink.com>...
>>It challenges you as a programmer if you have to do things that normally
would be accomplished by C or >Pascal.
>
>Is it good or bad?

Uhm... Well, good question, it just came to my mind when I was typing. What
I thought of was when I created a double linked list in my application to
store scripts. A pretty stright forward task in C or pascal, but I really
had to know the basics to have it done in Cobol. I guess what I thought of,
was that you do need to know your theory, because if the language itself
does not directly support it, you will have to simulate it somehow. So... It
would be good in the sense of demanding good computing theoretical
knowledge, and bad in the sense of being an impractical language for such
tasks.

>>It is powerful because it has the capability to simplify tasks that would
be tedious and complex in other >languages.
>
>It seems to contradict the previous statement. If not, elaborate, please:
>
>1) What 'tedious and complex' tasks?
>2) What 'other' languages?

In this particular case I was thinking of user i/o and database. Which both
are easy in Cobol using Screen sections and the database being a part of the
language. Other languages I thought of here was C and Pascal. However, I
realize that I was to fast here. Languages (No, please no definition war, I
don't care whether it is a language or not) like dBase, Paradox, Progress do
has these features too.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: newbie

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3775C5E2.E493A6BC@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<3770132e.21782866@news.btinternet.com> <7kqu14$gpi$1@news.cerf.net> <3772CB77.CE0D450B@earthlink.com> <7l0c2n$i6r$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 
> Paul M. Dorfman wrote in message <3772CB77.CE0D450B@earthlink.com>...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I appreciate this point, tending to view the situation rather as good than bad
from the standpoint of efficiency. In languages where an internal recursion
mechanism is available, many common algorithms are often unnecessarily
implemented this way. In those languages, including COBOL, whose compilers do
not support recursive calls, iteration is the only way to go, and it is always
more efficient. Note that it does not imply that COBOL is somehow better than
other tongues because of lieu of a certain feature. Nobody makes a C programmer
use recursion instead of iteration, either. There are also situations in SAS
when programming an algorithm explicitly from scratch is much more efficient
(sometimes even the only feasible solution) than using an existing prewritten
routine coded in the underlying software. 

Maybe owing to the very nature of business to which COBOL is most often applied,
few programmers attempt writing core algorithms in COBOL. This is unfortunate,
all the more that COBOL compilers, as a matter of rule, are excellently
optimized and, therefore, ideally suited for high-performance programming.
Taking binary search as an example, it takes several lines of code to write one
explicitly, thus making it practically always faster than linear search -
instead of arguing fruitlessly about SEARCH ALL being more efficient than SEARCH
only for a number of array items exceeding N=100 or so due to the overhead of
the binary search call. Data structures available in COBOL naturally lend
themselves to implementations of internal hashing (whose algorithms, granted,
require thorough understanding) instead of binary search. Even though the former
is, on the average, log(N) times faster than the latter, not to mention that it
requires no sorting, it is almost never attempted. You can add to the
list.                 
          
> >>It is powerful because it has the capability to simplify tasks that would
> be tedious and complex in other >languages.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> has these features too.

As much as it is fun to program from ground up, I see the absence of routines
internally implementing some of widely used computing chores as a major omission
from COBOL. For instance, after sorting, file match is the most common operation
in business data processing. Yet it is not there, even in /370. It is a typical
example of a routine that is not hard but tedious, and hence error-prone, to
code. Same pertains to control break processing. I see no reason why these
features should be available in EZ and SAS but absent from COBOL. They would not
detract a bit from a COBOL programmers' ingenuity; instead, it would leave them
more time for creative work. Another example that readily comes to mind is macro
processing.     

As far as 'definition war' and such goes, no language is a sufficient ground for
religion, even if it helps one to make a buck: It is but a tool. COBOL bigotry
is as funny as that with respect to any other programming language.
Unfortunately, here its smell gets too thick more often than a good dose of
humor would dictate.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL               
==================
```

## Thread: Rm Cobol 6.51 and JPEG Files — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Rm Cobol 6.51 and JPEG Files

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kom7t$pns$1@news.imp.ch>`

```
Hello together

I am working with RM cobol for Windows 6.51 (soon 7.0).
I would like to call a external programm from RM Cobol that
shows at the screen a JPEG File. The file Name is 11digits long.
(RM Cobol 7.0 should handle this) and ending of file is .JPG (quite
logic...)
The file Name would be supplied by my calling programm and I need
a status code back which allows me see if the JPEG was available.
The calling program would be suspended till the JPEG-picture is closed.
I am aware of the fact that there is a subprogram with RM that allows to
show BITMAPS. But I need JPEG.

I there is anybody who would be interested and has experience with
calls of DLL's from RM I would be happy to hear and pay.
thank you
john@casagrande.ch
Casagrande & Co.
Lucerne, Switzerland
```

#### ↳ Re: Rm Cobol 6.51 and JPEG Files

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kqus5$h0j$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7kom7t$pns$1@news.imp.ch>`

```
John Casagrande wrote in message <7kom7t$pns$1@news.imp.ch>...
>Hello together
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>thank you

This could be accomplished in a very easy way or a more difficult one. I
don't know RM so I can't tell if it supports DLL's or not.

The simple method is:
Have you heard of associations? That is, MS Win has associated an executable
with various file extensions. JPG is a well known extension and should be
associated with your internet browser if installed. Otherwise it could for
instance be Microsoft Photo. You may test this by at command line type:
"This is my jpeg file.jpg" followed by enter. If there is an association,
you should a) have your computer heavily working right now or b) have the
image on your screen.

So, assuming you have an association, I bet RM has a method for execution of
SYSTEM? If so, depending on their implementation of this command you should
be able to do this:

        CALL "SYSTEM" USING "This is my jpeg file.jpg"

and get the result you want. Whether the launch becomes syncron or
asyncronous depends on the RM implementation.

Sligthly more difficult is if RM deals with DLL's, then you would typically
rely on the same approach, but using Microsoft Windows API directly, for
instance CreateProcess or the obsolete WinExec. I mention WinExec because it
has a simpler interface and is still supported.

Finally, the most difficult option, get software for displaying jpg and link
it in.

Cheesle
```

- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<377118E0.1369E3C8@home.com>`

```
Jimmy, Calgary  AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rm Cobol 6.51 and JPEG Files

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktjmb$ffl$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7kom7t$pns$1@news.imp.ch> <7kqus5$h0j$1@news.cerf.net> <7krm6v$ssj$1@news.imp.ch>`

```
John Casagrande wrote in message <7krm6v$ssj$1@news.imp.ch>...
>Cheesle, THANK YOU VERY MUCH for the very helpful explanations. It looks
>like the easiest way
>you suggested by calling just a *.EXE program that supports the name of the
>file at the end
>plus some parameters is the easiest and cheapest way!

I am happy if I could contribute, however, you have missed something, sorry
for my poor English.

Do you remember my note about the associations in Windows? They make it
possible to launch documents rather than executables. Thus, instead of:

        CALL "SYSTEM" USING "IEXPLORE.EXE MyFile.jpg"

you could:

        CALL "SYSTEM" USING "MyFile.jpg"

To demonstrate this behavior, just find a dos prompt of your computer, look
for a .txt file and type:

        readme.txt    ENTER

replace readme with what you found, once you have pressed ENTER you will
have the file into  windows notepad (windows default, may be altered).

The benefit of this is obvious, you do not have to care about any
application, just make sure that the user has this association set.
Individual users may then have individual choices of what to use to display
a jpg file. I for one uses Microsoft Photo Editor. I agree with your
concerns that there is to much bells and whistles with IE, hence another
choice would definitely be preferable, if not for anything else so for the
size of IE.

Let me know if you still does not understand what I mean with associations,
and I will try once more.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rm Cobol 6.51 and JPEG Files

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktt42$lv4$1@news.imp.ch>`
- **References:** `<7kom7t$pns$1@news.imp.ch> <7kqus5$h0j$1@news.cerf.net> <7krm6v$ssj$1@news.imp.ch> <7ktjmb$ffl$1@news.cerf.net>`

```

Cheesle <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> schrieb in im Newsbeitrag:
7ktjmb$ffl$1@news.cerf.net...
wrote:
> I am happy if I could contribute, however, you have missed something,
sorry
> for my poor English.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Let me know if you still does not understand what I mean with
associations,
> and I will try once more.
>.....................
> Cheesle
>
Hello Cheesle

thank you for your concerns. I did understand what you explained with
associations and tried it.
I agree, that this the easiest way to open for example a JPEG file.
A problem I found by trying it was the following:
Because our JPEG Files were treated by Corel 8.0 the "associations" feature
resulted on each
open in a start of corel photopaint! I know how to change this with Start,
directory options,
file types..... (the strange names result because I have a german Win 98 and
I don't know to
translate them in correct English. By the way your English is great, my one
is poor!)

My cobol solution allows it now, to the operator to enter an article number
and if the
operator ends the entering with certain function key to show the
corresponding picture.
I call the I_View32.exe plus positioning of the picture on the screen.
Calling it with CALL "System" USING I_Viex32.exe filename, position, results
in an
immediately response in form of a picture on the selected position. It's
immediately and faster
than for example starting the I_View32 in the DOS Box. (About 2 seconds
waiting time).
It seems the I_View32 is started much faster by a CALL from Cobol.
We sell a lot to tourists here and they pay often in foreign money.

My next project is now to show the operator all the accepted types of
foreign money notes of the currency
the customer pays with. For example to avoid, that a french customer tries
to pay with a old 1000 franc
note of the 50's. Sound's great?! And all possible with an (new) old cobol
programm plus some JPEG
files!

Thank you again for your help

john@casagrande.ch
Casagrande & Co., Lucerne Switzerland
```

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)

```
"John Casagrande" <john@casagrande.ch> wrote:

>Hello together
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

John:

We provide an add-on dll to our COBOL sp2 product which supports the
ability to display JPG image files.  We also support both the 16 bit
and 32 bit versions of RM COBOL.

In addition, the method which is used to display the JPG bitmap screen
in sp2 is a CALL to our dynamic link library.

Our distributor in Germany is Donald Fitzgerald.  You can contact Mr.
Fitzgerald as follows:

EasiRun
Donald Fitzgerald
Am Arnsbacher Pfad 8
Usingen
Germany 61250

Phone:  49-608-191-603
Fax:    49-608-191-6049



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: Newbe: Creating a Cobol dll. How is it done ?? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Newbe: Creating a Cobol dll. How is it done ??

- **From:** "Svend" <svendl@enterdata.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ko4k3$ntn$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi,

I've got one "simple" question for you guys.
Being a C++ guy, I've been assigned to a project where
we're going to make use of some "old" cobol code in our new C++ GUI.

Since the Cobol-people on the project don't really understand the idead of
Dll's and such, they handed
it over to me (they got stuck in DOS :-) ).

Now, my question:
Do you have a sample program, wich compiles and links into a Dll, wich we
can use from our C++ app ? It's enough if it contains one function, taking 1
parameter, and
returns the same parameter back to the caller, as long as I can include the
file in a project and compile.

See, I really don't now how you would prototype such a function, so I'd
appreciate it if
you could give me an explanation of how it'f done, using MF Net Express 3.

Any help would be _greatly_ appreciated.

Thanks.

-Svend
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbe: Creating a Cobol dll. How is it done ??

- **From:** bpetit4354@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ku9be$ibd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7ko4k3$ntn$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi,


Here is a Dll: (Sample tested)

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. TESTDLL.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
      ****************************************************************
      *
      * Enable the PASCAL calling convention (number 3)
      * and call it WINAPI because it is used for WINAPI
      * functions.
      *
      ****************************************************************
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
	   CALL-CONVENTION 3 IS WINAPI.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      *
      * Short (Integer)  S9(4) Comp-5
      * Long  (Long)	 S9(8) Comp-5
      *
       01  VB-SHORT	 PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
       01  VB-LONG	 PIC S9(8) COMP-5.
       01  VB-CHAR	 PIC X(5).


       PROCEDURE DIVISION WINAPI.
       PROC-1.

	   ENTRY "testcall" USING BY VALUE VB-SHORT
				  BY VALUE VB-LONG
				  BY REFERENCE VB-CHAR.

	   IF VB-SHORT > VB-LONG
	      MOVE "A > B"  TO VB-CHAR
	      MOVE ZERO     TO RETURN-CODE
	   ELSE
	      MOVE "B > A"  TO VB-CHAR
	      MOVE 1	    TO RETURN-CODE
	   END-IF

	   EXIT PROGRAM.



To compile, link & debug:

       viscob testdll /debug+
       call vclinkxd testdll



Here under find TESTDLL.DEF:

       LIBRARY       testdll
       DESCRIPTION   'Visual COBOL Application'
       EXETYPE       WINDOWS
       CODE	      PRELOAD FIXED
       DATA	      PRELOAD FIXED SINGLE
       SEGMENTS      RTSCODE CLASS 'CODE' FIXED PRELOAD
       HEAPSIZE      8192
       EXPORTS       WEP			  @1 RESIDENTNAME
	             VC_GetFirstProcAddress	  @2
	             testcall			  @3


I hope you will understand.

Bernard




In article <7ko4k3$ntn$1@nntp.newmedia.no>,
  "Svend" <svendl@enterdata.no> wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

idead of

we

taking 1

include the

I'd

Express 3.
```

<a id="t-febab71d22"></a>
## Thread: Cookies — 3 msgs

### Cookies

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <rob _ a @ unipharm.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377009ec$0$32738@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
I developed a website that employs CGI scripts, and I used COBOL for that.
Acu-COBOL to be precise.

I have alot of customers using this site repeatedly on a daily basis and
some
of them are asking for Cookie ability, so that they do not need to keep
logging
into the website (username accessed).

I thought the browser was supposed to remember all of that, not the cookies.
And if so, do I need to code a 'cookie' into my CGI scripts.

Any enlightenment would be greatly appreciated.
_______________________________________
Robert Annandale

Auto reply has been spam proofed (I hope).
Reply to rob 'underscore' a 'at' unipharm 'dot' com
```

#### ↳ Re: Cookies

- **From:** swiegand@neo.rr.com (SkippyPB)
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F2120E60115BE742.C0907491933A41A0.04A9A6DEBA0D06ED@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<377009ec$0$32738@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
On Tue, 22 Jun 1999 15:16:43 -0700, "Robert Annandale" <rob _ a @
unipharm.com> enlightened us:

>I developed a website that employs CGI scripts, and I used COBOL for that.
>Acu-COBOL to be precise.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Robert Annandale

As I understand it, a cookie is nothing more than a file that is
usually kept in the user's browser's directory.  The web page would
look for this file (cookie) and insert the information from it into
the proper fields in the web page and then process it just as if the
user had typed the fields in.

Regards,

>
>Auto reply has been spam proofed (I hope).
>Reply to rob 'underscore' a 'at' unipharm 'dot' com
>
>

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

         My Law:  You can't fall off the floor


 Steve
```

- **From:** "Frank Y" <outofarea@accessld.net.NOSPAM>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1QPc3.1545$I3.49050@news12.ispnews.com>`

<a id="t-0ae6d88a46"></a>
## Thread: EBCDIC-ASCII conversions — 5 msgs

### EBCDIC-ASCII conversions

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kf8c3.28$Uh1.507@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
About a month ago I threatened to write up a "White Paper" on converting
COBOL data to other formats, and deal with the question of, "What do I do
about EBCDIC-ASCII conversions of COBOL data?"

Well, I started on the paper and some software I am going to throw in (this
will all be freeware), but before I get too deep into it I wanted to get
some opinions.

What I am looking at doing is dividing the paper into four main sections:

I Overview - EBCDIC, ASCII, Cobol and IEEE datatypes; why you cannot just
"download with conversion" .
II Moving COBOL data  from an EBCDIC machine to an ASCII machine, creating
standard IEEE data types.
III Moving COBOL data  from an EBCDIC machine to an ASCII machine, creating
COBOL data types.
IV.Moving COBOL data from an ASCII machine to an ASCII machine, creating
standard IEEE datatypes.

OK, does this look complete enough? Other ideas?

It is my intention to get this into a form such that it can serve as a
reference for all those people who ask, "How do I get data from my mainframe
so I can actually do something with it on my PC/Unix box in
COBOL/C/C++/BASIC/whatever?"

On the software side, someone recently posted some COBOL source using a
substitution table to go EBCDIC to ASCII. Would whomever that was please
contact me? I'd like to include it and want to make sure you get credit and
I don't go to jail for violating any copyrights.

For those questioners not having access to a COBOL compiler on the
destination box or who cannot get the COBOL code written with USAGE DISPLAY
SIGN SEPARATE on the mainframe, I am thinking about writing a DLL to handle
conversions, something like:

DECLARE SUB EBCDIC2ASCII ALIAS "Foo" LIB "Mike'sFreeLib.dll" (BufferIn AS
STRING, BufferOut AS STRING, Parameters as something) EXPORT

(I'll play with the exact parameters as I go along).

I want to create this in the form of a 32-bit DLL until I get the 16-bit DLL
compiler.
(That's right, I currently have a 32-bit DLL compiler but not a 16-bit).
Will this be useful? Is the 16-bit needed? Do I need an MS-DOS version,
too? Comments?

Lastly, for both the text and the software, I am looking for a couple of
volunteers to proof-read, edit and test. Anyone who participates gets a
credit.(No cash, no merchandise; but your Mom and Dad will be proud of you).

Please post suggestions and comments here; and even though it's bad
'netiquette' to ask for e-mail, I'd appreciate volunteers for testing and
editing writing directly to me.

Regards,
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC-ASCII conversions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7krduc$avj@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<Kf8c3.28$Uh1.507@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
You may want to mention that there ARE "national" flavors (or flavours) of
EBCDIC - so "EBCDIC" to "EBCDIC" isn't (even) always portable.

Similarly, true ASCII is only the 7-bit "stuff".  Everything in the
"top-half" of an 8-bit range is "national" (and/or company and/or operating
system) specific.
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC-ASCII conversions

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3771162C.C0D14BF6@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<Kf8c3.28$Uh1.507@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
At least the overview should also address double-byte character set
(DBCS), and Unicode.  I forsee the time when I will need to convert
these.

Similar questions could be addressed concerning sort sequences - not
only between character sets, but also between different languages and
capitalization.

Mention that it is possible to have a character in the old character set
but not the new one.

Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> About a month ago I threatened to write up a "White Paper" on converting
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> "download with conversion" .
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC-ASCII conversions

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktibf$evu$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<Kf8c3.28$Uh1.507@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote in message ...
>What I am looking at doing is dividing the paper into four main sections:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>OK, does this look complete enough? Other ideas?

This is only mainframe >>> PC, why not the other way around?

>For those questioners not having access to a COBOL compiler on the
>destination box or who cannot get the COBOL code written with USAGE DISPLAY
>SIGN SEPARATE on the mainframe, I am thinking about writing a DLL to handle
>conversions, something like:

I think this will be a great utility.

>I want to create this in the form of a 32-bit DLL until I get the 16-bit
DLL
>compiler.
>(That's right, I currently have a 32-bit DLL compiler but not a 16-bit).
>Will this be useful? Is the 16-bit needed? Do I need an MS-DOS version,
>too? Comments?

Although MS has claimed Y2K for Win 3.x, they are practically not supporting
it anymore. Considering those who will benefit such a utility I would
suggest you forget about the 16 bit version unless you yourself have joy of
doing it.

MS-DOS does not support DLL's not basically. Though, I believe Borland had
their Turbo Pascal 7 compiler for extended dos support DLL's (not using
graphical stuff of course), which would make it possible. So, what you would
have to do with MSDOS is to provide an .obj file for linking. It is my
opinion though, as stated above, those guys that want this, may probably
afford a win95 or better.

May I whish you good luck!

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: EBCDIC-ASCII conversions

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l05gq$69b$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<snip a lot>
> On the software side, someone recently posted some COBOL source using
> a substitution table to go EBCDIC to ASCII. Would whomever that was
> please contact me? I'd like to include it and want to make sure you
> get credit and I don't go to jail for violating any copyrights.
<snip>
> Regards,
> Michael Mattias
> Tal Systems
> Racine WI USA
> michael.mattias@gte.net

As Bill Klein pointed out in another reply to this post, there are many
different EBCDIC code pages in the IBM world. Every non-english language
has its own code page. OK, the letters A-Z and a-z, the numbers 0-9 and
some special signs have the same hex value in all code pages, but more
than a hundred signs are used very differently. Same for the ASCII code
page on the workstation.
So, if you write a ASCII2EBCDIC converter, you have to know both
codepages to translate the character strings correctly. Windows can give
you the current code page on the current PC, but you can only guess what
is the appropriate EBCDIC code page. You could pass the EBCDIC code page
as a parameter to your converter program and/or you could look for the
EBCDIC code page of the same language as the current ASCII code page.

I searched the IBM documentations for a complete set of translation
tables, but I found only Redbook with some samples. But I think that you
could transform most data with these few sample tables.

You can download the Redbook from:
http://www.redbooks.ibm.com/pubs/pdfs/redbooks/sg244846.pdf
It's the document number SG24-4846-00 with the title 'Multi-Language
Solutions for Client/Server Database Environment'. Opend the file with
Acrobat Reader and refer to the Appendix C: Sample code pages.
AFAIK, Redbooks are not restricted, you may use the samples without
breaking the laws!
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing problem with MF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ks17s$1k4@dfw-ixnews9.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Look at using the hex literal format (e.g. X"A1").  This has been supported
in all MF compilers for over a decade - also many other proprietary
compilers (such as IBM's) - and will be included in the next COBOL Standard.

I may be mistaken on the exact translation - but I think to get an (MF)
Alt-157, you would just convert the "157) which is decimal - to its hex
value and use that.  (You may need to add or subtract 1 - to handle ordinal
vs numbers starting at "00" - but I am not positive about that.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing problem with MF

- **From:** "Karl Wagner" <kewagner@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9_ec3.427$o6.8758@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
<bpetit4354@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi all, can you help me ?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>            Inspect My-Text converting ",f".?^?S >
to "������������o".
>
> I would like to print graphical characters:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Share what you know. Learn what you don't.

In general, MF COBOL uses the ANSI character set. Windows does not.
Windows provides a pair of API functions to deal with this, CharToOem()
and OemToChar(), where Char = Windows and Oem = ANSI.

The call is pretty trivial

 SPECIAL-NAMES.
     CALL-CONVENTION 74 IS APIENTRY.

 CALL APIENTRY "OemToChar" USING
      BY REFERENCE my-source-var
      BY REFERENCE my-target-var

 my-source-var and my-target-var can be the same variable.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing problem with MF

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktj4u$fcn$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <9_ec3.427$o6.8758@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Karl Wagner wrote in message <9_ec3.427$o6.8758@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>...
>In general, MF COBOL uses the ANSI character set. Windows does not.
>Windows provides a pair of API functions to deal with this, CharToOem()
>and OemToChar(), where Char = Windows and Oem = ANSI.

I think you mix up a little here, Windows does definitely use the ANSI
character set, I guess to talk of ASCII. You reference to the CharToOem and
OemToChar is however correct.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing problem with MF

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktj0g$f82$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
bpetit4354@my-deja.com wrote in message <7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi all, can you help me ?
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>I never had that kind of situation.

You do not say whether you are printing on a Windows computer or not.

I assume you're into windows.

I would give my guess that your application is old, that it's source is
entirely in ASCII, the runtime however, is that modern it uses the windows
printspooler. The Windows printspooler assumes ANSI characters to arrive,
not ASCII. Hence the mess. In addition to what you are already doing you got
the following options:

1. See if MF has an option of automatically convert from ASCII to ANSI when
printing.

2. Print directly bypassing the spooler.

3. Translate all your source and data to ANSI

If you are not into windows.

By some strange reason, most printer manufacturers they manage to provide
excellent localized software for their printer installation and maintenance,
the printer settings is however always american. I have yet to see any
printer direct from the manufacturer that is not set up with US letter paper
size and ditto character set, pretty annoying for us in Europe that do not
use Windows. Hey all, before you start yelling at me, I am targeting the
manufacturers, not US.
To the point: What you would have to do is to alter the default character
set of the printer to match your preferred.

If this doesn't help, may you be a little more precise with your question?

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing problem with MF

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kucaq$pa8$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7krha5$i65$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7ktj0g$f82$1@news.cerf.net> <7ku71p$hgd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>I'm printing on a Windows computer.
>MF Visual Cobol compiler is the core of MF Workbench.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I tried what you told me, but nothing until now.

You have to find out whether the MF application prints extended ascii or
ansi. It does not help very much that your application is ANSI if your data
is ascii. Set up the printer driver to print to a file. Open that file in
notepad and edit. Wherever the result is correct give you the answer. If it
displays correct in edit, then it is ascii, if it is correct in notepad it
is ansi. For the latter case, there must be something wrong with your
printer driver. If it is in ascii, you will have to continue inspect replace
if not MF has a switch you may use that translates on the fly.

Acucobol which I use, has a runtime switch which does the translation for
me.
Like:
    SET TRANSLATE-TO-ANSI TO 1.
    WRITE PRN-REC FROM "This is not an ansi string".
    SET TRANSLATE-TO-ANSI TO 0.
    WRITE PRN-REC FROM "This is in ansi".

I have set the TRANSLATE-TO-ANSI in my program startup once and for all,
hence no problems. Ask MF if they have something similar. If not, you will
have to deal with it manually as you do. Or, you may write directly to the
printer bypassing the spooler. That demands that you have set up the printer
to correct character set though (which may be a tedious task).

Cheesle
```

## Thread: OOP COBOL — 5 msgs

### OOP COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Poon" <mpoon10@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CyVb3.890$q6.25205@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Where can I find a good resource to learn OOP COBOL??
```

#### ↳ Re: OOP COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770358D.42976DC5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<CyVb3.890$q6.25205@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Michael Poon wrote:
> 
> Where can I find a good resource to learn OOP COBOL??

Michael, in order of preference :-

ISBN 0-9655945-0-5 - Elements of Object-Oriented Cobol - Wilson Price
        (http://www.objectz.com)  - Tutorial style - and file techniques

ISBN 0-13-261140-6 - Object-Oriented COBOL - Edmund C. Arranga and
Frank P Coyle ( I believe Edward Arranga has produced a later book)

        Theory and an example using a corporate in-house library system.

Enjoy ! - Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: OOP COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377041B0.A2EE40A1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<CyVb3.890$q6.25205@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Michael Poon wrote:
> 
> Where can I find a good resource to learn OOP COBOL??

Michael, 

Ooops ! I was a bit too swift on previous message - this is updated with
(B):-

(A) OO COBOL - in order of preference :-

ISBN 0-9655945-0-5 - Elements of Object-Oriented Cobol - Wilson Price
        (http://www.objectz.com)  - Tutorial style - and file techniques

ISBN 0-13-261140-6 - Object-Oriented COBOL - Edmund C. Arranga and
Frank P Coyle ( I believe Edward Arranga has produced a later book)

        Theory and an example using a corporate in-house library system.

(B) OO Background :

Not important to you at this stage, but for future reference, gives you
background on other OOPLs :-

ISBN 0-201-13688-0 Smalltalk 80 - the Language. 1989 - Adele Goldberg & 
David Robson. (Got mine from amazon.com). Smalltalk treats everything as
an object whereas we talk pic x and pic 9 etc..., turning them into
objects for GUIs. Adele was on the original Xerox PARC team. Interesting
for treatment of collections/dictionaries which COBOL 2000 has copied.

ISBN 1-57870-009-4  Handbook of Programming Languages - Volume I -
Object-Oriented Programming Languages - Series Editor-in-Chief - Peter
H.Salus.

Background on OO and the OO languages - Smalltalk, C++, Eiffel, Ada 95,
Modula-3, Java - no mention of COBOL (??????). The academic world can be
just as vitriolic as the rest of us - Adele Goldberg relates how she got
shanghaied (no pun intended!) into giving a lecture on Smalltalk to
Steve Jobs and the early Apple crowd - so that they could walk away with
the keys to the Xerox vault. A couple of Smalltalk people take a knock
at Java. An interesting chapter heading, "C++ Traps and Pitfalls".

Enjoy ! - Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-74595ea038"></a>
## Thread: Importing decimal values from a csv-file — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Importing decimal values from a csv-file

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37709BD4.E6FA9F8D@Triumph-International.de>`

```
Hi all,

I'm sure it's easy if I only knew....

I have to import some decimal values from a character delimited file
Example for a line to import:
10002;234;2.34;10023.34;

Unstring ... delimited by is working to get each field. But when I try
to move a string "2.34" to a variable defined as pic s9(7)V9(2) I get an
error. So how can this be done in a short way?

TIA - Stefan Meyer
```

#### ↳ Re: Importing decimal values from a csv-file

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770ceb3.85129972@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<37709BD4.E6FA9F8D@Triumph-International.de>`

```
On Wed, 23 Jun 1999 10:33:25 +0200, Stefan Meyer
<Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de> wrote:

>Hi all,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming a modern compiler, the following should work:

01  Unstrung-Field Pic X(13).
01  Numeric-Field Pic S9(7)V99.

Compute Numeric-Field = Function Numval (Unstrung-Field)

If there might be $ symbols in the data, use NUMVAL-C instead of
NUMVAL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Importing decimal values from a csv-file

- **From:** Stefan Meyer <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770D217.21F3BEC4@Triumph-International.de>`
- **References:** `<37709BD4.E6FA9F8D@Triumph-International.de> <3770ceb3.85129972@news1.ibm.net>`

```
Thanks...I forgot to look at intrinsic functions. I always forget them to
use. So sorry.

Stefan Meyer

Thane Hubbell wrote:

> On Wed, 23 Jun 1999 10:33:25 +0200, Stefan Meyer
> <Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-International.de> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> NUMVAL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Importing decimal values from a csv-file

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@ford.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kr3sa$ofq2@eccws1.dearborn.ford.com>`
- **References:** `<37709BD4.E6FA9F8D@Triumph-International.de>`

```
Try

 01  source-data                 VALUE '1.23'        PIC X(12).
 01  work-field                  JUST RIGHT          PIC X(12).
 01  work-number REDEFINES work-field                PIC Z(9).99.
 01  destin-field                VALUE ZERO   COMP-3 PIC S9(9)V99.
 01  sub1                                     COMP   PIC S9(9).


* Find the last character used in the source field
     PERFORM
       VARYING sub1 FROM LENGTH OF source-data BY -1
       UNTIL sub1 <= 1 OR
             source-data(sub1:1) NOT = SPACE
       CONTINUE
     END-PERFORM
* right justify the data into the destination field
     MOVE source-data(1:sub1)        TO work-field
* convert the formatted number
     MOVE work-number                TO destin-field.

Stefan Meyer wrote in message
<37709BD4.E6FA9F8D@Triumph-International.de>...
>Hi all,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
