<a id="t-61139a7216"></a>
## Thread: Cobol inspection check list — 1 msgs

### Cobol inspection check list

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3770D5B0.EF33E145@zip.com.au>`

```
Ray

Sorry it took so long, here is my first attemp.  Note I divorce
the style issues by saying that it complies with the style guide
that you have in place.  This should be supplemented by a style
checklist.  The focus of the walkthough is to find problems NOT
make the code pretty.

Rip this to shreds, I will post a Word doc on my web page once
this has been hashed by the group.  I am currently reading Yourdon
and will get to Gilb to understand this topic totally.  The one
thing I have picked up is that the checklist must fit on a page
(about 600 words).

1. The checklist
----------------
1. Code compiles before walk through
2. Code has passed automated quality checks (none implemented yet)
3. Code complies with coding standards
4. Program does what it is supposed to do
5. Variables and procedure names comply with naming standards
6. Names used are meaningful
7. Names used do what they imply.
8. Comments are meaningful, do not restate what the code does.
9. Program comments - does the program definition fit the program.
10. Are variable sizes appropriate, do the calculations have any
errors?
11. Code is maintainable.

1.1	Inputs required
-----------------------
ï¿½ Time - an effective review can not be achieved without proper
preparation.  It is not to be achieved with a quick skim of the
code.
ï¿½  A listing for each participant.  They should be given the
listing 24 hours before the actual meeting to allow time to go
through the program before hand.
ï¿½ Requirement documents available for review by all participants.
ï¿½ Any output generated from a preceding walk though.

1.2	Outcomes required
-------------------------
One of the following: Acceptance without modification, Acceptance
with minor modifications, walk through again.
If the program is accepted with minor modifications then a second
review will be done.  This review is by a person other than the
change author after the changes have been completed.
If the program is walked through again then all other outputs go
into the next walk through.
The review meeting should produce a list of the changes required. 
This can be in the form of an annoted program listing and a sheet
outlining any further details required.  These comments may in
fact highlight limitations with the design of the application
rather than the programmer's implementation.

2.	What is a walk through
------------------------------
A walk through is a process of eliminating problems before they
become major problems or even take time debugging.

....  to be extended, I assume you are sold on this .....

3.	Definitions
--------------------
3.1	Code compiles before walk through
----
The program must be complete before a walk through.  If the code
is incomplete you are giving advice and assistance not providing a
detailed working through of the code. While there is a probability
that you will go over some design issues while you are looking at
the code it is unlikely that you will step through the program
from beginning to end.

3.1.1	The code should compile without warnings.
-----
If the programmer leaves warnings in the code then there are
potential errors missed.  Some warnings produced by the compiler
can be safely ignored (eg move corresponding with filler items)
however it is often easy to work around these warnings giving a
clean compile.

3.2	Code has passed automated quality checks (none implemented
yet)
----
At a later date there will be some basic checking commands
developed to ensure simple questions are handled without human
interaction.  This will be advised as it becomes available.

3.3	Code complies with coding standards
----
Please refer to **your standards** for details:

3.4	Program does what it is supposed to do
----
This requires an understanding of the complete user requirements
and also the complete system that the program integrates.
It is very common for a one-line program modification to be
accepted and create serious problems with the system in which it
resides due to lack of consideration for the total picture. 
Impacts on other programs must be considered when modifying a
system.
The program must be reviewed with the user requirements in mind. 
It is possible that the author of the change has a different
concept than the designer.  If the code is superficially looked at
then this point is commonly missed.  It is also possible that the
author has the correct interpretation and the design document is
incorrect.  It is possible also that both the design and code
changes are incorrect.
This is difficult to pick up but the rewards of finding these
errors far outweigh trivial formatting errors in code.

3.5	Variables and procedure names comply with naming standards
----
Please refer to ** your standrds** naming conventions.  This
document outlines some abbreviations that are in use.

These are to be adhered to for; DB2 names, working storage
variables and file variables.

Some examples of what to check for:
- NO is not allowed.  It should be NBR (number) or NOT.
- NMBR is not allowed, correct abbreviation is NBR
- NUMBER is not allowed, it should be abbreviated to the standard
NBR

3.6	Names used are meaningful
----
Variables such as WS-SUB, WS-X and X are not to be used.
Paragraph names such as B100-READ-NEXT are not to be used, use
B100-READ-NEXT-CUSTOMER.

3.7	Names used do what they imply
----
Paragraphs must do what they imply.
ï¿½ READ-EMPLOYEE-FILE does not read the warehouse file, nor does it
calculate pay rates.  It should only read records.
ï¿½ Paragraphs with a name ending in -EXIT must only contain one
statement 'EXIT'.
Variables are used to store the values that the name describes.
ï¿½ WS-CUSTOMER-NBR is not used to store a temporary value in a
calculation.

3.8	Comments are meaningful, do not restate what the code does
---
This is probably the most difficult to judge.  It is often very
difficult to divorce the actual code from the comments provided.

* THIS CURSOR IS USED TO EXTRACT ALL THE ROWS FROM TM002H00     *
* ACCORDING TO THE SELECTION CRITERIA                           *

              EXEC SQL
                DECLARE MAIN_CSR1 CURSOR WITH HOLD FOR
                      SELECT REQUEST_NBR
                            ,CUST_NBR
                            ,ACTION_CD
                            ,ACTUAL_COMPLTD_DT
                            ,ACTN_RSN_CD
                            ,LM101_GRP_CUST_NBR
                        FROM TM002H00
                            ,LM101T00
                       WHERE ACTUAL_COMPLTD_DT = '01/01/0001'
                         AND ACTION_CD IN ('B', 'D', 'E', 'H',
'K', 'L')
                         AND PROC_STAT_CD = 'I'
                         AND CUST_NBR          = LM101_CUST_NBR
                       ORDER BY 1, 2
                WITH UR
              END-EXEC.

The above comment might have been better stated as 'select action
codes that require a down load file for processing'.

Included in this point is that if a piece of code is discussed for
a reasonable period of time without modification then that piece
of code needs a descriptive comment.

3.9	Program comments - does the program definition fit the program
----
Program comments are the comments at the top of the program. 
These should succinctly describe the function of the program. 
Below this is a log of the changes applied to this program in date
order (most recent change first).

Often programs are modified and small changes made to the internal
logic but the comments at the top are not altered to reflect the
current function.  This can also occur when one program is used
for a skeleton to another program.

For example a simple change may remove a restriction on a SQL
select and the comments in the immediate vicinity are changed (say
the paragraph comment) but the comment at the top of the program
still says that it is restricted.  When analysis on another change
comes along the program may be skipped for analysis because of
this comment.

3.10	Are variable sizes appropriate, do the calculations have any
errors
-----
It is possible that the variables storing numbers will be to small
to hold the potential values.

There are sometimes problems with the number of places in a
calculation.  For example 'PIC9V99 = PIC99 / 100' may give
different (and incorrect results) than 'PIC9V99 = PIC99 /
100.00'.  This will vary as the compiler changes but those errors
must be known and validated.

3.11	Code is maintainable
-----
This is a personal issue and must be done with care.  The
structure should follow the classic three-step process in the main
body:
	Perform a000-initialise
	Perform b000-process-records
		until end-of-records
	Perform c000-terminate
The code should not make it difficult to determine the underlying
business logic.

Extreme care must be taken with this item.  If the program is not
'what you would have done' consider whether the program meets the
objectives of the business and is readable.  DO NOT use 'it works'
as a judgement on this issue, while this is important the code
must be understandable, over the life of a program twice the cost
of writing it will be spent on maintenance, badly written programs
always take more.

Is the code extensible without modification?  
ï¿½ Is the selection criterion really static or can it be
parameterised for greater flexibility in the future.
```

<a id="t-f062c0c9a3"></a>
## Thread: RmCobol: exception status after calling... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RmCobol: exception status after calling...

- **From:** "Paulo Cruz" <scruz_paulo@mail.teleweb.pt>
- **Date:** 1999-06-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kqr56$nb5$1@fenix.maxitel.pt>`

```
Hi all
Although working with cobol for some  years now, it recently struck my
attention the fact that in RmCobol an exception status can't be caught after
a <call> statement whereas that is possible after a <call program>
statement.
As a matter of fact, in both cases one is able to detect the >exception
condition< but not the >exception status<.
Does this make sense ?
Any ideas on how to overcome this seemingly lack of consistency on the
compiler side ?
Thnks
PCruz
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to Fujitsu Cobol - need some help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ku93j$3pk@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<930243064.873.28@news.remarQ.com> <jxtc3.1682$4e1.79864@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7ku4va$17c$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Jason Bailey <jbailey5@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:7ku4va$17c$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> Docdwarf,
  <all original comments snipped>

Althought I sometimes (often?) disagree with DD's posting of his standard
"Do your own homework," I agree with it completely in this case (where the
student did not show any process of getting where they were).  Should there
be any question about the general issue of "homework questions" in this NG,
the following is (verbatim) what the COBOL FAQ says on the topic.  (You be
the judge of how the original question fits this.)

"18.1 Can I get help with homework via the newsgroups?

Yes, you can get help with your homework via these newsgroups. HOWEVER, that
does mean that you will receive help - you will NOT find many participants
who will be very happy if you ask them to do your homework FOR YOU. Some
hints that you should consider when drafting your post for assistance with
homework include:

 - Make it clear from the beginning that you are asking for homework help.
(Most of the newsgroup participants are very good at sniffing out those who
try and pose homework questions as "business need" questions - and they are
not very polite in replying to such questions).

  - Make certain that you specify what compiler and operating system your
homework is targeted at. Solutions may vary significantly based on this.
(You might also want to include what text book you are using.)

 - The more information that you can give that demonstrates that you really
have tried to solve the problem yourself (using your text book, class
material/presentations, lab assistance, etc), the more likely it is that you
will get useful and friendly responses. If you let us know what you have
found on your own and why you are still uncertain or confused, you will
usually get helpful responses; if it looks like you are asking us questions
without trying to solve it yourself, you are likely to get very pointed
replies.

NOTE: if you are looking for a COBOL tutor, (and don't want to see the "Do
your own homework" notes that occur in the COBOL newsgroups), you might want
to try,

   http://www.tutoraid.org/

"
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l5d9k$m52$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<930243064.873.28@news.remarQ.com> <7ku4va$17c$1@ash.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <7ku93j$3pk@dfw-ixnews4.ix.netcom.com> <37761c8a.288672584@news1.ibm.net> <Rgpd3.13781$4e1.117086@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...

>Mr Hubbel, a most interesting series of statements... now, pray tell,
>given what has been available for all to read here on the newsgroup what,
>outside of a Great Act of Charity, would have allowed anyone else to come
>to a similar conclusion?
>
Not to quibble Doc, but what, outside your preconceived ideas, led you to
believe otherwise? Note that there are lots of times I agree with you, and I
also tend to ignore rather stupid and/or trivial questions, particularly
when the answer can be found in any reference manual.

However, that is often not the case, and often even silly "problems" can
stump and experienced programmer for a variety of reasons, including simple
terminal blindness.  After all, every problem is trivial once you know the
answer.

An example is a similar answer that you gave yesterday on the status "02:"
problem for a sequential read.  Normally, I would have ignored it. You told
Mike to do his own homework.  I answered.  The reason is simple.  I know
that Mike is a *very* competent programmer.  I also knew he was coding in
the field, in a shack at the bottom of a quarry, with no manuals.  The
reason that I know all that is because he is my employee, and my phone was
off the hook.

Assumptions, Doc.
```

```
Precisely... but what is easy for many folks, it seems, is the plea of 'Do

'please'!

>    Nearly 99% of everything asked here (and answered concisely, I might

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Strange what you remember and call 'flaming'; if you believe that the
statement of:

'Please do your own homework'

is 'flaming' then I wonder what you make of

'Please pass the salt'

... but, be that as it may, permit me to ask... do you recall seeing me
respond to a posting which contained a snippet of code, or even a URL
directing one towards code, with 'Do your own homework'?  I don't.

If effort has been shown I respond with effort... if someone asks 'Where
do I find sample code to do a match/merge program on two sequential input
files... just so I can read it, of course' or 'What does File Status 39
mean?... well, the first is a standard bit of homework and the second
found in a manual.

>    There are many students who visit this newsgroup for insight from pros
>like ourselves.  They need nurturing as they grow not snide comments.

'Nurturing'?  Do you *honestly* want to encourage folks into your
profession who show absolutely no initiative aside from 'How do I do
this?'?  It may make *you* feel all warm and fuzzy if you get treated as a
Walking Manual/Textbook... me, it leaves cold.

>I for
>one call for anyone to post any question relating to COBOL in this forum.

You can call for Phillip Morris, for all I care, and you are welcome to do
so... but if it appears to me that someone is asking me to do homework I
will request otherwise.

>This is a forum of knowledge and as such there should never be a stunting of
>exchange of commentary.

Ummmmm... why are you so concerned with the commentary I am exchanging?

>    Lighten up and if you don't want to help someone, then don't.  There's
>certainly no guns to heads here.  But quit with the "Do your homework!"
>posts.

Please do not stunt my commentary.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New to Fujitsu Cobol - need some help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00

```
beginner

willing

each
other.

one
of them sent me an address where I possible could get help for my problem.

I'm sure the reaction to my posting is not representative for COBOL
programmers
and I know there are more nice and professional people in this business but
maybe

H. Kaempf
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New to Fujitsu Cobol and to COBOL programming - need some help

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%Q5d3.3733$nN3.22476@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<930243064.873.28@news.remarQ.com> <930362951.962.18@news.remarQ.com>`

```
Hansjoerg Kaempf wrote:
>I bought 2 books (COBOL in 24 hours and COBOL unleashed) installed the
>compiler which came with the books and started to learn. So far all
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>should, but they don't work.

When you say "they don't work", I'm not sure if you mean your menu
structure doesn't work, or that the subprograms aren't called when you
select them.  If the menu structure doesn't function properly, then
COBOL Unleashed chapter 25, "Character and Screen Based Interfaces",
covers this topic.  The sample program, a simple name and address
database, can be found on the CD as \source\CHAP25\NAMES.COB.  Of
course, there are a gazillion ways to do anything, but if you follow
the methods used in NAMES, your menu structure will work, just as
NAMES works.  If your menu structure works but the subprograms aren't
being executed, then it could be any of a whole range of possible
issues.  We need more info.
```

---

<a id="t-22e4e85144"></a>
## Thread: converting from c++ to mf cobol — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### converting from c++ to mf cobol

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Iwc3.23$q5.269285@news1.i1.net>`

```
I'm trying to convert the line below to cobol but I'm a bit confused on the
"(PCHAR) &iValToSet" part.  could some one give me some insight in how to
convert this:

    setsockopt(hSocket,SOL_SOCKET,SO_REUSEADDR,(PCHAR)
&iValToSet,sizeof(iValToSet));



This is the whole code for the line above:

BOOL TCPSetSockOpt(INT  hSocket)
{
    INT     iValToSet;
    LINGER  linger;
    DWORD dwNoBlock;

    iValToSet = 1;

    setsockopt(hSocket,SOL_SOCKET,SO_REUSEADDR,(PCHAR)
&iValToSet,sizeof(iValToSet));



Thanks

Tom Wright
```

#### ↳ Re: converting from c++ to mf cobol

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<POwc3.2638$LI5.241430@axe.netdoor.com>`
- **References:** `<9Iwc3.23$q5.269285@news1.i1.net>`

```
COBOL isn't known for it's treatment of pointers.  There is no way to
dereference a pointer and fields with a USAGE POINTER are rarely used
outside of calling a sub program.  FWIW, parameters are passed by reference
when calling sub programs unless explicitly specified otherwise.

Tom Wright wrote in message <9Iwc3.23$q5.269285@news1.i1.net>...
>I'm trying to convert the line below to cobol but I'm a bit confused on the
>"(PCHAR) &iValToSet" part.  could some one give me some insight in how to
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: converting from c++ to mf cobol

- **From:** "Darius Cooper" <Darius_cooper@Bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JYnd3.277$eK.63@news.megsinet.net>`
- **References:** `<9Iwc3.23$q5.269285@news1.i1.net>`

```
The (PCHAR) &iValToSet passes the address of the variable iValToSet. Using
only &iValToSet would accomplish the same thing, but the called function
expects the adress to point to a character. So, the (PCHAR) casts the
integer address to a character address.

As fo converting to COBOL, all you ought to need is a COBOL S9(9) COMP
variable [or whatever has the same length as a C integer on your system;
i.e. whatever would have a size equal to the word-size of your machine).

When your COBOL program calls the sub-routine and passes the COBOL variable,
the COBOL program will pass the address (by default).

Actually, the bigger problem would be passing items that the C-program
expects to be passed "by value".

What I have done in some cases is to write a layer of C functions as a
"wrapper" over the C-functions that I want to call.

Have fun,

- Darius Cooper


Tom Wright wrote in message <9Iwc3.23$q5.269285@news1.i1.net>...
>I'm trying to convert the line below to cobol but I'm a bit confused on the
>"(PCHAR) &iValToSet" part.  could some one give me some insight in how to
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-3502712284"></a>
## Thread: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** John Giddings <john.giddings@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com>`

```
I work at a company that has a WANG mainframe. We have been using the
ca-realia workbench on a limited scale for some pc programming. We hope
to do a lot more pc programming after we finish y2k changes. I have
recently acquired the book titled "COBOL UNLEASHED"---Which is a great
book by the way-- and noticed a part of it that said Micro focus is a
much better product than CA-REALIA. I was curious as to what the groups
opinions were. We have been getting the yearly upgrades and paying the
maintenance fees and I would like to know if a switch may be necessary
before we get dependent on one or the other.
Also if there any suggestions for other compilers, I would be glad to
hear those as well.

Thank you.
John Giddings

john.giddings@mindspring.com
```

#### ↳ Re: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wuUc3.276$iK6.25330@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com>`

```

John Giddings <john.giddings@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com...

We started using REALIA (later CA-REALIA) in 1986. Fine product.
Then bought by CA. Their Improvements got bogged down in trying to
implement the Workbench product - which finally arrived. Their corporate
philosophy seemed to be directed toward mainframe development on the
PC. Another part of their problem was concurrent development of
Visual Realia, since abandoned.

In developing for Windows, we chose Fujitsu - mainly for price - and
were overwhelmed with the features it offered compared to Realia,
including native COBOL (such as "FUNCTION xxxxx").

Also, CA has their annual technical conference in New Orleans and
the mayor of that city doesn't like gun manufacturers.

In sum, while not knowing much about MF, it seems to be the
industry standard and you probably won't go wrong if you choose it.
'Course, it's not my money.
```

#### ↳ Re: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3773034F.48D3FA59@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com>`

```
John Giddings wrote:
> 
> I work at a company that has a WANG mainframe. We have been using the
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> john.giddings@mindspring.com

Originally programmed in RM/COBOL V2 on Radio Shack Models II and 16
with M/S Xenix. Ugh - Not a SINGLE example program. But to give them
their due, like the Energizer Bunny its still chugalugging under Windows
until I get the literally hundreds of data files transferred to
NetExpress.

My end-user is a technocrat and ying-yanged me around looking at various
compilers. Originally used Microsoft until they gracefully bowed out to
MicroFocus (MS V5/ MF V3).

- Reviewed RM/COBOL but must confess bias because of above. Then the
end-user dumped a book on my desk, Liant's Graphics, which was as big as
the Good Book. (PS - "dumped" is used in its UK context, not that of N.
America !).

-  Looked at CA-Realia - the impression I formed was that it aint true
COBOL - they've added COBOL onto their stable of products urging you to
use their Visual Basic (whatever it was called). Strange lot, having
bought the product, when you quit they want everything back or a
certificate to say you have destroyed it.

-  Got an early copy of Fujitsu. Instructions said you could use any
editor - well I couldn't and they didn't answer my e-mail. And (personal 
idiosyncrasy) didn't like all the UPPERCASE stuff and complete Division
syntax. I'm sure it is a good product, (others have told me so), but the
version I looked at wasn't integrated. May have improved now.

- NetExpress - difficult to be impartial isn't it ? The original
attraction was Object Orientation (NOT THE CURRENT PRICE !) I'll stick
my neck out and IMHO the best IDE(Integrated Development Environment),
pretty good text-editor with typo errors being highlighted, (powerful
search tools for currently displayed source and I can check out where
I've used 'invoke MyParticularClass' in seconds across the whole
project). Has a MicroFocus Dialog Editor, also Dialog System, (their
Visual Basic toolkit, if you will), and tools for Internet etc...
Compile and link, individual programs or whole project, and a feature I
really like - if I change a copybook all, programs affected
automatically get re-compiled.

But it does have its imperfections - Dialog System covers (pure guess)
70 - 80% of what you might want to do with Windowing. OO/GUI covers
everything but lacks a really comprehensive set of examples - and,
unless you are already a Windows aficionado -  having figured out the
OO, it takes a while to get the GUI stuff straight in your head.

You could check out Accubol. And there are some sharewares - but one,
(wont quote name as I'm not sure), - its got to be dead in the water -
they reckon their approach to file-handling is better and have
substituted their own set of non-standard file VERBS - they might just
sell it to the Man on the Moon !

Don't know if that helps - you go figure !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** "Charles F. Townsend" <ctowns@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37738A3D.C6E08899@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com>`

```
John,
I am partial to Synkronix PERCobol (a little bias),  PERCobol supports Wang
Cobol syntax, popular IBM, HP and  some MF syntax.  PERCobol compiler is
available on a wide variety of platforms OS/390, OS/400, AIX, OS/2, HP-UX,
HP-MPE/iX, Linux, SCO, Solaris X/.86, Solaris Sparc, Windows NT and Windows
95/98,  Using PERCobol you can create Applications, Applets, Servlets,
COBOL Beans and build server side and client side applications.   PERCobol
supports building CICS Clients and IMS Clients.  Many popular technologies
can be used with PERCobol such as MQSeries, NFS, WebNFS and SQL data.

More information about PERCobol  and BlueJ (Synkronix graphical application
painter for COBOL and Java) can be located at http://www.synkronix.com

Regards,

Chuck Townsend
Synkronix, Inc.
http://www.synkronix.com
```

```
a look at: www.acucorp.com.
```

#### ↳ Re: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** John <jchafin@cgwinc.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<7l0moa$dua$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
John,

I've got 2 versions of Micro Focus sitting in a closet. I like CA-
Realia better. Coming from a mainframe environment, it was easier for
me to relate to the CA-Realia Workbench than to Micro Focus.

I currently use Workbench 3.0 and Flexus SP2 (for the gui) and am very
pleased with both. CA and Flexus provide first quality support. The CA
debugger and source editor are excellent. The Flexus forms designer is
comprehensive and easy to use. I use Installshield (another great tool)
for system deployment. The Ca-Realia and SP2 runtimes appear to be
bullet proof (I haven't had a single problem).

Hope this helps.

John Chafin
```

#### ↳ Re: CA-REALIA vs Microfocus

- **From:** redsky@ibm.net (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37762499.290735754@news1.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3772C660.73EB4437@mindspring.com>`

```
On Thu, 24 Jun 1999 19:59:28 -0400, John Giddings
<john.giddings@mindspring.com> wrote:

My take on the various compilers.

This does not cover all of them - only those with which I have
performed actual development.  

1.  Realia - Fastest file access there is.  Unless you use Btrieve,
however, you get file locking only.  Writing a new system using this
works just fine.  I have several networked systems and I just keep the
files closed when not accessing them.  You have to be careful with
open order and close order, to avoid deadly embrace. 

Windows development - 3rd party tools are the best bet - I use COBOL
sp2 from Flexus.  Natively, as I am sure you know, Visual Realia is
"Functionally Stabilized"  - So is my great grand father  - I think
you get the idea.

2.  MicroFocus -  Full suite of tools.  More than you want or desire
for the price sometimes.  Slow file access, but with good record
locking semantics.  Moderate integrity of files.  We had some serious
problems with "mysteriously growing files" when using compression that
MF eventually fixed.  This is the buggiest development system I have
used.  Their Windows GUI solution, Dialog system is inadequet, buggy,
unstable, alien in syntax, and difficult to grasp.  Even with that
though, I developed a full scale POS application using it, that does
work.  Many problems getting the systems to work when distributed
though.  I quit that job when Netexpress 2 came out.  Reportedly,
Dialog is better in v3, but my collegues there say that that the
projects still get corrupted.  MF has the most complete suite of tools
for offloading mainframe development, of the tools  I have used.

3.  Fujitsu - FASTEST and I mean FAST code execution.  Second fastest
file access.  Files seem stable, with record locking.  Documentation
is complete, but sometimes difficult to navigate.  Not great for
EBCDIC support presently.  Their Windows GUI solution is PowerCOBOL -
which I do not use.  I use the 32 bit Sp2 from Flexus with my Fujitsu
COBOL.  Fujitsu gives the most bang for the buck and it is my 32 bit
solution.  Not a buggy compiler, compared to the others.  Some
problems with printing to the "Generic Text Printer" - I overcome by
using another Flexus product - Form Print.  

For the PRICE especially, you can't beat Fujitsu COBOL - and for a
conversion FROM Wang to Fujitsu I think its a great choice.  I know
someone who has done just that.
```

<a id="t-9c50c24536"></a>
## Thread: Cobol digit conversion programming — 3 msgs

### Cobol digit conversion programming

- **From:** xzli@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ktrum$cu9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Dear Sir/Madam,


I have a problem in cobol programming.   i.e.


How to move   X(16)  format   number  into  s9(13)v99  format numeric
data like 68.25?



Original code  can work under  Mainframe system ,  but doesn't work
under Microfocus Cobol.

Original code is :


DATA DIVISION.

        01 AR-CPCUDETL-REC.
         03  AR-REC-TYPE          PIC X(001).
         03  AR-TRANS-TYPE        PIC X(003).
         03  FILLER               PIC X(357).
         03  AR-PREM-AMT          PIC X(016).
         03  FILLER               PIC X(423).
      *


        03  WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR.
          05  WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR1     PIC 9(13)    VALUE ZEROES.
          05  WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR2     PIC 99        VALUE ZEROES.

        03  WS-PREM-AMT-NUM   REDEFINES WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR.
          05  WS-PREM-AMT-NUM1      PIC S9(13)V99.


        01    WS-TOTAL             S9(13)V99 Value  0.


PROCEDURE DIVSION.



             MOVE AR-PREM-AMT(1:13)       TO   WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR1
             MOVE AR-PREM-AMT(15:2)       TO   WS-PREM-AMT-CHAR2


             ADD WS-PREM-AMT-NUM1      TO   WS-TOTAL.

      *  when above code  running,  it
      *  will report error of illegal charater in the numeric field.



I also try to move the charater varible to  display type numeric  like

       9(13).99  or -9(13).99

I still cannot use it, it will report same error.


Do you have any good idea to deal with this kind of moving value from
character?



Please send a copy of response to my email   xzli@hotmail.com




sincerely


John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

```
Back in 1954 when we wrote a program, we wrote a test data generator for
that program. The good news was that the programs down stream using the
files generated had test data made by that test data.  The bad news was (and
I would guess, still is) getting all of the possible test cases in the test
data. However, in today's world, I'll bet there are some good general
purpose test data generators.

Warren Simmons
```

<a id="t-dce56e22f0"></a>
## Thread: ANSI vs ASCII characters — 2 msgs

### ANSI vs ASCII characters

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ku9ne$mik@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```
Am I the only one who is confused?

In another thread there are several references to ANSI vs ASCII characters.
As someone who follows (reasonably closely) all the relevant Standards, I
don't know what they are talking about.

The ISO Standard for ASCII (which is only a 7-bit, not 8-bit definition) is
identical to the ANSI Standard (which also defines only the 7-bit
characters).  There is ONE (minor) difference and that has to do with the
currency sign symbol - and even that is defined in BOTH as being one of
several "symbols".  (Actually, I think the tilde vs the not-sign are also
"known" variations that both definitions allow.)

When it comes to the 8-bit character sets, then both ANSI and ISO (for
ASCII) are almost completely silent.  In other words, anything in the
"top-half" of an 8-bit character set is "up in the air" and not a part of
any ANSI or ASCII definition (or rather there are LOTS of conflicting
definitions for them).

Given all of this, can someone help me understand what is really meant by
the discussion of "ASCII" vs "ANSI" characters???

P.S.  If we start getting into DBCS (double-byte character sets) including
ISO 10646 or Unicode, or similar "variations" - there is an entire other
discussion - but neither of these would (normally?) be called either ASCII
or ANSI character sets.
```

#### ↳ Re: ANSI vs ASCII characters

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-06-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kubnv$p4o$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7ku9ne$mik@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein wrote in message <7ku9ne$mik@dfw-ixnews3.ix.netcom.com>...
>Am I the only one who is confused?
>Given all of this, can someone help me understand what is really meant by
>the discussion of "ASCII" vs "ANSI" characters???

I am sorry for probably being one of those to contribute to your confusion,
however, I will hereby try to heal the "damage".

You are absolutely correct that the ASCII is 7 bit. However, outside of USA,
this "label" has been used as the notation of the 8 bit set holding a number
of 256 characters, although it is not technically correct.

The problem we europeans struggle with, is precisely that you americans do
not have to pay attention to the 8 bit set. When the ANSI set where defined,
this was apparently ruled by Americans since the match of the first 7 byte
is (as you point out) almost identical, at least practical identical. The
rest of the letters in the ANSI characterset and the extended "ascii" set is
widely different. This is a big problem for developers targeting the
european market, or any market for that matter that has a character set
which extends the standard ascii. Most older applications in the PC market
dealed well with the characters, because there were national charactersets
on the national computers. Though, problems rose then too, like I mentioned
before, printers were preconfigured for american sheets and character sets
and so on. I can tell you I have been through many nasty printer setups
throughout the years :-)

The predefined printer setup isn't that big issue, if you use the windows
printer spooler, knows to deal with the driver/spooler *and* has a native
windows application. Native in the sense that it is entirely ansi coded and
has ansi data.

Funny things raise when you move applications, I have been into the
following:

    Application coded in ascii, data in ascii

    Application coded in ansi, data in ascii

    Application coded in ascii, data in ansi

All of these needs special preparation to be able to run. They turn
particularly difficult if you are so "lucky" to use an IDE for application
development. Consider loading an existing application coded in ascii into a
fully windows IDE that deals with ANSI only... It really turns out to be a
mess.

It is really amusing to run such programs under windows, whether to screen
or printer. What you expect to get is definitely not what you get.

The problem is our dear european characters, here is my history:

Back in the early days, there were 7 bits computers and printers (some of
the latter actually still in use today in Norway), Norwegian letters then
replaced the [\] and {|}, in other words, sorting were excellent, after all,
end users had rarely use of this characters.

So, the evolution to 8 bit arrived and national characters seemed to be
placed occasionally at best.
The Norwegian special letters ae, oe, aa or AE, OE, AA (I presume I cannot
show them real due too, guess what :-) ). The order I have presented them
above is the standard sort order. However, when put into the extended
character set and ANSI, they were placed in an unsortable order. Look:

Character            Ext Ascii            ANSI
ae                        145                     230
oe                        155                     248
aa                        134                     229
AE                        146                    198
OE                        157                    216
AA                        143                    197

As you can see, developers for the norwegian market does not get anything
free. Same applies to
Germany, France, Spain to mention a few.

So, basically what OemToAnsi and AnsiToOem does is to map back and forth.
These functions are a part of the Windows API, constructed to deal with this
problem.

I hope this cleared out some of your confuseness, if not, please let me know
and I will try again.

Cheesle
```

---

<a id="t-870fbf52df"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Examples — 6 msgs

### COBOL Examples

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3773EEEB.3CCD9CC4@home.com>`

```
Michael,

Your reply June 25 to "How to write a macro substitution in acucobol?".
Nicely done, as usual, (just as you did in the days of the old
MicroFocus Compuserve forum) - a concise example to the writer's
problem.

Perhaps, amongst others, Bill Klein will contribute to this.

I was about to start "Before it gets out of hand", but it is already 
out of hand - the number of COBOL links - you could spend all day just
cruising in the flotsam looking for gold nuggets on how to use COBOL.

We need a COBOL Master site initially in the following four categories
:-

1- Standards 

Please, a simple organizational chart showing which committee does what
and inter-relationship to ISO, UML etc. No great detail, just a summary
box-chart with links to specific detailed sites. Obviously this is an
area that should include copies of the current and any proposed
standards.

2 - Examples

Needs control so that we don't get overwhelmed with variations of the
same thing, but a resource that covers all the COBOL syntax, examples
being contributed by COBOL developers. Take the message you just sent,
it probably should be listed alphabetically under both "string" and
"reference modification".

Each bit of syntax should have its own examples :-

- Take evaluate; until you look at somebody else's code, it is not
immediately apparent that :-

	evaluate char
	  when char = "a"  invoke/perform "a-and-b-routine"
	  when char = "b"  invoke/perform "a-and-b-routine"
	  when char = "c"  .........

could have been written :-

	evaluate char
	  when char = "a"  
	  when char = "b"  invoke/perform "a-and-b-routine"
	  when char = "c"  .........

And, of course, many variations on the above.

Similarly, first time I used intrinsic functions - following the text
from the M/S COBOL manuals I put :-

		a = function numvalc (my-text)

My query threw their Tech Support guy. He went away, and having spoken
to another techhie, came back with the reply, "it should be :-

		compute a = function numvalc (my-text)

It is only in the last couple of years, looking at others' code, that I
found it could be coded :-

		move function numvalc (my-text) to a

Don't want to flog it to death, but you get the idea.


3 - Compilers & Toolkits

A summary of all known compilers and COBOL-specific toolkits, including
shareware, covering all operating systems with links to each .

4 - Interesting COBOL Sites

Not quite sure how you "name" this one but links to your own site and
others. Can be a bit questionable, some fall into the category of
"vanity publishing". There is one site where the author covers OO -
beautiful elegant coding, but when you look at his current examples,
doesn't do a damn thing for OO - your reaction would be "why should I do
this in OO?". I can achieve the same with performs.

That's it. Jump in folks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

PS : Funny lot MicroSoft - don't seem to comprehend us Canucks. If I
threw a stone hard enough, south-west of me, I could smash one of the
windows in Bill's University at Bellingham Wa. When using their COBOL
if I got mail from them it came postmarked BRUSSELS - yep, Belgium!
Maybe Bill should give up airmail and put his trust in Amtrak and CPR.
```

```
will be legal in the next one.  The example with the COMPUTE is the
"portable" way to do it today.
```

<a id="t-3c5a35e259"></a>
## Thread: Time estimations — 13 msgs

### Time estimations

- **From:** "Vince Hayes" <bigvince@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco>`

```
I have been programming for about 3 years and now I am currently writing
specs for others to code.   My question does any one know of any methods of
estimating the time needed for a program to be coded. My supervisor wants me
to come up with a method that I use, and that others can use.
```

```
Sorry Vince, but this has been beaten to death. Managers are always
looking for ways to quantify something that is part skill - part
intuition - part talent. Could you put an hour limit on a surgeon? You
never know what you are going to find till you open up that can o'
legacy and start stirring that ole code up.

George K

On Fri, 25 Jun 1999 21:00:20 -0400, "Vince Hayes"
<bigvince@bellsouth.net> wrote:

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37746FC5.F7B86EA@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <377461a3.17162347@news1.lig.bellsouth.net>`

```
The most accurate method is to ask the programmer and add 25% -
and track the programmers so you know the fudge factor for next
time. The most common method used is to pick an arbitrary number
usually based on some political compromise with no input from
those who have to deliver it.  Or take the programmer's estimate
and subtract 30% - this is not accurate but it makes people happy
(for a while). 

Seriously, there is a whole literature on estimating projects -
do a search at Amazon. Look for "function points" if all else
fails. Make sure you are clear about what they have to do (eg how
much testing and documenting). One book I liked was called "Rapid
Development".

Tim Josling

George K wrote:
> 
> Sorry Vince, but this has been beaten to death. Managers are always
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3774C451.458C582C@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <377461a3.17162347@news1.lig.bellsouth.net> <37746FC5.F7B86EA@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Tim Josling wrote:
> 
> much testing and documenting). One book I liked was called "Rapid
> Development".
> 

I will second that recomendation.  Steve McConnell is a procedures
man, he likes to lay out a track for you to follow.  If you want a
more pragmatic veiw look to Steve Maguire in debugging the
development process.  (there is a boxed set containing the three
bibles of process improvement at Amazon for about $60 US.)

Most estimates are done by the rule of thumb.  It requires a
really good understanding of the application to get it right most
of the time.  Scientific estimates are a skill and must be
carefully learned.

Whatever you do not add the 25% fudge factor.  Read up on critical
chain management.  If you pad, the pad will always be used!

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37745C32.4283C080@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco>`

```
Vince Hayes wrote:
> 
> I have been programming for about 3 years and now I am currently writing
> specs for others to code.   My question does any one know of any methods of
> estimating the time needed for a program to be coded. My supervisor wants me
> to come up with a method that I use, and that others can use.

As a former Systems & Programming Manager and now a loner, (call me
Lonesome Dove if you like - they film the TV series 30 miles west of
Calgary!), very briefly :-

1 - If anybody suggests using formulae, ( x lines of code equals y
man-hours), file it where it belongs - in the garbage bin.

2. There are essentially three criteria :-

  (i) In-house standards :-
       - for specifying the description of the problem to the 
	 programmer
       - shop standards on how programs should be written

  (ii) Program difficulty :-
	  (a) Complex (possible candidate for simplifying)
	  (b) Intermediate
	  (c) Simple

 (iii)  Programmer Proficiency :-
	  (a) Highly skilled
	  (b) Intermediate
	  (c) Beginner

What you are doing is matching up group (ii) against group (iii). Before
you become a target for attack by prima donnas, (God knows there are
plenty in data processing), try and seek consensus on what you come up
with. Your three years is OK but hardly makes you a seasoned programmer.
If necessary contact local user groups. Discuss with your own people why
it took x weeks/months to do a particular job, mentally grading programs
and people into the categories I've indicated above.

In the end it becomes a judgement call - the person dishing out specs
to programmers makes the decision on how long it should take. Make sure
your Manager/Supervisor is behind you before you take the first step.

You may get other suggestions to look at text books. Certainly read
them, but keep an open mind - these consulting guys write lots of blurb
for a living. (Consultant - somebody who borrows your watch to tell you
the time. Place the emphasis on the CON part of that word !)

Time Estimating is a topic one could write reams about - hope the above
helps.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8y8d3.2792$Z53.30688@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <37745C32.4283C080@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>  (ii) Program difficulty :-
>   (a) Complex (possible candidate for simplifying)
>   (b) Intermediate
>   (c) Simple


I agree with you, but suggest adding another category:

  (ii) Program difficulty :-
   (a) Super Complex (requires significant research)
   (b) Complex (possible candidate for simplifying)
   (c) Intermediate
   (d) Simple

Some systems are so complex or difficult that you can't do a detailed
design until you do some work to develop methods which do not yet exist,
or are not commonly available.  There is almost always a tiny bit of
this, but some systems break so much new ground that a lot of fundamental
research is required to learn 'how', or even 'if', you can do it.  These
systems can be the most fun, but they can break your head, too. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3775313B.6D9F6E95@home.com>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <37745C32.4283C080@home.com> <8y8d3.2792$Z53.30688@news2.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> systems can be the most fun, but they can break your head, too. :-)

Judson, 

Not sure I agree with your addition, "super-complex". Bear in mind I'm
coming at this from a system's perspective.

Initially the Systems Manager/Analysts, (back when analysis and
programming were separate jobs) are between a rock and hard place,
trying to get a clear-cut request from the users and then doing the
impossible, trying to match the users' demand for when the system will
be up and running and at the same time being aware of what programming
resources will be available, (on-going maintenance and finish-off for
current systems). A seasoned analyst has to stick his neck out and come
up with a target date for the users, before he has even talked to the
Chief/Senior Programmer. 

Yes systems can be complex - but this is the result of using egg-heads
to design, when they should be restricted to a think-tank role. Such
people can be an asset to a DP department when introducing new
innovations for the first time, e.g. optical character recognition,
point-of-sale, automation of warehousing etc. BUT, these people should
never be allowed to get too close to the users, and God forbid that you
actually let them design the system - Pass their brilliant concepts on
to a pedestrian analyst who will arrive at a workable solution. 

The point I'm making is that your category "super complex" evolves
because it was an egg-head who arrived at the original system concept.
As soon as a Chief/Senior Programmer sees the system divided into
program specs - he should immediately be banging on the DPM's door -
"This aint gonna fly". And as I wrote, even my category "complex" should
perhaps be given some analysis to see if it can be broken down into
acceptable modules.

Just throw this in for good measure - if and when the COBOL fraternity
gets into OO - then this is a completely new ballgame - conceptually it
is a new way of thinking, and with its reusability can take away a lot
of that complexity we are both referring to. 

An example of things that can go wrong because of complexity - I believe
it was the London Stock Exchange. I think we can all appreciate how
complex the LSE or NYSE systems would be. Some guy was appointed Manager
and over a period of years built up a team of analysts and programmers -
from memory I think they hit 300. They were buzzing around for about 5
years discussing the whole thing conceptually, and presumably started
programming. The Chairman got PO'd at constant delays and called in
consultants. The good news, "It's cost you 5 million so far and is
coming along", the bad news, "You are going to have to spend another 5
million before it's finished!" The chairman fired everybody.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Mzsd3.1130$mi1.9541@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <37745C32.4283C080@home.com> <8y8d3.2792$Z53.30688@news2.mia> <3775313B.6D9F6E95@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
>
>The point I'm making is that your category "super complex" evolves
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>into acceptable modules.

I understand your comments about getting "egg-heads" involved. :-)
But I am talking about systems where the user's requirements are so
complex and difficult to meet, that the challenge can be met, if at
all, only with the best, most experienced people.  Anything less and
it simply will never fly, even poorly.  These systems are by nature
highly complex, and cannot be built with a simple design, though of
course, you simplify the design as much as possible.  KISS has been
my motto for a long time. :-)

Let me give you a real world example that I have mentioned in the
newsgroups before.  In late 1996 I was contracted by the local county
government to rewrite their automobile license system.  I did the
design of their old system in 1977 and knew it would be a very large,
tough system.  A long time friend and associate who has a masters in
CS, has taught university level CS, and 20 years experience in and
around NASA, agreed to help.  My friend was totally flabbergasted by
the complexity of this system.  Legislatures think in terms of what
is politically expedient, not what will work in practice, and
bureaucracies are even worse.  The maze of rules involving issuing the
well over 200 different kinds of license plates in Alabama, with new
types of plates being created almost every month, is bad enough, but
the unbelievably vast number of exceptions, and exceptions to the
exceptions, is truly mind-boggling.  The county had seven programmers
totally dedicated simply to maintaining the old system.  To address
this amazingly complex situation, we designed as much flexibility into
the system as possible.  There is a data dictionary which defines the
basic field edits and valid values, and a host of table files used to
largely define how a given type of license is calculated.  The system
has help available for every field on every screen.  The help system
can search through lists of codes or valid field entries, and allow
the user to select, and the value is automatically inserted into the
screen.  I have a reputation for making complex things simple to use,
but it was a major effort just to teach the users how to maintain the
description tables.  Not from complicated user interface, which is
clean and straightforward, but because the concepts were so difficult
for non-techie users.  And this table driven approach was an important
design goal the users really wanted.  Now they can usually create a
new license type on the fly with no programmer overhead.  But that was
the easy part, for us.  There are areas in that system where field
validation edits and procedural algorithms must meet so many illogical
and often conflicting laws and regulations, that we are still not sure
that a logic which would entirely meet all requirements for a few of
those algorithms can even exist.  Some of those algorithms took weeks
to develop, and they were so complex that you couldn't grasp the whole
thing in your head at once.  We had to prototype those and keep working
on them until they were correct.  Believe me, this is something I
seldom have to do.  Yes, I may prototype an algorithm to debug it, but
not because I can't understand the whole thing.  Even so, the system
came up and flew the first time, with an extremely low count of minor
bugs (my friend and I said many a prayer of thanks over that! :).  When
we were through, I asked my friend how it compared with scientific
projects at NASA.  He thought a while and said "The only thing I can
compare it to is a stress analysis of an huge, complicated structure
for the space station.  Two successive teams of programmers literally
refused to continue working on it, saying it couldn't be done.  The
analysis would not have been done, except NASA found a programming
genius with a Ph.D. in math, who finally made it work."

The design requirements were created mostly by the users, the county
MIS staff and us.  I did all of the design myself, with a great deal
of assistance from my friend.  No 'egg-head' was involved. :-)  I've
built over 60 computer systems, including some that I was told were
impossible.  But I have only seen one this though.  The second toughest
was designing the old system this one replaced, back in 1977.
```

#### ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l2ls3$46k$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco>`

```
Guess, then triple it. When you have about ten years experience, then you
can probably get away with doubling it.  After thirty, you realize that it
is impossible unless you have worked with the programmer that is doing it
for ten years ... but that taking their guess and doubling it might work.

Another approach is to find out how much time is available, and doubling
that.

The third is to say something like "three weeks after the last change in
specifications".  I rather like that approach myself, as it is closer to the
truth ... no program ever gets finished.


Vince Hayes wrote in message ...
>I have been programming for about 3 years and now I am currently writing
>specs for others to code.   My question does any one know of any methods of
>estimating the time needed for a program to be coded. My supervisor wants
me
>to come up with a method that I use, and that others can use.
>
>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37760B91.EEDFE7D9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <7l2ls3$46k$2@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> Guess, then triple it. When you have about ten years experience, then
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The truth is that we must quote to get the work. The basic problem
is false precision.

Do you quote 365 days or 1 year.  If you quote 365 days then it
had better be correct.  If it is one year there is an implicit
plus or minus a month in there.

The project will start at 3 man years, go to 34 or 38 man months
as things progress, and only on the final day will you really get
the number of days.

The uncertainty funnel comes into play here.  As the project
progresses the certainty of your quote must become more accurate. 
The experience you have learned and understanding what the user
really wants (or how little he really understands his wants)
generates a certainty factor.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Time estimations

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l6ir8$bu8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<bCVc3.996$fI5.100@news2.mco> <7l2ls3$46k$2@news.igs.net> <37760B91.EEDFE7D9@zip.com.au>`

```

Ken Foskey wrote in message

>The truth is that we must quote to get the work. The basic problem
>is false precision.


<big snip>

While I agree, the fact is that I have always worked fixed price.  The basic
problem there is to avoid going bankrupt.<G>.  I am pretty carefull when it
comes to quotations.
```

## Thread: negative number problem — 7 this month / 9 total

### negative number problem

- **From:** Harish Murthy <worm@thewatercooler.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377356D1.129FD609@thewatercooler.com>`

```
Hi,
I have a field which is part of a record and is of  PIC S9(12)V9(3).
when i enter the negative number into this field and
save write this record to a file( line sequential file) thru COBOL,
it puts a 'p' at the end.
example: number stored in the above field is -100.30
and the value stored in the file is 00000000010030p
i dont want this 'p' but a '-' ( minus) sign preceding the number.
like -00000000010030.
Cud u kindly tell me how to do it.
Thanks
Harish
```

#### ↳ Re: negative number problem

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37740206.521@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<377356D1.129FD609@thewatercooler.com>`

```
Harish Murthy wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Harish

In Mainframe COBOL:
   x'F7' = 7 for PIC X and PIC S9
   x'D7' = -7 for PIC S9
   x'D7' = "P" for PIC X

You can reformat it for display purposes by defining a new variable;
	02  REFORMAT-NUM PIC ----,---,---,--9.999.
Then move it before displaying it;
	MOVE my-value to REFORMAT-NUM
	DISPLAY REFORMAT-NUM

Pete
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: negative number problem

- **From:** "Jack Lam" <jack_lam_98@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l1poq$61s$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<377356D1.129FD609@thewatercooler.com> <37740206.521@saif.com>`

```
Correct me if I am wrong; I have done a lot of programming in pl1 but not in
COBOL.
For Harish question, I think she should declare her variable as example:
 01 Record-io.
    02 CustName  PIC X(30)
    02 CAmount   PIC s9(12)V99 SIGN IS LEADING SEPARATE CHARACTER.

Note:  You will get this result -00000000010030  for -100.30
          and                            +00000000010030 for   100.30

Let's learn COBOL together.  Tell me is this correct. Thank.

Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com> wrote in message news:37740206.521@saif.com...
> Harish Murthy wrote:
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete
```

## Thread: Cobol data to TXT-ascii file — 1 msgs

## Thread: Makefile for Microsoft Nmake V1.2 running under Project Work Bench — 2 msgs

### Makefile for Microsoft Nmake V1.2 running under Project Work Bench

- **From:** "Chloe" <greg@primesoft.co.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kulgv$pud$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Can anyone tell me what the structure of a Makefile is that will compile and
link all .cbls to produce corresponding .exes in the current directory only
for the exes that are out of date?
This should be easy but not being a programmer the manuals are a little
tricky to understand. All makefiles I have been able to automatically
produce with PWB link all .objs to produce a single exe not individual
.exes.
I have experimented with pre-defined inference rule .cbl.exe and pre-defined
macro $< and suspect I have to do something with filename macro $* but can't
get it to work. The current directory contains about 180 .cbls
Any help would be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Makefile for Microsoft Nmake V1.2 running under Project Work Bench

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3773858A.6B4287C7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7kulgv$pud$1@newsource.ihug.co.nz>`

```
Chloe wrote:
> 
> Can anyone tell me what the structure of a Makefile is that will
> compile and link all .cbls to produce corresponding .exes in the
> current directory only for the exes that are out of date?

> This should be easy but not being a programmer the manuals are a
> little tricky to understand. All makefiles I have been able to
> automatically produce with PWB link all .objs to produce a single exe
> not individual .exes.

> I have experimented with pre-defined inference rule .cbl.exe and
> pre-defined macro $< and suspect I have to do something with filename
> macro $* but can't get it to work. The current directory contains
> about 180 .cbls

> Any help would be appreciated.


For a thorough discussion on Make look at the GNU make manual at
http://www.ultranet.com/~pauld/gmake/  (about 122 pages).  It is a
superset but most of what you want is in there just try and keep
things simple.

You can use GNU make though I have not tried it (yet).

To see a multiple source version of make download the parser
project from http://www.zipworlc.com.au/~waratah (yes I finally
got one) and look at it's make file.  It creates different targets
but to create a bank of programs make the default tag (the first
one) contain a list of programs.

I will eventually refine this makefile.  Email me any questions.

Good luck
Ken
```

<a id="t-2d4f1f369b"></a>
## Thread: How to write a macro substitution in acucobol? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to write a macro substitution in acucobol?

- **From:** Giorgio Cordini <gicordin@tin.it>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377371A5.A4E7F5B6@tin.it>`

```
Hello.
I am desperate. I have to move the content of a variable whose name is
contained in another variable in Acucobol.
But how to do it?

Ex.:
I know that in the variable salute there is "hello".
Now I move "salute" to title...
... how to obtain "hello" from title?

Thanks
Giorgio
```

#### ↳ Re: How to write a macro substitution in acucobol?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fCMc3.188$wC4.6627@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<377371A5.A4E7F5B6@tin.it>`

```
01  Work-Fields.
     10  SALUTE    PIC X(30) VALUE " Howdy and Hello, World".
     10  TITLE        PIC X(30) VALUE SPACE.
     10  SUB1        PIC s9(04) COMP.
      10 MaxPoint  PIC S(9(04) COMP  VALUE +0.
     10  FIVE          PIC S(04) COMP VALUE IS +5.


MOVE 1 TO SUB1
COMPUTE MaxPoint = (LENGTH OF SALUTE - 5)
PEFORM
    IF  SALUTE (Sub1:FIVE) = "Hello"
         MOVE Salute (Sub1:FIVE ) TO Title
         EXIT PERFORM
    ELSE
        ADD 1 TO SUB1
        IF  SUB1 GREATER MAXPOINT
           EXIT PERFORM
        END-IF
    END-IF
END-PERFORM.
IF Title GREATER SPACE
   DISPLAY "Found ""Hello"" IN SALUTE"
ELSE
   DISPLAY "Not Found"
END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to write a macro substitution in acucobol?

- **From:** "John H. Lindsay" <eil@aracnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3773997E.4355ECE3@aracnet.net>`
- **References:** `<377371A5.A4E7F5B6@tin.it>`

```
Hello Giorgio Cordini et al.:

You wrote:

................

> Ex.:
> I know that in the variable salute there is "hello".
> Now I move "salute" to title...
> ... how to obtain "hello" from title?

.................

What you want to do isn't a macro substitution (that takes place
either before or in the earliest stages of compilation).  Rather, it
is an attempt to use the contents of one variable to designate
another variable during execution, and depending on
implementation, that goes under several names: pointer or
reference variables (the variables whose contents are used to
designate the second variable), and based or parametric variables
(variables designated) for instance, or perhaps more to your
point, associative variables (referenced using the character
contents of one variable as the character string name of another
variable as found in the source program).

Cobol doesn't have such things (at least not as of when I taught
it about 10 years ago).  The attitude of the Cobol standards
people seems to be to steer clear of anything that is a bit of a
mind-bender in English.  Other languages of course do have
such things, but if you are in a Cobol environment, you had
better use what you have at hand.

Do try another tack, even if it is a long chained IF .... ELSE IF ...
...... to handle all the possibilities, but be sure to include an
unconditional ELSE at the end of it to catch the possibility of
bad character data which references no variable in your program.
```

- **Date:** 1999-06-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l0afu$8ga$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
//**************************************************************
//
//  http://www.silicontide.com/software/development/compilers/
//
//**************************************************************


Hi,

(1) First, I've recently developed a search system.  I have added
approximately 90 compiler/language websites to the database.
These sites, so far, are the author's sites.  Feel free to
check it out.  Hopefully you will find it useful.

(2) Secondly, if you know of any sites that are of value, please
submit them.

(3) Thirdly, all suggestions are welcome.  Please e-mail me with
any ideas you think would improve the system.

And finally, a little bug that I've discovered in approximately 85%
of non-Commercial search engines, is a failure when  "++" is entered.
The script will not complete.  I have been working on since I
discovered it last night.  It will be fixed soon.


http://www.silicontide.com/
http://www.silicontide.com/software/development/compilers/index.html


Have a great Day!!!


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Share what you know. Learn what you don't.
```

#### ↳ Re: File error code "02" in Fujitsu

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l2p1o$62p$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<6t4d3.7473$o6.122294@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
You have to treat the two digits separately.  The "0" means the read was ok.
The "2" means that it is one of more than one key of this value.  So '02'
means you read a valid record but there is at least one legal record
following with the same key.

C Apfelbeck wrote in message
<6t4d3.7473$o6.122294@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>...
>I am using Fujitsu cobol and am getting a file error code of "02" when
>attempting a read next on an indexed file.  Any ideas or help would be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> jeffrey p. ward wrote:
> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> compute average = ( a + b ) / 2

How about

Compute average-value = Function Mean (a b)

(which would also allow a and b to be values in a table - so you could
expand the calculation to)

Compute average-value = Function Mean (each-input-value (all))
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help...college student needs help w/cobol prgm.

- **From:** "Jerry Peacock" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OJzd3.193$H56.40270@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37757B5B.A3F8FF11@concentric.net> <37758324.F608EA1B@home.com> <7l5t9h$qij@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7l5t9h$qij@dfw-

> Compute average-value = Function Mean (each-input-value (all))

Oops! Average isn't the Mean. Function Mean (1 2 99) is "2" and
the average is "34".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help...college student needs help w/cobol prgm.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l6lmj$4tu@dfw-ixnews11.ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<37757B5B.A3F8FF11@concentric.net> <37758324.F608EA1B@home.com> <7l5t9h$qij@dfw-ixnews15.ix.netcom.com> <OJzd3.193$H56.40270@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry Peacock <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:OJzd3.193$H56.40270@typhoon01.swbell.net...
>
> William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe in mathematics (and/or statistics) the MEAN is not the "average" in
this sense, but the following is (verbatim - from the draft of the next
Standard - which does match the current Standard for this) what it says
about FUNCTION MEAN:

"The equivalent arithmetic expression shall be as follows:
    a) For one occurrence of argument-1,
        (argument-1)
    b) For two occurrences of argument-1,
        ((argument-1 sub1 + argument-1 sub2 ) / 2)
    c) For n occurrences of argument-1,
        ((argument-1 sub1 + argument-1sub2 +... + argument-1 subn ) / n)"

(I have used "sub1" "sub2" and "subn" where the text of the Standard uses
subscript.  Unfortunately, actual "subscripts" - of this type - don't show
up well in NGs.)

FYI, if you want a COBOL function that will return "2" from the list (1 2
99), then you need to use the FUNCTION MEDIAN.
```

<a id="t-5a6fa3347d"></a>
## Thread: mf, COBPATH, and many programs — 2 msgs

### mf, COBPATH, and many programs

- **From:** "Michael E. Potter" <mep@xnet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7l3rr2$ht2$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```
I am using MicroFocus COBOL on UNIX.

mf using an environment variable, COBPATH, to set the directory
path to the programs to be executed.  I have thousands of programs
in many directories so the COBPATH method does not lend itself
to my situation. i.e. my COBPATH will be huge and I will have
a correspondingly large search time to find programs.

I _do_ have a technique to determine the exact location of the
program based on the name of the program.

Does anyone know of a technique in mf COBOL that will allow me
to directly point to the program to be executed rather than
use COBPATH.

here is the original code:
-------------------------------------------
              CALL "program".
-------------------------------------------

here are the methods I have considered:

1) -------------------------------------------
              CALL "dir/subdir/program".

    I would rather not use that method as the programmer will
    have to hard code the dir and subdir.  Plus this is not
    easy when the program name is a variable.
-------------------------------------------

2) -------------------------------------------
              CALL MYCALL "program".

    Using my own program to resolve the name and in turn call the
    correct program will not work when variables are passed to program.
-------------------------------------------

3) -------------------------------------------
   COBPATH=dir

              CALL "subdir/program".

    Has anyone gotten this to work?  I only get program not found.
    plus this will not work when the program name is a variable.
-------------------------------------------

4) -------------------------------------------
          01 NewProgName PIC X(80).


              CALL MYPROGCONVERTED NewProgName "program".
              CALL NewProgName.

      Actually, i have not tried this yet.  please comment.
-------------------------------------------

allow me to add one more twist:
anything i do to change the program has to be via a preprocessor.
the original program runs on MVS; for ease of maintenance I
do not want to change the program by hand.
```

#### ↳ Re: mf, COBPATH, and many programs

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3775945e@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<7l3rr2$ht2$1@flood.xnet.com>`

```
Method nbr 4 is the way to go.
You resolve the program name in a central utility
program according to whatever rules you want to use.
You might want to have a dummy call of the program inside the central
utility to be sure hte linker links in the program:

move "prog1" to pgm-to-call
call "myconvpgm" using pgm-to-call
call pgm-to-call

------------

myconvpgm:
if pgm-to-call = "prog1"
    move "c:\dir1\dir2\prog1" to pgm-to-call
else
if pgm-to-call = "prog7"
    move "d:\mydir1\prog7" to pgm-to-call
else
...

move pgm-to-call to dummy
if dummy = "1"
    call "c:\dir1\dir2\prog1"
else
if dummy = "2"
    call "d:\mydir1\prog7"
else
....




Michael E. Potter <mep@xnet.com> wrote in message
news:7l3rr2$ht2$1@flood.xnet.com...
> I am using MicroFocus COBOL on UNIX.
>
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
> potter mep@xnet.com
```

## Thread: Newbie to Cobol Print problem — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Newbie to Cobol Print problem

- **From:** thomas@blackjack.force9.net
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<377658e7.4386964@NEWS.FORCE9.NET>`

```
Can anyone please please help. I am trying to set my printer (canon
bjc 4200) to run with my cobol programs using print control codes
how do I do this with code?
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie to Cobol Print problem

- **From:** pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com (PAL3000)
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37766648.917405@news.enteract.com>`
- **References:** `<377658e7.4386964@NEWS.FORCE9.NET>`

```
You can send Hex codes to the printer by using

  01 PRTR-CODE       PIC X(2)   X"FFFF".

The X in front of the " indicates a hexidecimal value.
THen do something like

MOVE PRTR-CODE TO PRT-REC.
WRITE PRT-REC WITHOUT ADVANCING.

Paul

On Sun, 27 Jun 1999 17:06:05 GMT, thomas@blackjack.force9.net wrote:

>Can anyone please please help. I am trying to set my printer (canon
>bjc 4200) to run with my cobol programs using print control codes
>how do I do this with code?

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out the
words REMOVETHIS from my email address
```

```
I can receive a kick in the shin - and will respond - but that doesn't
mean the kicker was doing an appropriate thing.

No question that I ("and most others") can receive HTML. But some
HTML commands screw up the newsgroup reader and subsequent
messages are hard or impossible to process (like setting font sizes).

My newsgroup reader (Outlook) even pops up a message when I
try to post a message with HTML ("You are about to send HTML to a
newsgroup. Most newsgroups consider this impolite, mean, evil,
and in really bad form. Do you really want to do this and be
considered a pariah and have your children stoned?")
```

## Thread: negative number problem — 1 msgs

### Re: negative number problem

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-06-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3776fc2d@news3.us.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<377356D1.129FD609@thewatercooler.com> <377359ac@news3.us.ibm.net> <3776f665.52397856@news1.ibm.net>`

```
I have seen people at least be surprised when sorting Text data containing
such numbers,
where in ASCII '+' is smaller than '-', but these are subtle effects. I
really didn't imply
that there was anything wrong with the construct. It is clearly the way to
go.

Anyway, sorting SIGNED numeric values as text is never a good thing to do.
As I said, the SIGN SEPARATE clause is how to do it.

news:3776f665.52397856@news1.ibm.net...
```

```
there is even something called the harmonic mean (also not supported by
Cobol).
It is the mean obtained by taking the reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of
the
reciprocals of a set of non-zero numbers.
```
